<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — 3-Mark Questions

### **Q1.** List any **six** factors to be considered in selecting a microcontroller for an embedded application. *(KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2)*

**Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

1. **Computing performance** — speed, instruction cycle, data width.
2. **Memory size** — on-chip ROM and RAM headroom.
3. **I/O and peripheral requirement** — number of ports, ADC, PWM, serial.
4. **Power consumption** — active and sleep current.
5. **Tool-chain availability** — compiler, debugger, IDE.
6. **Cost and packaging** — unit cost, supply voltage, form factor.
7. *(Bonus point if asked eight)* — upgradability and second-sourcing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often list *only* "speed" and "cost". At least **six distinct factors** must appear, each on a separate line, to score full marks.

---

### **Q2.** Compare the **8051** and **8052** microcontrollers in terms of on-chip memory, timers, and interrupts. *(KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2)*

**Model Answer (tabular, 3 marks):**

| Feature | 8051 | 8052 |
|---|---|---|
| On-chip ROM | 4 KB | 8 KB |
| On-chip RAM | 128 bytes | 256 bytes |
| Timers | 2 (T0, T1) | 3 (T0, T1, T2) |
| Interrupts | 5 (Reset, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, Serial — 6 if counted as vectors) | 6 vectors (adds T2) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many texts say 8051 has *5* interrupts and 8052 has *6*; the actual **vector count** is **6 and 8** respectively if you count the reset + the two UART flags as separate. Be explicit: *"8051 has 5 interrupt sources with 6 vectors including reset; 8052 has 6 sources with 8 vectors."*

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module 2 Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks** *(KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2)*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **internal architecture of the 8051** and list its salient features. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **factors to be considered in selecting a microcontroller** for an embedded system. Justify why the 8051 is the most widely used 8-bit family. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (a) — 7 marks

The 8051 internal architecture consists of:

1. **CPU (8-bit ALU, Accumulator A, B register, PSW, SP, DPTR)** — executes the CISC instruction set in 1, 2, or 4 machine cycles.
2. **Oscillator and Clock Circuit** — drives the entire chip from a single external crystal; the 12-to-1 divider produces the machine-cycle clock.
3. **Program Memory (4 KB internal ROM, expandable to 64 KB external)** — holds the executable code, accessed via PC through the 16-bit address bus.
4. **Data Memory (128 B internal RAM, expandable to 64 KB external)** — split into four 8-byte register banks, a 16-byte bit-addressable region, and a 80-byte general-purpose scratch.
5. **Special Function Registers (SFR area 0x80–0xFF)** — 21 control registers including P0, P1, P2, P3, TCON, TMOD, TL0, TH0, TL1, TH1, SCON, SBUF, IE, IP, PCON, PSW, ACC, B, SP, DPL, DPH.
6. **Four 8-bit I/O ports P0–P3** — 32 bidirectional lines; P0 is open-drain (needs pull-ups) and is multiplexed with the lower address/data bus in external-memory mode.
7. **Two 16-bit Timers/Counters (T0, T1)** — four operating modes (Mode 0 to Mode 3).
8. **Full-Duplex UART** — SCON, SBUF, independent baud-rate generator (T1 in Mode 2).
9. **Interrupt Controller** — 5 sources (INT0, INT1, T0, T1, Serial) with two priority levels.

**Salient features:**
- 8-bit, CISC, Harvard, 4 KB ROM, 128 B RAM, 32 I/O, 2 timers, UART, 5 interrupts, 12 MHz → 1 µs machine cycle.

> **Marking split:** Block diagram = 3 m, feature list = 2 m, timing/peripheral explanation = 2 m.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (b) — 7 marks

**Factors in selecting a microcontroller** (presented with application check):

1. **Speed and processing power** — must meet deadlines.
2. **Memory size** — ROM ≥ code size, RAM ≥ data + stack.
3. **I/O and peripherals** — pin count, ADC, PWM, communication.
4. **Power consumption** — battery vs mains.
5. **Tool-chain support** — assembler, C compiler (Keil, SDCC), debugger.
6. **Cost and packaging** — DIP for hobby, QFP for production.
7. **Availability and second-source** — multiple vendors.
8. **Future upgradability** — pin- and code-compatible family.

**Why the 8051 is the most widely used 8-bit family:**

1. It was the first popular single-chip 8-bit MCU with on-board ROM, RAM, and I/O (1980).
2. Its **CISC instruction set** is simple to learn — only 255 opcodes, 1-byte opcodes.
3. **Mature tool chain** — KEIL µVision, SDCC, RIDE, IAR — all support it.
4. **Multi-vendor** — Atmel, NXP, Silicon Labs, Maxim, Cypress, Infineon.
5. **Modern variants** have ISP/IAP, 1-cycle core, internal oscillator, ADC, PWM, brown-out reset.
6. **Predictable timing** — 12-clocks-per-machine-cycle is deterministic.
7. **Massive codebase** — every algorithm in every textbook has an 8051 reference.

> **Marking split:** Factor list = 4 m, justification = 3 m.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** *(KTU University Exam — July 2024, Module 2)*

**(a)** Explain the **architecture and features of the 8052 microcontroller** in detail. Compare it with the 8051. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **major variants of the 8051/52 family** available from Intel, Atmel, NXP, Dallas, and Silicon Labs. What is the role of the 8051 in modern industry? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (a) — 7 marks

The **8052** is a **superset** of the 8051, sharing the same pin-out and instruction set.

**Architecture additions over 8051:**

1. **8 KB on-chip program memory** (vs 4 KB) — code can range $0x0000 \rightarrow 0x1FFF$.
2. **256 bytes on-chip RAM** (vs 128 B) — high half ($0x80-0xFF$) overlaps with SFRs, accessed differently.
3. **Timer 2** — 16-bit, with **capture**, **auto-reload**, and **baud-rate generator** modes. Five SFRs: T2CON, RCAP2L, RCAP2H, TL2, TH2.
4. **Additional interrupt vector** for Timer 2 (interrupt address $0x002B$).
5. **Two new SFRs** of the original 8052: T2CON ($0xC8$), and the Timer 2 high-byte range.

**Comparison table (already in Section 2.4) — re-state with the following 5 differences:**
- 4 KB vs 8 KB ROM.
- 128 B vs 256 B RAM.
- 2 vs 3 timers.
- 5 vs 6 interrupt sources.
- 21 vs 26 SFRs.

**Key insight:** The 8052 is a *drop-in upgrade* — 8051 code runs unchanged.

> **Marking split:** Architecture = 3 m, new features = 2 m, comparison table = 2 m.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (b) — 7 marks

**Major variants of 8051/52 (grouped by vendor):**

| Vendor | Part Number | Distinguishing Feature |
|---|---|---|
| Intel | 8031 | ROM-less, needs external EPROM |
| Intel | 8051 / 8052 | Original mask-ROM versions |
| Intel | 8751 / 8752 | UV-EPROM versions for prototyping |
| Atmel (now Microchip) | AT89C51 / C52 | **Flash** (4K/8K) — *the most popular for labs* |
| Atmel | AT89S52 | ISP via SPI, 8 KB flash |
| Atmel | AT89C2051 | 20-pin, 2 KB flash, low-cost |
| NXP / Philips | P89V51RD2 | 64 KB flash, ISP via UART |
| NXP | P89LPC952 | 8051 + 10-bit ADC + PCA + PWM |
| Dallas / Maxim | DS89C450 | **1 machine cycle = 1 clock**, 33 MHz → 33 MIPS |
| Silicon Labs | C8051Fxxx | 25 to 100 MIPS, on-chip ADC/DAC, debug via JTAG |
| Cypress | FX2LP (CY7C68013) | 8051 core + high-speed USB 2.0 |
| Infineon | XC800 | Automotive 8051 with CAN, LIN |

**Role of the 8051 in modern industry:**

1. **Consumer appliances** — washing machines, microwave ovens, induction cooktops, air-conditioner remotes.
2. **Toy and hobby electronics** — RC cars, drones (low-end), robotics kits.
3. **Industrial control** — motor starters, conveyor timers, sensor data loggers, energy meters.
4. **Automotive subsystems** — HVAC flaps, instrument clusters on entry-level cars, anti-theft alarms.
5. **IoT edge nodes** — low-power 8051 + radio (Sub-GHz) for soil-moisture, weather, parking sensors.
6. **Education** — still the **default teaching MCU** in nearly every Indian engineering college.

> **Marking split:** Variants table = 4 m, modern industry role = 3 m.

---

### **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes observed in valuation:**
> 1. **Writing 8051 instead of 8052** in comparison tables — *if the question says 8052, you must answer 8052, not 8051.* 1-mark penalty each time.
> 2. **Confusing the ROM and RAM sizes** — 8051 = 4 KB ROM / 128 B RAM. Reversing them = 0 marks for that sub-question.
> 3. **Forgetting the 12-clocks-per-machine-cycle** fact when computing baud rates or delays.
> 4. **Calling 8051 a "RISC" processor** — it is **CISC**.
> 5. **Listing only 2 or 3 selection factors** when the question asks for 6 — you lose 1 mark per missing factor up to 3.
> 6. **Not drawing the block diagram** in architecture questions — KTU 2024 scheme mandates a diagram for the 7-mark architecture sub-question.
> 7. **Omitting units** in numerical answers (µs vs ms) — 0.5 mark deduction per occurrence.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2: Designing with 8051**

- ✅ The **8051** is an 8-bit, CISC, Harvard-architecture MCU from Intel (1980).
- ✅ **8051 stats to memorise:** 4 KB ROM, 128 B RAM, 4 × 8-bit ports, 2 timers, 5 interrupt sources, 1 UART, 12 MHz crystal → 1 µs machine cycle.
- ✅ **8052 stats to memorise:** 8 KB ROM, 256 B RAM, 3 timers, 6 interrupt sources, 1 UART — *code-compatible superset of 8051*.
- ✅ **Machine cycle** = 12 oscillator periods; instruction time = (1, 2, or 4) × machine cycle.
- ✅ **At least 6 selection factors** must be listed in any 14-mark answer — speed, memory, I/O, power, cost, tools.
- ✅ **8051 survives** because of simplicity, multi-vendor support, and a vast codebase — **not** because it is the fastest or most modern.
- ✅ **Baud rate formula** for 8051 UART (T1 Mode 2, SMOD = 0): $\text{TH1} = 256 - \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{384 \times \text{Baud}}$.
- ✅ **Timer overflow period** (Mode 1, 16-bit): $T_{ovf} = (65536 - X) \times \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}}$.
- ✅ **AT89C51** (Atmel/Microchip) is the **most popular modern variant** for lab courses — Flash-based, ISP-capable.
- ✅ **DS89C450** (Dallas) is the **fastest 8051** — 33 MIPS in single-cycle mode.
- ✅ **Cypress FX2LP** is the **USB-enabled 8051** used in USB oscilloscopes, logic analysers, and pendrive controllers.
- ✅ **Harvard architecture** → separate program and data address buses (8051 advantage for concurrent fetch and operand read).
- ✅ **Reset** vector = $0x0000$; all five interrupt vectors are placed at $0x0003$, $0x000B$, $0x0013$, $0x001B$, $0x0023$ for 8051.
- ✅ **8052 adds** interrupt vector at $0x002B$ (Timer 2 overflow).
- ✅ The 8051 is **not obsolete** — it is shipped by the **billion** each year in cost-sensitive and energy-metering applications.
- ✅ For KTU exam: **always draw the block diagram**; **always state the variant** (8051 or 8052) explicitly; **always include units** in numerics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Addressing Modes Supported by 8051
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECIT726 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Addressing Modes Supported by 8051 # 📘 MODULE 2 — Addressing Modes Supported by 8051 ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Addressing Modes in 8051** are the standardized methods by which the CPU specifies the *location* of the *operand(s)* (data) on which an instruction must operate. The 8051 microcontroller family supports **five primary addressing modes** that govern how source and destination operands are referenced during instruction execution: *Immediate, Register, Direct, Register Indirect,* and *Indexed* addressing. The Intel 8051 is an **8-bit** CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer) architecture with a **16-bit** external address bus (capable of addressing **64 KB** of code memory and **64 KB** of external data memory) and an **8-bit** internal data bus. Because the operand location can change between instructions, the *mode* of specifying the address is what we call an **addressing mode**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "How Do You Tell a Delivery Agent Where to Drop a Parcel?" Imagine you are instructing a delivery agent to deliver a package. There are several ways to do it: | Analogy | Mapping to 8051 | Mode Name | |---|---|---| | "Take the parcel labeled **25H**" (the data is *inside* the instruction) | Operand is embedded in the instruction stream | **Immediate** | | "Use the parcel on your **own hand** (R0–R7)" | Operand is already inside a CPU register | **Register** | | "Go to locker number **30H** in the building" | Operand address is given directly (8-bit/16-bit) | **Direct** | | "Locker number is written on **register R0**" | The address is *in a register* | **Register Indirect** | | "Locker number is **DPTR + offset A**" | Sum of base register + accumulator offset | **Indexed** | > [!NOTE] > **Why are addressing modes needed?** > Different programming scenarios (arithmetic, table lookup, memory copy, port I/O) demand different *granularities* of operand specification. The 8051 designers included five modes so that the programmer can write **compact, efficient, and fast** code for any data movement. ### 1.3 Physical/Architectural Constants - **Data word size:** **8 bits** (most internal operations). - **Address bus size:** **16 bits** → Addressable space = $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ bytes = **64 KB**. - **Internal RAM:** **128 bytes** (00H–7FH) — includes *Register Banks*, *Bit-Addressable Area*, and *General Purpose RAM*. - **Special Function Registers (SFRs):** **128 bytes** (80H–FFH) — overlapping with the upper 128 bytes of internal RAM. - **Number of general-purpose registers:** **32** (organized as 4 banks × 8 registers). - **Reset vector address:** **0000H**. - **External interrupt vectors:** 0003H, 000BH, 0013H, 001BH. --- ## 1.4 KTU 2024 — Syllabus Mapping According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (OECIT726), this topic falls under: > **CO2:** *Illustrate the architecture and instruction set of 8051 microcontroller.* > **Bloom's Level:** L2 (Understand) → L3 (Apply). This topic is a **high-frequency 14-mark question** area in End Semester Examinations (ESE). # 🧠 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Addressing Modes — Operational Logic The 8051 instruction set uses different **opcode formats** (1-byte, 2-byte, or 3-byte instructions) depending on the addressing mode. The CPU's *Instruction Decoder* decodes these formats to determine the operand location. ### Mode 1: Immediate Addressing (`#data`) **Logic:** The operand value **is part of the instruction itself** and resides in the program memory (ROM) immediately after the opcode byte. The symbol `#` indicates the data is a *constant*, not an address. **Why use it?** - Loading *constants* (e.g., initializing a counter, setting a delay value). - Fastest way to put a known value into a register. **Operational Steps:** 1. PC fetches the opcode. 2. PC auto-increments and fetches the next byte (the immediate data). 3. The data byte is loaded into the destination operand. **Assembly Examples:** ```assembly MOV A, #25H ; Load accumulator with hex constant 25 MOV R3, #45 ; Load R3 with decimal 45 (= 2DH) MOV DPTR, #2000H ; Load DPTR with 16-bit constant 2000H ORL A, #0F0H ; OR A with bitmask 1111 0000 ``` ### Mode 2: Register Addressing **Logic:** The operand resides in one of the **eight working registers R0–R7** of the currently selected register bank. The instruction encodes the register number (3 bits) within the opcode byte, so **no extra memory fetch** is needed. **Why use it?** - Most *compact and fastest* mode (single-byte instruction). - Default bank is **Bank 0** (addresses 00H–07H); bank selection is controlled by bits **RS1, RS0** in the **PSW (Program Status Word)** register. **Operational Steps:** 1. PC fetches the single-byte opcode. 2. The lower 3 bits of the opcode decode the register number (000 = R0 … 111 = R7). 3. The operation is performed directly on the register. **Register Bank Selection (PSW bits):** $$ \text{PSW} = \text{D7H} = \underbrace{b_7 b_6 b_5 b_4 b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0}_{CY \; AC \; F0 \; RS1 \; RS0 \; OV \; - \; P} $$ | RS1 | RS0 | Bank | Address Range | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 0 | 0 | Bank 0 | 00H – 07H | | 0 | 1 | Bank 1 | 08H – 0FH | | 1 | 0 | Bank 2 | 10H – 17H | | 1 | 1 | Bank 3 | 18H – 1FH | **Assembly Examples:** ```assembly MOV A, R0 ; Copy content of R0 to Accumulator ADD A, R5 ; Add R5 to A INC R3 ; Increment R3 ``` ### Mode 3: Direct Addressing **Logic:** The instruction contains the **8-bit absolute address** of the operand in the **Internal RAM** (00H–7FH) or in the **SFR area** (80H–FFH). The address is provided *explicitly* in the second byte of the instruction. **Why use it?** - The **only** way to access SFRs (P0, P1, P2, P3, TCON, SCON, IE, etc.). - Used to access the bit-addressable area (20H–2FH) and general-purpose RAM byte-by-byte. **Operational Steps:** 1. PC fetches opcode byte. 2. PC fetches the address byte. 3. CPU reads/writes the byte at that internal RAM/SFR address. **Assembly Examples:** ```assembly MOV A, 30H ; Copy contents of RAM location 30H into A MOV 30H, A ; Copy A into RAM location 30H MOV P1, A ; Send A to Port 1 (SFR at address 90H) MOV 90H, A ; Same as above (SFR direct address) ANL P1, #0FH ; AND Port 1 with mask (mixed mode) ``` > [!NOTE] > **SFR addresses ≥ 80H** are addressed as SFRs only by their direct 8-bit address. They are **not** accessible by the upper 128 bytes of internal RAM in the standard 8051. ### Mode 4: Register Indirect Addressing (`@Ri`) **Logic:** The **address** of the operand is held in a *pointer register* — **R0 or R1** (for 8-bit internal/external data memory) or **DPTR** (for 16-bit external data memory). The `@` symbol indicates *contents of*. **Why use it?** - Essential for **table traversal**, **array processing**, and **loop counters**. - The only mode by which R0/R1 can act as a *pointer*. - Used with instructions like `MOVX` (external memory) and `MOVC` (code memory). **Operational Steps:** 1. PC fetches opcode byte. 2. The CPU reads the pointer register (e.g., R0) to get the actual memory address. 3. The CPU reads/writes the byte at that address. **Valid Pointer Registers:** | Instruction | Pointer | Address Space | Range | |---|---|---|---| | `MOV A, @R0` | R0 or R1 | Internal RAM | 00H – FFH (lower 128) | | `MOVX A, @DPTR` | DPTR | External Data Memory | 0000H – FFFFH (64 KB) | | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | A + DPTR | Code Memory (ROM) | 0000H – FFFFH (64 KB) | **Assembly Examples:** ```assembly MOV R0, #30H ; R0 points to RAM[30H] MOV A, @R0 ; A ← contents of RAM[30H] INC R0 ; Point to next location (31H) MOV A, @R0 ; A ← contents of RAM[31H] ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Only **R0, R1, and DPTR** can be used as pointer registers. **R2–R7 CANNOT** be used in register indirect mode. Many students lose marks by writing `MOV A, @R2`. ### Mode 5: Indexed Addressing **Logic:** The effective address is computed as the **sum of a base register + the accumulator offset**. There are two sub-variants: **5a. Program Memory Look-up (DPTR-based):** $$ \text{Effective Address} = \text{DPTR} + \text{A} $$ **5b. Program Memory Look-up (PC-based):** $$ \text{Effective Address} = \text{PC} + \text{A} $$ **Why use it?** - Ideal for implementing **look-up tables (LUTs)** for sine waves, 7-segment codes, ASCII conversion, etc. **Assembly Examples:** ```assembly MOV DPTR, #4000H ; Base address of table MOV A, #05 ; Index 5 MOVC A, @A+DPTR ; A ← code memory [4000H + 5] = [4005H] ``` --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates **all addressing-mode formulas** and byte counts for KTU 2024 exam preparation. | # | Mode | Symbol | Syntax | Operands (Bytes) | Total Instruction Size | Address Space Accessed | |:---:|---|---|---|---|:---:|---| | 1 | Immediate | `#` | `MOV A, #25H` | 1 (opcode) + 1 (data) | **2 bytes** | Program memory (data literal) | | 2 | Register | *(none)* | `MOV A, R0` | 1 (opcode encodes reg) | **1 byte** | R0–R7 of current bank | | 3 | Direct | *(none)* | `MOV A, 30H` | 1 (opcode) + 1 (addr) | **2 bytes** | Internal RAM 00H–7FH, SFR 80H–FFH | | 4 | Register Indirect | `@` | `MOV A, @R0` | 1 (opcode) | **1 byte** | Pointed by R0/R1/DPTR | | 5 | Indexed | `@A+` | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | 1 (opcode) | **1 byte** | Code memory (ROM) | ### Key Equations for Indexed Mode $$ \text{EA}_{\text{DPTR}} = \text{DPTR} + \text{A} \quad \text{(16-bit effective address for look-up tables)} $$ $$ \text{EA}_{\text{PC}} = \text{PC}_{\text{current}} + \text{A} \quad \text{(used with MOVC A, @A+PC)} $$ ### Memory Capacity Equations $$ \text{Internal RAM} = 2^7 = 128 \text{ bytes} \quad (00\text{H} - 7\text{FH}) $$ $$ \text{SFR Space} = 2^7 = 128 \text{ locations} \quad (80\text{H} - \text{FFH}) $$ $$ \text{Code Memory (external)} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 = 64 \text{ KB} $$ $$ \text{External Data Memory} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 = 64 \text{ KB} $$ ### Real-World Engineering Utility - **Direct Addressing** → Configuring hardware SFRs (timer mode, serial baud rate, port direction). - **Indirect Addressing** → Implementing *stack-based parameter passing* and *C compiler-generated pointer code*. - **Indexed Addressing** → Generating arbitrary waveforms (sine, triangular) on DACs using a sine LUT. - **Immediate** → Writing boot-time configuration constants in firmware. - **Register** → Inner-loop arithmetic where 1-byte instructions maximize **MIPS efficiency**. --- ## 2.3 Instruction Encoding — The "Why" Behind the Size > [!NOTE] > **Why are some instructions 1 byte and others 3 bytes?** > The answer lies in *how much information the CPU needs* to find the operand. - **1-byte instructions** → Both opcode + operand reference fit in 8 bits (e.g., register direct). - **2-byte instructions** → Need one extra byte for an 8-bit address or 8-bit data (e.g., direct, immediate with small data). - **3-byte instructions** → Need an extra 2 bytes for a 16-bit address or 16-bit immediate data (e.g., `MOV DPTR, #2000H`). **Total program memory required** for a sequence can be estimated as: $$ \text{Memory}_{\text{used}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{Size}_{\text{opcode}_i} + \text{Size}_{\text{operand}_i} $$ # 🔬 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Immediate vs. Direct Addressing (Decoding the Difference) **Problem:** Explain what happens internally for the two instructions: ```assembly MOV A, #25H ; Instruction 1 (Immediate) MOV A, 25H ; Instruction 2 (Direct) ``` **Step-by-Step Decoding:** ### Instruction 1: `MOV A, #25H` (Immediate Addressing) | Step | Bus Cycle | Action | Result | |:---:|:---:|---|---| | 1 | Fetch Opcode | PC points to opcode `74H` (MOV A, #data) | Instruction Register = `74H` | | 2 | Fetch Operand | PC points to next byte `25H` | Data byte `25H` loaded | | 3 | Execute | `A ← 25H` (data is the constant itself) | A = `25H` | **Execution logic in detail:** $$ \text{Opcode} = 0111\,0100_{\text{b}} = 74\text{H} $$ The decoder identifies the upper nibble `0111` as *MOV A, #data*. The lower nibble `0100` is a don't-care pattern, and the next memory byte is the actual data. $$ \boxed{\text{Final Accumulator} = 25\text{H} = 0010\,0101_{\text{b}} = 37_{10}} $$ ### Instruction 2: `MOV A, 25H` (Direct Addressing) | Step | Bus Cycle | Action | Result | |:---:|:---:|---|---| | 1 | Fetch Opcode | PC points to opcode `E5H` (MOV A, direct) | Instruction Register = `E5H` | | 2 | Fetch Address | PC points to next byte `25H` | Address = `25H` | | 3 | Read Memory | CPU reads internal RAM at address `25H` | Operand value = `??` (depends on RAM) | | 4 | Execute | `A ← [25H]` | A = *contents of* RAM[25H] | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall (CRITICAL):** A *huge* number of students write the wrong answer for the question: *"What is the difference between `MOV A, #25H` and `MOV A, 25H`?"* The **first** loads the *value 25H* into A. The **second** loads A with the *contents of memory location 25H* — which could be **anything** depending on the program state. The `#` symbol makes all the difference. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Indexed Addressing with a Look-up Table **Problem:** A look-up table containing the squares of numbers 0–9 is stored in code memory starting at address **0200H**. Write an 8051 program to read the square of the number in A and store the result in R2. **Solution Program:** ```assembly MOV DPTR, #0200H ; DPTR = base of table MOVC A, @A+DPTR ; A = [0200H + A] = square(A) MOV R2, A ; R2 = result ``` **Step-by-Step Trace** (assume A initially contains 04H, looking up 4² = 16 = 10H): | Step | Cycle | PC | Action | Result | |:---:|:---:|:---:|---|---| | 1 | Fetch | 0000 | Read opcode `90H` (MOV DPTR, #data16) | Decode begins | | 2 | Fetch | 0001 | Read low byte `00H` | DPL = 00H | | 3 | Fetch | 0002 | Read high byte `02H` | DPH = 02H → DPTR = 0200H | | 4 | Fetch | 0003 | Read opcode `93H` (MOVC A, @A+DPTR) | Decode | | 5 | Compute | — | EA = A + DPTR = 04H + 0200H | EA = 0204H | | 6 | Read | — | Read code memory [0204H] | A = 10H (= 16) | | 7 | Fetch | 0004 | Read opcode `FAH` (MOV R2, A) | Decode | | 8 | Execute | 0005 | R2 ← A | R2 = 10H | **Mathematical proof of effective address computation:** $$ \text{EA} = \text{DPTR} + \text{A} = 0200\text{H} + 04\text{H} $$ $$ \text{EA} = \begin{aligned}[t] 0200_{\text{H}} \\ + \; 0004_{\text{H}} \\ \hline 0204_{\text{H}} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{EA} = 0204\text{H} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{A} = \text{CodeMem}[0204\text{H}] = 10\text{H}} $$ The carry bit from the addition is **discarded** because the 8051 wraps around at 16 bits, but in practice the table is placed to avoid overflow. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mode Selection by Operand Type > [!IMPORTANT] > This matrix is the **single most useful table** for KTU exam answers. Use it whenever the question says "Which addressing mode is used by…" | Operand Requirement | Best Mode | Reason | Example Instruction | |---|---|---|---| | A *literal constant* (e.g., delay count) | Immediate | The data must be embedded in the code | `MOV R2, #50` | | Operands *already in CPU registers* | Register | Saves memory, fastest (1 byte) | `ADD A, R3` | | A *specific* RAM byte or SFR | Direct | The exact address is known at assembly time | `MOV P1, A` | | An *array element* or *stack* | Register Indirect | The index/pointer may change at runtime | `MOV A, @R0` | | A *value from a table* in ROM | Indexed | A points to the table index, DPTR to the base | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | | A *byte from external RAM* | Register Indirect (`MOVX`) | Only way to access 64 KB external data | `MOVX A, @DPTR` | ### Decision Flowchart Logic A KTU examiner expects the student to be able to *justify* the mode choice. The justification logic is: $$ \text{Mode} = \begin{cases} \text{Immediate} & \text{if operand is a compile-time constant} \\ \text{Register} & \text{if operand is in R0–R7 and no SFR} \\ \text{Direct} & \text{if operand has a fixed 8-bit address (00H–FFH)} \\ \text{Register Indirect} & \text{if the operand address is computed at runtime} \\ \text{Indexed} & \text{if the operand is in code memory and indexed by A} \end{cases} $$ --- ## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Bit-Size Analysis of an Instruction **Problem:** State the addressing mode, instruction size, and execution time for `MOV 30H, #0A5H`. **Step-by-step analysis:** | Field | Value | Notes | |---|---|---| | Opcode | `75H` (MOV direct, #data) | Identified by assembler | | Address byte | `30H` | First operand is direct address 30H | | Data byte | `A5H` | Second operand is immediate | | **Mode 1** (for source) | Immediate | `#0A5H` | | **Mode 2** (for destination) | Direct | `30H` | **Machine code generation:** $$ \text{Bytes stored in ROM} = \underbrace{75\text{H}}_{\text{opcode}} \; \underbrace{30\text{H}}_{\text{address}} \; \underbrace{\text{A5H}}_{\text{data}} = 3 \text{ bytes} $$ **Machine cycles** (8051 uses 12 clock periods per machine cycle for most MOVs): $$ T_{\text{execution}} = 2 \text{ machine cycles} \times 12 \text{ oscillator periods} = 24 \text{ oscillator periods} $$ > [!NOTE] > For a 12 MHz crystal: $T_{\text{cycle}} = 12 / 12\,\text{MHz} = 1\,\mu s$. So the instruction takes **2 µs** to execute. This kind of calculation is occasionally asked in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions. --- ## 3.5 Comparative Summary Table — One Instruction in Five Modes The instruction `MOV A, X` (copy X into A) can be written in all five modes: | Mode | Instruction | Machine Code (opcode + operand) | Bytes | Comment | |---|---|---|:---:|---| | Immediate | `MOV A, #45H` | `74 45` | 2 | Loads constant 45H | | Register | `MOV A, R4` | `EC` | 1 | Encodes R4 (100) in opcode | | Direct | `MOV A, 45H` | `E5 45` | 2 | Reads from RAM[45H] | | Reg Indirect | `MOV A, @R0` | `E8` (when R0 holds 45H) | 1 | Reads from address in R0 | | Indexed | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | `93` (DPTR=table base, A=index) | 1 | Reads from code memory | # 🖼️ SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 8051 Internal RAM and SFR Memory Architecture The 8051 memory map is the single most important visualization for this topic. Different addressing modes access *different regions* of this map. ```mermaid flowchart TB A[8051 Memory Map]:::root A --> B[Code Memory 64 KB
0000H to FFFFH] A --> C[External Data Memory 64 KB
0000H to FFFFH] A --> D[Internal RAM 128 bytes
00H to 7FH] A --> E[SFR Area 128 bytes
80H to FFH] B --> B1["0000H Reset Vector
0003H Int0
000BH T0
0013H Int1
001BH T1
0023H Serial"] C --> C1["Accessed via MOVX
Only Register Indirect
@R0 @R1 @DPTR"] D --> D1[Register Banks 00H to 1FH] D --> D2[Bit Addressable 20H to 2FH] D --> D3[General Purpose 30H to 7FH] D1 --> D1a[Bank 0: 00H to 07H] D1 --> D1b[Bank 1: 08H to 0FH] D1 --> D1c[Bank 2: 10H to 17H] D1 --> D1d[Bank 3: 18H to 1FH] E --> E1[Port Registers P0 P1 P2 P3] E --> E2[Timer Registers TCON TMOD TH0 TL0 TH1 TL1] E --> E3[Serial Registers SCON SBUF] E --> E4[Control Registers IE IP PCON PSW ACC B SP DPTR] classDef root fill:#1a1a2e,stroke:#e94560,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef mem fill:#16213e,stroke:#0f3460,color:#fff classDef sub fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — How an Addressing Mode Resolves the Operand The CPU's instruction decoder follows a fixed pipeline to resolve any operand. The diagram below shows the *resolution path* for each of the five modes. ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Program Counter PC]:::pc --> F1[Fetch Opcode Byte] F1 --> D[Instruction Decoder]:::dec D -->|Mode Immediate| M1[Fetch Next ROM Byte as Data] D -->|Mode Register| M2[Decode Lower 3 bits as Reg ID] D -->|Mode Direct| M3[Fetch Next Byte as 8 bit Address] D -->|Mode Indirect| M4[Read Pointer R0 R1 or DPTR] D -->|Mode Indexed| M5[Compute EA = DPTR + A] M1 --> X1[Operand Resolved] M2 --> X2[Operand Resolved] M3 --> X3[Read Internal RAM or SFR] M4 --> X4[Read Memory at Pointer] M5 --> X5[Read Code Memory at EA] X1 --> EX[Execute Instruction] X2 --> EX X3 --> EX X4 --> EX X5 --> EX classDef pc fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef dec fill:#4ecdc4,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Indexed Addressing Look-up Table Execution ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant CPU as 8051 CPU participant PC as Program Counter participant ROM as Code Memory participant DPTR as DPTR Register participant A as Accumulator Note over CPU: Instruction: MOVC A, @A+DPTR PC->>ROM: Fetch opcode 93H ROM-->>CPU: 93H (1 byte) CPU->>DPTR: Read base address (e.g., 0200H) CPU->>A: Read offset (e.g., 05H) CPU->>CPU: Compute EA = 0200H + 05H = 0205H CPU->>ROM: Read code memory at 0205H ROM-->>CPU: Data byte (e.g., 19H) CPU->>A: Store result Note over A: A now contains the looked-up value ``` ## 4.4 Mode Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TB ROOT[8051 Addressing Modes]:::r ROOT --> I[Immediate
#data] ROOT --> REG[Register
R0 to R7] ROOT --> DIR[Direct
8 bit address] ROOT --> RI[Register Indirect
@R0 @R1 @DPTR] ROOT --> IDX[Indexed
@A+DPTR or @A+PC] I --> I1["Example: MOV A, #25H"] REG --> REG1["Example: ADD A, R3"] DIR --> DIR1["Example: MOV A, 30H"] RI --> RI1["Example: MOVX A, @DPTR"] RI --> RI2["Example: MOV A, @R0"] IDX --> IDX1["Example: MOVC A, @A+DPTR"] IDX --> IDX2["Example: MOVC A, @A+PC"] classDef r fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#e74c3c,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ## 4.5 Block Diagram — Operand Location for Each Mode ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SRC[Source Operand Location] S1["ROM Program Memory
Immediate Data"] S2["CPU Register R0 R7
Register Mode"] S3["Internal RAM 00H to 7FH
Direct Mode"] S4["SFR 80H to FFH
Direct Mode"] S5["Pointed by R0 R1 DPTR
Register Indirect"] S6["Code Mem DPTR+A or PC+A
Indexed Mode"] end CPU[8051 CPU]:::c DST[Destination A or RAM or SFR]:::c S1 --> CPU S2 --> CPU S3 --> CPU S4 --> CPU S5 --> CPU S6 --> CPU CPU --> DST classDef c fill:#16a085,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` --- > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Interpretation of the diagrams:** The block diagrams collectively show that the 8051's addressing flexibility comes from the *interplay between the PC, the internal RAM/SFR, and external memory*. The same instruction `MOV A, X` can resolve to any of six different *physical sources* (code memory, registers, internal RAM, SFR, external RAM, indexed code) by merely changing the addressing mode — without altering the *opcode category* significantly. # 🎯 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks) --- ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper 1]** **Differentiate between Immediate addressing mode and Direct addressing mode in 8051 with suitable examples. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer (Point-wise):** | Aspect | Immediate Addressing | Direct Addressing | |---|---|---| | Symbol | Uses `#` prefix | No prefix | | Operand meaning | The data value itself | The address of the data | | Example | `MOV A, #25H` | `MOV A, 25H` | | What A receives | The constant `25H` | The *contents* of RAM location `25H` | | Memory locations used | Program memory (ROM) | Internal RAM / SFR | | Instruction size | 2 bytes (`74 25`) | 2 bytes (`E5 25`) | > **Examiner's Note:** Both examples are essential. The `#` symbol is the *key distinguishing feature*. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **List any three addressing modes of 8051. State one instruction for each. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** 1. **Register Addressing** — `MOV A, R0` *(1 byte instruction, R0 in current bank)*. 2. **Direct Addressing** — `MOV A, 30H` *(reads contents of internal RAM location 30H)*. 3. **Register Indirect Addressing** — `MOV A, @R0` *(R0 holds the address; reads from that address)*. > **Examiner's Note:** Mentioning the *effective address* or *what the instruction does* fetches full marks. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > 1. **Always** state the *addressing mode category* before writing each example — partial marks are awarded even if the example has a typo. > 2. **Never** confuse `MOV` and `MOVX` — `MOVX` is used *only* for external data memory (register indirect with `@DPTR` or `@Ri`). > 3. **Always** specify the *byte size* of each instruction in 14-mark answers — it is a frequently missed mark. > 4. For indexed addressing, **mention both** `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` and `MOVC A, @A+PC` for full credit. > 5. Many students lose marks by writing the wrong opcode — **do not memorize wrong opcodes**; the assembler does the conversion. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2 Choice 1]** **(a)** Explain the five addressing modes supported by the 8051 microcontroller with at least one assembly language example for each. Mention the *instruction size* (number of bytes) for each example. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write a program to access a look-up table of 10 bytes stored in code memory starting at address **0300H**. The table contains the cube of numbers 0 to 9. The index is stored in internal RAM location `40H`. Store the result in `R2`. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Question A(a) — 7 Marks** The 8051 supports **five addressing modes**: **1. Immediate Addressing (1 mark)** - The operand is a constant embedded in the instruction. - Symbol: `#` prefix indicates immediate data. - Example: `MOV A, #0FH` — loads the constant 0FH into A. Instruction size = **2 bytes** (`74 0F`). - Used for: Loading constants, initialisation, mask values. **2. Register Addressing (1 mark)** - Operands reside in one of the 8 working registers (R0–R7) of the active bank. - Example: `MOV A, R5` — copies the contents of R5 to A. Instruction size = **1 byte** (`ED`). - Used for: Fast inner-loop arithmetic. **3. Direct Addressing (2 marks)** - The instruction contains the 8-bit address of the operand (Internal RAM 00H–7FH or SFR 80H–FFH). - Example 1: `MOV A, 30H` — reads contents of internal RAM[30H]. Size = **2 bytes** (`E5 30`). - Example 2: `MOV P1, A` — writes A to SFR Port 1 (address 90H). Size = **2 bytes** (`F5 90`). - Used for: Accessing SFRs and named internal RAM locations. **4. Register Indirect Addressing (2 marks)** - The address of the operand is held in a pointer register: R0, R1, or DPTR. - Example 1: `MOV A, @R0` — reads the byte at the address contained in R0. Size = **1 byte** (`E8`). - Example 2: `MOVX A, @DPTR` — reads from external data memory at DPTR. Size = **1 byte** (`E0`). - Used for: Arrays, strings, stack-based parameter passing, external memory access. **5. Indexed Addressing (1 mark)** - Effective address = Base Register + Accumulator offset. - Example: `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` — reads code memory at (DPTR + A). Size = **1 byte** (`93`). - Used for: Look-up tables (LUTs) for wave generation, character conversion. > **Total bytes saved diagram (optional):** 1-byte modes are Register, Indirect, and Indexed; 2-byte modes are Immediate (8-bit) and Direct; 3-byte modes include `MOV DPTR, #data16`. --- #### **Model Solution to Question A(b) — 7 Marks** **Program logic:** 1. Read index $N$ from RAM[40H]. 2. Load DPTR with table base 0300H. 3. Move $N$ into A. 4. Use indexed addressing to fetch the cube from code memory. 5. Store result in R2. **Assembly Program:** ```assembly ORG 0000H START: MOV DPTR, #0300H ; DPTR = base of cube table MOV R0, #40H ; R0 = pointer to index in RAM MOV A, @R0 ; A = N (the index, 0 to 9) MOVC A, @A+DPTR ; A = [0300H + N] = N cubed MOV R2, A ; R2 = result HERE: SJMP HERE ; Infinite loop to stop END ``` **Trace** (assume RAM[40H] = 03H, looking up 3³ = 27 = 1BH): | Step | Action | Result | |---|---|---| | 1 | `MOV DPTR, #0300H` | DPTR = 0300H | | 2 | `MOV R0, #40H` | R0 = 40H | | 3 | `MOV A, @R0` | A = 03H | | 4 | Compute EA = 0300H + 03H | EA = 0303H | | 5 | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | A = 1BH (since 3³ = 27 = 1BH) | | 6 | `MOV R2, A` | R2 = 1BH | **Justification of mode choice:** Indexed addressing is the only way to access code memory for table lookups; the base DPTR is fixed and the index A varies, so `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` is optimal. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2 Choice 2]** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the internal RAM organisation of 8051. How does the register bank selection work using the PSW register? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Compare **Register Indirect Addressing** with **Indexed Addressing** in 8051. State the instructions used, the pointer register, the address space accessed, and the typical application for each. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Question B(a) — 7 Marks** **Internal RAM Organisation (refer Section 4.1 diagram):** The 8051 internal RAM of 128 bytes (00H–7FH) is divided into three functional regions: 1. **Register Banks (00H–1FH)** — 32 bytes arranged as 4 banks of 8 registers each: - Bank 0: 00H–07H (R0–R7, default) - Bank 1: 08H–0FH - Bank 2: 10H–17H - Bank 3: 18H–1FH 2. **Bit-Addressable Area (20H–2FH)** — 16 bytes = 128 individually addressable bits. Bit addresses range from `00H` to `7FH` of the *bit* address space (NOT the byte address space). 3. **General-Purpose RAM (30H–7FH)** — 80 bytes for user data, stack, and variables. **SFR Area (80H–FFH)** — 128 Special Function Registers including P0 (80H), P1 (90H), P2 (A0H), P3 (B0H), ACC (E0H), B (F0H), PSW (D0H), SP (81H), DPTR (82H–83H), etc. **PSW Register and Bank Selection (2 marks):** The **Program Status Word (PSW)** is at SFR address **D0H** and has the following bit layout: $$ \text{PSW} = \underbrace{CY}_{D7} \; \underbrace{AC}_{D6} \; \underbrace{F0}_{D5} \; \underbrace{RS1}_{D4} \; \underbrace{RS0}_{D3} \; \underbrace{OV}_{D2} \; \underbrace{-}_{D1} \; \underbrace{P}_{D0} $$ **Bank selection bits (RS1, RS0):** | RS1 | RS0 | Bank Selected | RAM Range | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 0 | 0 | Bank 0 | 00H – 07H | | 0 | 1 | Bank 1 | 08H – 0FH | | 1 | 0 | Bank 2 | 10H – 17H | | 1 | 1 | Bank 3 | 18H – 1FH | **Example:** To use Bank 2, the programmer executes `SETB RS1` and `CLR RS0` (or `MOV PSW, #10H`). > The **default bank after reset is Bank 0** (because PSW is cleared to 00H). --- #### **Model Solution to Question B(b) — 7 Marks** **Comparison Table:** | Aspect | Register Indirect | Indexed | |---|---|---| | **Syntax** | `MOV A, @R0` / `MOVX A, @DPTR` | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` / `MOVC A, @A+PC` | | **Pointer register** | R0, R1 (8-bit) or DPTR (16-bit) | DPTR or PC (base) + A (offset) | | **Address space** | Internal RAM (R0/R1) or external data memory (DPTR) | Code memory (ROM) only | | **Effective address** | EA = [pointer register] | EA = [base] + [A] | | **Instruction prefix** | `MOV` (internal) or `MOVX` (external) | `MOVC` (code memory) | | **Typical application** | Array traversal, stack, external I/O | Look-up tables, character fonts, wave generation | | **Instruction size** | 1 byte | 1 byte | | **Pointer mutability** | Pointer can be incremented/decremented freely | DPTR is usually fixed; A is the variable index | **Example comparison code:** *Register Indirect (sum 5 array elements stored in RAM 30H–34H):* ```assembly MOV R0, #30H ; Pointer to array start MOV R1, #05 ; Counter MOV A, #00 LOOP: ADD A, @R0 ; Add element to A INC R0 ; Move pointer DJNZ R1, LOOP ; Repeat ``` *Indexed (sine look-up):* ```assembly MOV DPTR, #SINE_TABLE MOV A, ANGLE_INDEX MOVC A, @A+DPTR ; Fetch sine value MOV DAC_PORT, A SINE_TABLE: DB 0, 25, 50, ... ``` **Justification (key insight):** Register indirect addressing is a *runtime pointer dereference*; indexed addressing is a *compile-time base + runtime offset* computation optimised for ROM tables. Both are 1-byte instructions but access fundamentally different memory spaces. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose 1–3 Marks Each:** > > 1. **Forgetting the `#` symbol** — Writing `MOV A, 25H` when they mean `MOV A, #25H`. The `#` changes the meaning entirely. > 2. **Confusing `MOV` and `MOVX`** — `MOVX` is exclusively for **external data memory**; `MOV` handles internal RAM/SFR. > 3. **Wrong register used in indirect mode** — Only R0, R1, and DPTR can be pointer registers. R2–R7 cannot. > 4. **Not stating the byte size** — KTU answer keys often allocate 1 mark for the instruction size (1, 2, or 3 bytes). > 5. **Mixing up `MOVC` and `MOVX`** — `MOVC` = code memory (read-only ROM); `MOVX` = external data memory (read/write). > 6. **Skipping the diagram** — Question B(a) often requires a "neat diagram" — full 7 marks require it. > 7. **Forgetting to mention PSW bits RS1, RS0** for bank selection. > 8. **Writing `MOVC A, @A+PC`** without explaining that the offset is added to the *current* PC, not a chosen address. --- ## 5.4 ⚡ TOPIC RECAP — Rapid Revision Checklist > **Print or save this checklist before the exam. ⏱️** ✅ The 8051 supports **exactly 5 addressing modes** (KTU syllabus). ✅ **Immediate (`#data`)** → constant in ROM, 2-byte instruction, used for initialisation. ✅ **Register (R0–R7)** → operand in CPU register, 1-byte, fastest, default bank = Bank 0. ✅ **Direct (8-bit address)** → access internal RAM (00H–7FH) and SFRs (80H–FFH), 2-byte, only way to access SFRs. ✅ **Register Indirect (`@R0`, `@R1`, `@DPTR`)** → address held in a pointer register, 1-byte, used with `MOV` (internal), `MOVX` (external data). ✅ **Indexed (`@A+DPTR`, `@A+PC`)** → 16-bit EA = base + A, 1-byte, only for code memory using `MOVC`, used for LUTs. ✅ **Default register bank** after reset = Bank 0 (PSW = 00H). ✅ **PSW bits RS1, RS0** control which of the 4 register banks is active. ✅ **Bit-addressable area** = 20H–2FH (16 bytes, 128 bits). ✅ **SFR area** = 80H–FFH (overlaps upper 128 bytes of internal RAM in 8052; in 8051 only SFRs are accessible here). ✅ **Code memory** = 64 KB (16-bit address), accessed via `MOVC`. ✅ **External data memory** = 64 KB (16-bit address), accessed via `MOVX` with `@DPTR` or `@Ri`. ✅ **Remember the symbols**: `#` = immediate, `@` = indirect/indexed base, no symbol = register or direct. ✅ **Instruction sizes summary**: Register, Indirect, Indexed = **1 byte**; Immediate (8-bit), Direct = **2 bytes**; `MOV DPTR, #data16` = **3 bytes**. ✅ **Rule of thumb for table look-up**: If the data is in **ROM**, use `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`; if it's in **external RAM**, use `MOVX A, @DPTR`; if it's in **internal RAM**, use `MOV A, @R0`. ✅ **Real-world applications**: Sine wave generation, 7-segment code conversion, ASCII conversion, key-debounce LUTs, polynomial evaluation. > **🎯 Final Advice:** Practice *at least one example program* for each addressing mode and trace the bytes/cycles manually — this single habit will help you secure 12+ marks out of 14 on any Module 2 addressing-mode question.

topics.title: The 8051 Instruction Set
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT726 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The 8051 Instruction Set

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 8051 Instruction Set** is the complete collection of machine-level and assembly-level operations that the **Intel 8051 microcontroller** (an **8-bit CISC architecture** with a **Harvard-style memory organization**) can decode and execute. It comprises **111 distinct instructions** (exactly **255 opcodes** when byte-level variations are counted) categorized into **5 functional groups**: Data Transfer, Arithmetic, Logical, Boolean (Bit-Manipulation), and Program Branching.

Every instruction in the 8051 is **1, 2, or 3 bytes** long and is fetched from **Program Memory (ROM)** in 1 to 3 machine cycles (where **1 machine cycle = 12 oscillator clock periods** on the classic 8051).

The instruction set is a **CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer)** style design — meaning a single mnemonic may have multiple addressing modes, each producing a different machine opcode.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy 🍳

Think of the 8051 instruction set as a **kitchen recipe book** 📖 for a chef (the CPU):
- The **chef (ALU + CU)** can only perform actions explicitly listed in the book.
- The **ingredients (operands)** can be fetched from specific shelves — the refrigerator (`Internal RAM` 0x00–0x7F), the deep freezer (`SFRs` 0x80–0xFF), or pre-packaged in the recipe itself (`Immediate data`).
- The **recipe format (addressing mode)** dictates *where* the chef looks for ingredients. "Take salt from the top shelf" is different from "take salt from the box labelled 'SALT' on the counter".

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Fact (Board-Favourite):** The 8051 has **8 Addressing Modes** and **111 Instructions** producing **255 opcodes**. The program counter is **16-bit (PC)** allowing access to **64 KB** of code memory, while the data pointer **DPTR** is also **16-bit** allowing **64 KB** external data memory access.

## 1.3 Why This Topic is High-Yield for KTU Examinations

- Direct 14-mark questions on **instruction format, addressing modes, and program tracing** appear almost every semester.
- Questions often involve **writing small assembly snippets** to compute values, sort data, or manipulate SFRs.
- The examiner loves testing the **PSW flags (CY, AC, OV, P)** and **stack pointer (SP)** behavior post-execution.

# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of 8051 Instructions

The 8051 instruction set is broadly classified into **five functional groups**:

### 🔹 Group 1: Data Transfer Instructions (29 instructions)
Used to move data **between internal RAM, SFRs, external RAM, and program memory** without altering any flag in the **PSW (Program Status Word)** register.

### 🔹 Group 2: Arithmetic Instructions (24 instructions)
Perform **addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, increment, decrement, and decimal adjust**. These are the **only instructions that affect the CY, AC, OV, and P flags** of the PSW.

### 🔹 Group 3: Logical Instructions (25 instructions)
Include **AND, OR, XOR, NOT, rotate, swap, and clear** operations. Operate **byte-wise** on the accumulator or between accumulator and a direct byte.

### 🔹 Group 4: Boolean (Bit-Level) Instructions (17 instructions)
A **signature feature of the 8051** — the microcontroller has a **dedicated bit-manipulation engine** with a **1-bit accumulator (Carry flag / CY)** and a **bit-addressable memory space** of **16 bytes (128 bits)** in the SFR region (addresses 0x80–0xFF that end in 0 or 8).

### 🔹 Group 5: Program Branching Instructions (16 + ACALL/LCALL group)
Control the **flow of execution**: unconditional/conditional jumps, subroutine calls, returns, and no-operation.

## 2.2 The 8 Addressing Modes of the 8051

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why addressing modes matter for KTU:** Examiners frequently ask students to *identify* and *classify* the addressing mode of a given instruction. This is a guaranteed 3-mark question.

| # | Addressing Mode | Syntax Pattern | Example | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Register** | `Rn` where n = 0..7 | `MOV A, R3` | Operands are in registers R0–R7 of the selected bank. |
| 2 | **Direct** | `direct (00–FF)` | `MOV A, 30H` | Specifies the actual internal RAM or SFR address. |
| 3 | **Register Indirect** | `@Ri` (i=0,1) | `MOV A, @R0` | Address of operand is held in R0 or R1 (for internal/external RAM lower 256 bytes). |
| 4 | **Immediate** | `#data` | `MOV A, #25H` | Constant is part of the instruction itself. |
| 5 | **Indexed (Code ROM)** | `@A+DPTR` / `@A+PC` | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | Used for lookup tables in program memory. |
| 6 | **Relative** | `rel` (signed 8-bit offset) | `SJMP rel` | Used only by certain conditional jumps; offset −128 to +127. |
| 7 | **Absolute** | `addr11` | `AJMP addr11` | 11-bit address; jumps within the current 2-KB page. |
| 8 | **Long** | `addr16` | `LJMP addr16` | 16-bit address; can jump anywhere in 64-KB code space. |

## 2.3 The PSW (Program Status Word) Register — Flag Reference

The **PSW** is an **SFR at address 0xD0** with the following bits (only the **CY (Carry), AC (Auxiliary Carry), OV (Overflow), and P (Parity)** flags are user-testable in arithmetic):

| Bit | Name | Address (Bit) | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| PSW.7 | **CY** | 0xD7 | **Carry flag** — set on borrow in subtraction, carry out in addition. |
| PSW.6 | **AC** | 0xD6 | **Auxiliary carry** — set on carry from bit 3 to bit 4 (used in BCD). |
| PSW.5 | **F0** | 0xD5 | **User-definable flag 0**. |
| PSW.4 / PSW.3 | **RS1 / RS0** | 0xD4 / 0xD3 | **Register bank select** (00 = Bank 0, default). |
| PSW.2 | **OV** | 0xD2 | **Overflow flag** — set on signed arithmetic overflow. |
| PSW.1 | — | 0xD1 | Reserved. |
| PSW.0 | **P** | 0xD0 | **Parity flag** — automatically set to maintain even parity of the accumulator. |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Relationships** that frequently appear in numerical/tracing questions.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Program memory size | $2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ bytes} = 64 \text{ KB}$ | PC is 16-bit |
| External data memory | $2^{16} = 64 \text{ KB}$ | Accessed via **DPTR** + **MOVX** |
| Internal RAM size | $128 \text{ bytes}$ (0x00–0x7F) | + 128 bytes SFR (0x80–0xFF) |
| Stack growth | Grows **upward** in internal RAM | SP initialized to 0x07 |
| Machine cycle | $T_{cy} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz} \Rightarrow T_{cy} \approx 1.085 \mu s$ |
| Timer tick rate | $\dfrac{f_{osc}}{12}$ | $11.0592 \text{ MHz} \Rightarrow 0.9216 \text{ MHz}$ |
| Baud rate (Mode 1) | $\text{BAUD} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \cdot f_{osc}}{32 \cdot 12 \cdot (256 - \text{TH1})}$ | Timer 1 auto-reload mode |
| Auxiliary carry trigger | $D_3 \to D_4$ carry in ADD/ADDC | Used by **DA A** for BCD correction |
| Overflow flag (signed) | $\text{OV} = C_7 \oplus C_6$ | XOR of carries into and out of MSB |
| Parity flag | $P = 1$ if accumulator has **odd** number of 1s | Maintains **even parity** of A |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of the 8051 Instruction Set

The compactness of the 8051 ISA makes it ideal for:
- 🏭 **Industrial control systems** (PLCs, motor drivers)
- 🏥 **Medical devices** (glucose meters, infusion pumps)
- 🚗 **Automotive subsystems** (early dashboard ECUs, window controllers)
- 🏠 **Consumer appliances** (washing machines, microwave ovens)
- 🎓 **Education** (de-facto teaching microcontroller worldwide)

The **bit-addressable instructions** in particular enable **direct I/O pin toggling** in a single cycle — a capability microcontrollers without a bit engine need multiple instructions to replicate.

# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The PSW Flag Computation for an ADD Instruction

> **Problem (KTU-style trace):** Execute `ADD A, #0x9C` on a fresh 8051 with `A = 0x65`. Determine the final value of A and the state of CY, AC, OV, and P.

### Step 1 — Identify the addressing mode and operation
The instruction `ADD A, #0x9C` uses the **Immediate addressing mode** (operand `0x9C` is embedded in the instruction) and the **Register** mode for the destination (Accumulator is the implicit A).

### Step 2 — Perform the binary addition

$$A = 0x65 = 0110\,0101_b$$
$$data = 0x9C = 1001\,1100_b$$

Adding bit by bit (LSB first):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bit 0: } & 1 + 0 = 1,\ \text{no carry} \\
\text{Bit 1: } & 0 + 0 = 0,\ \text{no carry} \\
\text{Bit 2: } & 1 + 1 = 0,\ \text{carry out} = 1 \\
\text{Bit 3: } & 0 + 1 + 1 (\text{carry-in}) = 0,\ \text{carry out} = 1 \\
\text{Bit 4: } & 0 + 1 + 1 = 0,\ \text{carry out} = 1 \\
\text{Bit 5: } & 1 + 0 + 1 = 0,\ \text{carry out} = 1 \\
\text{Bit 6: } & 1 + 0 + 1 = 0,\ \text{carry out} = 1 \\
\text{Bit 7: } & 0 + 1 + 1 = 0,\ \text{carry out} = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Final accumulator value

$$A_{\text{final}} = 0000\,0001_b = 0x01$$

### Step 4 — Flag analysis

- **CY (Carry Flag, PSW.7):** Carry out from bit 7 = **1**
- **AC (Auxiliary Carry, PSW.6):** Carry out from bit 3 to bit 4 = **1**
- **OV (Overflow, PSW.2):** $C_7 \oplus C_6$. Here $C_7 = 1$ (carry out of bit 7) and $C_6 = 1$ (carry into bit 7). So $\text{OV} = 1 \oplus 1 = 0$.
- **P (Parity, PSW.0):** $A = 0x01 = 0000\,0001_b$ has **one** '1' bit (odd). To maintain even parity, the P flag is set to **1**.

> **Final state:** A = 0x01, CY = 1, AC = 1, OV = 0, P = 1

## 3.2 Derivation: Stack Pointer Behavior During a PUSH/POP Pair

> **Problem:** Trace the stack after the following sequence on a fresh 8051 (initial SP = 0x07):
> ```
> MOV SP, #0x40      ; Explicitly set SP
> MOV 30H, #0x55     ; Store data in internal RAM
> PUSH 30H           ; Push onto stack
> MOV 30H, #0xAA     ; Overwrite
> POP 31H            ; Pop into a different location
> ```

### Step 1 — Initialize stack pointer
`MOV SP, #0x40` → SP now points to internal RAM address **0x40**.

### Step 2 — `PUSH 30H` operation
The 8051 push sequence is:
1. Increment SP.
2. Write operand into address SP.

$$
\begin{aligned}
SP &\leftarrow SP + 1 = 0x40 + 1 = 0x41 \\
\text{Internal RAM}[0x41] &\leftarrow \text{data at 30H} = 0x55
\end{aligned}
$$

Final SP after push = **0x41**.

### Step 3 — `POP 31H` operation
The 8051 pop sequence is:
1. Read data from address SP.
2. Decrement SP.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{destination 31H} &\leftarrow \text{Internal RAM}[SP] = \text{Internal RAM}[0x41] = 0x55 \\
SP &\leftarrow SP - 1 = 0x41 - 1 = 0x40
\end{aligned}
$$

Final state: `31H = 0x55`, SP = 0x40. The original data is preserved even after the source was overwritten — a classic KTU-style "show that push/pop protects data" question.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — All 5 Instruction Groups at a Glance

| Group | Flag Effect | Typical Mnemonics | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Transfer** | **No flag affected** | `MOV`, `MOVC`, `MOVX`, `PUSH`, `POP`, `XCH`, `XCHD`, `SWAP` | Copying bytes between RAM, SFRs, code ROM, external RAM. |
| **Arithmetic** | **Affects CY, AC, OV, P** | `ADD`, `ADDC`, `SUBB`, `INC`, `DEC`, `MUL`, `DIV`, `DA A` | Numeric computation, BCD arithmetic. |
| **Logical** | **Affects P only** | `ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL`, `CLR A`, `CPL A`, `RL`, `RLC`, `RR`, `RRC`, `SWAP A` | Bit masking, toggling, bitwise XOR encryption. |
| **Boolean (Bit)** | **Affects CY only** | `ANL C, bit`, `ORL C, bit`, `MOV C, bit`, `CLR C`, `SETB C`, `CPL C`, `JC`, `JNC`, `JB`, `JNB`, `JBC` | Single-pin I/O, software flags, tight control loops. |
| **Program Branching** | **No flag affected** | `ACALL`, `LCALL`, `RET`, `RETI`, `AJMP`, `SJMP`, `LJMP`, `JZ`, `JNZ`, `CJNE`, `DJNZ`, `NOP` | Loops, conditionals, subroutines, delays. |

## 3.4 Derivation: The CJNE (Compare and Jump if Not Equal) Logic

The 8051's `CJNE A, direct, rel` performs an **internal subtraction WITHOUT storing the result** and then decides the branch. The CY flag is set internally as if `SUBB` were executed.

> **Logic Equation:**

$$
\text{Take the branch} \iff A \neq \text{direct}
$$

$$
\text{CY} = 
\begin{cases}
1, & \text{if } A < \text{direct} \\
0, & \text{if } A \geq \text{direct}
\end{cases}
$$

> **Real Use:** `CJNE` is the heart of almost every "if-else" or "for-loop with early exit" in 8051 assembly, especially for keypad scanning, polling, and debounce loops.

# 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — 8051 Instruction Execution Flow

The following diagram shows how an instruction moves through the **8051 execution pipeline** from fetch to flag update.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Oscillator 11.0592 MHz] --> B[Machine Cycle Generator 12 Clocks per Cycle]
    B --> C[Program Counter PC 16-bit]
    C --> D[Fetch Opcode from Code ROM at PC]
    D --> E{Instruction Length 1, 2, or 3 bytes?}
    E -- 1 byte --> F[Decode only]
    E -- 2 bytes --> G[Fetch operand byte 1]
    E -- 3 bytes --> H[Fetch operand bytes 1 and 2]
    F --> I[Decode via Instruction Decoder]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{Instruction Group}
    J -- Data Transfer --> K1[MOV MOVC MOVX PUSH POP XCH]
    J -- Arithmetic --> K2[ADD ADDC SUBB INC DEC MUL DIV DA A]
    J -- Logical --> K3[ANL ORL XRL CLR CPL Rotate Swap]
    J -- Boolean --> K4[Bit ops on CY and bit-addressable space]
    J -- Branching --> K5[Jumps Calls Returns NOP]
    K1 --> L[Execute on internal RAM SFR or external bus]
    K2 --> M[ALU updates PSW flags CY AC OV P]
    K3 --> N[ALU updates parity P only]
    K4 --> O[Bit engine updates CY only]
    K5 --> P[PC modified relative absolute or long]
    L --> Q[PC increment next cycle]
    M --> Q
    N --> Q
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[Repeat for next instruction]
```

## 4.2 Addressing Mode Selection Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Operand source] --> Q1{Is the value embedded in the instruction?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> A1[Immediate #data]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is the operand in R0 to R7?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> A2[Register Rn]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is operand address given as a number 0x00 to 0xFF?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> A3[Direct]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Is the address held in R0 or R1?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> A4[Register Indirect @R0 or @R1]
    Q4 -- No --> Q5{Lookup table access from code ROM?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> A5[Indexed A plus DPTR or A plus PC]
    Q5 -- No --> Q6{Jump target encoded in instruction?}
    Q6 -- 8-bit offset --> A6[Relative]
    Q6 -- 11-bit page --> A7[Absolute AJMP ACALL]
    Q6 -- 16-bit full --> A8[Long LJMP LCALL]
```

## 4.3 Bit-Addressable Memory Map (Boolean Instruction Targets)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SFR_Bit_Region
        A0[Port 0 0x80] --> A1[Port 1 0x90]
        A1 --> A2[Port 2 0xA0]
        A2 --> A3[Port 3 0xB0]
        A3 --> A4[PSW 0xD0]
        A4 --> A5[ACC 0xE0]
        A5 --> A6[B Reg 0xF0]
    end
    subgraph Bit_Addresses
        B0[Bits 0x80 to 0x87 P0]
        B1[Bits 0x88 to 0x8F TCON]
        B2[Bits 0x90 to 0x97 P1]
        B3[Bits 0x98 to 0x9F SCON]
        B4[Bits 0xA0 to 0xA7 P2]
        B5[Bits 0xA8 to 0xAF IE]
        B6[Bits 0xB0 to 0xB7 P3]
        B7[Bits 0xB8 to 0xBF IP]
        B8[Bits 0xC0 to 0xC7 not bit]
        B9[Bits 0xC8 to 0xCF not bit]
        B10[Bits 0xD0 to 0xD7 PSW]
        B11[Bits 0xD8 to 0xDF not bit]
        B12[Bits 0xE0 to 0xE7 ACC]
        B13[Bits 0xE8 to 0xEF not bit]
        B14[Bits 0xF0 to 0xF7 B register]
        B15[Bits 0xF8 to 0xFF not bit]
    end
    A0 --- B0
    A1 --- B2
    A2 --- B4
    A3 --- B6
    A4 --- B10
    A5 --- B12
    A6 --- B14
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Only the 16 SFRs whose addresses end in `0` or `8` (hex) are bit-addressable. This gives the 8051 its 128 bit-addressable locations (16 × 8 = 128), accessible individually by `MOV C, P1.0` or `SETB P3.7`.

## 4.4 Call/Return Stack Frame Mechanism

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as Main Program
    participant SP as Stack Pointer in Internal RAM
    participant SUB as Subroutine
    CPU->>SP: SP initial = 0x40
    Note over CPU,SP: LCALL 0x2050 encountered
    SP->>SP: SP incremented to 0x41
    SP->>SP: PCH 0x20 written to 0x41
    SP->>SP: SP incremented to 0x42
    SP->>SP: PCL 0x50 written to 0x42
    CPU->>SUB: Execution jumps to 0x2050
    Note over SUB: Subroutine body executes
    SUB->>SP: RET encountered
    SP->>SP: Read PCL from 0x42
    SP->>SP: SP decremented to 0x41
    SP->>SP: Read PCH from 0x41
    SP->>SP: SP decremented to 0x40
    SP->>CPU: Return to original PC
```

# 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three addressing modes of the 8051 microcontroller with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Immediate Addressing Mode:** The operand (data) is a constant part of the instruction.
   - Example: `MOV A, #25H` → Loads the value 0x25 directly into the accumulator.

2. **Register Addressing Mode:** The operand is held in one of the 8 general-purpose registers (R0–R7) of the currently selected bank.
   - Example: `MOV A, R3` → Copies the contents of register R3 into the accumulator.

3. **Direct Addressing Mode:** The instruction contains the 8-bit address of the operand in internal RAM (0x00–0x7F) or SFR region (0x80–0xFF).
   - Example: `MOV A, 30H` → Loads the accumulator with the byte stored at internal RAM address 0x30.

*(Other valid answers: Register Indirect, Indexed, Relative, Absolute, Long — examiner gives full credit for any 3.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Explain the function of the Program Status Word (PSW) register in 8051. Name any four flags it contains.**

**Model Answer:**

The **PSW** is an **8-bit Special Function Register (SFR) at address 0xD0** in the 8051. It holds the status of the ALU after arithmetic, logical, and bit operations and also controls the selection of the active register bank.

**Four flags it contains:**

1. **CY (PSW.7) — Carry Flag:** Set when there is a carry out of bit 7 in addition or a borrow in subtraction.
2. **AC (PSW.6) — Auxiliary Carry Flag:** Set when there is a carry from bit 3 to bit 4. Used by the `DA A` instruction for BCD correction.
3. **OV (PSW.2) — Overflow Flag:** Set on signed arithmetic overflow; computed as $C_7 \oplus C_6$.
4. **P (PSW.0) — Parity Flag:** Automatically maintained to keep the accumulator in **even parity** (set if A has an odd number of 1-bits).

*(Bonus credit: mention F0, RS1, RS0.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently confuse **P (Parity)** with the parity of the *result* only — actually it is **always** set/cleared to maintain *even parity* of the accumulator. If A = 0x03 (binary `0000 0011`, two 1s = even), P = 0; if A = 0x01 (one 1 = odd), P = 1. Many students miss this and lose 1 mark.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[MODULE 2 — INTERNAL CHOICE]** Pick **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the different types of **jump instructions** available in the 8051, with syntax and addressable range. Discuss how `SJMP`, `AJMP`, and `LJMP` differ in terms of machine code size and jump range. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Write an 8051 assembly program to **add two 8-bit numbers stored in internal RAM locations 30H and 31H**, store the sum in 32H, and place any carry in bit P1.0. **[7 marks]**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-A (a)

**The 8051 supports three sizes of unconditional jumps:**

| Instruction | Opcode Size | Jump Range | Address Encoded | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `SJMP rel` | **2 bytes** | −128 to +127 from current PC | **8-bit signed relative offset** | Short loops, local branches |
| `AJMP addr11` | **2 bytes** | 0 to 2047 within current **2-KB page** | **11-bit absolute** | Mid-range function calls |
| `LJMP addr16` | **3 bytes** | 0 to 65535 (entire 64-KB code memory) | **16-bit full address** | Inter-page jumps, startup vectors |

**Step-by-step explanation:**

1. **`SJMP` (Short Jump):** The 2-byte encoding is `opcode` + `rel`, where `rel` is a *signed 2's complement* 8-bit offset added to the **PC value AFTER the instruction is fetched** (PC already points to the byte after the offset byte when the jump occurs).
2. **`AJMP` (Absolute Jump):** Encodes the **lower 11 bits** of the destination. The upper 5 bits are inherited from the current PC's high byte, restricting the jump to the **current 2-KB page** of code memory. Total size: 2 bytes.
3. **`LJMP` (Long Jump):** A 3-byte instruction (`opcode` + `high byte` + `low byte`). The destination address is a full 16-bit value, enabling jump to any of the **64 KB** of program memory.

**Conditional jumps (bonus content):** `JZ`, `JNZ`, `JC`, `JNC`, `DJNZ`, `CJNE`, `JB`, `JNB`, `JBC` — all use the **relative addressing mode** with the same −128 to +127 range as `SJMP`.

> **Conclusion:** `SJMP` is the most byte-efficient (2 bytes) but range-limited; `AJMP` covers larger 2-KB pages also in 2 bytes; `LJMP` is the most powerful (3 bytes) with unlimited range — typically used in initialization code to reach the `main` function.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-A (b)

```asm
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN           ; Jump over interrupt vector area (best practice)

        ORG 0030H           ; Place main code at a safe address
MAIN:   MOV A, 30H          ; Step 1: Load first number into A         (2 bytes, 1 cycle)
        ADD A, 31H          ; Step 2: Add second number to A            (2 bytes, 1 cycle)
        MOV 32H, A          ; Step 3: Store the sum (lower 8 bits)      (2 bytes, 1 cycle)
        JNC SAVE_DONE       ; Step 4: If no carry, skip setting P1.0    (2 bytes, 2 cycles)
        SETB P1.0           ; Step 5: Set P1.0 to indicate carry        (2 bytes, 1 cycle)
SAVE_DONE:
        END
```

**Code-trace explanation:**

1. The accumulator receives the byte at internal RAM location 30H.
2. `ADD A, 31H` performs unsigned addition. If the result exceeds 255, the **CY flag in PSW** is automatically set.
3. The lower 8 bits of the sum are stored at location 32H.
4. `JNC` (Jump if No Carry) is a **relative conditional jump** that branches forward if CY = 0. If CY = 1, the flow falls through to `SETB P1.0`.
5. `SETB P1.0` sets bit 0 of **Port 1** to logic '1', providing an external indication of overflow.

> **Total bytes used:** 12 bytes of code memory. **Total cycles in worst case:** approximately 6 to 8 machine cycles.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Two common mistakes cost students 1–2 marks each:
> 1. **Using `SUBB` or `SUB` confusion:** 8051 has **NO `SUB` instruction** — only `SUBB`. To subtract without carry, you **must clear CY first** via `CLR C`.
> 2. **Forgetting to use `JNC`/`JC` correctly:** After an `ADD`, the **CY flag is set ONLY on unsigned overflow (sum > 255)**. The `OV` flag is for signed overflow — do not mix them up.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **bit-addressable memory organization** of the 8051. Explain the instructions `SETB`, `CLR`, and `ANL C` with examples. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Write an 8051 assembly program to **multiply two numbers** stored at internal RAM locations 40H and 41H. Store the 16-bit result in locations 42H (low byte) and 43H (high byte). **[7 marks]**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-B (a)

**Bit-Addressable Memory Map (refer Section 4.3 for the schematic):**

The 8051 has a **16-byte region in the SFR area (addresses 0x80, 0x88, 0x90, …, 0xF8)** that is *bit-addressable*. Since each byte contains 8 bits, this provides **16 × 8 = 128 individually addressable bits** in the SFR region, in addition to the 16 bit-addressable registers at the bottom of internal RAM (addresses 0x20–0x2F → bits 0x00–0x7F).

The 16 bit-addressable SFRs are:
- 0x80 (P0), 0x88 (TCON), 0x90 (P1), 0x98 (SCON), 0xA0 (P2), 0xA8 (IE), 0xB0 (P3), 0xB8 (IP), 0xC0, 0xC8, 0xD0 (PSW), 0xD8, 0xE0 (ACC), 0xE8, 0xF0 (B), 0xF8.

**Instruction explanations:**

1. **`SETB bit`** — Sets the specified bit to logic 1.
   - Example: `SETB P1.7` → Makes pin 1.7 of Port 1 output HIGH.

2. **`CLR bit`** — Clears the specified bit to logic 0.
   - Example: `CLR P3.0` → Makes pin 3.0 of Port 3 output LOW.
   - Special: `CLR C` clears the carry flag used as a 1-bit accumulator.

3. **`ANL C, bit`** — Performs a logical AND between the carry flag (CY) and the specified bit; result is stored in CY (does NOT affect the source bit).
   - Example:
     ```asm
     MOV C, P1.0    ; Sample P1.0 into carry
     ANL C, P1.1    ; CY = P1.0 AND P1.1
     JC  BOTH_HIGH  ; Jump if both pins were high
     ```

> **Real-world use:** The `ANL C, /bit` variant (with the bar) provides AND-NOT functionality. `ORL C, /bit` provides OR-NOT. These are essential in **contact debounce, key-press detection, and Boolean logic emulation** with a single instruction.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-B (b)

```asm
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN

        ORG 0030H
MAIN:   MOV A, 40H          ; Load multiplicand into A              (2 bytes, 1 cycle)
        MOV B, 41H          ; Load multiplier into B register       (2 bytes, 1 cycle)
        MUL AB              ; Multiply A and B; result in B:A        (1 byte, 4 cycles)
        MOV 42H, A          ; Store low byte of result               (2 bytes, 1 cycle)
        MOV 43H, B          ; Store high byte of result              (2 bytes, 1 cycle)
        END
```

**Code-trace explanation:**

1. The 8051 has a **dedicated `MUL AB` instruction** that multiplies the unsigned values in A and B.
2. The **16-bit result is placed in B (high byte) and A (low byte)**, i.e., `B:A`.
3. The **OV flag is set if the product exceeds 255 (i.e., the result needs the high byte)** — useful for detecting overflow.
4. **CY is always cleared** by `MUL AB`.
5. We then store the lower 8 bits of the result at 42H and the upper 8 bits at 43H.

**Verification with sample data:** If 40H = 0x0A (10) and 41H = 0x05 (5), then `MUL AB` gives A = 0x32 (50) and B = 0x00. So 42H = 0x32 and 43H = 0x00. Correct!

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **The result is in B:A, NOT A:B.** The 8051 stores the high byte in B and the low byte in A after `MUL AB`. Reversing this order is the most common mistake.
> 2. **`MUL AB` and `DIV AB` affect ONLY the OV and CY flags** (CY = 0 always, OV set on overflow). They do NOT touch AC or P — students who claim otherwise lose marks.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Checklist for The 8051 Instruction Set:**

- 📌 **111 instructions / 255 opcodes** — a signature stat for the 8051.
- 📌 **5 groups:** Data Transfer (no flags), Arithmetic (CY/AC/OV/P), Logical (P only), Boolean (CY only), Branching (no flags).
- 📌 **8 addressing modes:** Register, Direct, Register Indirect, Immediate, Indexed, Relative, Absolute (AJMP/ACALL), Long (LJMP/LCALL).
- 📌 **PSW bits — CY, AC, F0, RS1, RS0, OV, _, P** — be able to draw this register on demand.
- 📌 **Parity flag = 1 when accumulator has ODD number of 1s** (so A always has even parity).
- 📌 **Auxiliary carry = carry from bit 3 to bit 4** — used by `DA A` for BCD.
- 📌 **Overflow = $C_7 \oplus C_6$** — only meaningful for signed arithmetic.
- 📌 **Stack grows UP** from SP; default SP = 0x07; explicit `MOV SP, #0x40` is best practice.
- 📌 **Bit-addressable memory = 16 bytes in SFR + 16 bytes in internal RAM 0x20–0x2F** = 256 individually addressable bits.
- 📌 **`MUL AB` puts result in B:A** (high in B, low in A). `DIV AB` puts quotient in A, remainder in B.
- 📌 **`MOVC` reads from code ROM (for lookup tables)**, `MOVX` reads from external RAM, `MOV` handles internal RAM + SFRs.
- 📌 **`CJNE` does an internal SUB** without storing the result — it just sets CY and tests equality.
- 📌 **`DJNZ` decrements and jumps if not zero** — the workhorse of 8051 delay loops.
- 📌 **`ACALL/AJMP` are 2-byte with 11-bit address (2-KB page)**, `LCALL/LJMP` are 3-byte with 16-bit full address.
- 📌 **`RET` pops PC**, `RETI` pops PC and also re-enables interrupts.
- 📌 **Cycles per machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods.** At 11.0592 MHz → 1.085 μs per machine cycle.

---

> 🎯 **End of Module 2 Notes — The 8051 Instruction Set**
> *Prepared in KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 format for the OECIT726 Embedded Systems course (2024 Scheme).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamental Issues in Hardware Software Co-Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT726 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fundamental Issues in Hardware Software Co-Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hardware/Software Co-Design** is a unified, concurrent design methodology in which the hardware and software components of an embedded system are conceived, modeled, partitioned, optimized, and validated together rather than being developed independently. It addresses the **design gap** between the rapidly evolving IC fabrication capability (Moore's Law) and the comparatively stagnant designer productivity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *"Hardware/Software Co-Design is the collaborative and concurrent design of hardware and software components of an embedded system, addressing partitioning, allocation, scheduling, mapping, verification, and synthesis under unified design constraints (performance, power, cost, time-to-market)."*

### Key Terminology (KTU Board Frequently Tested)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Co-Design** | Joint design of HW and SW |
| **Co-Synthesis** | Automated generation of HW and SW from a single specification |
| **Co-Simulation** | Joint simulation of HW and SW models before fabrication |
| **Co-Verification** | Formal and functional validation of HW/SW interaction |
| **Partitioning** | Deciding which functions run on HW vs. SW |
| **Allocation** | Assignment of tasks to computational resources |
| **Scheduling** | Ordering of task execution on resources |
| **Mapping** | Binding tasks to specific architectural blocks |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🏗️ Real-World Analogy — "Building a Restaurant Kitchen"**

Imagine you are designing a restaurant kitchen where the **head chef (processor)** must cook multiple dishes (**tasks**) in parallel under strict time pressure (**deadline**). Some dishes (like chopping vegetables) are best done by a **junior cook (software running on a GPP)** — flexible, cheap, easy to retrain. Other dishes (like making dough in bulk or grilling 500 steaks) are best done by a **dedicated machine (custom hardware / ASIC)** — fast, power-efficient, but expensive and inflexible.

**Hardware/Software Co-Design** is exactly this: looking at the *menu* (system specification) and deciding:
- Which dishes go to the chef (software on a CPU)?
- Which dishes go to specialized machines (hardware in ASIC/FPGA)?
- How do they coordinate (bus, shared memory, communication interface)?

The **fundamental issues** are: *what to partition, how to allocate, when to schedule, where to map*, and *how to verify* everything works *before* spending millions fabricating silicon.

## 1.3 Why Co-Design is Necessary — The Design Productivity Gap

The **design productivity gap** is one of the most critical motivations for co-design:

$$\text{Design Gap} = \frac{\text{IC Capability (transistors/chip)}}{\text{Designer Productivity (transistors/designer-day)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Industry Metric:** The IC capability grows at **~58% per year** (Moore's Law), but designer productivity grows at only **~21% per year**. This **widening gap** is the primary driver for raising the abstraction level — exactly what co-design enables by automating partitioning, synthesis, and verification.

---

## 2.1 Traditional Design Flow vs. Co-Design Flow

In the **traditional approach** (the pre-co-design era):
1. Hardware team builds the prototype board.
2. Software team *waits* for hardware to be ready.
3. Integration happens *late* — bugs are catastrophic and expensive.

In the **co-design approach**:
1. A **single executable specification** (e.g., in C/C++/SystemC) is created first.
2. The system is **partitioned** into HW/SW at a high abstraction level.
3. **Co-simulation** validates the design *before* fabrication.
4. Iterative refinement of the partition minimizes risk and cost.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common mistake students make is to define co-design as merely "designing HW and SW together." The examiner expects the term **"concurrent, unified, and constraint-driven from a single executable specification"** — mere collaboration is not co-design.

---

## 3.1 The Three Pillars of Co-Design

1. **Co-Specification** — Capturing the *what* in a single formal or executable model.
2. **Co-Implementation** — Executing the *how* (partitioning + synthesis).
3. **Co-Validation** — Proving the design is *correct* (co-simulation + co-verification).

Each pillar addresses a fundamental issue: **productivity**, **correctness**, and **predictability**.

---

## 4.1 Fundamental Issues — The KTU Classification

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 2), the **fundamental issues in hardware/software co-design** fall under these five categories:

1. **Representation Issues** — How to specify the system formally.
2. **Partitioning Issues** — What to put in HW vs. SW.
3. **Allocation & Scheduling Issues** — How to use resources efficiently.
4. **Target Architecture Issues** — Choosing the right mix of processors and ICs.
5. **Verification & Validation Issues** — Proving correctness *before* tape-out.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Co-Design Issues — Detailed Breakdown
