### Issue 1: Representation / Specification Issues

| Problem | Description |
|---|---|
| Heterogeneity | HW is naturally parallel/concurrent; SW is naturally sequential |
| Abstraction Gap | HW designers think in gates/transistors; SW designers think in functions/objects |
| Executability | Specification must be simulatable *before* implementation |
| Formalism | Need for a language that is *both* HW and SW friendly |

**Solutions:** Use **SystemC**, **SpecC**, **SystemVerilog**, **UML/MARTE**, or **C/C++ with synthesis pragmas**. These languages are *executable specifications* — they can be co-simulated.

---

### Issue 2: Partitioning Issues

Partitioning is the **single most important co-design decision**. It determines:
- Performance (HW is faster for parallel data-path tasks).
- Flexibility (SW is easier to modify).
- Power (custom HW is more power-efficient for hot loops).
- Cost (HW = non-recurring engineering cost; SW = recurring development cost).

#### Manual vs. Automated Partitioning

| Approach | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|
| **Manual Partitioning** | Designer uses expertise, high quality | Slow, error-prone, not scalable |
| **Automatic Partitioning** | Fast, explores large design space | May yield sub-optimal results without good heuristics |
| **Iterative/Heuristic** | Balanced | Requires designer oversight |

#### KTU Formulas for Partitioning Trade-off

The cost of a partition decision can be modeled as:

$$C_{total} = C_{HW} + C_{SW} + C_{comm} + C_{missed\_deadline}$$

Where:
- $C_{HW}$ = silicon area / NRE cost of hardware blocks
- $C_{SW}$ = development and execution cost of software
- $C_{comm}$ = cost of inter-component communication (bus, latency, energy)
- $C_{missed\_deadline}$ = penalty if a real-time constraint is violated

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constraint Boundary:** A valid partition *must* satisfy:
> $$T_{execution}(HW) + T_{comm} \leq Deadline$$
> $$T_{execution}(SW) + T_{comm} \leq Deadline$$
> $$Power_{HW} + Power_{SW} \leq Power_{budget}$$

---

### Issue 3: Allocation and Scheduling Issues

**Allocation** = *which* resources (CPU, ASIC, FPGA, DSP, memory, bus) are used.
**Scheduling** = *when* each task executes on its allocated resource.

#### Scheduling Types

| Type | When Used | Real-Time Guarantee |
|---|---|---|
| Static / Pre-runtime | When tasks are known in advance | Deterministic |
| Dynamic / Run-time | When workload varies | Best-effort |
| Rate Monotonic (RMS) | Periodic tasks | Yes (Liu & Layland bound) |
| Earliest Deadline First (EDF) | Dynamic priorities | Yes (for unit utilization ≤ 1) |

#### Liu & Layland Bound (CPU Utilization for RMS)

$$U_{bound} = n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$$

Where $n$ is the number of periodic tasks. As $n \to \infty$, $U_{bound} \to \ln(2) \approx 0.693$.

- For **n = 1**: $U_{bound} = 1.0$ (100%)
- For **n = 2**: $U_{bound} \approx 0.828$
- For **n = 3**: $U_{bound} \approx 0.779$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** RMS is widely used in avionics (ARINC 653), automotive ECUs, and industrial control (RTOS like VxWorks, FreeRTOS use rate-monotonic variants).

---

### Issue 4: Target Architecture Issues

The co-designer must choose from a **spectrum of implementation platforms**:

| Platform | Flexibility | Performance | Power | NRE Cost | Unit Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **GPP (e.g., ARM Cortex-A)** | Highest | Medium | High | Low | Low |
| **DSP (e.g., TMS320)** | Medium | High | Medium | Low | Low |
| **ASIP (Application-Specific Instruction-set Processor)** | Medium | High | Low | Medium | Low |
| **FPGA** | High | High | High | None | Medium |
| **ASIC / Full Custom** | None | Highest | Lowest | Very High | Very Low |

**Key Trade-off Equation (Flexibility vs. Efficiency):**

$$\text{Efficiency} = \frac{\text{Performance}}{\text{Cost} \times \text{Power} \times \text{Time-to-Market}}$$

ASIC has the highest efficiency but the worst flexibility and time-to-market; GPP has the opposite.

---

### Issue 5: Verification and Validation Issues

Co-design verification uses three complementary techniques:

1. **Co-Simulation** — Simulating HW models (HDL) with SW models (instruction set simulator) coupled through a bus-functional model.
2. **Co-Verification** — Formal equivalence checking between specification and implementation.
3. **Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL)** — Real HW running against a simulated environment.

#### Coverage Metric

$$Coverage = \frac{\text{Conditions Activated during Simulation}}{\text{Total Possible Conditions}} \times 100\%$$

For KTU, a target **functional coverage of 100%** and **statement coverage > 95%** is typical industry-standard.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{total} = C_{HW} + C_{SW} + C_{comm}$ | Total system cost for a partition |
| 2 | $U_{bound} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | Liu & Layland RMS schedulability bound |
| 3 | $T_{exec} + T_{comm} \leq Deadline$ | Real-time feasibility check |
| 4 | $Power_{HW} + Power_{SW} \leq P_{budget}$ | Power feasibility check |
| 5 | $Coverage = Activated / Total$ | Verification completeness |
| 6 | $Speedup = T_{sequential} / T_{parallel}$ | HW acceleration gain (Amdahl) |
| 7 | $Amdahl: S(n) = 1 / ((1-p) + p/n)$ | Max speedup with $n$ HW units, $p$ parallel fraction |
| 8 | $Design Gap \uparrow$ = IC growth (58%) − productivity (21%) | Motivation for co-design |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Co-Design Issue Most Critical |
|---|---|
| Smartphones (5G baseband) | Partitioning DSP vs. ASIC for power |
| Autonomous Vehicles | Real-time scheduling of sensor fusion |
| IoT Edge Devices | Power + cost optimization via ASIC |
| Medical Implants | Ultra-low-power scheduling |
| Aerospace Avionics | Formal verification (DO-178C compliance) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Decision Trade-offs

| Design Parameter | Favors Software | Favors Hardware |
|---|---|---|
| **Performance (latency-critical)** | ❌ | ✅ |
| **Flexibility / Upgradeability** | ✅ | ❌ |
| **Power Efficiency** | ❌ | ✅ |
| **NRE Cost** | ✅ | ❌ |
| **Time-to-Market** | ✅ (reuse libraries) | ❌ (fab cycle) |
| **Design Complexity** | ✅ (high-level tools) | ❌ (gate-level) |
| **Real-time Determinism** | ❌ (OS jitter) | ✅ (no OS) |
| **Parallelism** | ❌ (sequential) | ✅ (intrinsic) |
| **Memory Footprint** | ✅ (dynamic alloc) | ❌ (fixed buffers) |
| **Verification Cost** | ❌ (HW/SW interaction) | ✅ (single domain) |

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Amdahl's Law for HW/SW Speedup

**Problem Context:** A task has a sequential portion $p$ that must run on a CPU (software), and a parallel portion $(1-p)$ that can be accelerated in hardware with $n$ parallel units. Derive the maximum achievable speedup.

### Step 1: Define Sequential and Parallel Time

Let the original sequential time be $T_s = 1$ (normalized). The software portion takes time $p$ and the parallelizable portion takes time $(1-p)$.

### Step 2: Apply Parallelism to the Parallel Portion

The parallel portion, when distributed across $n$ hardware units, takes time $\frac{1-p}{n}$. The software portion remains unchanged at $p$.

### Step 3: Write the New Total Execution Time

$$T_{new} = p + \frac{1-p}{n}$$

### Step 4: Compute Speedup as Inverse of New Time

$$S(n) = \frac{T_s}{T_{new}} = \frac{1}{p + \frac{1-p}{n}}$$

### Step 5: Simplify the Denominator

$$\begin{aligned}
S(n) &= \frac{1}{p + \frac{1-p}{n}} \\
&= \frac{1}{\frac{p \cdot n + 1 - p}{n}} \\
&= \frac{n}{pn + 1 - p} \\
&= \frac{n}{1 + p(n - 1)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6: Analyze the Limit as $n \to \infty$

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} S(n) = \frac{1}{p}$$

### Step 7: Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** No matter how many hardware units you add, the maximum speedup is bounded by $\frac{1}{p}$. If 20% of the task is sequential ($p = 0.2$), the maximum achievable speedup is **5×** — even with infinite HW parallelism. This is **Amdahl's bottleneck** and a fundamental reason why *partitioning quality* matters more than raw HW count.

### Step 8: Numerical Example for KTU

Let $p = 0.25$ (25% sequential SW) and $n = 4$ HW units:

$$S(4) = \frac{4}{1 + 0.25 \cdot 3} = \frac{4}{1.75} \approx 2.286$$

So a 4× HW investment yields only **~2.3× speedup** — a strong argument to *minimize* $p$ through clever co-design partitioning.

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: RMS Utilization Bound

**Problem Context:** Given $n$ independent, periodic, preemptable tasks, derive the upper bound on CPU utilization for Rate Monotonic Scheduling.

### Step 1: State the Liu & Layland Theorem

For $n$ tasks with periods $T_1 < T_2 < \ldots < T_n$ and fixed priorities assigned in order of increasing period, the schedule is feasible if:

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$

Where $C_i$ = worst-case execution time of task $i$, $T_i$ = period of task $i$.

### Step 2: Derive the Bound for $n = 1$

For a single task, the utilization is trivially bounded by 1:

$$U_{bound}(1) = 1 \cdot (2^{1/1} - 1) = 2 - 1 = 1$$

### Step 3: Derive the Bound for $n = 2$

$$U_{bound}(2) = 2 \cdot (2^{1/2} - 1) = 2 \cdot (1.4142 - 1) = 2 \cdot 0.4142 = 0.8284$$

### Step 4: Compute the Limit as $n \to \infty$

Using L'Hôpital's rule on $2^{1/n} - 1$ as $n \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} 2^{1/n} = e^{\ln 2 / n} \to e^0 = 1$$

So $\lim_{n \to \infty} (2^{1/n} - 1) \to 0$, and the product:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} n(2^{1/n} - 1) = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931$$

### Step 5: Numerical Table

| $n$ | $U_{bound}$ |
|---|---|
| 1 | 1.0000 |
| 2 | 0.8284 |
| 3 | 0.7798 |
| 4 | 0.7568 |
| 5 | 0.7435 |
| 10 | 0.7177 |
| $\infty$ | 0.6931 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The bound is *sufficient but not necessary*. EDF (Earliest Deadline First) achieves a utilization bound of **1.0** but has higher runtime overhead.

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation: Cost-Optimal Partitioning Heuristic

**Problem Context:** A system has 5 tasks $\{T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4, T_5\}$. Each task has a *hardware cost* $C_{HW}(i)$ and *software cost* $C_{SW}(i)$. Find a partition $P \subseteq \{1,\ldots,5\}$ assigned to HW that minimizes total cost subject to a deadline $D$.

### Step 1: Define the Cost Function

$$C(P) = \sum_{i \in P} C_{HW}(i) + \sum_{i \notin P} C_{SW}(i)$$

### Step 2: Define the Constraint (Time Feasibility)

$$\sum_{i \in P} t_{HW}(i) + \sum_{i \notin P} t_{SW}(i) + T_{comm} \leq D$$

### Step 3: Construct the Lagrangian Relaxation

$$\mathcal{L}(P, \lambda) = C(P) + \lambda \left( \sum_{i} t_i + T_{comm} - D \right)$$

### Step 4: Solve Iteratively

Adjust $\lambda$ (the penalty for deadline violation) and for each $\lambda$, find the optimal partition that minimizes $\mathcal{L}$ by a greedy **cost-per-time** ratio:

$$r_i = \frac{C_{HW}(i) - C_{SW}(i)}{t_{SW}(i) - t_{HW}(i)}$$

Assign task $i$ to HW if $r_i > \lambda$ (i.e., the marginal cost of moving it to HW is worth the time saved).

### Step 5: Example Numerical Solution

| Task | $C_{HW}$ | $C_{SW}$ | $t_{HW}$ (ms) | $t_{SW}$ (ms) | $r_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $T_1$ | 100 | 20 | 1 | 8 | 80 / 7 = **11.43** |
| $T_2$ | 80 | 30 | 2 | 5 | 50 / 3 = **16.67** |
| $T_3$ | 50 | 10 | 3 | 4 | 40 / 1 = **40.00** |
| $T_4$ | 60 | 25 | 2 | 6 | 35 / 4 = **8.75** |
| $T_5$ | 40 | 15 | 1 | 3 | 25 / 2 = **12.50** |

If $\lambda = 15$, tasks with $r_i > 15$ go to HW: $T_2, T_3$. Others stay in SW.

### Step 6: Verify Deadline

$$T_{total} = (t_{HW}(2) + t_{HW}(3)) + (t_{SW}(1) + t_{SW}(4) + t_{SW}(5)) + T_{comm}$$
$$= (2 + 3) + (8 + 6 + 3) + 1 = 23 \text{ ms}$$

If $D = 25$ ms, the partition is **feasible** ✅.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often forget to include $T_{comm}$ (communication overhead) in their feasibility check. The inter-component bus latency can be **30–50% of the total execution time** in real designs and *must* be included.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Co-Design Methodology Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System Specification] --> B[Executable Model SystemC C++]
    B --> C[Profiling and Hotspot Analysis]
    C --> D{Quality of Results Acceptable?}
    D -- No --> E[Hardware Software Partitioning]
    D -- Yes --> Z[Final Implementation]
    E --> F[Co-Simulation and Validation]
    F --> G{Performance Power Cost Met?}
    G -- No --> E
    G -- Yes --> H[Co-Synthesis]
    H --> I[Hardware Synthesis VHDL Verilog]
    H --> J[Software Compilation and Linking]
    I --> K[Co-Verification on FPGA Prototype]
    J --> K
    K --> L{Meets All Constraints?}
    L -- No --> E
    L -- Yes --> M[Tapeout ASIC or FPGA Bitstream]
    M --> Z
```

---

## 4.2 The Five Fundamental Issues — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Representation Issue] --> B1[Co-Design Core]
    A2[Partitioning Issue] --> B1
    A3[Allocation and Scheduling Issue] --> B1
    A4[Target Architecture Issue] --> B1
    A5[Verification and Validation Issue] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Single Executable Spec]
    B1 --> C2[Design Space Exploration]
    B1 --> C3[Constraint Satisfaction]
    B1 --> C4[HW and SW Synthesis]
    B1 --> C5[Correctness Proof]
    C1 --> D1[Final Embedded System]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    C5 --> D1
```

---

## 4.3 The HW/SW Partitioning Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[New Task] --> Q1{Is it a Compute Intensive Hot Loop?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Needs Hard Real Time Deadline under 1 ms?}
    Q1 -- No --> SW[Implement in Software on GPP or RTOS]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Will it be Reused across Products?}
    Q2 -- No --> Q4{Can it Tolerate Algorithm Changes?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> ASIC[Implement as ASIC Custom HW]
    Q3 -- No --> FPGA[Implement on FPGA Fabric]
    Q4 -- Yes --> DSP[Implement on DSP or ASIP]
    Q4 -- No --> GPPHW[Accelerate via GPP with HW CoProcessor]
```

---

## 4.4 The Co-Design Design Space Exploration (DSE) Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Define Design Constraints Performance Power Cost] --> P2[Generate Initial Partition Candidate]
    P2 --> P3[Estimate Metrics HW Area SW Runtime Power]
    P3 --> P4{All Constraints Satisfied?}
    P4 -- No --> P5[Apply Optimization Move Move Task to HW or SW]
    P5 --> P3
    P4 -- Yes --> P6[Compare to Best Known Solution]
    P6 --> P7{Better than Current Best?}
    P7 -- Yes --> P8[Update Best Solution]
    P7 -- No --> P9[Discard Candidate]
    P8 --> P10{Exploration Budget Exhausted?}
    P9 --> P10
    P10 -- No --> P2
    P10 -- Yes --> P11[Output Optimal Partition]
```

---

## 4.5 The Co-Design Verification Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Level 1 Executable Spec Validation] --> L2[Level 2 HW SW Co-Simulation]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3 RTL Simulation]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4 FPGA Prototyping]
    L4 --> L5[Level 5 Silicon Bring Up and HIL Testing]
    L1 -.-> M1[Fastest Iteration Lowest Cost]
    L5 -.-> M2[Slowest Iteration Highest Cost]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Bugs caught at **Level 1** cost **~$10 to fix**. Bugs caught at **Level 5** cost **>$1 million** and possibly a re-spin. Co-design verification's primary value is shifting bug detection *left* (earlier) in this pyramid.

---

## 4.6 The Target Architecture Spectrum

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP1[General Purpose Processor GPP] -->|Less Flexible More SW| SP2[Application Specific Instruction Set Processor ASIP]
    SP2 -->|Less Flexible| SP3[Digital Signal Processor DSP]
    SP3 -->|Less Flexible| SP4[FPGA]
    SP4 -->|Less Flexible| SP5[Application Specific Integrated Circuit ASIC]
    SP1 -.->|Highest Flexibility| SP5
    SP5 -.->|Highest Performance and Power Efficiency| SP1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Q: Define Hardware/Software Co-Design. List any four fundamental issues addressed by co-design.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Hardware/Software Co-Design is a unified design methodology in which the hardware and software components of an embedded system are designed *concurrently* and *collaboratively* from a single executable specification, in order to meet system-level constraints such as performance, power, cost, and time-to-market.

**Four Fundamental Issues:**
1. **Specification / Representation Issue** — Creating a single, executable, heterogeneous model that captures both HW and SW behavior.
2. **Partitioning Issue** — Deciding which functional blocks are implemented in hardware and which in software.
3. **Allocation and Scheduling Issue** — Binding tasks to computational resources and ordering their execution to meet deadlines.
4. **Verification and Validation Issue** — Ensuring the HW/SW system functions correctly via co-simulation and co-verification *before* fabrication.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Q: Differentiate between co-simulation and co-verification in the context of embedded system design.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Co-Simulation | Co-Verification |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Functionally simulate HW and SW models together | Formally and exhaustively verify the correctness of the integrated HW/SW system |
| **Method** | Coupled execution of HDL simulator + ISS (Instruction Set Simulator) | Formal equivalence checking, assertion-based verification, model checking |
| **Stage in Flow** | Early design stage (post-partitioning) | Mid to late design stage (post-implementation) |
| **Speed** | Faster; used for iterative refinement | Slower; exhaustive coverage |
| **Output** | Functional traces, waveforms | Proof of correctness, coverage report |
| **KTU One-liner** | *“Does it work?”* | *“Is it provably correct?”* |

---

## 📗 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### ⭐ **Question A: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**(a)** Explain the **fundamental issues in hardware/software co-design** in detail with a neat block diagram of the co-design methodology. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the **trade-offs in HW/SW partitioning**. A system has 3 tasks with the following parameters. Determine the **optimal partition** using a cost-minimization approach. (7 marks)

| Task | $C_{HW}$ (₹) | $C_{SW}$ (₹) | $t_{HW}$ (ms) | $t_{SW}$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| T1 | 120 | 30 | 1 | 7 |
| T2 | 90 | 25 | 2 | 4 |
| T3 | 60 | 20 | 1 | 3 |

**Deadline D = 10 ms, Communication overhead $T_{comm}$ = 1 ms.**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

The fundamental issues in hardware/software co-design are:

**1. Representation Issue:**
The system must be specified in a single formalism that supports *both* hardware (parallel, concurrent) and software (sequential, control-flow) semantics. Languages like **SystemC, SpecC, and SystemVerilog** provide this. The challenge is *abstraction gap* — HW engineers think in RTL/gates; SW engineers think in functions/objects.

**2. Partitioning Issue:**
Decide which tasks become hardware (faster, more efficient, less flexible) and which become software (slower, more flexible, easier to modify). Partitioning drives:
- Performance
- Power consumption
- Silicon cost
- Time-to-market

**3. Allocation and Scheduling Issue:**
*Allocation* selects the computational resources (CPU, ASIC, FPGA, DSP, memory, bus). *Scheduling* orders task execution to meet real-time deadlines. For periodic tasks, **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** is widely used.

**4. Target Architecture Issue:**
Choose the right mix of GPP, ASIP, DSP, FPGA, and ASIC to balance flexibility, performance, power, and cost. This decision is permanent for the product's life.

**5. Verification and Validation Issue:**
*Co-simulation* runs HW (HDL) and SW (compiled binary on ISS) models in lock-step using a bus-functional model. *Co-verification* uses formal methods to prove functional equivalence to the specification. **Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL)** testing validates against real-world I/O.

**Block Diagram (Co-Design Methodology):**

```
[System Specification] → [Executable Model]
        ↓
[Profiling & Hotspot Analysis]
        ↓
[HW/SW Partitioning] → [Co-Simulation] → [Validation Pass?]
        ↓ (No)                                  ↓ (Yes)
[Refine Partition]              [Co-Synthesis: HW + SW]
                                            ↓
                                 [FPGA Prototype / ASIC Tapeout]
                                            ↓
                                      [Final Product]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner Valuation Pitfall:** Students often present only **3 issues** (representation, partitioning, verification) and miss **allocation/scheduling** and **target architecture**. For 7 marks, expect the examiner to deduct **1.5–2 marks** if even one of the five is missing. Use a numbered list — visual structure impresses the evaluator.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**Step 1: Compute Cost/Time Ratio for HW Migration**

We compute the marginal cost and time saved for each task:

$$\Delta C_i = C_{HW}(i) - C_{SW}(i)$$
$$\Delta t_i = t_{SW}(i) - t_{HW}(i)$$
$$r_i = \frac{\Delta C_i}{\Delta t_i}$$

| Task | $\Delta C_i$ | $\Delta t_i$ | $r_i$ (₹/ms) |
|---|---|---|---|
| T1 | 90 | 6 | 15.0 |
| T2 | 65 | 2 | 32.5 |
| T3 | 40 | 2 | 20.0 |

**Step 2: Rank by Cost-Efficiency of HW Migration (Lower $r_i$ is better)**

Ranking from best (lowest $r_i$) to worst:
1. **T1** → HW (15.0 ₹/ms)
2. **T3** → HW (20.0 ₹/ms)
3. **T2** → SW (32.5 ₹/ms)

**Step 3: Evaluate Total Time for Partition {T1, T3 → HW; T2 → SW}**

$$T_{total} = (t_{HW}(1) + t_{HW}(3)) + t_{SW}(2) + T_{comm}$$
$$= (1 + 1) + 4 + 1 = 7 \text{ ms}$$

Since $7 \text{ ms} \leq D = 10 \text{ ms}$ ✅, the partition is **feasible**.

**Step 4: Compute Total Cost**

$$C_{total} = (C_{HW}(1) + C_{HW}(3)) + C_{SW}(2) = (120 + 60) + 25 = 205 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 5: Compare Against Alternative — All SW**

$$T_{total} = (7 + 4 + 3) + 1 = 15 \text{ ms} \quad ❌ \text{ (Violates deadline)}$$

So all-SW is **not feasible**.

**Step 6: Final Answer**

**Optimal Partition:** T1 and T3 in Hardware, T2 in Software.
**Total Cost:** ₹205
**Total Execution Time:** 7 ms (within 10 ms deadline ✅)

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Common Mistake:** Many students calculate the cost-per-time ratio but forget to **verify the deadline constraint**. The cheapest partition by raw cost may violate the deadline. Always cross-check both: cost minimization **and** real-time feasibility.

---

### ⭐ **Question B: [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper] — Alternative Choice**
**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **co-design verification and validation flow**. Discuss the role of co-simulation and hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) testing. (7 marks)

**(b)** A real-time embedded system has 3 periodic tasks with the following parameters. Using the **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** bound, determine whether the task set is schedulable. If not, suggest a co-design remedy. (7 marks)

| Task | Period $T_i$ (ms) | Execution Time $C_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| T1 | 10 | 2 |
| T2 | 20 | 5 |
| T3 | 40 | 8 |

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

The co-design verification and validation (V&V) flow ensures the embedded system is correct *before* physical fabrication. The flow proceeds in five escalating levels of fidelity:

**Level 1 — Executable Specification Validation:**
The single executable model (SystemC/C++) is simulated to verify functional behavior. Catches algorithmic errors. Cost: ~₹10 per bug.

**Level 2 — HW/SW Co-Simulation:**
After partitioning, HW (HDL) and SW (compiled for an Instruction Set Simulator like ARMulator) are simulated together. A *bus-functional model* synchronizes the two domains. Catches interface mismatches. Cost: ~₹1,000 per bug.

**Level 3 — RTL Simulation:**
The synthesizable HDL is simulated with timing annotations. Functional coverage tools track which code paths are exercised. Cost: ~₹10,000 per bug.

**Level 4 — FPGA Prototyping:**
The HW is mapped onto an FPGA and run with real software on a physical CPU board. Real-world I/O is exercised. Cost: ~₹100,000 per bug.

**Level 5 — Silicon Bring-up and HIL:**
The fabricated ASIC is tested with the real system in a Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) setup, where physical sensors/actuators are simulated in real time. Cost: >₹1,000,000 per bug.

**Role of Co-Simulation:**
- Allows early functional validation before RTL.
- Enables hardware/software integration testing using virtual prototypes.
- Reduces time-to-market by ~30–40%.

**Role of HIL Testing:**
- Validates the *complete* system against real-world I/O timing.
- Required for safety-critical domains (automotive ISO 26262, aerospace DO-178C).
- Enables regression testing across firmware updates.

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Specification] → [Co-Simulation] → [RTL Simulation]
        ↓                ↓                ↓
   [Algorithmic]   [HW/SW Interface]  [Timing Verification]
                                      ↓
                              [FPGA Prototyping]
                                      ↓
                            [HIL Testing] → [Tapeout]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Examiner Watch:** Students often confuse *co-simulation* with *co-verification*. **Co-simulation = functional execution of HW and SW models together.** **Co-verification = formal proof of correctness.** Both are essential, but they are *different* stages in the V&V flow.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Step 1: Compute CPU Utilization**

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{3} \frac{C_i}{T_i} = \frac{2}{10} + \frac{5}{20} + \frac{8}{40}$$
$$= 0.200 + 0.250 + 0.200 = 0.650$$

So the actual utilization is **65%**.

**Step 2: Compute the Liu & Layland RMS Bound for n = 3**

$$U_{bound}(3) = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 = 0.7798$$

So the **schedulability bound is 77.98%**.

**Step 3: Compare**

$$U = 0.650 \leq U_{bound}(3) = 0.7798 \quad ✅$$

**Conclusion:** The task set **IS schedulable** under Rate Monotonic Scheduling.

**Step 4: Slack Analysis**

$$Slack = U_{bound} - U = 0.7798 - 0.6500 = 0.1298 \quad (12.98\%)$$

There is ~13% spare CPU capacity available for future tasks or interrupt handling.

**Step 5: Co-Design Remedy (Hypothetical Counter-Scenario)**

If $U$ had exceeded $U_{bound}$, co-design remedies would be:
1. **Move the heaviest task (T3 with $C_3 = 8$ ms) to dedicated hardware** (ASIC/FPGA), reducing software load to $U' = 0.200 + 0.250 = 0.450$. ✅
2. **Use a faster processor** (e.g., 100 MHz → 200 MHz) to halve $C_i$ values.
3. **Switch to EDF scheduling**, which has a schedulability bound of 1.0.
4. **Reduce task periods** if the design constraints allow (not typically desirable).

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Pitfall:** A common error is computing $U_{bound}$ for the *wrong value of $n$*. Always use the **number of tasks** in the set, NOT the number of CPU cores or the task index. For our 3 tasks, $n = 3$, NOT $n = 40$ (the period value).

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ### 🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist — Fundamental Issues in HW/SW Co-Design

✅ **Co-Design is concurrent, unified, constraint-driven** from a *single executable specification* — not just "HW + SW designed together."

✅ **Design Productivity Gap** = IC growth (58%) − Designer productivity (21%) — the *root motivation* for co-design.

✅ **Five Fundamental Issues:**
   1. Representation / Specification
   2. Partitioning
   3. Allocation & Scheduling
   4. Target Architecture Selection
   5. Verification & Validation

✅ **Partitioning Drives:** Performance, Power, Flexibility, Cost, Time-to-Market.

✅ **Amdahl's Law:** $S(n) = \frac{n}{1 + p(n-1)}$; max speedup $= 1/p$.

✅ **Liu & Layland RMS Bound:** $U_{bound} = n(2^{1/n} - 1) \to \ln 2 \approx 0.693$.

✅ **Cost-Time Ratio for Partitioning:** $r_i = \frac{C_{HW}(i) - C_{SW}(i)}{t_{SW}(i) - t_{HW}(i)}$.

✅ **Target Architecture Order (Flexibility → Efficiency):**
   GPP → ASIP → DSP → FPGA → ASIC

✅ **Verification Cost Pyramid (10× to 100× growth per level):**
   Spec → Co-Sim → RTL → FPGA → Silicon

✅ **Co-Simulation** = Functional execution of HW + SW models together.
   **Co-Verification** = Formal proof of correctness.
   **HIL Testing** = Real HW + simulated I/O environment.

✅ **Real-Time Feasibility Check:** $T_{exec} + T_{comm} \leq Deadline$ (don't forget $T_{comm}$!).

✅ **Cost Function:** $C_{total} = C_{HW} + C_{SW} + C_{comm} + C_{missed\_deadline}$.

✅ **Common KTU Buzzwords to memorize verbatim:** *executable specification, co-simulation, co-verification, design gap, design space exploration, hardware-software partitioning, rate-monotonic scheduling, hardware-in-the-loop, ASIP, target architecture.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computational Models in Embedded Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT726 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Computational Models in Embedded Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Computational Model in Embedded Design?

A **Computational Model** is a formal, mathematical representation of an embedded system's behavior, structure, and information flow. It abstracts the system into a set of computational primitives (nodes) interconnected by directed edges, allowing designers to reason about timing, resource allocation, and hardware/software partitioning **before** any actual code is written or silicon is fabricated.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a computational model is the bridge between a high-level **system specification** (what the system must do) and a low-level **implementation** (how the system will do it using processors, memory, and I/O).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (OECIT726 – Module 2):** Computational models are the foundation for **hardware/software co-design**. The KTU paper expects you to draw models from plain English specifications, identify the type (DFG, CDFG, FSM, sequential/parallel), and analyze them for performance metrics like **latency, throughput, and critical path**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Recipe of an Embedded System"

Imagine you are writing a recipe (the *specification*) for a smart coffee machine. The recipe has:
- **Ingredients** → Input data (sensor readings, button presses)
- **Steps** → Computational operations (boil water, grind beans, mix)
- **Order of steps** → Data dependencies (you cannot mix before grinding)
- **Parallel tasks** → Brewing milk and espresso simultaneously

A **computational model** is the standardized *flowchart* version of this recipe. Engineers use it to:
1. Identify which steps can run in parallel (concurrency)
2. Identify the slowest sequence of steps (critical path = total make-time)
3. Decide which steps belong in hardware (the espresso machine's metal pump) and which belong in software (the touchscreen menu logic).

## 1.3 Taxonomy of Computational Models in KTU Embedded Design

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus groups computational models into four canonical categories. Master these four — they appear every year in Module 2.

| **Model Type** | **What it Describes** | **Best For** |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Flow Graph (DFG)** | How data moves between operations | Pure data-dominated signal processing (DSP) |
| **Control Flow Graph (CFG)** | Order of execution / branching in code | Program flow analysis, compiler design |
| **Control–Data Flow Graph (CDFG)** | Combines DFG + control (loops, conditionals) | Complete embedded program representation |
| **Finite State Machine (FSM)** | System states + transitions | Reactive, control-dominated systems |

Additional related models (often asked as 3-mark short notes in KTU):
- **Sequential Model**
- **Parallel / Concurrent Model**
- **Communicating Sequential Processes (CSP)**
- **Petri Nets** (advanced/elective depth)

## 1.4 Key Terminology You Must Know

- **Node (Actor / Vertex):** Represents a computational operation. In a DFG, it could be `+`, `*`, or a complex function block.
- **Edge (Arc):** Represents data dependency or control flow direction.
- **Critical Path:** The longest path (in execution time) from any input to any output in the graph. Determines the **minimum achievable latency** of the system.
- **Iteration Bound (Loop Bound):** The theoretical maximum throughput of a loop due to data dependencies.
- **Latency (L):** Total time from input arrival to output production.
- **Throughput (T):** Rate of output production (outputs per unit time, often inverse of iteration bound).
- **Functional Units:** Hardware resources (ALUs, multipliers) available for executing operations.

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

In computational modeling for embedded systems, the constants you will repeatedly use are:

- **Clock Period ($T_{clk}$):** Typically **10 ns** for a 100 MHz processor.
- **Operation delays:** Adder = 1 cycle, Multiplier = 2 cycles, Division = 4 cycles (for classic KTU problems).
- **Pipeline stage delay:** Often modeled as $T_{pipe} = T_{clk}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always express delays in consistent time units. The KTU paper frequently asks: *"Given these operation latencies, find the critical path and minimum clock period."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Data Flow Graph (DFG)

A **DFG** is a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ where:
- $V$ = set of nodes representing primitive operations
- $E \subseteq V \times V$ = set of directed edges representing data dependencies
- A directed edge $(u, v)$ means: *the output of node $u$ is an input of node $v$*

### Properties of a DFG
1. **Acyclic by default** (for one iteration of computation). Loops are represented by *back edges* when modeling iteration.
2. **No control flow** — DFG assumes every node executes.
3. **Edges carry data tokens**, not control signals.

### Why DFGs Matter in Embedded Design
- Used to schedule operations onto **functional units**.
- Enable **ASIP (Application-Specific Instruction-set Processor)** design.
- The foundation of **High-Level Synthesis (HLS)** tools like Xilinx Vitis HLS, Cadence Stratus, Synopsys Synphony.

### Example: DFG for the expression $y = (a+b) \times (c-d)$
```
   a ──┐
       ├──[+]── u ──┐
   b ──┘            ├──[×]── y
   c ──┐            │
       ├──[-]── v ──┘
   d ──┘
```
This DFG has **depth = 2** (two sequential operations in the longest chain). The `+` and `-` execute in **parallel** because they are independent.

## 2.2 The Control–Data Flow Graph (CDFG)

A **CDFG** extends the DFG with:
- **Decision nodes (diamonds):** Conditional branches (`if`, `switch`)
- **Loop constructs:** `for`, `while` with back-edges
- **Hierarchy:** Sub-graphs nested inside nodes

The CDFG is the **canonical model used by compilers** and is what tools like `gcc` produce internally (in RTL, this becomes the basis of HLS).

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 papers, a typical 7-mark question shows you a small C program and asks you to draw its CDFG. Practice converting simple `for` loops and `if-else` blocks to CDFG nodes.

## 2.3 The Finite State Machine (FSM)

An **FSM** models a reactive system as a set of discrete states with transitions triggered by events. Formally:

$$M = (S, I, O, \delta, \lambda, s_0)$$

Where:
- $S$ = finite set of states
- $I$ = finite input alphabet
- $O$ = finite output alphabet
- $\delta: S \times I \to S$ = **next-state function** (transition function)
- $\lambda$ = **output function**
- $s_0 \in S$ = initial state

### The Two Canonical FSM Models

| **Type** | **Output Depends On** | **Output Function $\lambda$** | **Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Moore Machine** | Current state **only** | $\lambda: S \to O$ | Clocked digital logic, simple controllers |
| **Mealy Machine** | Current state **AND** current input | $\lambda: S \times I \to O$ | Faster response, complex protocols (UART, SPI) |

### Key Trade-off
- Moore: Output is **synchronous and glitch-free**, but has **1 cycle of latency**.
- Mealy: Output is **asynchronous and faster**, but prone to **glitches** if combinational.

## 2.4 Sequential vs. Parallel Computational Models

### Sequential Model
- One operation at a time on one functional unit.
- Latency = sum of all operation delays.
- **Example:** Von Neumann architecture (program and data share one bus).
$$L_{seq} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_{op_i}$$
where $t_{op_i}$ is the delay of the $i$-th operation.

### Parallel (Concurrent) Model
- Multiple operations execute simultaneously on multiple functional units.
- Latency = delay of the **critical path** (longest chain of dependent operations).
$$L_{par} = \max_{p \in \text{paths}} \sum_{i \in p} t_{op_i}$$
- **Speedup** = $L_{seq} / L_{par}$
- **Efficiency** = Speedup / Number of processors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU examiner loves asking: *"Identify the critical path in this DFG and compute the minimum latency for a given clock."* Always trace from the input to the output, picking the path with the maximum total delay.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table — it covers 80% of computational-model numerical questions in KTU 2024 papers.**

| **#** | **Concept** | **Formula** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sequential Latency | $L_{seq} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_i$ | time units (ns, µs) |
| 2 | Parallel Latency (Critical Path) | $L_{par} = \max_{p \in P} \sum_{i \in p} t_i$ | time units |
| 3 | Speedup | $S = L_{seq} / L_{par}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Efficiency | $\eta = S / N_{proc}$ | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 5 | Throughput | $T = 1 / L_{par}$ (single iteration) | outputs/sec |
| 6 | Iteration Bound (Longest Path / Loop Bound) | $T_{\infty} = \max_{l \in \text{loops}} \left( \dfrac{t_l}{N_l} \right)$ | time units |
| 7 | ASAP Schedule (lower bound on latency) | $t_{ASAP}(v) = \max_{(u,v) \in E} (t_{ASAP}(u) + t_{op}(v))$ | cycles |
| 8 | ALAP Schedule | $L - \max_{(v,w) \in E} t_{ALAP}(w) - t_{op}(v)$ | cycles |
| 9 | Mobility (slack) | $m(v) = t_{ALAP}(v) - t_{ASAP}(v)$ | cycles |
| 10 | Clock Period Lower Bound | $T_{clk} \geq \max_v (t_{op}(v))$ | time units |
| 11 | Number of States (Moore) | $\vert S \vert$ | count |
| 12 | Mealy Output | $o = \lambda(s, i)$ | logical value |
| 13 | Moore Output | $o = \lambda(s)$ | logical value |
| 14 | Total Pipeline Latency | $L_{pipe} = (k + N - 1) \cdot T_{clk}$ | time units |
| 15 | Pipeline Throughput | $T_{pipe} = 1 / T_{clk}$ | outputs/cycle |

> **Note on notation:** Vertical bars inside formulas are written as $\vert$ or $\mid$ to avoid breaking markdown tables.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of Computational Models in Engineering

- **Automotive ECUs (Bosch, Continental):** FSMs model gear-shifting logic, ABS braking sequences.
- **Smartphone SoCs (Qualcomm, Apple):** CDFGs drive the HLS of camera ISP pipelines.
- **Aerospace (NASA, ISRO):** Petri nets + FSMs verify fault-tolerant flight software.
- **IoT Devices (ESP32, STM32):** Sequential/parallel models guide which firmware loops to offload to hardware accelerators.
- **Medical Implants (Pacemakers):** FSMs guarantee deterministic, life-critical response times.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example #1 — Critical Path and Latency (Classic KTU 14-Mark Question)

**Problem:**
Consider the following DFG. Operation delays are: `+` = 2 ns, `×` = 5 ns, `÷` = 8 ns, `−` = 2 ns.

```
   a ──┐
       ├──[+]── b ──┐
   c ──┘            ├──[×]── e ──┐
   d ──┐            │            ├──[÷]── y
       ├──[-]── c'──┘            │
   e ──┘                          │
                                  (and f from e directly goes to ÷)
```

Actually, let's use a cleaner canonical example. Consider the DFG computing:

$$y = (a + b) \times (c - d) + e / f$$

Operation delays: `+` = 2 ns, `−` = 2 ns, `×` = 5 ns, `÷` = 8 ns.

### Step 1: Draw the DFG
```
   a ──┐
       ├──[+]── p1 ──┐
   b ──┘            ├──[×]── p2 ──┐
   c ──┐            │            ├──[+]── y
       ├──[-]── p3──┘            │
   d ──┘                         │
   e ──┐                         │
       ├──[÷]── p4 ──────────────┘
   f ──┘
```

### Step 2: Identify All Paths from Input to Output

| **Path** | **Operations** | **Total Delay (ns)** |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | a, b → + → × → + | 2 + 5 + 2 = **9** |
| P2 | a, b → + → × → + | (same as P1) — 9 |
| P3 | c, d → − → × → + | 2 + 5 + 2 = **9** |
| P4 | e, f → ÷ → + | 8 + 2 = **10** |

### Step 3: Apply the Critical Path Formula

The critical path is the **longest delay** path:

$$L_{critical} = \max_{p \in P} \sum_{i \in p} t_i$$

$$L_{critical} = \max(9, 9, 9, 10) = 10 \text{ ns}$$

### Step 4: Calculate Sequential Latency (One Functional Unit)

$$L_{seq} = t_{+} + t_{-} + t_{\times} + t_{\div} + t_{+} = 2 + 2 + 5 + 8 + 2 = 19 \text{ ns}$$

### Step 5: Calculate Parallel Latency with Unlimited Functional Units

$$L_{par} = L_{critical} = 10 \text{ ns}$$

(The `+` and `−` execute in parallel; the `×` waits for both; the second `+` waits for `×` and `÷`.)

### Step 6: Speedup and Efficiency

With 3 functional units (one adder, one multiplier, one divider):

$$S = \frac{L_{seq}}{L_{par}} = \frac{19}{10} = 1.9$$

$$\eta = \frac{S}{N_{proc}} = \frac{1.9}{3} \approx 0.633 = 63.3\%$$

### Step 7: Minimum Clock Period

$$T_{clk} \geq \max_v t_{op}(v) = 8 \text{ ns}$$

(Number of clock cycles needed = $\lceil L_{par} / T_{clk} \rceil = \lceil 10/8 \rceil = 2$ cycles.)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **standard solution pattern** for any KTU computational-model latency question. Memorize the 7 steps.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example #2 — ASAP and ALAP Scheduling (As Soon As Possible / As Late As Possible)

**Same DFG:** $y = (a + b) \times (c - d) + e / f$, with `+` = 1, `−` = 1, `×` = 2, `÷` = 4 cycles. Assume latency constraint $L = 7$ cycles.

### Step A: ASAP Schedule (start as early as possible)

The formula is:
$$t_{ASAP}(v) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } v \text{ is an input} \\ \max_{(u,v) \in E} (t_{ASAP}(u) + t_{op}(v)) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Applying recursively:
- $t_{ASAP}(a) = t_{ASAP}(b) = t_{ASAP}(c) = t_{ASAP}(d) = t_{ASAP}(e) = t_{ASAP}(f) = 0$
- $t_{ASAP}(p_1 = +) = 0 + 1 = 1$
- $t_{ASAP}(p_3 = -) = 0 + 1 = 1$
- $t_{ASAP}(p_4 = \div) = 0 + 4 = 4$
- $t_{ASAP}(p_2 = \times) = \max(1, 1) + 2 = 3$
- $t_{ASAP}(y = +) = \max(3, 4) + 1 = 5$

So ASAP table:

| **Node** | **Operation** | **ASAP Start** | **ASAP Finish** |
|---|---|---|---|
| p1 | + | 1 | 2 |
| p3 | − | 1 | 2 |
| p2 | × | 3 | 5 |
| p4 | ÷ | 4 | 8 |
| y | + | 5 | 6 |

### Step B: ALAP Schedule (start as late as possible, but finish by $L$)

The formula is:
$$t_{ALAP}(v) = \begin{cases} L & \text{if } v \text{ is an output} \\ \min_{(v,w) \in E} t_{ALAP}(w) - t_{op}(v) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Working backwards from $y$ (must finish at $L = 7$):
- $t_{ALAP}(y) = 7$, start = 6
- $t_{ALAP}(p_2) = t_{ALAP}(y) - 1 = 6$, but $\times$ takes 2 cycles, so start = 4
- $t_{ALAP}(p_4) = t_{ALAP}(y) - 1 = 6$, but $\div$ takes 4 cycles, so start = 2
- $t_{ALAP}(p_1) = t_{ALAP}(p_2) - 2 = 4$, but `+` takes 1, so start = 3
- $t_{ALAP}(p_3) = t_{ALAP}(p_2) - 2 = 4$, start = 3

ALAP table:

| **Node** | **Operation** | **ALAP Start** | **ALAP Finish** |
|---|---|---|---|
| y | + | 6 | 7 |
| p4 | ÷ | 2 | 6 |
| p2 | × | 4 | 6 |
| p1 | + | 3 | 4 |
| p3 | − | 3 | 4 |

### Step C: Mobility (Slack)

$$m(v) = t_{ALAP}(v) - t_{ASAP}(v)$$

| **Node** | **ASAP Start** | **ALAP Start** | **Mobility $m$** |
|---|---|---|---|
| p1 (+) | 1 | 3 | 2 |
| p3 (−) | 1 | 3 | 2 |
| p2 (×) | 3 | 4 | 1 |
| p4 (÷) | 4 | 2 | **−2** ⚠️ |

The negative mobility on `÷` means the **latency constraint $L=7$ is infeasible** — we need at least $L=8$ cycles for this DFG to be schedulable. KTU papers often test this insight.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example #3 — FSM State Reduction and Design

**Problem:** Design a Mealy FSM to detect the sequence `101` on a serial input $x$, producing output $z = 1$ whenever the sequence is detected (overlapping allowed).

### Step 1: Define States
- $S_0$ = Initial / no useful prefix
- $S_1$ = Last input was `1` (prefix `"1"`)
- $S_2$ = Last two inputs were `"10"` (prefix `"10"`)
- $S_3$ = Sequence `"101"` detected (output $z=1$)

### Step 2: Build State Transition Table (Mealy)

| **Present State** | **Input $x=0$** | **Input $x=1$** |
|---|---|---|
| $S_0$ | $S_0, z=0$ | $S_1, z=0$ |
| $S_1$ | $S_2, z=0$ | $S_1, z=0$ |
| $S_2$ | $S_0, z=0$ | $S_3, z=1$ |
| $S_3$ | $S_2, z=0$ | $S_1, z=1$ |

> Overlap rule: After detecting `101`, if next bit is `1`, we are back at prefix `"1"` → state $S_1$. This is why the FSM has only 3 non-absorbing states.

### Step 3: State Encoding (Binary)

| **State** | **$Q_1 Q_0$** |
|---|---|
| $S_0$ | 00 |
| $S_1$ | 01 |
| $S_2$ | 10 |
| $S_3$ | 11 |

### Step 4: Derive Next-State and Output Equations

Using K-maps with $Q_1, Q_0, x$ as variables:

$$Q_1^{+} = \bar{Q_1} Q_0 \bar{x} + Q_1 \bar{Q_0} x + Q_1 Q_0$$

$$Q_0^{+} = \bar{Q_0} x + Q_1 x + Q_1 Q_0$$

$$z = Q_1 Q_0 x + Q_1 \bar{Q_0} \bar{x} = Q_1 (Q_0 \oplus \bar{x})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU, you only need to **draw the state diagram and produce the transition table** for full marks. Boolean minimization is bonus.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Model

| **System Characteristic** | **Best Model** | **Justification** |
|---|---|---|
| Pure signal processing (filtering, FFT) | **DFG** | No control flow, data-dominated |
| Embedded C program with loops | **CDFG** | Captures both control and data |
| Washing machine controller | **FSM (Moore)** | Discrete states (idle, wash, rinse, spin) |
| UART protocol receiver | **FSM (Mealy)** | Asynchronous input, fast response needed |
| Multi-processor SoC | **CSP / Process Networks** | Explicit inter-process communication |
| Distributed system with timing constraints | **Petri Nets** | Concurrency + synchronization modeling |
| DSP filter chain | **Pipelined Sequential** | High throughput via overlapping stages |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DFG to Schedule Mapping (Mermaid Block-Level Flow)

The following diagram illustrates how a DFG (top) is decomposed into a valid schedule on three functional units (bottom).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[Input Stage]
        a1[Input a]:::input
        b1[Input b]:::input
        c1[Input c]:::input
        d1[Input d]:::input
        e1[Input e]:::input
        f1[Input f]:::input
    end

    subgraph OP1[Operation Nodes]
        opA["ADD a plus b"]:::add
        opB["SUB c minus d"]:::sub
    end

    subgraph OP2[Operation Nodes Stage2]
        opC["MUL result1 result2"]:::mul
        opD["DIV e f"]:::div
    end

    subgraph OP3[Final Stage]
        opE["ADD result3 result4"]:::add
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output]
        y1[Output y]:::output
    end

    a1 --> opA
    b1 --> opA
    c1 --> opB
    d1 --> opB
    opA --> opC
    opB --> opC
    e1 --> opD
    f1 --> opD
    opC --> opE
    opD --> opE
    opE --> y1

    classDef input fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef add fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sub fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mul fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef div fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 CDFG Representation of a Simple Loop

Consider the C fragment:

```c
sum = 0;
for (i = 0; i < N; i++) {
    sum = sum + a[i];
}
```

The corresponding CDFG:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start([Start]):::start
    init["i = 0, sum = 0"]:::init
    cond{"i less than N"}:::cond
    body["sum = sum + a of i"]:::body
    inc["i = i + 1"]:::inc
    output[Return sum]:::output
    endnode([End]):::end

    start --> init --> cond
    cond -- TRUE --> body --> inc --> cond
    cond -- FALSE --> output --> endnode

    classDef start fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px
    classDef init fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px
    classDef cond fill:#ffcc80,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px
    classDef body fill:#ce93d8,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px
    classDef inc fill:#90caf9,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px
    classDef output fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px
    classDef end fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Mealy vs. Moore FSM Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MEALY[Mealy Machine]
        M_In[Input x]:::io --> M_Comb1[Combinational Logic delta]:::logic
        M_FF[State Register Q1 Q0]:::ff
        M_Comb1 --> M_FF
        M_FF --> M_Comb1
        M_Comb1 --> M_Out[Output z lambda of s and x]:::io
    end

    subgraph MOORE[Moore Machine]
        R_In[Input x]:::io2 --> R_Comb2[Combinational Logic delta]:::logic2
        R_FF2[State Register Q1 Q0]:::ff2
        R_Comb2 --> R_FF2
        R_FF2 --> R_Comb2
        R_FF2 --> R_Out[Output z lambda of s]:::io2
    end

    classDef io fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef logic fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ff fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef io2 fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef logic2 fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ff2 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Sequential vs. Parallel Execution Timeline

```mermaid
gantt
    title Sequential vs Parallel Execution Timeline
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s

    section Sequential
    Op1 Add :seq1, 0, 2
    section Parallel
    Op2 Sub :par1, 0, 2
    section Sequential (continued)
    Op3 Mul :seq2, 2, 5
    section Parallel (continued)
    Op4 Div :par2, 2, 8
    section Sequential (final)
    Op5 Add :seq3, 7, 2
    section Parallel (final)
    Op5b Add :par3, 8, 2
```

> **Reading the diagram:** In sequential mode, all 5 ops stack vertically totalling 19 ns. In parallel mode, Op1 and Op2 run together, then Op3 starts at t=2, while Op4 runs alongside until t=8. The final Op5 starts at t=8 (the later of Op3 and Op4) and ends at t=10 — confirming our analytical $L_{par} = 10$ ns.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Differentiate between a Data Flow Graph (DFG) and a Control Flow Graph (CFG).

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **DFG** | **CFG** |
|---|---|---|
| Represents | Data dependencies | Control dependencies |
| Edges mean | Data tokens flow | Possible execution paths |
| Used for | DSP, signal processing | Program flow, compiler design |
| Loops | No native loop structure (back edges only) | Native `for/while/if-else` constructs |
| Output | One output per node | Possibly branching outputs |

A DFG is **acyclic** (in one iteration) and assumes every node fires. A CFG has **branching edges** representing `if-else` decisions, and cycles representing loops. (3 marks)

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is the difference between a Moore machine and a Mealy machine? Give one example each.

**Model Answer:**
- **Moore Machine:** Output depends only on the **current state** — $O = \lambda(S)$. Output changes synchronously with state transitions. **Example:** Traffic light controller (output = `RED/YELLOW/GREEN`).
- **Mealy Machine:** Output depends on the **current state AND the current input** — $O = \lambda(S, I)$. Output is asynchronous and reacts immediately. **Example:** Vending machine coin detector (output = product dispense on coin insert).

Moore is slower (1 cycle latency) but glitch-free; Mealy is faster but may glitch. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: DFG Analysis & Scheduling

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Adapted)]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** Draw the Data Flow Graph (DFG) for the following expression and identify the critical path. Operation delays are: `+` = 1 ns, `−` = 1 ns, `×` = 3 ns, `÷` = 4 ns.
>
> $$y = ((a + b) \times c) - (d \div e) + f$$
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Using the ASAP scheduling algorithm, schedule the DFG into a minimum number of clock cycles. Assume clock period $T_{clk} = 4$ ns. Compute the speedup over sequential execution.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1:** Decompose the expression into primitive operations:
- $p_1 = a + b$ (addition)
- $p_2 = p_1 \times c$ (multiplication)
- $p_3 = d \div e$ (division)
- $p_4 = p_2 - p_3$ (subtraction)
- $p_5 = p_4 + f$ (addition)

**Step 2:** Draw the DFG:
```
a ─┐
   ├──[+]── p1 ──[×]── p2 ──[-]── p4 ──[+]── y
b ─┘                 │                │
                    c─┘   d ─┐         │
                              ├──[÷]── p3
                         e ─┘            f ─┐
                                              │
                                              └── (input to + for p5)
```

**Step 3:** Enumerate all paths from input to output:

| **Path** | **Operations** | **Delay (ns)** |
|---|---|---|
| Path 1: a,b → + → × → − → + | 1 + 3 + 1 + 1 | 6 |
| Path 2: d,e → ÷ → − → + | 4 + 1 + 1 | 6 |

**Step 4:** Critical path delay:
$$L_{critical} = \max(6, 6) = 6 \text{ ns}$$

So the **critical path** is either Path 1 or Path 2, both giving **6 ns**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** Compute sequential latency:
$$L_{seq} = t_{+} + t_{\times} + t_{\div} + t_{-} + t_{+} = 1 + 3 + 4 + 1 + 1 = 10 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2:** Apply ASAP formula. Let cycle 0 = start.

| **Node** | **Op** | **Delay (cycles)** | **ASAP Start** | **ASAP Finish** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| p1 | + | $\lceil 1/4 \rceil = 1$ | 0 | 1 |
| p3 | ÷ | $\lceil 4/4 \rceil = 1$ | 0 | 1 |
| p2 | × | $\lceil 3/4 \rceil = 1$ | 1 | 2 |
| p4 | − | $\lceil 1/4 \rceil = 1$ | $\max(2, 1) = 2$ | 3 |
| p5 | + | 1 | 3 | 4 |

**Step 3:** Number of clock cycles needed = **4 cycles** (vs. 5 in purely sequential timing).

**Step 4:** Compute speedup:
$$S = \frac{L_{seq}}{L_{par}} = \frac{10 \text{ ns}}{4 \text{ cycles} \times 4 \text{ ns/cycle}} = \frac{10}{16} = 0.625$$

Wait — this is a slowdown because the clock period was chosen larger than the smallest op delay. Let's recompute assuming the clock fits the slowest op:

If we use $T_{clk} = 4$ ns (matching the divider), each op takes 1 cycle:
$$L_{par, cycles} = 4, \quad L_{par, time} = 4 \times 4 = 16 \text{ ns}$$

Speedup $S = 10 / 16 = 0.625$ (a slowdown — illustrating **Amdahl's limit** when resources are underutilized).

> Better answer: Use the **optimal** clock $T_{clk} = 3$ ns (largest single op is × = 3 ns), giving $L_{par} = 4 \times 3 = 12$ ns, $S = 10/12 = 0.83$.

**Step 5:** Resource allocation: 1 adder, 1 multiplier, 1 divider — 3 functional units.

**Efficiency:**
$$\eta = S / N_{proc} = 0.83 / 3 \approx 0.277 = 27.7\%$$

**(7 marks)**

---

### Question B: FSM Design (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Adapted)]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** Design a Mealy FSM that detects the overlapping sequence `"0110"` from a serial input $x$. The output $z = 1$ when the sequence is detected. Draw the state diagram and the state transition table.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Convert this Mealy machine into an equivalent Moore machine. State the number of states in the Moore machine and justify.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — Mealy FSM for `0110`

**Step 1:** Identify the four prefix states:
- $S_0$ = no useful prefix
- $S_1$ = last input was `0` (prefix `"0"`)
- $S_2$ = last two inputs were `"01"` (prefix `"01"`)
- $S_3$ = last three inputs were `"011"` (prefix `"011"`)
- $S_4$ = sequence `"0110"` detected (output $z=1$)

**Step 2:** Build state transition table (Mealy):

| **Present State** | **Next State / Output for $x=0$** | **Next State / Output for $x=1$** |
|---|---|---|
| $S_0$ | $S_1 / 0$ | $S_0 / 0$ |
| $S_1$ | $S_1 / 0$ | $S_2 / 0$ |
| $S_2$ | $S_1 / 0$ | $S_3 / 0$ |
| $S_3$ | $S_4 / 1$ | $S_2 / 0$ |
| $S_4$ | $S_1 / 0$ | $S_0 / 0$ |

**Step 3:** State diagram (text representation):
```
            1/0                 1/0
   ┌──→ S0 ──0/0──→ S1 ──1/0──→ S2
   │    ↑          │            │
   │    │          │0/0         │0/0
   │    │          ↓            ↓
   │    └───── 0/0 ←── S3 ←──1/0─┘
   │                │
   │                0/1
   │                ↓
   │               S4 ──1/0──→ S0
   │                │
   └────────0/0─────┘
```

**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Moore Conversion

**Rule:** In a Moore machine, **the output is associated with the state itself**, not the transition. So whenever a Mealy transition produces $z=1$, in the Moore equivalent the destination state is split into a new state that holds the output $z=1$.

**Step 1:** Identify all Mealy transitions producing $z=1$:
- Only one: $S_3 \xrightarrow{0/1} S_4$

**Step 2:** Split $S_4$ into two states:
- $S_4$ (where $z=1$, entered from $S_3$ on $x=0$)
- $S_4'$ (where $z=0$, re-entered from $S_4$ on $x=0$ — because overlapping: after detecting `0110`, if next bit is `0`, we're back at prefix `"0"`)

**Step 3:** Moore state table:

| **State** | **Output $z$** | **Next on $x=0$** | **Next on $x=1$** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S_0$ | 0 | $S_1$ | $S_0$ |
| $S_1$ | 0 | $S_1$ | $S_2$ |
| $S_2$ | 0 | $S_1$ | $S_3$ |
| $S_3$ | 0 | $S_4$ (z=1) | $S_2$ |
| $S_4$ | 1 | $S_1$ | $S_0$ |

**Step 4:** Total states = **5** (same as Mealy in this case). Justification: Moore machines generally require **the same or one more state** than Mealy because the output must be encoded into the state itself. The Moore machine here has **1 extra cycle of latency** (output appears 1 clock after the detecting input).

**(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks in Computational Model questions (Module 2):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to compute BOTH sequential AND parallel latencies** — KTU expects a comparison. Writing only one latency is worth 2 out of 7 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Moore and Mealy outputs** — In Moore, output = `λ(state)`. In Mealy, output = `λ(state, input)`. Drawing a Mealy state with output inside the bubble is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Missing the overlapping-detection rule in FSMs** — After detecting `101`, the next bit `1` returns you to a partial-match state, not back to $S_0$. Many students reset to $S_0$ and lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Not labeling the operation delays on DFG edges or nodes** — Always annotate each `+`, `−`, `×`, `÷` node with its delay. A "naked" DFG is worth at most 3 out of 7 marks.
> 5. **Ignoring clock period constraints in scheduling** — If $T_{clk}$ is given, the number of cycles = $\lceil t_{op}/T_{clk} \rceil$, and you must convert. A common 2-mark pitfall.
> 6. **Drawing DFG with control-flow diamond nodes** — That's a CDFG, not a DFG. Read the question carefully. KTU deducts 1 mark for this confusion.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Computational Models in Embedded Design**

- ✅ **DFG** = acyclic graph of data operations; nodes = ops, edges = data dependencies.
- ✅ **CFG** = control flow with branches and loops; nodes = basic blocks, edges = control transfers.
- ✅ **CDFG** = DFG + control; the canonical compiler IR. Draw `for` loops as back-edges; `if-else` as diamond decision nodes.
- ✅ **FSM (Moore):** Output = `λ(state)`. Synchronous, 1-cycle latency, no glitches.
- ✅ **FSM (Mealy):** Output = `λ(state, input)`. Asynchronous, faster, may glitch.
- ✅ **State minimization rule:** Two states are equivalent if, for every input, they produce the same output and transition to equivalent states.
- ✅ **Critical path = max-delay path** in the DFG → sets minimum parallel latency.
- ✅ **Sequential latency = sum of all op delays**; **Parallel latency = critical path delay**.
- ✅ **Speedup $S = L_{seq}/L_{par}$**; **Efficiency $\eta = S/N_{proc}$**.
- ✅ **ASAP schedule:** forward recursion, $t(v) = \max_{(u,v)}(t(u) + t_{op}(v))$.
- ✅ **ALAP schedule:** backward recursion from output $L$, gives mobility/slack.
- ✅ **Iteration bound** (for loops) = $\max_{l}(t_l / N_l)$ — sets maximum achievable throughput.
- ✅ **Pipeline latency** = $(k + N - 1) \cdot T_{clk}$ where $k$ = stages, $N$ = inputs.
- ✅ **DFG → CDFG upgrade:** Wrap the DFG in a `for`/`while` loop to model iterative DSP.
- ✅ **Common delays (assume unless stated):** `+` = 1, `−` = 1, `×` = 2, `÷` = 4 cycles.
- ✅ **Typical KTU clock period:** $T_{clk} = 10$ ns (100 MHz) or $T_{clk} = 20$ ns (50 MHz).
- ✅ **Always annotate your DFG nodes with operation delays** before computing any metric.
- ✅ **Use the formula sheet (Section 2.5) verbatim** in numerical answers to avoid forgetting steps.

> **Final Tip for KTU 2024 Exam:** Most Module 2 computational model questions are **graphical + numerical**. Practice drawing **5 different DFGs** and **3 different FSMs** by hand before the exam. The 7-mark question almost always combines a drawing with a critical-path computation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Unified Modelling Language (UML)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECIT726 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Unified Modelling Language (UML) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Unified Modelling Language (UML)** is a **standardized, general-purpose visual modelling language** standardized by the **Object Management Group (OMG)** that provides a **set of graphical notations** to **specify, visualize, construct, and document** the artefacts of a software-intensive system, including those of **embedded and real-time systems**. In the context of **KTU OECIT726 – Embedded Systems (Module 2)**, UML is presented as the **de-facto industry standard** for modelling the architecture, behaviour, and deployment of embedded software running on microcontrollers, SoCs, RTOS-based platforms, and heterogeneous hardware. **Key Historical Standardization Facts:** - **Version 2.5.1** is the current widely deployed specification (published by **OMG**). - UML is **not a programming language**, but a **visual modelling language**. - It unifies the notations introduced by **Booch, Rumbaugh (OMT), and Jacobson (OOSE)** — collectively called the **"Three Amigos"**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Architectural Blueprint of an Embedded Product"** > Think of UML as the **architect's blueprint** for a building. Before construction begins, an architect draws: > - **Floor plans** (where rooms sit) → *Class & Component Diagrams* > - **Electrical wiring routes** (how power flows) → *Sequence & Communication Diagrams* > - **Traffic-flow arrows during fire drills** (dynamic behaviour) → *State & Activity Diagrams* > - **Site placement on land** (where the building sits) → *Deployment Diagrams* > > Similarly, an embedded systems engineer uses UML to draw the **"blueprint" of the firmware** *before* writing a single line of C or assembly. It removes ambiguity between hardware and software teams and forms the foundation for **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)**. ### 1.3 Why UML in Embedded Systems? - **Captures both hardware and software** views in one notation. - **Bridges** electrical engineers (schematics) and software engineers (code). - **Supports auto-code generation** for C/C++ from state machines. - **Enables simulation and verification** of control logic *before silicon* is available. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Summary Sheet ### 2.1 The Four Pillars of UML (Building Blocks) UML is constructed from four fundamental building blocks, which form the most frequently asked 3-mark question in KTU examinations. | Pillar | Description | Common Notation | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **1. Things** | The *objects* drawn on a diagram (structural, behavioural, grouping, annotational). | Classes, Interfaces, Actors, Use Cases, Notes | | **2. Relationships** | The *glue* that connects things. | Association, Dependency, Generalization, Realization | | **3. Diagrams** | The *pictures* that show a system's aspects. | 14 diagram types in UML 2.5 | | **4. Extensibility Mechanisms** | Allows UML to be *tailored* for specific domains (e.g., SysML for embedded). | Stereotypes, Tagged Values, Constraints | ### 2.2 The Two Major Diagram Categories > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Classification** > UML diagrams are split into two master categories. Examiner questions almost always begin with this table. | Category | Purpose | Diagrams (UML 2.5.1) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Structure Diagrams** (Static view) | Show the **static** architecture — *what* the system is made of. | Class, Object, Component, **Deployment**, Composite Structure, Package, Profile | | **Behaviour Diagrams** (Dynamic view) | Show the **dynamic** behaviour — *how* the system acts over time. | Use Case, Activity, **State Machine**, Sequence, Communication, Interaction Overview, Timing | > [!NOTE] > **Bolded diagrams are MOST relevant to Embedded Systems (KTU Module-2 focus):** *Deployment* shows how firmware maps to hardware; *State Machine* models finite-state controllers; *Use Case* captures user/device interactions. ### 2.3 The 14 UML Diagrams — High-Yield Cheat Sheet | # | Diagram | Static / Dynamic | Embedded-Systems Relevance (1–5) | | :-: | :--- | :---: | :---: | | 1 | Class Diagram | Static | 5 | | 2 | Object Diagram | Static | 2 | | 3 | Component Diagram | Static | 4 | | 4 | **Deployment Diagram** | Static | **5** | | 5 | Composite Structure | Static | 3 | | 6 | Package Diagram | Static | 2 | | 7 | Use Case Diagram | Dynamic | 4 | | 8 | **State Machine Diagram** | Dynamic | **5** | | 9 | Activity Diagram | Dynamic | 4 | | 10 | Sequence Diagram | Dynamic | 4 | | 11 | Communication Diagram | Dynamic | 2 | | 12 | Interaction Overview | Dynamic | 2 | | 13 | Timing Diagram | Dynamic | 3 | | 14 | Profile Diagram | Static | 2 | ### 2.4 The Four Standard Relationship Types > [!IMPORTANT] > These four relationships appear on **every structural diagram**. Memorize the arrowheads. | Relationship | Symbol (Arrowhead) | Semantic Meaning | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Association** | Plain line (→ optional) | *"uses / has-a"* — structural connection between peers | | **Dependency** | Dashed arrow (`- - >`) | *"depends on / uses temporarily"* — supplier change affects client | | **Generalization** | Hollow triangle arrow (`▷`) | *"is-a / inheritance"* — child inherits from parent | | **Realization** | Dashed hollow triangle (`- - ▷`) | *"implements / fulfils contract"* — class implements an interface | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Application of UML | | :--- | :--- | | **Automotive (AUTOSAR)** | Modelling ECU software components and their interfaces. | | **Avionics (DO-178C)** | Software level design via State Machines and Sequence diagrams. | | **IoT / Consumer Electronics** | Specifying smart-device firmware architecture. | | **Medical Devices (FDA / IEC 62304)** | Traceability from Use-Case to code via model-driven development. | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > **Module-2 Note:** Because UML is a *conceptual* topic (no algebraic derivation exists), this section provides a **rigorous analytical breakdown** of the diagram taxonomy, relationship rules, and a worked-out example showing the *logic* of selecting the correct diagram. ### 3.1 Analytical Step — Selecting the Correct UML Diagram **Problem Context:** A KTU student is asked to model an **automatic washing machine controller** that supports states like *Idle → Wash → Rinse → Spin → Done* with transitions triggered by a timer and a door sensor. **Step 1 — Identify the question keyword.** The keywords **"states"** and **"transitions"** are explicit. **Step 2 — Match keywords to UML diagram families.** - "States" + "Transitions" → matches the **State Machine Diagram**. - UML Static diagrams (Class, Component, Deployment) do **not** depict time-evolving states. - UML Activity diagrams show flow of *activities*, not *state changes of a single object*. **Step 3 — Conclude.** The **State Machine Diagram** is the correct choice. The system's behaviour is described by discrete states and triggered transitions — a finite-state automaton. ### 3.2 Step-by-Step Construction Logic of a UML State Machine The following is the *algorithm* a designer follows to draw a state machine (commonly asked as a 7-mark problem): **Step 1 — Identify the entity (object/class) being modelled.** For the washing machine, the entity is the class `WashingMachineController`. **Step 2 — Enumerate the stable states.** $$\text{States} = \{\text{Idle},\ \text{Wash},\ \text{Rinse},\ \text{Spin},\ \text{DoorOpen},\ \text{Done}\}$$ **Step 3 — Enumerate the events (triggers).** $$\text{Events} = \{\text{start\_button},\ \text{timer\_expired},\ \text{door\_opened},\ \text{door\_closed}\}$$ **Step 4 — Formulate transitions as ordered pairs.** $$T : (S_{\text{current}},\ E) \longrightarrow S_{\text{next}}$$ For example: - $$(\text{Idle},\ \text{start\_button}) \longrightarrow \text{Wash}$$ - $$(\text{Wash},\ \text{timer\_expired}) \longrightarrow \text{Rinse}$$ - $$(\text{Rinse},\ \text{timer\_expired}) \longrightarrow \text{Spin}$$ - $$(\text{Spin},\ \text{timer\_expired}) \longrightarrow \text{Done}$$ - $$(S_{\text{any}},\ \text{door\_opened}) \longrightarrow \text{DoorOpen}$$ **Step 5 — Identify the initial and final pseudo-states.** - **Initial state** (filled black circle) → `Idle` - **Final state** (filled circle inside a ring) → `Done` **Step 6 — Verify closure and determinism.** Ensure that for every `(state, event)` pair relevant to that state, **exactly one** next-state is defined — the FSM is *deterministic*. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three "Most Confused" Diagrams > [!IMPORTANT] > This comparison is a **favourite 14-mark question** in KTU Module-2. | Comparison Axis | State Machine Diagram | Activity Diagram | Sequence Diagram | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Primary focus** | States of a *single object* | Workflow of *activities / processes* | *Time-ordered* message exchange | | **Time dimension** | Implicit (transitions) | Implicit (flow tokens) | **Explicit (top-to-bottom lifeline)** | | **Nodes** | States (rounded rectangles) | Actions / Decisions / Forks | Objects (lifelines) | | **Edges** | Transitions (labelled with event) | Control flow arrows | Messages (numbered, labelled) | | **Concurrency support** | Orthogonal regions (∥) | Fork / Join nodes | Par / Seq fragments | | **Best for** | Reactive embedded controllers | Business processes, algorithms | Protocol / bus communication | | **Example** | Washing Machine FSM | Boot-up procedure of an RTOS | SPI master–slave handshake | ### 3.4 Comprehensive UML Diagram Classification — Full Analytical View | Sl. No. | Diagram | Belongs to (Category) | Embedded Use-Case | | :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Class | Structure | Modelling drivers, HALs, sensor objects | | 2 | Object | Structure | Snapshot of memory at runtime | | 3 | Component | Structure | Showing firmware modules (.c/.h files) | | 4 | Deployment | Structure | Mapping tasks to MCU cores / boards | | 5 | Composite Structure | Structure | Internal ports of an SoC subsystem | | 6 | Package | Structure | Grouping header files / namespaces | | 7 | Profile | Structure | Adding embedded-specific stereotypes | | 8 | Use Case | Behaviour | Capturing user–device interaction | | 9 | Activity | Behaviour | RTOS task scheduling flowchart | | 10 | State Machine | Behaviour | FSM for control logic (washing machine) | | 11 | Sequence | Behaviour | CAN / I²C / UART message sequence | | 12 | Communication | Behaviour | Object links with numbered messages | | 13 | Interaction Overview | Behaviour | High-level sequence-of-sequences | | 14 | Timing | Behaviour | GPIO toggling, PWM waveforms | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 UML Building-Block Hierarchy (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["UML 2.5 Specification
Object Management Group"] --> B["Four Building Blocks"] B --> C["Things
Classes, Actors, Use Cases, Components"] B --> D["Relationships
Association, Dependency, Generalization, Realization"] B --> E["Diagrams
14 Types"] B --> F["Extensibility
Stereotypes, Tagged Values, Constraints"] E --> G["Structure Diagrams
7 Types"] E --> H["Behaviour Diagrams
7 Types"] G --> G1["Class Diagram"] G --> G2["Object Diagram"] G --> G3["Component Diagram"] G --> G4["Deployment Diagram"] G --> G5["Composite Structure"] G --> G6["Package Diagram"] G --> G7["Profile Diagram"] H --> H1["Use Case Diagram"] H --> H2["Activity Diagram"] H --> H3["State Machine Diagram"] H --> H4["Sequence Diagram"] H --> H5["Communication Diagram"] H --> H6["Interaction Overview"] H --> H7["Timing Diagram"] ``` ### 4.2 Embedded-Systems Modelling Workflow (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["System Requirements
Textual SRS"] --> P2["Use Case Diagram
WHO interacts"] P2 --> P3["Class Diagram
WHAT objects exist"] P3 --> P4["State Machine Diagram
HOW object behaves"] P4 --> P5["Sequence Diagram
WHEN messages flow"] P5 --> P6["Component Diagram
WHICH files implement"] P6 --> P7["Deployment Diagram
WHERE it runs on hardware"] P7 --> P8["Auto Code Generation
C / C++ for MCU"] ``` ### 4.3 The Four Relationship Symbols (Visual Cheat-Sheet) ```mermaid flowchart LR A1["Class A"] ---|Association| B1["Class B"] A2["Client"] -.->|Dependency| B2["Supplier"] A3["Child"] -->|Generalization| B3["Parent"] A4["Concrete"] -.->|Realization| B4["Interface"] ``` > **Reading the symbols (from Section 2.4):** > - Solid line = **Association** (peer-to-peer) > - Dashed arrow = **Dependency** (temporary use) > - Solid hollow triangle = **Generalization** (inheritance) > - Dashed hollow triangle = **Realization** (interface implementation) ### 4.4 Example — Washing Machine State Machine (Schematic) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle Idle --> Wash : start_button / motor_on Wash --> Rinse : timer_expired Rinse --> Spin : timer_expired Spin --> Done : timer_expired Done --> Idle : reset_button Wash --> DoorOpen : door_opened Rinse --> DoorOpen : door_opened Spin --> DoorOpen : door_opened DoorOpen --> Idle : door_closed Done --> [*] ``` > [!NOTE] > The above `stateDiagram-v2` Mermaid block is a **direct textual representation** of a UML state machine — each rounded rectangle is a state, each arrow is a labelled transition, and the filled black circle is the initial pseudo-state. Students may reproduce this format in their KTU answer sheets using a hand-drawn equivalent. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. Define Unified Modelling Language (UML). Name its four building blocks.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** UML is a standardized, general-purpose visual modelling language defined by the Object Management Group (OMG). It provides graphical notations to specify, visualize, construct, and document the artefacts of software-intensive systems, including embedded and real-time systems. **Four Building Blocks (2 marks):** 1. **Things** — the objects drawn on a diagram (structural, behavioural, grouping, annotational). 2. **Relationships** — the glue between things (Association, Dependency, Generalization, Realization). 3. **Diagrams** — the pictures that depict a system from different viewpoints. 4. **Extensibility Mechanisms** — Stereotypes, Tagged Values, and Constraints that adapt UML to specific domains. --- **Q2. Differentiate between Structure Diagrams and Behaviour Diagrams in UML. Give two examples of each.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Structure Diagrams | Behaviour Diagrams | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Purpose** | Show the *static* architecture of the system. | Show the *dynamic* behaviour over time. | | **Question answered** | *What* is the system made of? | *How* does the system act? | | **Example 1** | Class Diagram | State Machine Diagram | | **Example 2** | Deployment Diagram | Sequence Diagram | In embedded systems, **Deployment Diagrams** (structure) are used to map firmware to hardware, while **State Machine Diagrams** (behaviour) are used to model reactive control logic. --- ### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]` **(a)** Explain the **four types of relationships** used in UML with suitable diagrams. Discuss their semantic difference. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a **digital wristwatch** (with states *Time, Stopwatch, Alarm, Set*), draw the **State Machine Diagram** and list the events and transitions. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question A **(a) Four UML Relationships (7 marks):** The four standard relationships that connect UML things are: 1. **Association (1.5 marks)** - Represents a *structural relationship* between peer classes — "uses / has-a". - **Symbol:** plain line (optionally with a navigability arrow `→`). - **Example:** A `Microcontroller` class is *associated with* a `Sensor` class. 2. **Dependency (1.5 marks)** - Represents that a *change in the supplier* affects the *client* (temporary usage). - **Symbol:** dashed arrow `- - >`. - **Example:** A `DisplayDriver` *depends on* a `Timer` class for delay routines. 3. **Generalization (2 marks)** - Represents *inheritance* — "is-a" relationship from child to parent. - **Symbol:** solid line with hollow triangle arrow `▷`. - **Example:** `STM32F4_Controller` *generalizes* `ARM_Cortex_M4`. 4. **Realization (2 marks)** - Represents that a *concrete class implements* the contract of an *interface*. - **Symbol:** dashed line with hollow triangle `- - ▷`. - **Example:** `I2CDriver` *realizes* the `ICommunication` interface. | Relationship | Arrowhead | Use | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Association | Plain line | Peer structural link | | Dependency | Dashed arrow | Temporary reliance | | Generalization | Hollow triangle | Inheritance | | Realization | Dashed hollow triangle | Interface implementation | --- **(b) State Machine Diagram for Digital Wristwatch (7 marks):** **Step 1 — States (1 mark):** - Time mode, Stopwatch mode, Alarm mode, Set mode. **Step 2 — Events (1 mark):** - `Mode_Button_Pressed`, `Start_Stop_Button`, `Lap_Button`, `Increment_Button`. **Step 3 — Transitions (3 marks):** - (Time, Mode_Button_Pressed) → Stopwatch - (Stopwatch, Mode_Button_Pressed) → Alarm - (Alarm, Mode_Button_Pressed) → Set - (Set, Mode_Button_Pressed) → Time - (Stopwatch, Start_Stop_Button) → RunningStopwatch - (RunningStopwatch, Start_Stop_Button) → Stopwatch - (Set, Increment_Button) → IncrementHour **Step 4 — Initial and Final States (1 mark):** - Initial → Time; no explicit final state (the system runs continuously). **Step 5 — Diagram (1 mark):** A hand-drawn state machine (or the Mermaid `stateDiagram-v2` block in Section 4.4) is accepted. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]` **(a)** List and briefly explain the **14 diagrams of UML 2.5** with one-line purpose statements. Classify them as Structure or Behaviour. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Draw a **Deployment Diagram** for a typical embedded system consisting of: - A **STM32 MCU** (host node), - A **Temperature Sensor** (slave device), - An **LCD Display** (output device), - Connected via the **I²C bus**.
