Explain the notation of nodes, artifacts, and communication paths. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question B **(a) The 14 UML 2.5 Diagrams (7 marks):** **Structure Diagrams (4 marks — half-mark each):** 1. **Class Diagram** — static structure of classes and their relationships. 2. **Object Diagram** — runtime snapshot of class instances. 3. **Component Diagram** — organization of software components and their dependencies. 4. **Deployment Diagram** — physical mapping of software artefacts to hardware nodes. 5. **Composite Structure Diagram** — internal structure of a class / component. 6. **Package Diagram** — grouping of model elements into packages. 7. **Profile Diagram** — domain-specific extensions to UML. **Behaviour Diagrams (3 marks — half-mark each):** 8. **Use Case Diagram** — functional requirements from the user's view. 9. **Activity Diagram** — workflow of activities and decisions. 10. **State Machine Diagram** — states and transitions of a single object. 11. **Sequence Diagram** — time-ordered messages between objects. 12. **Communication Diagram** — messages between linked objects, numbered. 13. **Interaction Overview Diagram** — combination of activity and sequence logic. 14. **Timing Diagram** — state/value changes against a time axis. --- **(b) Deployment Diagram for an Embedded System (7 marks):** **Components and Notation (2 marks):** - **Node** = `3D-rectangle` (hardware device). - **Artifact** = `document` symbol (software file / firmware). - **Communication Path** = solid line labelled with the protocol (e.g., I²C). - **Stereotype** = `<<…>>` in double guillemets. **Hand-Drawn Description (3 marks):** ``` ┌────────────────────────┐ │ <> │ │ STM32_MCU_Node │ ◄──── Artifact: "main.hex" │ │ │ ┌──────────────────┐ │ │ │ <> │ │ │ │ Firmware_App │ │ │ └──────────────────┘ │ └─────────┬──────────────┘ │ I²C Bus │ (SDA, SCL) ┌────────┴────────┐ │ │ ┌──┴──────────┐ ┌───┴────────┐ │ <> │ │ <> │ │ TempSensor │ │ LCD_16x2 │ │ (LM75) │ │ Display │ └─────────────┘ └────────────┘ ``` **Explanation of Notation (2 marks):** - The **STM32 MCU node** executes the `Firmware_App` artifact (`main.hex`). - The **Temperature Sensor** and **LCD** are separate device nodes. - All three are connected via the **I²C communication path**. - The deployment diagram shows *physical* mapping, unlike a component diagram which is purely logical. --- ### 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Costing 1–3 Marks in KTU Board Exams** > 1. **Confusing State Machine with Activity diagrams** — A state machine describes *states of a single object*; an activity describes *workflow of activities*. Examiners allocate zero marks if the wrong diagram is drawn. > 2. **Drawing wrong arrowheads** — A solid triangle is *Generalization*; a *dashed* triangle is *Realization*. Mixing them up costs 1 mark per relationship. > 3. **Forgetting the initial pseudo-state** — Every state machine must begin with a filled black circle labelled `[*]` or the equivalent. > 4. **Writing "UML is a programming language"** — This is the most common 0-mark definition error. Always state: *"UML is a visual **modelling** language."* > 5. **Omitting the OMG / version reference** — Examiners expect *"standardized by the Object Management Group (OMG), UML 2.5.1"*. > 6. **Deployment vs Component confusion** — A deployment diagram shows **hardware nodes and physical wiring**, NOT just software files. --- ### 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist — UML Introduction** - [x] **UML** = **Unified Modelling Language**, a **visual modelling language** (NOT a programming language). - [x] Standardized by the **Object Management Group (OMG)**; current version **UML 2.5.1**. - [x] **Four building blocks:** Things, Relationships, Diagrams, Extensibility Mechanisms. - [x] **Four relationships:** Association, Dependency, Generalization, Realization. - [x] **Two diagram families:** 7 **Structure** + 7 **Behaviour** = 14 total. - [x] **Most-used in Embedded Systems:** *Deployment*, *State Machine*, *Use Case*, *Sequence*, *Component*, *Class*. - [x] **State Machine keywords** (for 7-mark FSM questions): states, events, transitions, initial pseudo-state, final state. - [x] **Deployment Diagram keywords**: node, artifact, communication path, device stereotype `<>`. - [x] **Activity vs State Machine** — Activity = workflow of actions; State Machine = states of one object. - [x] UML is the **foundation of Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** and is used in **AUTOSAR, DO-178C, IEC 62304** compliant embedded development. - [x] Always draw arrowheads correctly — examiners award marks for **notation accuracy**.

topics.title: Hardware Software Trade-offs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT726 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hardware Software Trade-offs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hardware-Software Trade-off** is the fundamental design decision-making process in embedded system engineering where a given system functionality can be realized either as **dedicated hardware logic** (gates, ASICs, FPGAs, custom circuits) or as **software instructions** (firmware running on a general-purpose processor/microcontroller/DSP). The decision involves balancing competing engineering constraints — performance, cost, power, flexibility, time-to-market, and reliability.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, this topic sits at the heart of **Hardware-Software Co-design**, where partitioning an application into the optimal mix of hardware accelerators and software modules determines whether the embedded product succeeds commercially and operationally.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Kitchen Restaurant Problem**
> Imagine you run a restaurant and need to prepare 1,000 meals.
> - **Hardware approach** = Buying 10 industrial ovens and hiring 10 chefs. Expensive upfront (Non-Recurring Engineering cost), but very fast.
> - **Software approach** = Buying 1 oven and writing a detailed recipe book. Cheap to start, easy to update, but slower.
> - **Co-design** = Buying 2 industrial ovens (hardware for bottlenecks like baking) AND using a recipe book (software for variety like sauces and garnishes).
>
> The "trade-off" is choosing *which parts* to make automatic (hardware) and *which parts* to keep flexible (software).

## 1.3 Standard KTU Metrics (Bold Highlights)

The following metrics are **bold**-level important for KTU valuation:

- **NRE Cost (Non-Recurring Engineering)** = one-time cost to design the hardware (in ₹/$).
- **Per-Unit Cost** = manufacturing cost of each copy.
- **Time-to-Market** = weeks/months from design to product launch.
- **Performance** = measured in **MIPS** (Million Instructions Per Second) for software and **Clock Frequency (MHz/GHz)** for hardware.
- **Power Dissipation** = measured in **mW** or **W**.
- **Flexibility** = ease of modifying/upgrading after deployment.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked "Define hardware-software trade-off," always mention the **co-existence of both domains** and the **need for partitioning** — never treat hardware and software as isolated choices.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Trade-off Decision

The hardware-software partitioning follows a structured decision flow:

- **Step 1 — Identify System Functionalities**
  Decompose the system specification into atomic tasks (e.g., signal filtering, encryption, motor control, user I/O).

- **Step 2 — Profile Each Task**
  Estimate computational load, real-time deadline, data dependency, and power budget for each task.

- **Step 3 — Evaluate Implementation Alternatives**
  For each task, evaluate *at least two* implementation candidates:
  1. Pure software on a general-purpose processor.
  2. Dedicated hardware logic (custom or reconfigurable).

- **Step 4 — Apply Selection Criteria**
  Use the criteria listed in §2.2 to choose the better candidate.

- **Step 5 — Validate via Co-simulation**
  Hardware-software co-simulation verifies that timing, power, and functional constraints are met *together* (not just in isolation).

- **Step 6 — Iterate if Constraints Fail**
  Move a task from software to hardware (or vice versa) and re-simulate.

## 2.2 Core Trade-off Criteria (The "Why" Behind Each Decision)

- **Performance & Speed**
  *Why:* Hardware executes operations in parallel and in a single clock cycle, while software executes sequentially.
  *How:* Critical loops (DSP filters, encryption) → hardware; control logic → software.

- **Cost**
  *Why:* Hardware has high NRE (mask, fabrication, verification) but low per-unit cost at volume.
  *How:* Low-volume products → software; high-volume products → hardware.

- **Power Consumption**
  *Why:* A hardware block clocked only when active can be orders of magnitude lower power than a processor running continuously.
  *How:* Battery-powered devices push critical DSP into hardware.

- **Flexibility & Upgradability**
  *Why:* Software can be patched over-the-air; hardware cannot be modified after fabrication.
  *How:* Evolving standards (e.g., wireless protocols) → software; stable standards → hardware.

- **Time-to-Market**
  *Why:* Software development on a standard MCU is faster than ASIC design (which takes 12–18 months).
  *How:* Startups and prototype products → software-first.

- **Reliability & Determinism**
  *Why:* Hardware has predictable timing; software with caches/branching is non-deterministic.
  *How:* Hard real-time systems (airbag ECU, ABS brakes) → hardware or RTOS-controlled software.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **examinable** in KTU ESE 2024 Scheme. Use `\vert` and `\mid` instead of `|` to keep markdown tables intact.

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{total} = P_{dynamic} + P_{static}$ | Total power = switching + leakage | W |
| 2 | $P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f$ | Dynamic power dissipation (CMOS) | W |
| 3 | $P_{static} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{dd}$ | Static / leakage power | W |
| 4 | $Throughput = \dfrac{N_{tasks}}{T_{total}}$ | Tasks completed per unit time | tasks/s |
| 5 | $T_{exec} = \dfrac{N_{cycles}}{f_{clk}}$ | Execution time of a software task | s |
| 6 | $Speedup = \dfrac{T_{sw}}{T_{hw}}$ | Hardware speedup over software | dimensionless |
| 7 | $Cost_{total} = NRE + (N_{units} \cdot C_{unit})$ | Total product cost | ₹ or \$ |
| 8 | $BreakEven = \dfrac{NRE_{hw} - NRE_{sw}}{C_{sw} - C_{hw}}$ | Volume at which hardware becomes cheaper | units |

Where:
- $\alpha$ = switching activity factor (0 to 1)
- $C$ = load capacitance (F)
- $V_{dd}$ = supply voltage (V)
- $f$ = clock frequency (Hz)
- $I_{leak}$ = leakage current (A)
- $N_{cycles}$ = number of clock cycles
- $f_{clk}$ = clock frequency (Hz)
- $N_{units}$ = production volume
- $C_{unit}$ = per-unit manufacturing cost

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECUs:** Engine control = hardware (deterministic); infotainment = software (flexible).
- **Smartphones:** Modem baseband = hardware ASIC; apps = software on ARM cores.
- **IoT Sensors:** Sleep/wake logic = hardware; data aggregation = software on Cortex-M0.
- **Aerospace:** Flight control = redundant hardware; mission planning = software.
- **Medical Devices:** Pacemaker timing = hardware; diagnostics = software.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Execution Time of a Software Task

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is **frequently asked** in KTU Module 2 questions. Memorize the logical steps, not just the formula.

**Given:**
- A software task requiring $N_{cycles}$ clock cycles.
- Processor clock frequency $= f_{clk}$ (in Hz).
- Each clock cycle duration $= T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}}$ seconds.

**Derivation:**

Step 1 — Define the duration of one clock period.

$$
T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}}
$$

*Explanation:* Frequency is the number of cycles per second, so its reciprocal is the time per cycle.

Step 2 — Total execution time equals the number of cycles multiplied by the time per cycle.

$$
T_{exec} = N_{cycles} \cdot T_{clk}
$$

Step 3 — Substitute $T_{clk}$ from Step 1 into Step 2.

$$
T_{exec} = N_{cycles} \cdot \frac{1}{f_{clk}}
$$

Step 4 — Final compact form (the **KTU high-yield formula**).

$$
T_{exec} = \frac{N_{cycles}}{f_{clk}}
$$

*Explanation:* If a filter algorithm needs 10,000 cycles and the MCU runs at 50 MHz, then $T_{exec} = 10000 / (50 \times 10^{6}) = 200 \ \mu s$. If hardware completes the same filter in 50 clock cycles at the same frequency, $T_{hw} = 1 \ \mu s$, giving a speedup of 200×.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Break-Even Volume for Hardware vs Software

This derivation answers: *At what production volume does ASIC become cheaper than microcode?*

**Given:**
- $NRE_{hw}$ = non-recurring engineering cost of hardware design.
- $NRE_{sw}$ = non-recurring engineering cost of software development.
- $C_{hw}$ = per-unit cost of hardware implementation.
- $C_{sw}$ = per-unit cost of software implementation.
- $N$ = number of units produced.

**Derivation:**

Step 1 — Total cost using hardware path.

$$
Cost_{hw} = NRE_{hw} + (N \cdot C_{hw})
$$

Step 2 — Total cost using software path.

$$
Cost_{sw} = NRE_{sw} + (N \cdot C_{sw})
$$

Step 3 — At break-even, the two costs are equal.

$$
NRE_{hw} + (N \cdot C_{hw}) = NRE_{sw} + (N \cdot C_{sw})
$$

Step 4 — Isolate $N$ on one side.

$$
NRE_{hw} - NRE_{sw} = N \cdot C_{sw} - N \cdot C_{hw}
$$

Step 5 — Factor out $N$ on the right-hand side.

$$
NRE_{hw} - NRE_{sw} = N \cdot (C_{sw} - C_{hw})
$$

Step 6 — Solve for $N$ (the break-even production volume).

$$
N = \frac{NRE_{hw} - NRE_{sw}}{C_{sw} - C_{hw}}
$$

*Explanation:* If hardware has ₹10 lakh NRE and ₹50 per unit, and software has ₹1 lakh NRE and ₹200 per unit, the break-even is $N = (10 - 1) \text{ lakh} / (200 - 50) = 6000$ units. Beyond 6,000 units, hardware is more economical.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Hardware Speedup

**Given:**
- Software execution time $= T_{sw}$.
- Hardware execution time $= T_{hw}$.

**Derivation:**

Step 1 — Speedup is the ratio of software time to hardware time.

$$
S = \frac{T_{sw}}{T_{hw}}
$$

Step 2 — Substitute the execution-time formula from §3.1.

$$
S = \frac{N_{sw} \, / \, f_{clk}}{N_{hw} \, / \, f_{clk}} = \frac{N_{sw}}{N_{hw}}
$$

*Explanation:* At the same clock frequency, the speedup equals the ratio of cycle counts. If software needs 10,000 cycles and hardware finishes in 200 cycles, the speedup is 50×.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Hardware vs Software)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is a **favorite KTU question** — usually appears as a 7-mark sub-part.

| Criterion | Hardware (ASIC / FPGA / Custom) | Software (Firmware on Processor) |
|---|---|---|
| **Performance** | Very high; parallel; single-cycle ops | Lower; sequential; instruction-fetch overhead |
| **Power** | Low (custom clock-gated blocks) | Higher (whole CPU active) |
| **NRE Cost** | Very high (₹10L–₹1Cr) | Low to moderate (₹1L–₹5L) |
| **Per-Unit Cost (volume)** | Low at high volume | Moderate (memory cost dominates) |
| **Flexibility** | None (ASIC) / Limited (FPGA) | High (firmware update) |
| **Time-to-Market** | Long (12–18 months) | Short (weeks) |
| **Determinism** | High (predictable) | Lower (caches, interrupts) |
| **Design Complexity** | VLSI / RTL design | C / Embedded C / RTOS |
| **Verification** | Formal methods, timing closure | Unit tests, integration tests |
| **Reusability** | Low (application-specific) | High (library code, OS APIs) |
| **Best Suited For** | DSP, codecs, encryption, motor control | Control logic, UI, protocols, AI inference |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A crypto block requires 50,000 cycles in software on a 100 MHz ARM Cortex-M3. An equivalent hardware accelerator completes it in 200 cycles at the same clock. Calculate (a) software execution time, (b) hardware execution time, (c) speedup.

**Solution:**

(a) Software execution time:

$$
T_{sw} = \frac{50000}{100 \times 10^{6}} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \ s = 500 \ \mu s
$$

(b) Hardware execution time:

$$
T_{hw} = \frac{200}{100 \times 10^{6}} = 2 \times 10^{-6} \ s = 2 \ \mu s
$$

(c) Speedup:

$$
S = \frac{500 \ \mu s}{2 \ \mu s} = 250
$$

**Conclusion:** The hardware accelerator is **250× faster** than the software implementation — justifying its use in a security-critical embedded device.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hardware-Software Partitioning Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: System Specification] --> B[Decompose into Functional Tasks]
    B --> C{Task Profiling}
    C --> D[Estimate Cycles, Power, Deadline]
    D --> E{Is Task Real-Time Critical?}
    E -- Yes --> F{High Volume Production?}
    E -- No --> G[Implement in SOFTWARE]
    F -- Yes --> H[Implement in HARDWARE ASIC]
    F -- No --> I[Implement in FPGA / Reconfigurable HW]
    H --> J[Co-Simulation Validation]
    I --> J
    G --> J
    J --> K{Constraints Met?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Final Partitioned Design]
    K -- No --> M[Re-partition: Move Task Across Domains]
    M --> C
    L --> N[End: HW-SW Co-Design Complete]
```

*Explanation:* This flowchart captures the iterative co-design process — the core concept of hardware-software trade-offs in embedded systems.

## 4.2 Architecture of a Co-Designed Embedded System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU[General Purpose CPU Core] --> BUS[System Bus AXI / AHB]
    DSP[Hardware DSP Accelerator] --> BUS
    CRYPTO[Hardware Crypto Block] --> BUS
    MEM[Shared Memory / Cache] --> BUS
    IO[GPIO and Peripheral Controller] --> BUS
    BUS --> SW1[Software Task 1 Control Loop]
    BUS --> SW2[Software Task 2 Communication Stack]
    SW1 -.->|runs on| CPU
    SW2 -.->|runs on| CPU
    DSP -.->|offloaded from| CPU
    CRYPTO -.->|offloaded from| CPU
```

*Explanation:* The CPU handles flexible software tasks, while DSP and crypto blocks are hardware accelerators offloaded for performance. Communication occurs over a shared system bus.

## 4.3 Cost-Volume Trade-off Graph (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Production Volume Axis X] --> B[Cost Axis Y]
    B --> C[Total Cost Curve Decreases with Volume]
    C --> D[Hardware Cost Falls Steeply After Break-even]
    C --> E[Software Cost Falls Slowly]
    D --> F[Intersection Point = Break-even Volume]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Beyond Break-even: Hardware Wins]
    F --> H[Before Break-even: Software Wins]
```

*Explanation:* Below the break-even volume, software is cheaper; above it, hardware is more economical — a key KTU trade-off concept.

## 4.4 Partitioning Strategy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    APP[Embedded Application] --> P1[Partitioning Tool]
    P1 --> HW[Hardware Block ASIC / FPGA]
    P1 --> SW[Software Block Firmware / RTOS]
    HW --> COSIM[Hardware Software Co-Simulation]
    SW --> COSIM
    COSIM --> VAL{Validation Pass?}
    VAL -- Yes --> FAB[Fabrication and Deployment]
    VAL -- No --> P1
```

*Explanation:* Modern co-design tools (e.g., Xilinx Vivado, Cadence, Synopsys) automate the partitioning process and verify the design via co-simulation.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define hardware-software trade-off in embedded systems. List any four criteria used for trade-off decisions.**

**Model Answer:**
Hardware-software trade-off is the design decision of implementing a given functionality either in dedicated hardware logic or in software running on a general-purpose processor, based on engineering constraints. The four key criteria are:
1. Performance (speed, latency)
2. Cost (NRE and per-unit)
3. Power consumption
4. Flexibility and time-to-market

*(Examiner expects at least 4 criteria with one-line justification each.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q: Differentiate between hardware implementation and software implementation of an embedded task.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Hardware Implementation | Software Implementation |
|---|---|---|
| Execution | Parallel, single-cycle | Sequential, instruction-by-instruction |
| NRE Cost | High | Low |
| Flexibility | None (ASIC) or limited (FPGA) | High — easily updatable |
| Determinism | High | Lower (interrupts, caches) |
| Time-to-Market | Long | Short |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **hardware-software co-design** process. List and describe any four factors that influence the hardware-software trade-off decision in an embedded system.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A signal-processing algorithm requires **120,000 clock cycles** in software on a processor running at **80 MHz**. The same algorithm, when implemented in hardware, completes in **400 cycles** at the same clock. Compute:
   (i) Software execution time
   (ii) Hardware execution time
   (iii) Speedup factor

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Hardware-Software Co-design Process:**
Hardware-software co-design is a concurrent design methodology where both hardware and software modules of an embedded system are designed together rather than sequentially. The steps are:

1. **System Specification:** Define functional and non-functional requirements (speed, power, cost).
2. **Partitioning:** Decide which tasks go to hardware and which to software.
3. **Co-simulation:** Simulate the hardware and software together to verify timing and functionality.
4. **Implementation:** Synthesize hardware (RTL → netlist) and compile software (C → binary).
5. **Integration & Testing:** Combine on the target platform (SoC / FPGA) and validate.

**Four Factors Influencing the Trade-off:**

- **Performance Requirements:** Real-time, latency-sensitive tasks (e.g., video encoding) → hardware.
- **Cost Constraints:** Low-volume products → software (to amortize NRE); high-volume → hardware.
- **Power Budget:** Battery-operated wearables push heavy computation into dedicated hardware blocks.
- **Time-to-Market:** Tight deadlines favor software on a standard MCU; longer schedules allow ASIC design.
- **(Optional 5th) Flexibility:** Evolving protocols (e.g., 5G) require software updates; stable standards (e.g., USB) can be hardened in hardware.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**(i) Software execution time:**

$$
T_{sw} = \frac{N_{sw}}{f_{clk}} = \frac{120000}{80 \times 10^{6}} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \ s = 1.5 \ ms
$$

**(ii) Hardware execution time:**

$$
T_{hw} = \frac{N_{hw}}{f_{clk}} = \frac{400}{80 \times 10^{6}} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \ s = 5 \ \mu s
$$

**(iii) Speedup factor:**

$$
S = \frac{T_{sw}}{T_{hw}} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 300
$$

**Conclusion:** The hardware implementation is **300 times faster** than the software implementation. The decision to migrate this DSP block to hardware is justified when real-time deadlines cannot be met by software alone.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **advantages and disadvantages** of implementing functionality in **hardware** versus **software** in embedded systems. Use a comparison table.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An embedded system is being designed for two implementation options:
   - **Option H (Hardware/ASIC):** NRE = ₹15,00,000; per-unit cost = ₹80
   - **Option S (Software/MCU):** NRE = ₹2,00,000; per-unit cost = ₹250
   
   Determine the **break-even production volume** at which both options cost the same. Recommend which option to choose for a production run of **20,000 units**.

#### Model Solution to (a)

| Criterion | Hardware (ASIC) | Software (MCU Firmware) |
|---|---|---|
| Speed | Very high — parallel execution | Lower — sequential |
| Power | Low (clock gating possible) | Higher (whole MCU active) |
| NRE Cost | ₹10 Lakh to ₹1 Crore | ₹1–5 Lakh |
| Per-Unit Cost | Low at high volume | Moderate (memory cost) |
| Flexibility | Very low — fixed after fab | Very high — firmware updatable |
| Time-to-Market | 12–18 months | A few weeks |
| Determinism | Highly deterministic | Less deterministic (RTOS overhead) |
| Verification Effort | Formal methods, timing closure | Unit + integration testing |
| Best Suited For | DSP, codecs, encryption | UI, control logic, networking |

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Given:**
- $NRE_{hw} = 15,00,000$; $C_{hw} = 80$ ₹
- $NRE_{sw} = 2,00,000$; $C_{sw} = 250$ ₹

**Step 1 — Apply break-even formula:**

$$
N = \frac{NRE_{hw} - NRE_{sw}}{C_{sw} - C_{hw}} = \frac{15,00,000 - 2,00,000}{250 - 80} = \frac{13,00,000}{170}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate:**

$$
N = 7647.06 \approx 7648 \ units
$$

**Step 3 — Decision for 20,000 units:**

Since the planned production volume (20,000) **far exceeds** the break-even point (7,648), the **hardware (ASIC) option is more economical**.

**Verification — Total cost comparison:**

$$
Cost_{hw} = 15,00,000 + (20000 \times 80) = 15,00,000 + 16,00,000 = 31,00,000
$$

$$
Cost_{sw} = 2,00,000 + (20000 \times 250) = 2,00,000 + 50,00,000 = 52,00,000
$$

**Savings with hardware:** $₹\ 52,00,000 - ₹\ 31,00,000 = ₹\ 21,00,000$ (40% cheaper at this volume).

**Recommendation:** Choose **Option H (Hardware/ASIC)** for a 20,000-unit production run.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — always write $ms$, $\mu s$, or $MHz$. Losing 1 mark per missing unit.
> 2. **Writing "hardware is faster" without quantification** — KTU expects a numerical speedup or cycle-count comparison.
> 3. **Confusing NRE with per-unit cost** — students often mix them up in break-even derivations, leading to zero in sub-part (b).
> 4. **Ignoring co-design** — trade-off is *not* a binary "hardware OR software" choice; mention **co-existence and co-simulation** for full marks.
> 5. **Skipping the conclusion** — always end with a one-line recommendation (e.g., "Hardware is recommended for 20,000 units").

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Hardware-Software Trade-offs**

- **Definition:** Choosing hardware vs software for each task in an embedded system, optimizing performance, cost, power, and flexibility.
- **Key Trade-off Axes:** Performance ⇄ Flexibility, NRE ⇄ Per-Unit Cost, Speed ⇄ Time-to-Market, Determinism ⇄ Upgradability.
- **Hardware Wins When:** High volume, deterministic real-time, low power, performance-critical.
- **Software Wins When:** Low volume, evolving standards, tight schedule, frequent updates needed.
- **Core Formula 1:** $T_{exec} = \dfrac{N_{cycles}}{f_{clk}}$
- **Core Formula 2:** $Speedup = \dfrac{T_{sw}}{T_{hw}}$
- **Core Formula 3:** $N_{breakeven} = \dfrac{NRE_{hw} - NRE_{sw}}{C_{sw} - C_{hw}}$
- **Core Formula 4:** $P_{dynamic} = \alpha C V_{dd}^{2} f$ (CMOS power)
- **Process:** Specification → Partitioning → Co-Simulation → Implementation → Validation.
- **Tools:** Xilinx Vivado, Cadence, Synopsys, MATLAB/Simulink HDL Coder.
- **Real-Time Examples:** ABS brake ECU (HW), Smartphone modem (HW + SW), IoT sensor node (SW-dominant).
- **Always remember:** Co-design ≠ choice; it is **partitioning + co-simulation + optimization**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Development: Hardware Design and Development, VLSI and Integrated Circuit Design, Recap of Electronic Design Automation (EDA) Tools, The PCB Layout Design, Printed Circuit Board (PCB) Fabrication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT726 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design and Development: Hardware Design and Development, VLSI and Integrated Circuit Design, Recap of Electronic Design Automation (EDA) Tools, The PCB Layout Design, Printed Circuit Board (PCB) Fabrication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Hardware Design and Development

**Formal Definition:**
Hardware Design and Development in embedded systems refers to the systematic engineering process of conceptualizing, specifying, scheming, prototyping, validating, and realizing the physical electronic platform that hosts the embedded application. It encompasses component selection, schematic capture, subsystem interfacing, power budgeting, clock-tree design, signal-integrity planning, and mechanical/thermal co-design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Hardware design is *not* just soldering components onto a board. It is a top-down discipline that translates a system specification (e.g., "measure temperature every 100 ms and transmit via BLE") into a manufacturable, testable, EMC-compliant electronic assembly.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Think of designing an embedded hardware platform like **architecting a small office building**. Before pouring concrete (fabricating the PCB), the architect must decide: how many rooms (peripherals), where the elevator goes (bus architecture), how thick the floors (power planes), where the fire exits are (ESD/reset paths), and how the building connects to the city grid (connectors, I/O). Skipping any of these decisions leads to a building nobody wants to work in — or worse, one that collapses under its own weight (latch-up, brown-out, crosstalk).

---

### 1.2 VLSI and Integrated Circuit Design

**Formal Definition:**
**Very-Large-Scale Integration (VLSI)** is the process of creating an **Integrated Circuit (IC)** by combining millions (or billions) of transistors — along with passive elements, interconnects, and I/O pads — onto a single semiconductor die. IC design converts a Register-Transfer Level (RTL) behavioral description into a geometric layout (GDSII) that satisfies timing, power, area, and yield constraints.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core IC Families:** **SSI** (Small-Scale, <10 gates), **MSI** (<100), **LSI** (<10,000), **VLSI** (<1 M), and **ULSI/SoC** (>1 M to billions). Modern microcontrollers like the STM32 family are pure **ULSI/SoC** devices.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Imagine **a city built inside a thimble**. VLSI design is the urban planning of that city. Each transistor is a "house," the metal layers are the "streets," and the layout designers are the civil engineers deciding the street widths (wire pitch), traffic rules (clock distribution), and zoning laws (well/substrate contacts). Moore's Law is the city's *growth rate* — it tells you how many houses you can cram into the thimble every 18 months.

---

### 1.3 Electronic Design Automation (EDA) Tools — A Recap

**Formal Definition:**
**Electronic Design Automation (EDA)** is the category of software tools used to design, simulate, verify, and lay out electronic systems — from discrete PCBs to full-custom ASICs. EDA bridges the gap between human-readable design intent (schematics, HDL code) and the physically-realizable mask data that drives silicon fabs and PCB fabs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** EDA tools are the *compiler* of the hardware world. Just as you cannot write a C program with Notepad and expect it to produce an optimized binary, you cannot hand-draw a billion-transistor layout and expect a working chip. Tools are non-negotiable.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
EDA is like **AutoCAD + SAP + Mathematica combined for electronics**. AutoCAD draws it, SAP tracks every component and stock, and Mathematica verifies that the differential equations governing electron flow will actually behave correctly.

---

### 1.4 The PCB Layout Design

**Formal Definition:**
**PCB Layout Design** is the geometric placement of components and routing of copper traces on a printed circuit board, governed by a strict set of **Design Rule Checks (DRC)** that ensure manufacturability, electrical correctness, signal integrity, and EMC compliance. The output is a set of **Gerber (RS-274X) and drill (Excellon) files** sent to fabrication.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A PCB layout is a **detailed street map of a city**: red zones are forbidden (keep-out areas), copper pours are *plazas* (ground planes), vias are *underpasses* connecting different elevations (layers), and trace width is *road width* — wider for high-traffic (high-current) highways.

---

### 1.5 Printed Circuit Board (PCB) Fabrication

**Formal Definition:**
**PCB Fabrication** is the sequence of chemical, mechanical, and photolithographic processes used to convert Gerber/Excellon data into a physical, multi-layer, copper-clad laminate structure that mechanically supports and electrically interconnects electronic components.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Substrate Metric:** The industry-standard laminate is **FR-4** (Flame-Retardant grade 4 epoxy-glass), with a **dielectric constant $\varepsilon_r \approx 4.2$ to $4.6$** at 1 MHz, and a typical glass-transition temperature **$T_g \geq 130^\circ$C** (high-$T_g$ FR-4 is $T_g \geq 170^\circ$C).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
PCB fabrication is **photo-engraving on a copper-coated sandwich**. You coat copper with light-sensitive film, expose it through a negative (Gerber), wash away the unexposed resist, then chemically etch away the unwanted copper — much like developing a photograph, but in reverse: the *dark* parts of the negative are the copper that *stays*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Embedded Hardware Design Flow (Step-by-Step)

The KTU-recommended top-down flow is:

1. **System Specification** — capture functional, performance, cost, power, environmental, and regulatory requirements.
2. **Architecture Partitioning** — decide hardware/firmware split; choose MCU/SoC, peripherals, memory hierarchy.
3. **Component Selection & BOM (Bill of Materials) Build** — based on availability, lifecycle (avoid NRND/EOL parts), cost, and qualification.
4. **Schematic Capture** — draw the circuit using **E-CAD tools** (KiCad, Altium, OrCAD).
5. **Pre-layout Simulation** — SPICE for analog, IBIS for high-speed digital, behavioral models for sensors.
6. **PCB Layout** — placement + routing.
7. **Design Rule Check (DRC)** and **Electrical Rule Check (ERC)** — automated verification.
8. **Prototype Fabrication & Assembly** — usually SMT (Surface-Mount Technology) reflow.
9. **Bring-up, Test, and Debug** — JTAG/SWD, oscilloscope, logic analyzer.
10. **EMC Pre-compliance & DFM/DFT (Design for Manufacturing/Test)** — iterate.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistake:** Jumping directly to PCB layout *before* closing the schematic. Always freeze the schematic, run ERC, and obtain an approved BOM **before** any routing begins. Re-spins of PCBs cost money and time.

---

### 2.2 VLSI Design Styles (Hierarchical, Yield-Cost Trade-off)

| Design Style | Transistor Customization | Mask Cost | NRE Cost | Per-Unit Cost | Typical Volume | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Full-Custom** | All transistors hand-drawn | **Highest** (full set) | Very High | Lowest (after ramp) | Very High | Microprocessors, Analog/RF |
| **Standard Cell** | Pre-designed cells from library | High | High | Low | High | ASICs, SoC |
| **Gate Array** | Only interconnect customized | Medium | Medium | Medium | Medium | Glue logic, prototyping |
| **FPGA** | Pre-fabricated, programmable | None (pre-made) | **None** | High per unit | Low | Prototyping, low-volume |
| **Structured ASIC** | Mix of standard cells + hard IP | Medium-Low | Low | Low-Medium | Low-Medium | Cost-sensitive ASIC |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** The choice is governed by *time-to-market* vs *unit cost*. An FPGA prototype that ships in volume is a strategic and financial mistake; a full-custom chip ordered for 100 units is a financial disaster.

---

### 2.3 Moore's Law and the Scaling Engine

**Statement:** The number of transistors on a dense IC roughly *doubles every 18 to 24 months*.

**Mathematical Form:**

$$N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{\dfrac{t - t_0}{T_d}}$$

where $N(t)$ = transistor count at year $t$, $N_0$ = baseline count at year $t_0$, and $T_d$ = doubling period (typically **18 months** for early projections, **24 months** in modern era).

> [!NOTE]
> **The MOS Scaling Theorem (Dennard, 1974):** When linear dimensions scale by factor $S > 1$, oxide thickness scales as $1/S$, doping as $S$, supply voltage as $1/S$, current as $1/S$, and *power density remains constant*. Dennard scaling broke down circa **2006** (the "Power Wall"), which is why modern chips use **multi-core** instead of single-core clock escalation.

---

### 2.4 PCB Trace — The Microstrip Model

For a trace of width $W$, thickness $T$, on a substrate of thickness $H$ with dielectric constant $\varepsilon_r$ (over a ground plane):

**Characteristic Impedance (IPC-2141 / microstrip):**

$$Z_0 = \frac{87}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r + 1.41}} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{5.98 H}{0.8 W + T}\right) \;\; \Omega \quad \text{(for } 0.1 < \frac{W}{H} < 3.0,\; \varepsilon_r < 15\text{)}$$

**Effective Dielectric Constant:**

$$\varepsilon_{eff} = \frac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2} \cdot \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 12 \frac{H}{W}}} + 0.04 \left(1 - \frac{W}{H}\right)^2 \right)$$

**Propagation Delay:**

$$t_{pd} = \frac{3.33 \sqrt{\varepsilon_{eff}}}{1} \;\;\text{ps/mm} \;\;\; \text{(approx. 4–7 ps/mm on FR-4)}$$

**DC Resistance per Unit Length:**

$$R_{dc} = \frac{\rho_{\text{Cu}}}{W \cdot T} \;\; \Omega/\text{m}$$

where $\rho_{\text{Cu}} = 1.724 \times 10^{-8} \; \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{m}$ at $20^\circ$C.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (All High-Yield Equations)

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Moore's Law | $N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{(t-t_0)/T_d}$ | dimensionless | $T_d = 1.5$–$2.0$ yr |
| 2 | MOS gate delay | $\tau \propto C \cdot V / I$ | seconds | Dennard scaling |
| 3 | Power (dynamic) | $P = \alpha C V_{DD}^2 f$ | watts | $\alpha$ = activity factor |
| 4 | Power (static/leakage) | $P_{leak} = V_{DD} I_{leak}$ | watts | Dominates at <90 nm |
| 5 | Microstrip $Z_0$ | $Z_0 = \frac{87}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r+1.41}} \ln\!\left(\frac{5.98 H}{0.8 W + T}\right)$ | $\Omega$ | IPC-2141 |
| 6 | Effective permittivity | $\varepsilon_{eff}$ (Bahl–Garg eqn.) | dimensionless | $1 < \varepsilon_{eff} < \varepsilon_r$ |
| 7 | Trace delay | $t_{pd} \approx 3.33\sqrt{\varepsilon_{eff}}$ | ps/mm | on FR-4 |
| 8 | Trace DC resistance | $R_{dc} = \rho/(W T)$ | $\Omega/\text{m}$ | $\rho_{Cu}=1.724\times10^{-8}$ |
| 9 | Skin depth | $\delta = \sqrt{2\rho/(\omega\mu)}$ | meters | At GHz frequencies |
| 10 | Current capacity (IPC-2152) | $I = k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725}$ | A | $A$ = cross-section mils² |
| 11 | Thermal via resistance | $\theta_{via} = \dfrac{1}{n_{via} \cdot k_{Cu} \cdot A_{via}/L_{via}}$ | K/W | $k_{Cu} = 400$ W/m·K |
| 12 | Yield (Poisson) | $Y = e^{-AD}$ | dimensionless | $A$ = chip area, $D$ = defect density |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Pipe-symbol safeguard:** Whenever a formula needs a vertical bar (e.g., $\vert x \vert$), typeset it as `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` — never as a raw `|` — to prevent the markdown table parser from breaking.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Moore's Law: Doubling Time from Two Data Points

**Problem:** A 2014 chip has $N_0 = 1.0 \times 10^9$ transistors. A 2024 chip of the same family has $N = 5.0 \times 10^9$. Find the effective doubling period $T_d$.

**Step 1 — State the governing law:**

$$N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{\dfrac{t - t_0}{T_d}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values:**

$$5.0 \times 10^{9} = 1.0 \times 10^{9} \cdot 2^{\dfrac{2024 - 2014}{T_d}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the constant factor (divide both sides by $N_0$):**

$$5 = 2^{\dfrac{10}{T_d}}$$

**Step 4 — Take the binary logarithm $\log_2$ of both sides:**

$$\log_2(5) = \frac{10}{T_d}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate $\log_2(5)$ exactly using change of base:**

$$\log_2(5) = \frac{\ln 5}{\ln 2} = \frac{1.60944}{0.69315} = 2.32193$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $T_d$ in years:**

$$T_d = \frac{10}{2.32193} = 4.306 \text{ years}$$

**Step 7 — Engineering interpretation:**

$$T_d \approx 4.3 \text{ years/doubling} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{far slower than the historical 18 months}$$

This empirically validates the "**Moore's Law slowdown**" widely cited after ~2015.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Dynamic Power Dissipation of a CMOS Gate

**Step 1 — Energy stored in a MOSFET's gate capacitance $C_L$ at supply $V_{DD}$:**

$$E = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Energy per transition (charging from 0 to $V_{DD}$):**

$$E_{charge} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Energy discharged on the falling transition (recycled into the supply, but with crow-bar losses):**

$$E_{discharge} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Total per full switching cycle:**

$$E_{cycle} = C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by switching frequency $f$ to get power per gate (ignoring short-circuit loss):**

$$P = E_{cycle} \cdot f = C_L V_{DD}^{2} f$$

**Step 6 — Introduce activity factor $\alpha$ (probability of switching per clock):**

$$P_{dyn} = \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^{2} \, f$$

**Step 7 — Total chip power (summing all $N$ gates):**

$$P_{chip} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i C_i V_{DD}^{2} f = \alpha_{avg} C_{total} V_{DD}^{2} f$$

**Step 8 — Numerical example (work through it):**

A SoC with $N = 1 \times 10^{8}$ gates, $C_{avg} = 1 \,\text{fF}$, $V_{DD} = 1.0\,\text{V}$, $f = 1\,\text{GHz}$, $\alpha = 0.05$.

$$C_{total} = 1 \times 10^{8} \times 1 \times 10^{-15} = 1 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{F} = 100 \,\text{nF}$$

$$P_{dyn} = 0.05 \times 1 \times 10^{-7} \times (1.0)^{2} \times 1 \times 10^{9}$$

$$P_{dyn} = 0.05 \times 100 = 5 \;\text{W}$$

The package and heatsink design must dissipate **5 W** continuously.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — PCB Microstrip Impedance (FR-4, USB High-Speed Trace)

**Problem:** A USB 2.0 differential pair on a 4-layer FR-4 board requires $Z_{diff} = 90\;\Omega$ ($\pm 10\%$). Compute the single-ended $Z_0$ and verify trace width for $H = 0.20$ mm (8 mil prepreg), $T = 35\,\mu\text{m}$ (1 oz copper), $\varepsilon_r = 4.4$.

**Step 1 — Convert units to a consistent set (meters or mils):**

$$H = 200 \,\mu\text{m},\;\; T = 35 \,\mu\text{m},\;\; \varepsilon_r = 4.4$$

**Step 2 — Use IPC-2141 single-ended microstrip $Z_0$ formula (target $\approx 50\;\Omega$ for $Z_{diff} \approx 90\;\Omega$):**

$$Z_0 = \frac{87}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r + 1.41}} \ln\left(\frac{5.98 H}{0.8 W + T}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the log-term at $Z_0 = 50\;\Omega$:**

$$50 = \frac{87}{\sqrt{4.4 + 1.41}} \ln\left(\frac{5.98 \times 200}{0.8 W + 35}\right)$$

$$50 = \frac{87}{\sqrt{5.81}} \ln\!\left(\frac{1196}{0.8W + 35}\right)$$

$$50 = \frac{87}{2.4107} \ln\!\left(\frac{1196}{0.8W + 35}\right)$$

$$50 = 36.087 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{1196}{0.8W + 35}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Divide both sides by 36.087:**

$$\frac{50}{36.087} = 1.3855 = \ln\!\left(\frac{1196}{0.8W + 35}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Exponentiate:**

$$e^{1.3855} = \frac{1196}{0.8W + 35} = 3.996$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $W$:**

$$0.8W + 35 = \frac{1196}{3.996} = 299.30$$

$$0.8W = 299.30 - 35 = 264.30$$

$$W = 330.4 \,\mu\text{m} \approx 13 \;\text{mil}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering rule-of-thumb:** For $H = 8$ mil FR-4 ($\varepsilon_r \approx 4.4$), a trace of **$W \approx 13$ mil** (i.e., $W/H \approx 1.6$) gives $Z_0 = 50\;\Omega$. For $W/H = 2$ you get $Z_0 \approx 45\;\Omega$, a useful lower bound.

**Step 7 — Effective permittivity check:**

$$\varepsilon_{eff} = \frac{4.4 + 1}{2} + \frac{4.4 - 1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 12(200/330.4)}} + 0.04\left(1 - \frac{330.4}{200}\right)^{2}\right)$$

$$\varepsilon_{eff} = 2.7 + 1.7 \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 7.27}} + 0.04 \cdot 0.427\right)$$

$$\varepsilon_{eff} = 2.7 + 1.7 \left(\frac{1}{2.879} + 0.0171\right) = 2.7 + 1.7 \cdot 0.364 = 2.7 + 0.619 = 3.32$$

**Step 8 — Propagation delay:**

$$t_{pd} = 3.33 \sqrt{3.32} = 3.33 \cdot 1.823 = 6.07 \;\text{ps/mm}$$

For a 100 mm USB trace, $t_{pd} \approx 607\;\text{ps}$, well within the 480 Mbps UI budget.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Trace DC Resistance and Voltage Drop

**Problem:** A 1.0 A load is fed through a 100 mm long, 250 µm wide, 35 µm thick copper trace. Find the IR drop.

**Step 1 — Compute cross-section:**

$$A = W \cdot T = 250 \times 10^{-6} \times 35 \times 10^{-6} = 8.75 \times 10^{-9}\;\text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute length in meters:**

$$L = 0.100\;\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute resistance:**

$$R_{dc} = \frac{\rho L}{A} = \frac{1.724 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.100}{8.75 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.197\;\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Compute voltage drop at 1 A:**

$$V_{drop} = I \cdot R = 1.0 \times 0.197 = 197\;\text{mV}$$

**Step 5 — Verify against IPC-2152 current capacity (sanity check):**

Cross-section in circular mils: $A_{cmil} = W_{mil} \times T_{mil} = 9.84 \times 1.38 \approx 13.6$ mils². For a 10 °C rise, **$\approx 1.2$ A** is allowed — we are within the 1 A budget. ✓

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Yield using the Poisson Defect Model

**Problem:** A die has area $A = 100\;\text{mm}^{2}$, the process has defect density $D = 0.5\;\text{defects/cm}^{2}$. Compute yield.

**Step 1 — Convert area to cm²:**

$$A = 100\;\text{mm}^{2} = 1.0\;\text{cm}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Poisson yield model:**

$$Y = e^{-A D}$$

$$Y = e^{-1.0 \times 0.5} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:** About **60.7%** of the fabricated dies will be functional — a typical figure for a mature 28 nm class process.

---

### 3.6 KTU-Style Numerical Master Table

| # | Quantity | Equation Used | Plug-In | Final Answer |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transistor doubling time | Moore's Law | $N_0=1\text{B}, N=5\text{B}, \Delta t=10$ yr | $T_d = 4.3$ yr |
| 2 | Dynamic power of SoC | $P = \alpha C V^2 f$ | $C=100$ nF, $V=1$V, $f=1$GHz, $\alpha=0.05$ | $P = 5$ W |
| 3 | $50\;\Omega$ microstrip width | IPC-2141 $Z_0$ | $H=200\mu\text{m}, T=35\mu\text{m}, \varepsilon_r=4.4$ | $W \approx 330\;\mu\text{m}$ |
| 4 | Effective $\varepsilon$ | Bahl–Garg | $W=330\;\mu\text{m}, H=200\;\mu\text{m}$ | $\varepsilon_{eff} \approx 3.32$ |
| 5 | Trace $t_{pd}$ | $3.33\sqrt{\varepsilon_{eff}}$ | $\varepsilon_{eff}=3.32$ | $6.07$ ps/mm |
| 6 | Trace $R_{dc}$ | $\rho L/A$ | $L=100$ mm, $A=8.75\times10^{-9}$ m² | $0.197\;\Omega$ |
| 7 | Voltage drop | $V = IR$ | $I=1$ A, $R=0.197\;\Omega$ | $197$ mV |
| 8 | Die yield | $Y = e^{-AD}$ | $A=1$ cm², $D=0.5$/cm² | $60.7\%$ |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Embedded Hardware Design Flow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[stepA: System Spec] --> B[stepB: Architecture Partitioning]
    B --> C[stepC: Component Selection and BOM]
    C --> D[stepD: Schematic Capture]
    D --> E[stepE: Pre Layout Simulation]
    E --> F[stepF: PCB Layout]
    F --> G[stepG: DRC and ERC Checks]
    G --> H{stepH: Rules Pass?}
    H -- No --> D
    H -- Yes --> I[stepI: Prototype Fabrication]
    I --> J[stepJ: Bring up and Test]
    J --> K{stepK: EMC Pass?}
    K -- No --> D
    K -- Yes --> L[stepL: Production Release]
```

> **Interpretation:** The two **diamond nodes (H and K)** are the *re-spin decision gates*. A "No" forces a return to schematic capture — the most expensive possible loop in the cycle.

### 4.4 PCB Fabrication Process (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[stepA: Inner Layer Imaging] --> B[stepB: Etching Inner Copper]
    B --> C[stepC: Lay Up and Bonding Press]
    C --> D[stepD: Drilling]
    D --> E[stepE: Plating Through Holes]
    E --> F[stepF: Outer Layer Imaging]
    F --> G[stepG: Outer Layer Etching]

```

> **Layer-by-layer analogy:** Each **inner layer** is photo-engraved separately. The **bonding press** fuses them with prepreg (B-stage epoxy). **ENIG** (Electroless Nickel Immersion Gold) is the most popular lead-free surface finish for fine-pitch BGAs.
