## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Define Moore's Law. List any two factors limiting its continued validity in deep sub-micron nodes.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Moore's Law, formulated by Gordon Moore in 1965, states that *the number of transistors that can be economically integrated on a single IC die approximately doubles every 18 to 24 months*. Mathematically, $N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{(t-t_0)/T_d}$ with $T_d \in [1.5,\, 2.0]$ years.

**Two limiting factors (1 mark for any two):**
- **Power wall:** Dennard scaling broke down at **~90 nm**; supply voltage cannot be reduced further without exponentially increasing leakage current, capping frequency.
- **Lithography limits:** Below ~7 nm, EUV lithography wavelength (~13.5 nm) approaches physical atomic limits; double/quadruple patterning escalates cost.
- **Interconnect RC delay:** Copper resistivity rises sharply as wire cross-section approaches mean-free-path; resistivity also degrades with grain-boundary scattering.
- **Quantum tunneling:** Gate oxide (high-$\kappa$ hafnium-based) approaches ~1 nm, allowing electron tunneling even when nominally "off."
- **Economic limit:** Mask costs for a 5 nm node exceed **$50 M per set**, limiting the technology to a few very high-volume players.

---

#### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — Differentiate between Full-Custom, Standard-Cell, and Gate-Array VLSI design styles.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Style | Transistor Customization | Mask Cost | Per-Unit Cost | Time-to-Market | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Full-Custom** | Every transistor & interconnect hand-drawn | **Highest** (all masks custom) | Lowest (high vol) | Longest | Intel Core CPUs, RF front-ends |
| **Standard-Cell** | Pre-designed cell library; layout is automated | High (full set, but cells reused) | Low | Medium | SoC digital blocks (ARM cores) |
| **Gate-Array** | Only interconnect (metal layers) is customized | Medium (base array pre-fab'd) | Medium | Short | Mature glue-logic ASICs |

**Key trade-off (1 mark):** Full-custom is area- and performance-optimal but expensive; gate-array is fast-to-market but wastes 20–40 % silicon; standard-cell is the *industry sweet spot* for high-volume ASICs.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, sub-divided into (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**, with **internal choice at sub-part level only**. Below we provide *two fully independent 14-mark question sets*, exactly as the question paper would.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Hardware Design + EDA Recap

**Q.A.(a) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Explain the top-down flow of embedded hardware design and development. Briefly state the role of the **schematic capture**, **ERC/DRC**, and **BOM** stages.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Specification (1 mark):** Functional, performance, cost, power, environmental, and regulatory requirements captured. Example: "Battery-powered soil moisture node, 5-year lifetime, LoRa uplink every 30 min."
2. **Architecture partitioning (1 mark):** Decide HW/SW split, select MCU, choose sensors/actuators, define I/O map.
3. **Component selection & BOM (1 mark):** The Bill of Materials is the *single source of truth* for all purchased parts, including manufacturer part numbers, alternates, lifecycle status (avoid NRND), and approved-supplier list.
4. **Schematic capture (1 mark):** Circuit drawn using E-CAD (KiCad/Altium). The output is a **netlist** — a structured connectivity list.
5. **Pre-layout simulation (1 mark):** SPICE for analog signal-conditioning stages, IBIS for high-speed buffers; sensor models co-simulated.
6. **PCB layout (1 mark):** Placement and routing constrained by DRC.
7. **DRC & ERC (1 mark):** *ERC* checks electrical correctness (e.g., unconnected power pins, output-to-output shorts); *DRC* enforces manufacturer design rules (trace spacing, width, via annular ring). Both must pass **before** any Gerbers are released.
8. **Prototype bring-up, EMC pre-compliance, DFM/DFT iteration** (0 marks — just naming).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often omit the *iteration* path. A "No" at DRC or ERC forces return to schematic — failing to mention this **costs 1 mark**.

---

**Q.A.(b) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Describe the **Electronic Design Automation (EDA)** tool flow for a VLSI ASIC. List the major tools used at synthesis, P&R, and physical-verification stages, and explain why verification dominates the design schedule.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Design entry** (1 mark): HDL (Verilog/VHDL/SystemVerilog) at RTL. **Tools:** Any text editor; Vivado editor, Emacs.
2. **Functional simulation** (1 mark): Test-bench driven. **Tools:** ModelSim, QuestaSim, Vivado XSim, Verilator.
3. **Logic synthesis** (1 mark): RTL → gate-level netlist mapped to standard-cell library. **Tools:** Synopsys **Design Compiler**, Cadence **Genus**.
4. **Place & Route (P&R)** (1 mark): Cells are placed, clock-tree synthesized, signal routes assigned. **Tools:** Cadence **Innovus**, Synopsys **ICC2**, OpenROAD (open source).
5. **Physical verification** (1 mark): **DRC** (Design Rule Check — geometric conformity) and **LVS** (Layout-vs-Schematic — connectivity match). **Tools:** Mentor **Calibre**, Cadence **PVS**, **Magic** (open).
6. **Static Timing Analysis (STA)** (1 mark): All paths checked against timing constraints at multiple corners (SS / FF / TT). **Tools:** Synopsys **PrimeTime**, Cadence **Tempus**.
7. **Sign-off & tape-out** (1 mark): GDSII (database II) data sent to foundry.

**Why verification dominates the schedule (1 mark embedded in above):** The cost of an undetected RTL bug grows $\sim 10\times$ at every abstraction drop (RTL → gate → layout → silicon). Post-silicon re-spins cost **$1–10 M** and 3–6 months; therefore $>70\%$ of total engineering effort is verification (formal, simulation, emulation, FPGA prototyping, gate-level, post-layout, post-silicon validation).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Do not confuse **DRC** (geometry rules) with **ERC** (electrical/schematic). Examiners give **zero marks** if you interchange them.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — PCB Layout + Fabrication (Alternative Choice)

**Q.B.(a) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Explain the **PCB layout design process**. Discuss the significance of (i) layer stack-up, (ii) ground planes, and (iii) controlled-impedance routing. With reference to IPC-2141, derive the expression for the characteristic impedance of a microstrip trace.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Layout process flow (2 marks):** (a) Import netlist from schematic; (b) set **board outline** and **layer stack-up**; (c) define **design rules** (clearance, width, via style); (d) place components — *strategic parts first* (connectors, large ICs, power); (e) route power & ground first (planes or thick traces); (f) route critical high-speed signals with length matching; (g) fan-out for BGAs; (h) copper pour; (i) **DRC** final pass; (j) generate **Gerber RS-274X** and **Excellon drill** files.

2. **Layer stack-up (1 mark):** Defines the number of signal/power/ground layers, the prepreg & core thicknesses, and the $Z_0$ of every trace. A typical 4-layer stack is: **Top-Sig / Prepreg / GND / Core / VCC / Prepreg / Bot-Sig** — symmetric to prevent warp.

3. **Ground planes (1 mark):** Provide a *low-inductance return path*, act as a *reference* for microstrip impedance, and serve as *heat spreaders* and *EMI shields*.

4. **Controlled-impedance routing (1 mark):** Required when edge-rate × length > $l/6$ (where $l$ is signal rise-time spatial length). Typical targets: USB 2.0 = **$90\;\Omega$ differential / $45\;\Omega$ single-ended**, HDMI = **$100\;\Omega$ differential**, Ethernet = **$100\;\Omega$ differential**.

5. **Derivation of $Z_0$ (1 mark):** For a microstrip of width $W$, thickness $T$, on substrate thickness $H$ and permittivity $\varepsilon_r$:

$$Z_0 = \frac{87}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r + 1.41}} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{5.98\,H}{0.8\,W + T}\right)\;\Omega \quad (\text{IPC-2141})$$

6. **Effective permittivity** (1 mark): $\varepsilon_{eff}$ lies between 1 and $\varepsilon_r$ because part of the field is in air. For FR-4 with $W/H = 1.6$, $\varepsilon_{eff} \approx 3.3$.

---

**Q.B.(b) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Describe the **step-by-step process of PCB fabrication** from inner-layer imaging to final electrical test. List any **four surface finishes** and state one application of each.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Inner-layer imaging (1 mark):** Copper-clad laminate cleaned, photoresist applied, exposed through a **photomask (Gerber)**, developed. Unexposed resist is washed away, leaving resist where copper must *stay*.
2. **Inner-layer etching (1 mark):** Sodium persulfate or cupric chloride dissolves exposed copper. Resist is then stripped.
3. **Lay-up & bonding press (1 mark):** Inner layers stacked with **prepreg (B-stage epoxy)** and outer copper foils, then hot-pressed at ~$180^\circ$C and ~$2\,\text{MPa}$ for ~2 hours. This cures the prepreg into C-stage, fusing the layers.
4. **Drilling (1 mark):** CNC drills form through-holes and vias. **Hole tolerance** ± 75 µm typical.
5. **Plating (1 mark):** Electroless copper deposits a thin layer inside hole walls; electrolytic plating thickens it to ~25 µm. This makes vias *conductive* between layers.
6. **Outer-layer imaging + etching + solder mask + surface finish + silkscreen + profiling + E-test** (remaining steps — name them).

**Four surface finishes (any four — 1 mark each, 4 marks):**

| Finish | Composition | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **HASL (Hot Air Solder Leveling)** | Sn-Pb (leaded) or Sn-Ag-Cu (lead-free) | Cost-sensitive, large-pitch through-hole consumer electronics |
| **ENIG (Electroless Nickel Immersion Gold)** | ~3-6 µm Ni(P) + ~0.05-0.1 µm Au | **Fine-pitch BGA, QFN, key consumer** — flat, lead-free, shelf-stable |
| **OSP (Organic Solderability Preservative)** | Azole-based organic coating | Mid-volume lead-free, low cost; **not** for multiple reflows |
| **Immersion Silver (ImAg)** | ~0.1-0.3 µm Ag | High-frequency **RF and 5G** — excellent skin-effect conductivity |
| **Immersion Tin (ImSn)** | ~0.8-1.2 µm Sn | Press-fit connectors; risk of tin whiskers |
| **Hard Gold (ENEPIG)** | ENIG + electroless Pd + Au | **Wire-bonding pads** (medical, aerospace), high-reliability |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Confusing **HASL** (molten solder, uneven surface) with **ENIG** (planar, BGA-friendly). Examiners specifically test this — describing HASL as "flat" loses a mark.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings (Common Pitfalls Summary)

> [!CAUTION]
> **Top Five Marks-Loss Traps in This Module**
> 1. **Confusing DRC with ERC.** DRC = *geometric* rules; ERC = *electrical/schematic* rules.
> 2. **Wrong Moore's Law formula form.** Writing $N = N_0 + 2^t$ instead of $N = N_0 \cdot 2^{(t-t_0)/T_d}$ is **zero** marks.
> 3. **Stating that PCB fabrication uses "positive" photoresist without specifying that the dark Gerber areas = unexposed = retained copper.** (Industry standard: **dry-film photoresist, negative-tone, UV exposure**.)
> 4. **Citing only one EDA tool** in a design-flow question. Examiners expect *at least three* — schematic, simulation, and P&R.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert units** in IPC-2141 calculations. Mixing mils and microns in the same equation is the most common arithmetic error.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Hardware design** is a *top-down discipline* — spec → architecture → schematic → layout → prototype → test.
- **VLSI design styles** are cost-time trade-offs: **Full-Custom** (perf, $$$), **Standard-Cell** (sweet spot), **Gate-Array** (fast), **FPGA** (proto, low-vol), **Structured ASIC** (cost-sensitive ASIC).
- **Moore's Law**: $N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{(t-t_0)/T_d}$, $T_d \in [1.5,\, 2.0]$ years.
- **Dennard scaling** broke at ~90 nm; modern chips use **multi-core** to stay within the *power wall*.
- **EDA flow**: HDL → sim → synthesis → P&R → DRC/LVS → STA → GDSII → fab.
- **Open-source EDA**: KiCad, Magic, Icarus, Verilator, OpenROAD, SkyWater 130 nm PDK.
- **PCB layer stack-up** defines $Z_0$, return path, and thermal budget. 4-layer minimum for any mixed-signal design.
- **Ground planes** are non-negotiable for EMC, SI, and thermal spreading.
- **Microstrip $Z_0$ (IPC-2141):** $Z_0 = \frac{87}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r + 1.41}} \ln\!\left(\frac{5.98 H}{0.8 W + T}\right)$.
- **$\varepsilon_{eff}$** lies between 1 (air) and $\varepsilon_r$ (substrate); for FR-4 + $W/H=1.6$, $\varepsilon_{eff} \approx 3.3$.
- **Trace delay on FR-4:** $t_{pd} \approx 4$–$7$ ps/mm.
- **Copper resistivity:** $\rho_{Cu} = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}\;\Omega\!\cdot\!\text{m}$ at $20^\circ$C.
- **DRC** = geometric; **ERC** = electrical; **LVS** = layout-vs-schematic.
- **PCB fab process**: inner imaging → etch → lay-up & press → drill → plate → outer imaging → etch → solder mask → surface finish → silkscreen → profile → E-test.
- **Surface finishes ranked by planarity (best to worst for BGA):** **ENIG / ENEPIG > Immersion Ag > OSP > HASL** (HASL is the least flat).
- **Yield** is modeled as $Y = e^{-AD}$ (Poisson) — a 100 mm² die at $D=0.5/\text{cm}^2$ yields ~$60.7\%$.
- **Always** design for **DFM** (Design for Manufacturability) and **DFT** (Design for Test) — boundary-scan, test-points, panel-friendly placement.
- **Never** start PCB layout before the schematic is **frozen** and **ERC-clean**.
- **Always** thermal-couple high-power devices to inner copper planes with **thermal vias** ($\theta_{via} = 1/(n \cdot k \cdot A/L)$).
- **KTU 2024 emphasis:** *open-source* EDA tools (KiCad, Magic) and the *SkyWater 130 nm academic shuttle* are first-class citizens in viva questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Firmware Design and Development: Embedded Firmware Design, Embedded Firmware Development Languages, Programming in Embedded C
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT726 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Firmware Design and Development: Embedded Firmware Design, Embedded Firmware Development Languages, Programming in Embedded C

---

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Embedded Firmware?

**Embedded Firmware** is a specialized class of computer software (often written in low-level languages such as C or assembly) that is permanently programmed into the non-volatile memory (ROM, EPROM, Flash, EEPROM) of an embedded system. It is the *operational brain* of the device, residing closest to the hardware abstraction layer (HAL) and providing deterministic control over system resources, peripherals, and real-time I/O operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Official Definition (verbatim-style):**
> *"Firmware is the software embedded into a hardware device's non-volatile memory that provides low-level control for the device's specific hardware. In embedded systems, firmware is the bridge between the hardware and any higher-level application software."*

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Reflex Arc" of a Machine

Imagine the human body: your conscious mind (the **application layer**) decides to pick up a glass. But the actual finger movement, the grip pressure, the muscle contractions — all happen automatically, without thinking. That is **firmware**.
- The **hardware** = your muscles and nerves.
- The **firmware** = the reflex arc in your spinal cord (immediate, deterministic, hard-coded response).
- The **application software** = your conscious thought process.

Just as a reflex must execute within milliseconds without deliberation, embedded firmware must respond to sensor inputs and timer events within **strict, bounded time limits** (often microseconds). This property is called **determinism**, and it is the single most important reason firmware exists as a separate discipline from "regular" software.

> [!NOTE]
> **Firmware vs. Software vs. Hardware — KTU Exam Favourite**

| Aspect | Hardware | Firmware | Application Software |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Physical electronic components | Code stored in ROM/Flash | Code in RAM / secondary storage |
| **Mutability** | Fixed at fabrication | Updated rarely (OTA/ICSP) | Updated frequently |
| **Location** | PCB / silicon die | Non-volatile memory of MCU | Mass storage (SD, HDD) |
| **Execution** | Always-on physical behaviour | On reset / power-on | On user demand / OS scheduling |
| **Examples** | Resistors, transistors, ARM core | Bootloader, BIOS, RTOS kernel | Web browser, MS Word, games |
| **Determinism** | 100% deterministic | Must be deterministic | Often non-deterministic |

## 1.2 The Firmware Stack — Where it Lives

Modern embedded systems follow a layered stack. Firmware occupies the lower three layers, with **application logic** sitting on top:

| Layer (top → bottom) | Typical Component | Language |
|---|---|---|
| Application Layer | Business logic, user UI, control algorithms | C / C++ / Python |
| Middleware | Communication stacks (TCP/IP, BLE, MQTT) | C / C++ |
| Real-Time OS (RTOS) | Scheduler, IPC, mutex, semaphores | C / assembly |
| Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL) | Driver wrappers around registers | C / C++ |
| **Firmware Core** | Boot code, ISR, low-level drivers | **C / Assembly** |
| Hardware (MCU) | ARM Cortex-M core, peripherals, memory | — |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU exam answers, always state the layer in which your discussed component lives. Examiners give extra marks for *architectural awareness*.

## 1.3 Why Dedicated Firmware Engineering? — The "Why" Question

Firmware design is separated from generic software engineering for four hard engineering reasons that KTU examiners love to ask:

1. **Resource Constraints** — Typical 8-bit MCUs have **2 KB to 64 KB** of Flash and **128 B to 8 KB** of RAM. Compare with a laptop having **16 GB+ RAM**. The firmware must be *lean by design*.
2. **Real-Time Determinism** — A hard real-time system (e.g., anti-lock braking) *must* respond within deadline or the system fails. Garbage collection and unbounded loops are forbidden.
3. **Direct Hardware Manipulation** — Firmware writes directly to special function registers (SFRs) using pointers and bitwise operations. There is no operating system in bare-metal cases to protect memory.
4. **Power & Reliability** — Firmware must handle brown-out, watchdog resets, sleep modes, and operate for **10+ years** on a coin cell (e.g., IoT sensors).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Embedded Firmware Design — The Engineering Process

A formal firmware design follows a **top-down waterfall** or **V-model** approach. KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly mandates the following six-stage design methodology:

### Stage 1: Requirements Engineering
- **Functional requirements** — what the system must do (e.g., "read temperature every 100 ms").
- **Non-functional requirements** — performance, latency, power, cost, certification (e.g., IEC 61508 for industrial).
- **Constraints** — MCU family, clock speed, memory budget, peripherals available.

### Stage 2: Architecture Selection
Choose one of three canonical architectures:

| Architecture | Description | When to Use | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Super-loop (Polled)** | `while(1) { service_task_1(); service_task_2(); }` | Simple, ≤3 tasks, no strict deadlines | Toy, rice cooker, remote control |
| **Event-driven (Interrupt-driven)** | Main loop + ISRs handle urgent events | Periodic + aperiodic events, moderate complexity | Data loggers, simple motor control |
| **RTOS-based** | Pre-emptive scheduler with tasks/threads | Multiple concurrent threads, complex timing | Drones, automotive ECUs, wearables |

### Stage 3: Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL) Design
- Wrap every register-level access in a function (e.g., `GPIO_WritePin(GPIOA, PIN_5, HIGH)`).
- Provides **portability** — moving from STM32 to NXP only changes HAL implementation.

### Stage 4: Module / Driver Development
- One module per peripheral: `uart.c`, `spi.c`, `adc.c`, `timer.c`.
- Each module exposes a clean public API and hides register details.

### Stage 5: Application Logic
- Control algorithms, state machines, sensor fusion.
- Built using HAL + drivers.

### Stage 6: Testing & Validation
- Unit testing (Unity/CMock frameworks), on-target testing, hardware-in-loop (HIL).

## 2.2 Embedded Firmware Development Languages

### 2.2.1 The Language Spectrum

The choice of language trades off **abstraction** against **control**. KTU expects you to justify the choice in any design question.

| Language | Abstraction | Control | Code Size | Real-time | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Assembly** | Lowest | 100% | Tiny | Excellent | Boot code, ISR, DSP inner loops |
| **C** | Low | High | Small | Excellent | **Industry default** for firmware |
| **C++** | Medium | Medium-High | Medium-Large | Good | Complex applications (AUTOSAR) |
| **Embedded C++** | Medium | High | Medium | Good | Safety-critical (MISRA C++ subset) |
| **Java (Embedded Java)** | High | Low | Large | Poor | Smart cards, set-top boxes |
| **Python (MicroPython/CircuitPython)** | Highest | Lowest | Large | Poor | Prototyping, education, ESP32 |
| **Ada / Rust** | Medium | High | Small | Excellent | Safety-critical (avionics, medical) |
| **VHDL/Verilog** | Hardware | 100% | N/A | N/A | FPGA firmware |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why C dominates embedded firmware (~80% of industry code)**
> 1. Zero runtime overhead (no garbage collector).
> 2. Direct memory-mapped register access via pointers.
> 3. Predictable compilation — output assembly is small and inspectable.
> 4. Massive compiler support (GCC, IAR, Keil, Clang).
> 5. Vast ecosystem of vendor HAL libraries (STM32 HAL, ESP-IDF, Arduino).

### 2.2.2 C vs. C++ in Embedded — The Real Trade-off

| Criterion | C | C++ |
|---|---|---|
| Binary size | Smaller | +10–30% (exceptions, RTTI) |
| Learning curve | Easy | Harder (templates, OOP) |
| Standardised | ISO C11/C17 | ISO C++17/20 |
| Industry adoption | Universal | Growing in automotive |
| Real-time safety | **MISRA-C** widely accepted | MISRA-C++ exists but harder |
| When to choose C | Resource-constrained, safety-critical, regulatory | When you need OOP, generics, RAII |

> [!TIP]
> **MISRA-C** (Motor Industry Software Reliability Association) is a *coding standard*, not a language. It defines a safe subset of C for safety-critical firmware. KTU examiners love to mention MISRA in design questions.

## 2.3 Programming in Embedded C — The Key Constructs

### 2.3.1 Fixed-Width Integer Types (`<stdint.h>`)

In standard C, `int` may be 16 or 32 bits depending on the compiler. Embedded firmware must be **bit-exact** across compilers — hence the fixed-width types:

```c
#include <stdint.h>

uint8_t   status_flags;   // Exactly 8 bits,  range 0..255
int16_t   sensor_value;   // Exactly 16 bits, range -32768..32767
uint32_t  timestamp_ms;   // Exactly 32 bits
volatile uint32_t * const GPIOA_ODR = (uint32_t *)0x40020014;
```

> [!WARNING]
> Never use `int` in register-level code. The standard only guarantees it to be at least 16 bits — on some compilers it is 16, on others 32. This is the **#1 source of portability bugs** in embedded C.

### 2.3.2 The `volatile` Qualifier — A KTU Favourite

The keyword `volatile` tells the compiler: *"This variable may change at any time without the program's action — do not cache it in a register, re-read from memory every time."*

Three places where `volatile` is **mandatory**:

1. **Memory-mapped peripheral registers** (because hardware changes them).
2. **Variables shared between ISR and main code** (the ISR can change them asynchronously).
3. **Variables shared between RTOS tasks** (use volatile + atomic access, or mutex).

```c
volatile uint8_t rx_byte;             // Modified by UART ISR
volatile uint32_t systick_ticks;      // Modified by SysTick handler

uint8_t main(void) {
    while (rx_byte == 0) { /* wait */ }   // Compiler must re-read each loop
}
```

### 2.3.3 Bit Manipulation Idioms

Embedded C lives and dies by bitwise operations. KTU 2024 expects fluency:

| Operation | Code | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Set** bit `n` | `reg \|= (1U << n);` | `GPIOA->ODR \|= (1U << 5);` |
| **Clear** bit `n` | `reg &= ~(1U << n);` | `GPIOA->ODR &= ~(1U << 5);` |
| **Toggle** bit `n` | `reg ^= (1U << n);` | `GPIOA->ODR ^= (1U << 5);` |
| **Test** bit `n` | `(reg >> n) & 1U` | `if ((GPIOA->IDR & (1U << 5)) != 0)` |
| **Modify** bits in field | `reg = (reg & ~mask) \| (val << pos);` | Set 4-bit field starting at bit 8 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bit-Banding vs. Bit-Band Alias** (ARM Cortex-M3/M4 only)
> A unique feature of ARM Cortex-M3/M4 is the *bit-band alias region*. The 1 MB SRAM region `0x20000000–0x200FFFFF` and 1 MB peripheral region `0x40000000–0x400FFFFF` are aliased so that each individual bit is addressable as a 32-bit word. The alias address is given by:

| Bit-band alias address formula | Use |
|---|---|
| `alias_addr = alias_base + (byte_offset × 32) + (bit_number × 4)` | Convert any bit in bit-band region to its alias word address |

This allows **atomic single-bit access** without read-modify-write races. KTU questions on Cortex-M architecture often ask this.

### 2.3.4 Memory-Mapped I/O — The Heart of Embedded C

All peripherals are exposed as **special function registers (SFRs)** at fixed addresses. Access is via pointers:

```c
#define GPIOA_BASE   0x40020000UL
#define GPIOA_ODR    (*(volatile uint32_t *)(GPIOA_BASE + 0x14))

// Or using vendor structures
GPIOA->ODR |= (1U << 5);    // Set pin 5 high
```

### 2.3.5 Interrupt Service Routines (ISR) in C

An ISR is a function the hardware calls asynchronously. On ARM Cortex-M, ISRs are normal C functions marked with the `__interrupt` attribute (or simply have the matching vector-table name):

```c
void EXTI0_IRQHandler(void) {
    if (EXTI->PR & (1U << 0)) {        // Check pending bit
        EXTI->PR |= (1U << 0);         // Clear pending (write-1-to-clear)
        toggle_LED();
    }
}
```

**KTU Golden Rules for ISRs:**
1. Keep ISR **as short as possible** (set a flag, post to a queue, then return).
2. **Never** call blocking functions (`HAL_Delay`, `printf`, `malloc`).
3. **Never** use floating-point without an FPU-aware context save.
4. Clear the interrupt pending bit before returning.

### 2.3.6 Const, Struct, Union, and Bit-Fields

**Bit-fields** — for mapping hardware registers to readable struct members:

```c
typedef struct {
    uint32_t MODE  : 2;    // Bits 0-1
    uint32_t CNTF  : 1;    // Bit 2
    uint32_t DIR   : 1;    // Bit 3
    uint32_t RESERVED : 28;
} GPIO_Config_t;

volatile GPIO_Config_t * const GPIOA_MODE = (GPIO_Config_t *)0x40020000;
```

**Unions** — for accessing the same memory as different types (e.g., reading a 32-bit status register as four bytes):

```c
typedef union {
    uint32_t word;
    struct {
        uint8_t byte0;
        uint8_t byte1;
        uint8_t byte2;
        uint8_t byte3;
    } bytes;
} Status_t;
```

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept / Symbol | Definition / Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bit-band alias address** | `alias = alias_base + (byte_off × 32) + (bit × 4)` | Bytes / bits → address |
| 2 | **Tick period** | `T = 1 / f_clock` | seconds |
| 3 | **Timer reload for 1 ms tick** | `ARR = (f_clk / prescaler) × T - 1` | counts |
| 4 | **ADC sampling rate (Nyquist)** | `f_s ≥ 2 × f_signal_max` | Hz |
| 5 | **Baud-rate divisor** | `BRR = f_clk / (16 × baud)` | counts (USART) |
| 6 | **Power dissipation (CMOS)** | `P = C × V² × f` | Watts (dynamic) |
| 7 | **Response time (super-loop)** | `T_max = Σ t_task_i` | seconds (worst case) |
| 8 | **Memory footprint of struct** | `sizeof(struct) = Σ size_of(fields)` padded to alignment | bytes |
| 9 | **Endianness test** | `0xAA in byte[0]` → little-endian | per compiler |
| 10 | **Watchdog timeout** | `T_wdt = (prescaler × reload) / f_clk` | seconds |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Exam Pitfall:** Always show the **unit** in the final answer. KTU examiners deduct half-mark for unit omissions in numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section builds *from first principles* three of the most-tested KTU exam derivations on this topic. Each step is written out in full.

---

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — Designing a SysTick Timer Reload Value for 1 ms Interrupt

**Problem (KTU pattern):** An ARM Cortex-M4 MCU is clocked at $f_{clk} = 72 \text{ MHz}$. The SysTick timer is clocked by the processor clock (no prescaler). Compute the reload value to generate an interrupt every **1 ms**.

**Step 1 — Identify the goal.**
We want the timer to count down from a value $N$ to $0$ in exactly $T = 1 \text{ ms}$, decrementing once per clock cycle.

**Step 2 — Write the count-to-time relationship.**
If the counter starts at $N$ and counts one tick per clock period, the time to reach zero is:

$$
T = \frac{N}{f_{clk}}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $N$.**
Rearranging the equation for $N$:

$$
N = T \times f_{clk}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**
Using $T = 1 \text{ ms} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$ and $f_{clk} = 72 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}$:

$$
N = (1 \times 10^{-3}) \times (72 \times 10^{6})
$$

**Step 5 — Perform the multiplication.**

$$
N = 72\,000 \text{ ticks}
$$

**Step 6 — Adjust for SysTick's 24-bit down-counter with reload-on-zero.**
The SysTick register is 24-bit wide, so $N_{max} = 2^{24} - 1 = 16\,777\,215$. Since $72\,000 < 16\,777\,215$, the value is **valid**. The reload register must be set to:

$$
\text{STK\_LOAD} = N - 1 = 72\,000 - 1 = 71\,999
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The "$-1$" arises because the counter is a *down-counter that fires an interrupt when it transitions from 1 to 0*. Counting 1, 2, …, N-1, N is N+1 ticks if we loaded N. So we load N−1 to fire after N ticks. This is a classic KTU "fence-post" trick.

**Step 7 — Convert to C code.**

```c
#define SYSCLK_HZ     72000000U
#define TICK_HZ       1000U                // 1 ms

void SysTick_Init(void) {
    SysTick->LOAD = (SYSCLK_HZ / TICK_HZ) - 1U;   // 71999
    SysTick->VAL  = 0U;
    SysTick->CTRL = (1U << 0)   // Enable counter
                  | (1U << 1)   // Enable interrupt
                  | (1U << 2);  // Clock source = processor
}

void SysTick_Handler(void) {
    ms_ticks++;          // ISR body — minimal
}
```

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Bit-Band Alias Address Calculation

**Problem (KTU pattern):** On a Cortex-M4, the GPIOA output data register (ODR) is at address `0x40020014`. The peripheral bit-band alias base is `0x42000000`. Compute the alias address of **bit 5** of ODR (the pin that drives the green LED on the STM32F4 Discovery board).

**Step 1 — State the formula.**
For a bit in the peripheral bit-band region, the alias address is:

$$
A_{alias} = A_{alias\_base} + (A_{byte} - A_{region\_base}) \times 32 + n \times 4
$$

where:
- $A_{alias\_base} = 0x42000000$
- $A_{region\_base} = 0x40000000$ (peripheral bit-band region start)
- $A_{byte} = 0x40020014$
- $n = 5$ (the bit number)

**Step 2 — Compute the byte offset.**

$$
\Delta = A_{byte} - A_{region\_base} = 0x40020014 - 0x40000000 = 0x00020014
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by 32 (left-shift by 5).**

$$
\Delta \times 32 = 0x00020014 \ll 5
$$

Computing bit-by-bit:

$$
0x00020014 = 0b\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0010\,0000\,0000\,0001\,0100
$$

Shifting left by 5:

$$
0x00020014 \times 32 = 0x0040\,0280
$$

**Step 4 — Add the bit offset $n \times 4$.**

$$
n \times 4 = 5 \times 4 = 20 = 0x14
$$

So:

$$
A_{alias} = 0x42000000 + 0x0040\,0280 + 0x14
$$

**Step 5 — Final addition.**

$$
A_{alias} = 0x4240\,0294
$$

**Step 6 — Verify with the C code.**

```c
#define BITBAND_PERI(addr, bit) \
    ((volatile uint32_t *)(0x42000000UL + (((uint32_t)(addr) - 0x40000000UL) << 5) + ((bit) << 2)))

// Access:
*BITBAND_PERI(0x40020014, 5) = 1;   // Atomic set of bit 5
```

> [!NOTE]
> Bit-banding is a **read-modify-write atomic** at the bus level. The `volatile uint32_t *` cast to that alias address allows the compiler to issue a single STR instruction to flip that bit — no read, mask, write, and no risk of an ISR racing with you.

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Worst-Case Response Time of a Super-Loop Scheduler

**Problem (KTU pattern):** A bare-metal firmware uses a super-loop with three tasks: $T_1$ (read sensor, $t_1 = 5$ ms), $T_2$ (send UART packet, $t_2 = 12$ ms), $T_3$ (update display, $t_3 = 8$ ms). Compute (a) the cycle time, (b) the worst-case response time of $T_2$ in the worst scheduling slot, and (c) the CPU utilisation.

**Step 1 — Define the cycle time $T_c$.**
In a sequential super-loop, the cycle time is the sum of all task execution times:

$$
T_c = t_1 + t_2 + t_3
$$

Substituting:

$$
T_c = 5 + 12 + 8 = 25 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the worst-case response time of $T_2$.**
In the worst case, $T_2$ arrives just after it has been serviced in the cycle. It must wait for the remaining tasks in the current cycle plus all tasks ahead of it in the next cycle.

For a periodic task $T_i$ with period $P_i$ and execution time $t_i$ in a super-loop, the worst-case response time is:

$$
R_i^{WC} = T_c - t_i \quad \text{(for sequential non-pre-emptive loop)}
$$

Wait — let us re-derive. In a strict super-loop with *no pre-emption*, the worst-case waiting time of $T_2$ is when it arrives immediately after $T_2$ finishes. The remaining time before it is serviced is the sum of the durations of the other two tasks in the cycle:

$$
R_2^{WC} = t_1 + t_3 = 5 + 8 = 13 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify against the deadline.**
If $T_2$'s deadline is 20 ms, the system is **feasible** because $R_2^{WC} = 13 \text{ ms} < 20 \text{ ms}$.

**Step 4 — Compute CPU utilisation.**
For a single super-loop cycle of duration $T_c$ with no idle time:

$$
U = \frac{T_c}{T_c} = 100\%
$$

This is unrealistic for any real system. A more useful metric is the **CPU load** when tasks are periodic with periods $P_i$:

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{t_i}{P_i}
$$

If $P_1 = 10$ ms, $P_2 = 30$ ms, $P_3 = 50$ ms, then:

$$
U = \frac{5}{10} + \frac{12}{30} + \frac{8}{50}
$$

Computing each term:

$$
U = 0.500 + 0.400 + 0.160 = 1.060
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Overloaded system!** $U = 1.06 > 1.0$ means the super-loop **cannot** meet all deadlines. The student must either (a) reduce execution time (optimise code), (b) raise MCU clock, or (c) migrate to an **RTOS** with pre-emptive scheduling.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Bare-Metal vs. RTOS Firmware

| Property | Bare-Metal Super-Loop | RTOS-Based |
|---|---|---|
| **Complexity** | Low | Medium-High |
| **Memory footprint** | < 1 KB (code) | 4–32 KB (kernel) |
| **Determinism** | Bounded by $T_c$ | Bounded by tick rate |
| **Pre-emption** | None | Yes |
| **Task count** | ≤ 5 practical | 20+ comfortable |
| **Power modes** | Direct control | Mediated by idle hook |
| **Code size** | Tiny (≤ 32 KB) | Moderate (≥ 64 KB) |
| **KTU rule of thumb** | Use if MCU ≤ Cortex-M0+ and tasks < 5 | Use if MCU ≥ Cortex-M3 and tasks > 5 |

---

## 3.5 Embedded C Coding Standards — MISRA-C Highlights

MISRA-C is a *required* topic for any safety-critical firmware. KTU may ask for 3–4 rules.

| MISRA Rule | Description | Bad Code | Good Code |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rule 1.1** | No unreachable code | Code after `return;` | Remove dead code |
| **Rule 10.1** | No implicit integer conversions | `uint8_t + int` | Explicit cast |
| **Rule 12.2** | No shifting by ≥ bit-width | `1U << 32` | Mask first |
| **Rule 13.2** | No implicit &&/&& side effects | `if (x++ && y)` | Split into two `if` |
| **Rule 17.4** | Pointer arithmetic only on arrays | `p++;` on void* | `uint8_t *p; p++;` |
| **Rule 21.1** | No dynamic memory in safety code | `malloc()` | Static allocation |
| **Rule 22.1** | No recursive functions | `void f() { f(); }` | Convert to iteration |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->


## 4.4 Mermaid — Super-Loop Firmware Execution Flow (Bare-Metal)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MCU Power On or Reset] --> B[Initialize Stack Pointer from Vector Table]
    B --> C[Run Startup Code in startup.s<br/>Copy .data, Zero .bss]
    C --> D[Call main Function]
    D --> E[Peripheral Initialization<br/>GPIO, UART, ADC, Timers]
    E --> F[Enable Global Interrupts<br/>CPSIE i or sei]
    F --> G{Infinite Super Loop<br/>while one}

    G --> H[Task 1: Read Sensor via ADC]
    H --> I[Task 2: Process Data - Filter or Compute]
    I --> J[Task 3: Send Result via UART]
    J --> K[Task 4: Update Display or LEDs]
    K --> G

    ISR[Interrupt Service Routine<br/>Triggered by Hardware] -.preempts.-> G
    ISR --> ISR1[Save Context, Service Device]
    ISR1 --> ISR2[Set Flag or Post Semaphore]
    ISR2 --> ISR3[Restore Context, Return from Interrupt]
    ISR3 --> G

    style A fill:#FF6347,color:#FFF
    style G fill:#FFD700,color:#000
    style ISR fill:#9370DB,color:#FFF
```

## 4.5 Mermaid — Memory Map of a Typical ARM Cortex-M4 Firmware (Linker Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MEM["FLASH MEMORY - 0x08000000 to 0x0807FFFF"]
        VEC[Interrupt Vector Table<br/>Size 0x188 bytes]
        CDATA[.text - Program Code<br/>Reset_Handler, main, ISRs]
        CRODATA[.rodata - Constants<br/>String literals, lookup tables]
        CINIT[.init_array - C++ Constructors]
    end

    subgraph RAM["SRAM - 0x20000000 to 0x2001FFFF"]
        RDATA[.data - Initialized Globals<br/>int sensor_id = 0xCAFE]
        BSS[.bss - Zero-Initialized Globals<br/>uint8_t buffer[1024]]
        HEAP[Heap - malloc region<br/>Optional in embedded]
        STACK[Stack - Local Variables<br/>and ISR Context Saves<br/>Grows downward]
    end

    VEC --> CDATA
    CDATA --> CRODATA
    CRODATA --> CINIT

    RDATA --> BSS
    BSS --> HEAP
    HEAP --> STACK

    style VEC fill:#FF6347,color:#FFF
    style CDATA fill:#FFA500,color:#000
    style RDATA fill:#32CD32,color:#000
    style BSS fill:#1E90FF,color:#FFF
    style STACK fill:#9370DB,color:#FFF
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### **Q1.** *Define embedded firmware. How is it different from application software?*

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Sample), Module 3]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Embedded firmware** is the low-level software permanently stored in the non-volatile memory of an embedded device that directly controls hardware peripherals and provides a deterministic execution platform for higher-level application code. **(1 mark)**

| Aspect | Firmware | Application Software |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Flash / ROM of MCU | Hard disk / SSD of PC |
| **Update frequency** | Rare (firmware update) | Frequent |
| **Determinism** | Real-time, bounded latency | Best-effort, non-real-time |
| **Resource budget** | KBs of RAM / Flash | GBs |
| **Example** | STM32 bootloader, RTOS kernel | Chrome, MS Word |

**(2 marks for comparison table + examples)**

---

### **Q2.** *Why is the C language preferred over C++ and Java for embedded firmware development? List any four reasons.*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Sample), Module 3]**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 each):**

1. **Tiny code size and zero runtime overhead** — no garbage collector, no exception handling, no RTTI ⇒ fits in 32 KB Flash.
2. **Direct memory-mapped I/O access** via pointers — C does not hide hardware addresses behind abstractions.
3. **Deterministic compilation** — output assembly is small, predictable, and inspectable ⇒ essential for hard real-time systems.
4. **Universal compiler support** for all MCUs (GCC, IAR, Keil, ARM CC) and well-defined standards (C99, C11, C17).

---

## 📗 PART B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, the student must answer either **Question A** OR **Question B** in full (14 marks total). Each sub-part is 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 marks)** — *Firmware Architecture & Language Selection*

#### Part (a) — 7 marks
**Explain the three main firmware design architectures used in embedded systems. Compare their advantages, limitations, and suitable use cases.**

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Super-Loop (Polled) Architecture (2 marks)**
A single `while(1)` infinite loop in `main()` calls each task function sequentially. Tasks run one after the other, no pre-emption.

```c
int main(void) {
    System_Init();
    while (1) {
        Read_Sensor();     // 5 ms
        Process_Data();    // 2 ms
        Send_UART();       // 3 ms
        Update_Display();  // 4 ms
    }
}
```

- *Advantages:* zero overhead, smallest code, easy to understand, deterministic in a coarse sense.
- *Limitations:* no pre-emption, blocking delays freeze the whole system, CPU utilisation = 100% (no idle for sleep), no multi-priority.
- *Use case:* Microwave oven, washing machine, simple sensor node.

**2. Event-Driven (Interrupt-Driven) Architecture (2.5 marks)**
A main loop performs low-priority background work; high-priority events are handled by hardware interrupts. Tasks are scheduled by the interrupt controller.

```c
volatile uint8_t event_flag = 0;
void EXTI0_IRQHandler(void) { event_flag = 1; }   // ISR
int main(void) {
    Init_Peripherals();
    while (1) {
        if (event_flag) { Handle_Event(); event_flag = 0; }
        Sleep_CPU();              // Idle until next interrupt
    }
}
```

- *Advantages:* efficient power use, fast response to critical events, ISR priority supported by NVIC.
- *Limitations:* complex to debug, ISR must be tiny, race conditions on shared variables (need volatile + atomic).
- *Use case:* IoT battery-powered sensors, motor control with encoder feedback.

**3. RTOS-Based (Multi-Task) Architecture (2.5 marks)**
A real-time kernel (FreeRTOS, Zephyr, RT-Thread) runs multiple tasks with pre-emptive priority-based scheduling, inter-process communication (queues, semaphores, mutexes).

```c
void Task_Sensor(void *p) {
    while (1) {
        xSemaphoreTake(sem_sensor, portMAX_DELAY);
        Read_Sensor();
    }
}
void Task_UART(void *p) {
    while (1) {
        xQueueReceive(q_uart, &msg, portMAX_DELAY);
        Send_UART(msg);
    }
}
```

- *Advantages:* pre-emptive, modular, supports complex timing, easier to scale to many tasks.
- *Limitations:* 4–32 KB extra Flash for kernel, learning curve, requires careful priority analysis.
- *Use case:* Drones, automotive ECUs, wearables, multi-protocol IoT gateways.

#### Part (b) — 7 marks
**Compare C, C++, and Assembly for embedded firmware development. State the criteria a designer should use to choose a language.**

**Model Solution:**

| Criterion | Assembly | C | C++ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Code size** | Smallest | Small | +10–30% larger |
| **Execution speed** | Fastest possible | Near-optimal | Slower (vtables, exceptions) |
| **Readability** | Poor | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Portability** | Zero (CPU-specific) | High | High |
| **Hardware access** | Native | Via pointers/volatile | Via pointers/volatile |
| **Real-time safety** | Best | Excellent (with MISRA) | Good (MISRA-C++) |
| **Toolchain support** | Limited | Universal | Growing |
| **Learning curve** | Steep | Easy | Moderate |

**Selection Criteria (3 marks):**
1. **Resource budget** — If Flash < 16 KB, prefer C or even Assembly for the boot path.
2. **Real-time requirements** — Hard real-time (≤ 100 µs deadline) ⇒ C with MISRA or Assembly for ISR.
3. **Maintainability and team size** — Large teams favour C++ for OOP, design patterns.
4. **Certification** — DO-178C (aviation), IEC 61508 (industrial) require a *language subset* like MISRA-C; C is more battle-tested than C++.
5. **Hardware abstraction** — All three can do it, but C is the lingua franca of vendor HALs.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "C++ is faster than C" — this is almost always **false** for embedded. Markers will deduct 1 mark for this misconception.

---

### **Question B (14 marks)** — *Embedded C Programming Constructs*

#### Part (a) — 7 marks
**Explain the role of the `volatile` keyword in embedded C. Give two scenarios where omitting `volatile` causes a bug, with code examples.**

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
The `volatile` qualifier in C is a type qualifier that informs the compiler that an object may change its value at any time *without any action being taken by the code the compiler finds nearby*. Consequently, the compiler must **always** load the value from memory before using it, and **always** store the modified value back to memory after a write. It disables most compiler optimisations such as register-caching and constant-folding for that variable.

**Scenario 1: Memory-mapped peripheral register (2.5 marks)**
The flags register of a UART changes whenever a byte arrives. The compiler, if not told otherwise, will *assume* the variable never changes within the loop and elide the re-read:

```c
// BAD: Compiler may optimise away the loop
uint32_t *UART_SR = (uint32_t *)0x40011000;
while ((*UART_SR & 0x40) == 0) { /* wait for TXE */ }   // BUG

// GOOD: Mark the pointer as volatile
volatile uint32_t *UART_SR = (volatile uint32_t *)0x40011000;
while ((*UART_SR & 0x40) == 0) { /* wait for TXE */ }   // CORRECT
```

**Scenario 2: Variable shared between ISR and main code (2.5 marks)**

```c
// BAD: Compiler caches flag in register
uint8_t data_ready = 0;
ISR(USART1_Handler) { data_ready = 1; }
int main(void) {
    while (!data_ready) { /* ... */ }   // INFINITE LOOP - compiler may keep reading 0
    Process_Data();
}

// GOOD: Mark shared variable as volatile
volatile uint8_t data_ready = 0;
ISR(USART1_Handler) { data_ready = 1; }
int main(void) {
    while (!data_ready) { __WFI(); }    // Wait For Interrupt + volatile re-reads
    Process_Data();
}
```

#### Part (b) — 7 marks
**Write an Embedded C program to (i) configure PA5 as output push-pull, (ii) toggle it every 500 ms using SysTick, and (iii) explain bit-wise operations used.**

**Model Solution:**

**C Program (5 marks):**

```c
#include "stm32f4xx.h"

void GPIO_Init(void);
void SysTick_Init(void);
void Delay_ms(uint32_t ms);

volatile uint32_t ms_ticks = 0;

int main(void) {
    GPIO_Init();
    SysTick_Init();
    while (1) {
        GPIOA->ODR ^= (1U << 5);    // Toggle PA5
        Delay_ms(500);
    }
}

void GPIO_Init(void) {
    RCC->AHB1ENR |= (1U << 0);                     // (1) Enable GPIOA clock
    GPIOA->MODER &= ~(3U << (5 * 2));              // (2) Clear MODER bits for PA5
    GPIOA->MODER |=  (1U << (5 * 2));              // (3) Set PA5 as General Purpose Output
    GPIOA->OTYPER &= ~(1U << 5);                   // (4) Push-pull (reset default)
    GPIOA->OSPEEDR |= (2U << (5 * 2));             // (5) High speed
    GPIOA->PUPDR &= ~(3U << (5 * 2));              // (6) No pull-up/pull-down
}

void SysTick_Init(void) {
    SysTick->LOAD = (16000000U / 1000U) - 1U;     // 1 ms tick @ 16 MHz
    SysTick->VAL  = 0U;
    SysTick->CTRL = (1U << 0) | (1U << 1) | (1U << 2);
}

void SysTick_Handler(void) {
    ms_ticks++;
}

void Delay_ms(uint32_t ms) {
    uint32_t start = ms_ticks;
    while ((ms_ticks - start) < ms) { __WFI(); }
}
```

**Explanation of Bit-wise Operations (2 marks):**

| Operation | Code | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| Set bit | `RCC->AHB1ENR \|= (1U << 0);` | `1U << 0` = `0b0001`. OR-set writes 1 to bit 0. |
| Clear bits | `GPIOA->MODER &= ~(3U << (5 * 2));` | `3U << 10` = `0b1100 0000 0000`. AND-NOT clears the 2-bit field. |
| Insert value | `GPIOA->MODER \|= (1U << (5 * 2));` | Sets MODE = 01 (output) for PA5. |
| Toggle | `GPIOA->ODR ^= (1U << 5);` | XOR with 1 flips bit 5 atomically. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common mistakes that cost 1–2 marks:**
> 1. Forgetting `volatile` on `ms_ticks` — the compiler caches it in a register and the loop never terminates.
> 2. Writing `1 << 5` instead of `1U << 5` — signed left-shift of a 32-bit value is implementation-defined per the C standard; `1U` is unsigned and portable.
> 3. Forgetting to enable the GPIO clock before configuring MODER — the peripheral will appear dead.
> 4. Using a magic literal like `0x40020000` without `#define` — KTU deducts 0.5 for poor coding style.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

| # | Concept | One-Line Recall |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Firmware** | Software permanently embedded in non-volatile memory that directly controls hardware. |
| 2 | **Firmware vs Software** | Firmware is hardware-tied, deterministic, real-time; software is generic, OS-managed. |
| 3 | **Determinism** | Bounded, predictable response time to events — the defining trait of firmware. |
| 4 | **Firmware stack** | App → Middleware → RTOS → HAL → Low-level → Hardware. |
| 5 | **Three architectures** | Super-loop, Event-driven, RTOS-based. |
| 6 | **Language preference** | C is the industry default (~80% of code); C++ for OOP; Assembly only for boot/ISR inner loops. |
| 7 | **Fixed-width types** | Always use `<stdint.h>` types: `uint8_t`, `int16_t`, `uint32_t`. |
| 8 | **`volatile`** | Mandatory for hardware registers and ISR-shared variables. Disables register-caching optimisations. |
| 9 | **Bit manipulation** | Set = `\|= (1U<<n)`, Clear = `&= ~(1U<<n)`, Toggle = `^= (1U<<n)`. |
| 10 | **Bit-band alias** | `0x42000000 + ((addr − 0x40000000) << 5) + (bit << 2)` on Cortex-M3/M4. |
| 11 | **Memory-mapped I/O** | All peripherals accessed via SFR pointers with `volatile`. |
| 12 | **ISR golden rules** | Short body, no blocking calls, clear pending bit, save context. |
| 13 | **Super-loop WC response** | $R_i^{WC} = T_c - t_i$ for sequential service. |
| 14 | **CPU utilisation** | $U = \sum t_i / P_i$; if $U > 1$, system is overloaded. |
| 15 | **MISRA-C** | A safe-subset coding standard mandated in automotive, aviation, medical firmware. |
| 16 | **MISRA Rule 10.1** | No implicit integer conversions — always cast explicitly. |
| 17 | **MISRA Rule 12.2** | No shifting by ≥ operand bit-width. |
| 18 | **MISRA Rule 21.1** | No dynamic memory (`malloc`) in safety-critical code. |
| 19 | **Linker sections** | Flash holds `.text`+`.rodata`; RAM holds `.data`+`.bss`+heap+stack. |
| 20 | **Why C++ is bigger** | Exception handling, RTTI, vtables add 10–30% to binary size. |

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip (2024 Scheme):**
> In 14-mark questions, **always draw a diagram** (flowchart, layer diagram, or table) even if not explicitly asked. Examiners report that well-illustrated answers score 1.5–2 marks higher than text-only answers of identical content. Use the Mermaid-style flow diagrams shown above as your model.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integration and Testing of Embedded Hardware and Firmware
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT726 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Integration and Testing of Embedded Hardware and Firmware

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Integration and Testing of Embedded Hardware and Firmware** is the systematic process of combining the physical hardware components (microcontroller, sensors, actuators, peripherals) with the embedded software (firmware, device drivers, RTOS) on a target platform, and then validating the complete system behavior against the specification through structured testing methodologies, debugging tools, and verification techniques.

In KTU 2024 parlance, this is referred to as the **Hardware–Software Co-Validation** phase of the Embedded System Design Life Cycle. It bridges the gap between the **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)** development and the final **Product Validation Stage**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Car Manufacturing**
>
> Imagine a car is being built in a factory:
> - **Hardware Integration** is like attaching the engine, wheels, brakes, and electronics to the chassis.
> - **Firmware Integration** is like loading the Engine Control Unit (ECU) software, ABS firmware, and infotainment OS.
> - **Testing** is like taking the car to a test track, a crash-test facility, and a diagnostic computer to verify everything works together.
>
> Just as you cannot sell a car with an unhooked steering wheel, an embedded system is non-functional until its hardware and firmware are integrated and tested *as a unified whole*.

## 1.3 Why is Integration & Testing the Hardest Phase?

According to industry data, approximately **70% of embedded system bugs** are caught during integration and testing, while only **30%** are caught in unit/development phase. The primary reasons are:

- **Concurrency Issues** — interrupts, DMA, and RTOS task scheduling cause race conditions.
- **Hardware–Software Timing Mismatches** — propagation delays, setup/hold violations.
- **Driver Mismatches** — wrong register maps, bit-field errors, peripheral initialization order.
- **Power Sequencing Bugs** — brown-outs, inrush currents, peripheral wake-up glitches.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** When answering theory questions, always mention the **V-Model** of embedded development — testing is *planned in parallel* with each design phase, not postponed to the end.

## 1.4 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Standard KTU Definition |
|------|--------------------------|
| **BSP** (Board Support Package) | A collection of boot code, device drivers, and configuration files tailored to a specific hardware board. |
| **JTAG** (Joint Test Action Group) | IEEE 1149.1 standard 4-wire debug/test interface used for boundary scan and in-circuit emulation. |
| **SWD** (Serial Wire Debug) | ARM-specific 2-wire alternative to JTAG, used in Cortex-M microcontrollers. |
| **HIL** (Hardware-in-the-Loop) | Real-time simulation where a real controller interacts with a simulated plant. |
| **ICE** (In-Circuit Emulator) | Hardware tool that replaces the target MCU to allow step-by-step firmware debugging. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Embedded System Integration Process — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield:** Understand the **Layered Integration Model** — integration happens from the **bottom up**, starting with the bare-metal bring-up, then peripheral drivers, then application code.

### Step 1 — Hardware Bring-Up (Bare-Metal Blinky)
- Power ON the board.
- Verify voltage rails using a **Digital Multimeter (DMM)** and **Oscilloscope**.
- Toggle a single GPIO (e.g., blink an LED) to confirm the **clock tree** and **reset vector** are functional.
- This is the *Hello World* of embedded integration.

### Step 2 — Bootloader and Memory Initialization
- Load the **First Stage Bootloader (FSBL)** via JTAG/SWD.
- Initialize the **Stack Pointer (SP)** and **Program Counter (PC)**.
- Configure the **Memory Protection Unit (MPU)** if present.
- Copy the **.data** section from Flash to RAM (since RAM is volatile).
- Zero-initialize the **.bss** section.

### Step 3 — Peripheral Driver Integration
- Integrate drivers one peripheral at a time (UART → SPI → I2C → ADC → PWM).
- Use a **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)** provided by the silicon vendor (e.g., STM32 HAL, ESP-IDF) or write bare-metal CMSIS drivers.

### Step 4 — Middleware & RTOS Integration
- Integrate the **RTOS kernel** (FreeRTOS, Zephyr, RTX).
- Configure tick interrupts, priority levels, and task stacks.
- Validate the **scheduler** with idle task observation.

### Step 5 — Application Layer Integration
- Link the application code with drivers, RTOS, and libraries.
- Generate the final **ELF / HEX / BIN** file using the build system.
- Flash to the target's non-volatile memory (Flash / EEPROM).

### Step 6 — System Validation
- Run end-to-end test cases.
- Measure power, latency, throughput, and reliability.

## 2.2 Testing Methodologies in Embedded Systems

Embedded testing is broadly classified into **four levels** (mapped to KTU 2024 Module 4 syllabus):

### A. Unit Testing
- Tests the smallest testable unit (a function, a driver routine).
- Performed on the **host PC** using mocks/stubs.
- Tools: **Ceedling**, **Unity**, **CMock**, **CppUTest**.

### B. Integration Testing
- Tests the interaction between two or more modules.
- Example: Verifying that the UART driver correctly hands bytes to a parser module.
- Detects interface mismatches, parameter type mismatches, and timing issues.

### C. System Testing
- Tests the complete embedded system against its specification.
- Includes functional, performance, stress, and regression tests.

### D. Acceptance Testing
- Done with the customer/end-user to verify the product meets requirements.
- Often performed in the actual deployment environment.

## 2.3 Hardware Testing Tools

| Tool | Purpose | Typical Use |
|------|---------|-------------|
| **Logic Analyzer** | Captures multiple digital signals simultaneously | Bus protocol decoding (I2C, SPI, UART) |
| **Oscilloscope** | Measures analog voltage waveforms | Signal integrity, noise, rise time |
| **DMM** (Digital Multimeter) | DC voltage, current, resistance | Power rail verification |
| **JTAG/SWD Debugger** | Boundary scan, flash programming, step-debug | J-Link, ST-Link, CMSIS-DAP |
| **Spectrum Analyzer** | Frequency-domain analysis | RF noise, EMI testing |
| **In-Circuit Emulator (ICE)** | Real-time trace, breakpoints, watchpoints | Firmware debugging on the actual MCU |
| **Signature Analyzer** | Compares node signatures to known-good values | Manufacturing fault detection |

## 2.4 Firmware Testing Strategies

### Strategy 1 — Static Analysis
- Analyzes source code without executing it.
- Detects syntax errors, type mismatches, unreachable code, and potential memory leaks.
- Tools: **PC-lint**, **Coverity**, **MISRA-C checkers**, **Clang Static Analyzer**.

### Strategy 2 — Dynamic Analysis
- Executes the firmware on real hardware or a simulator.
- Detects runtime errors like buffer overflows, null pointer dereferences, and stack corruption.
- Tools: **Valgrind**, **AddressSanitizer (ASan)**, **GDB**, **Ozone debugger**.

### Strategy 3 — Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) Testing
- The **real MCU** runs the firmware, but the **external plant** (sensors, actuators) is **simulated**.
- Allows testing of failure scenarios safely (e.g., sensor disconnected, motor stall).

### Strategy 4 — On-Target Testing
- Tests are executed *on the target MCU itself* using frameworks like **Unity** or **Google Test** ported for embedded use.
- Output is sent via UART, RTT (Real-Time Transfer), or semihosting.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Important KTU 2024 Note:** No complex numerical formulas dominate this topic, but a few **timing and memory equations** are examinable.

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|--------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Flash Programming Time | $T_{prog} = \frac{N \times t_{byte}}{f_{clk}}$ | $N$ = bytes, $t_{byte}$ = programming time per byte, $f_{clk}$ = clock |
| 2 | JTAG TCK Frequency | $f_{TCK} = \frac{1}{T_{TCK}}$ | Typically $\leq$ 50 MHz in modern debuggers |
| 3 | Bit-Baud Rate (UART) | $Baud = \frac{f_{clk}}{16 \times (UBRR + 1)}$ | $UBRR$ = USART Baud Rate Register |
| 4 | Interrupt Latency | $t_{latency} = t_{sync} + t_{exec} + t_{vector}$ | $t_{sync}$ = synchronization cycles |
| 5 | RAM Usage | $S_{ram} = S_{data} + S_{bss} + S_{stack} + S_{heap}$ | Bytes |
| 6 | Flash Usage | $S_{flash} = S_{code} + S_{rodata} + S_{init}$ | Bytes |
| 7 | CPU Utilization | $U = \frac{T_{active}}{T_{total}} \times 100\,\%$ | Expressed as a percentage |
| 8 | Test Coverage | $C = \frac{T_{executed}}{T_{total}} \times 100\,\%$ | Used in code coverage reports |
| 9 | Code Density (Assembly) | $D = \frac{N_{inst}}{S_{code}}$ | Instructions per byte |
| 10 | Boundary Scan Cell Delay | $t_{bs} = t_{capture} + t_{update}$ | Used in JTAG test clocks |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **automotive industry** (AUTOSAR standard), **medical devices** (IEC 62304), and **aerospace** (DO-178C), rigorous integration and testing is *legally mandated*. For example:
- A fault in the firmware of a **pacemaker** is life-threatening.
- An **aircraft autopilot** bug can cause fatal crashes (e.g., Boeing 737 MAX).
- A **brake-by-wire** system must undergo HIL testing for **1 million cycles** before certification.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

### Example 1: UART Baud Rate Calculation (ARM Cortex-M @ 16 MHz)

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **frequently asked KTU numerical** that combines testing with peripheral configuration.

**Given:** 
- System Clock $f_{clk} = 16\,\text{MHz} = 16 \times 10^6\,\text{Hz}$
- Desired Baud Rate $B = 9600\,\text{bps}$
- UART Mode: Normal (oversampling by 16)

**Step 1:** Recall the KTU-standard UART baud formula:

$$
Baud = \frac{f_{clk}}{16 \times (UBRR + 1)}
$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to solve for $UBRR$:

$$
UBRR + 1 = \frac{f_{clk}}{16 \times Baud}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the known values:

$$
UBRR + 1 = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{16 \times 9600}
$$

**Step 4:** Simplify numerator and denominator:

$$
UBRR + 1 = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{153600}
$$

**Step 5:** Perform the division:

$$
UBRR + 1 = 104.1667
$$

**Step 6:** Take the integer part for the UBRR register (rounded down to 104):

$$
UBRR = 103
$$

**Step 7:** Calculate the **actual achieved baud rate** with $UBRR = 103$:

$$
Baud_{actual} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{16 \times (103 + 1)} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{1664}
$$

**Step 8:** Compute the final value:

$$
Baud_{actual} = 9615.38\,\text{bps}
$$

**Step 9:** Calculate the **percent error**:

$$
\% \text{Error} = \frac{\vert Baud_{actual} - Baud_{desired} \vert}{Baud_{desired}} \times 100\,\%
$$

$$
\% \text{Error} = \frac{\vert 9615.38 - 9600 \vert}{9600} \times 100\,\% = 0.16\,\%
$$

**Result:** Error is **0.16%**, well within the UART specification tolerance of ±2%, so the configuration is valid.

---

### Example 2: Flash Programming Time Estimation

**Given:** 
- Flash memory size $N = 256\,\text{KB} = 262144\,\text{bytes}$
- Programming time per byte $t_{byte} = 50\,\mu\text{s}$
- Erase time per page $t_{erase} = 1\,\text{ms}$ (page size = 2 KB)
- Number of pages = $262144 / 2048 = 128$ pages
- JTAG clock $f_{clk} = 4\,\text{MHz}$

**Step 1:** Calculate the **erase time**:

$$
T_{erase} = 128 \times 1\,\text{ms} = 128\,\text{ms} = 0.128\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the **programming time**:

$$
T_{prog} = 262144 \times 50\,\mu\text{s} = 13107200\,\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to seconds:

$$
T_{prog} = 13.1072\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the **JTAG transfer time** (assuming 4-byte data per TCK cycle):

$$
T_{transfer} = \frac{262144 \times 8}{4 \times 4 \times 10^6} = \frac{2097152}{16 \times 10^6} = 0.131\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 5:** Total flash update time:

$$
T_{total} = T_{erase} + T_{transfer} + T_{prog}
$$

$$
T_{total} = 0.128 + 0.131 + 13.1072
$$

$$
T_{total} = 13.366\,\text{s}
$$

**Result:** A full firmware flash cycle takes approximately **13.37 seconds** at this configuration. Engineers use this to estimate the **bootloader update duration** in OTA (Over-The-Air) systems.

---

### Example 3: Interrupt Latency Budget Analysis

**Given:**
- MCU clock $f_{clk} = 72\,\text{MHz}$, period $T = 13.89\,\text{ns}$
- Synchronization cycles: $t_{sync} = 2$ cycles
- Worst-case instruction execution: $t_{exec} = 4$ cycles
- Vector fetch: $t_{vector} = 3$ cycles
- ISR entry: $t_{isr} = 1$ cycle

**Step 1:** Total cycles for interrupt response:

$$
N_{total} = t_{sync} + t_{exec} + t_{vector} + t_{isr}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values:

$$
N_{total} = 2 + 4 + 3 + 1 = 10\,\text{cycles}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the absolute latency:

$$
t_{latency} = N_{total} \times T = 10 \times 13.89\,\text{ns}
$$

**Step 4:** Final result:

$$
t_{latency} = 138.9\,\text{ns}
$$

**Interpretation:** For a real-time control loop with a **1 ms period**, this latency consumes only **0.0139%** of the available time, leaving ample budget for the actual ISR work.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Debug Tool

> [!TIP]
> This matrix is a **KTU 2024 high-value comparison question** — often asked in the 14-mark section.

| Debug Tool | Cost | Real-Time Trace | Breakpoints | Memory Inspection | Best For |
|------------|------|-----------------|-------------|--------------------|----------|
| **JTAG (J-Link)** | High | Yes (with ETM) | Unlimited | Full | Production debugging, ARM Cortex |
| **SWD (ST-Link)** | Low | Limited | Unlimited | Full | STM32 development, education |
| **ICE (Full Emulator)** | Very High | Yes (cycle-accurate) | Conditional | Full | Safety-critical (DO-178C) |
| **Logic Analyzer** | Medium | Capture only | No | No | Bus protocol analysis |
| **Printf via UART** | Free | No | No | No | Quick sanity checks |
| **GDB + OpenOCD** | Free | Limited | Yes | Yes | Open-source projects |
| **RTT (Segger)** | Free with J-Link | Yes | Yes | Yes | High-speed logging |

## 3.3 Test Case Template (KTU Standard Format)

A standard test case has the following fields, also used in **IEEE 829** standard:

| Field | Description | Example |
|-------|-------------|---------|
| Test ID | Unique identifier | TC-UART-001 |
| Description | What is being tested | Transmit 1 byte via UART2 |
| Preconditions | Required state | Clock configured, baud = 9600 |
| Test Steps | Actions to perform | Call `UART_Send('A')` |
| Expected Result | Pass criterion | Logic analyzer shows 0x41 frame |
| Actual Result | Observed outcome | 0x41 frame observed at TX pin |
| Status | Pass / Fail | Pass |
| Remarks | Observations | No framing errors in 1000 iterations |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Followed
- All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `hw1`, `fw1`, `test1`).
- No reserved keywords used as standalone node names.
- Labels are plain uppercase text without bold/markdown formatting.
- Backslashes are escaped where required for compatibility.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Embedded System Integration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[POWER ON HARDWARE] --> B1[VERIFY VOLTAGE RAILS USING DMM]
    B1 --> C1[CLOCK TREE INITIALIZATION]
    C1 --> D1[BOOTLOADER LOAD VIA JTAG OR SWD]
    D1 --> E1[STACK POINTER AND PROGRAM COUNTER SETUP]
    E1 --> F1[COPY DATA SECTION FROM FLASH TO RAM]
    F1 --> G1[ZERO INITIALIZE BSS SECTION]
    G1 --> H1[PERIPHERAL DRIVER INTEGRATION]
    H1 --> I1[UART DRIVER TEST]
    H1 --> I2[SPI DRIVER TEST]
    H1 --> I3[I2C DRIVER TEST]
    H1 --> I4[ADC AND PWM DRIVER TEST]
    I1 --> J1[RTOS KERNEL INTEGRATION]
    I2 --> J1
    I3 --> J1
    I4 --> J1
    J1 --> K1[APPLICATION LAYER LINKING]
    K1 --> L1[GENERATE HEX OR BIN FILE]
    L1 --> M1[FLASH FIRMWARE TO TARGET]
    M1 --> N1[SYSTEM LEVEL VALIDATION]
    N1 --> O1{PASS OR FAIL}
    O1 -- PASS --> P1[RELEASE FOR PRODUCTION]
    O1 -- FAIL --> Q1[DEBUG AND FIX]
    Q1 --> D1
```

## 4.3 V-Model for Embedded Systems (Mapping Design to Testing)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REQ[REQUIREMENTS SPECIFICATION] --> VAL1[ACCEPTANCE TEST DESIGN]
    SYS[SYSTEM ARCHITECTURE DESIGN] --> VAL2[SYSTEM TEST DESIGN]
    MOD[MODULE DESIGN] --> VAL3[INTEGRATION TEST DESIGN]
    COD[CODE IMPLEMENTATION] --> VAL4[UNIT TESTING]
    VAL4 --> COD
    VAL3 --> MOD
    VAL2 --> SYS
    VAL1 --> REQ
    COD --> VAL3
    MOD --> VAL2
    SYS --> VAL1
    REQ --> P1[FINAL PRODUCT VALIDATION]
```

## 4.4 JTAG Boundary Scan Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[TAP CONTROLLER] --> T2[INSTRUCTION REGISTER]
    T1 --> T3[BOUNDARY SCAN CELL CHAIN]
    T3 --> T4[DEVICE 1 PINS]
    T3 --> T5[DEVICE 2 PINS]
    T3 --> T6[DEVICE 3 PINS]
    T2 --> T7[BYPASS REGISTER]
    T2 --> T8[IDCODE REGISTER]
    T1 --> T9[TCK TMS TDI TDO SIGNALS]
```

## 4.5 Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[REAL FIRMWARE ON TARGET MCU] --> R2[ACTUATOR COMMAND SIGNALS]
    R2 --> S1[SIMULATED PLANT MODEL]
    S1 --> S2[SENSOR FEEDBACK SIGNALS]
    S2 --> R1
    S1 --> M1[FAULT INJECTION MODULE]
    M1 --> S1
    R1 --> D1[DATA LOGGING AND VISUALIZATION]
    D1 --> A1[REAL TIME PERFORMANCE METRICS]
```

## 4.6 Layered Architecture of Integrated Embedded System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[APPLICATION LAYER] --> L2[RTOS MIDDLEWARE LAYER]
    L2 --> L3[HARDWARE ABSTRACTION LAYER HAL]
    L3 --> L4[DEVICE DRIVER LAYER]
    L4 --> L5[PHYSICAL HARDWARE LAYER]
    L5 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is a Board Support Package (BSP)? List its main components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Board Support Package (BSP)** is a collection of software that enables an operating system or RTOS to function on a specific hardware platform.

**Main components:**
1. **Bootloader / Startup code** — initializes CPU, clocks, and memory.
2. **Device drivers** — for board-specific peripherals (UART, I2C, GPIO).
3. **Board configuration headers** — pin muxing, clock tree setup.
4. **Linker scripts** — define memory regions (.text, .data, .bss).
5. **HAL (Hardware Abstraction Layer)** — vendor-provided abstraction (e.g., STM32 HAL).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between JTAG and SWD debugging interfaces.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | JTAG | SWD |
|---------|------|-----|
| Wires required | 4 (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO) | 2 (SWDIO, SWCLK) |
| Standard | IEEE 1149.1 | ARM-specific |
| Pin count | Higher | Lower (suits small MCUs) |
| Speed | Generally faster | Adequate for Cortex-M |
| Use case | Boundary scan + debug | Debug only |

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, ESE Pattern]**

**Q.A (a) Explain the various levels of testing in embedded systems with examples. (7 marks)**
**Q.A (b) Describe the JTAG architecture and its role in boundary scan testing. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

Embedded system testing is structured in **four hierarchical levels**:

**1. Unit Testing (1.5 marks):**
- Tests a single function or module in isolation.
- Example: Testing a `uart_send_byte()` function on the host PC with mocked register writes.
- Tools: Ceedling, Unity, Google Test.

**2. Integration Testing (2 marks):**
- Tests the interaction between two or more modules.
- Example: Verifying that the ADC driver produces correct samples, and the filtering module correctly processes them.
- Detects interface mismatches and data type errors.

**3. System Testing (2 marks):**
- Tests the complete product against specifications.
- Includes functional, performance, stress, and security tests.
- Example: Running a 24-hour soak test on a washing machine controller.

**4. Acceptance Testing (1.5 marks):**
- Performed by the end customer.
- Example: Customer verifies that a smart thermostat meets the user manual claims.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**JTAG (Joint Test Action Group)** is the **IEEE 1149.1** standard for testing interconnects on PCBs and debugging MCUs.

**Key Components (3 marks):**
1. **TAP (Test Access Port) Controller** — state machine with 16 states (Test-Logic-Reset, Run-Test/Idle, Shift-DR, etc.).
2. **TDI (Test Data In)** — serial data input.
3. **TDO (Test Data Out)** — serial data output.
4. **TMS (Test Mode Select)** — controls state transitions.
5. **TCK (Test Clock)** — synchronous clock for all operations.
6. **TRST (Test Reset)** — optional asynchronous reset.

**Boundary Scan Cells (2 marks):**
- Each IC pin has a **boundary scan cell** between the core logic and the external pin.
- Cells can **capture** (read pin state) or **update** (drive pin state).
- The cells are chained serially to form a **scan chain**.

**Role in Testing (2 marks):**
- Detects **opens, shorts, and stuck-at faults** on PCBs without physical probes.
- Used for **in-system programming (ISP)** of Flash memory.
- Enables **debugging** via breakpoints, watchpoints, and register inspection.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

### **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, ESE Pattern]**

**Q.B (a) With a neat diagram, explain the Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) testing methodology. (7 marks)**
**Q.B (b) Discuss the role of logic analyzers and oscilloscopes in embedded hardware testing. Compare their use cases. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) Testing** is a real-time simulation technique where the **actual embedded controller** interacts with a **simulated plant**.

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** (Refer to Section 4.5 above for the Mermaid schematic.)

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
- The plant (e.g., motor, engine, sensor) is modeled mathematically in software (MATLAB/Simulink, LabVIEW).
- The real firmware running on the target MCU sends control signals (PWM, GPIO) to the simulated plant via **DAC** or **digital I/O**.
- The simulator responds with **simulated sensor feedback** (ADC values, encoder counts) routed back to the MCU.

**Advantages (2 marks):**
1. Safe testing of fault conditions (e.g., sensor disconnected, motor stall).
2. Repeatable test scenarios with controlled inputs.
3. Reduced development cost (no need for real plant hardware).
4. Critical for **automotive (AUTOSAR)**, **aerospace (DO-178C)**, and **medical** certifications.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Logic Analyzer (3.5 marks):**
- Captures **multiple digital signals** simultaneously (typically 8 to 64+ channels).
- Displays timing relationships between signals.
- Decodes bus protocols: **I2C, SPI, UART, CAN, I2S**.
- Used for **debugging communication**, **protocol violations**, and **timing analysis**.
- Example: Saleae Logic Pro 8, Tektronix TLA series.

**Oscilloscope (2.5 marks):**
- Measures **analog voltage waveforms** with high precision.
- Typically 2 or 4 channels.
- Used for **signal integrity**, **rise/fall time**, **noise analysis**, and **power rail ripple**.
- Example: Rigol DS1054Z, Keysight InfiniiVision.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Logic Analyzer | Oscilloscope |
|--------|----------------|--------------|
| Signal type | Digital | Analog + Digital |
| Channels | Many (8–64+) | Few (2–4) |
| Use | Protocol decoding | Signal integrity |
| Sampling rate | High on digital | High on analog |

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "unit" and "integration" testing** — Unit = single module; Integration = interaction of 2+ modules. Examiners *strictly* deduct 1 mark if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Forgetting the 4 JTAG signal names** — Students often write only "TMS, TCK" and forget TDI, TDO. This loses **1.5 marks** in a 7-mark question.
> 3. **Calling HIL testing "software testing"** — HIL specifically involves **real hardware** + **simulated plant**. The hardware element is critical to mention.
> 4. **Omitting examples** in theory questions. KTU 2024 explicitly rewards **examples** (often worth 1–2 marks).
> 5. **Not mentioning IEEE 1149.1** when discussing JTAG — this is a **standard KTU expected keyword**.
> 6. **Confusing ISP (In-System Programming) with JTAG debugging** — ISP is for *flashing*; debugging is for *step-by-step execution*.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision — Memorize These Bullets:**

- [x] **BSP** = Bootloader + Drivers + Config Headers + Linker Scripts.
- [x] **JTAG** uses 4 wires (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO); **SWD** uses 2 (SWDIO, SWCLK).
- [x] **JTAG Standard = IEEE 1149.1**; **Boundary Scan** detects PCB-level opens/shorts.
- [x] **Four testing levels**: Unit → Integration → System → Acceptance.
- [x] **HIL Testing** = real firmware + simulated plant.
- [x] **Logic Analyzer** → digital signals, protocol decoding; **Oscilloscope** → analog signals, signal integrity.
- [x] **UART Baud Formula** (KTU 2024 high-yield): $Baud = \frac{f_{clk}}{16 \times (UBRR + 1)}$.
- [x] **Interrupt Latency** ≈ $t_{sync} + t_{exec} + t_{vector} + t_{isr}$ cycles.
- [x] **Integration is bottom-up**: Bare-metal → Drivers → RTOS → Application.
- [x] **V-Model** maps each design phase to a corresponding test phase.
- [x] **Static Analysis Tools**: PC-lint, Coverity, Clang Static Analyzer.
- [x] **Dynamic Analysis Tools**: Valgrind, GDB, AddressSanitizer, Ozone.
- [x] **On-Target Testing Frameworks**: Unity, CppUTest, Google Test (ported).
- [x] **Embedded code density** is higher than application code due to resource constraints.
- [x] **JTAG TAP Controller** has **16 states** (Test-Logic-Reset is the entry state).
- [x] **UART baud error tolerance** is typically **±2%** for proper communication.
- [x] **Embedded build outputs**: ELF (debug), HEX (Intel), BIN (raw binary).
- [x] **Real-time trace** is supported by **ETM (Embedded Trace Macrocell)** on ARM cores.
- [x] **Test coverage metric** $C = \frac{T_{executed}}{T_{total}} \times 100\,\%$.

---

> **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — INTEGRATION AND TESTING OF EMBEDDED HARDWARE AND FIRMWARE**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech OECIT726 Examination Standards*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT726 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
