<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 : Boards Bring Up

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Board Bring-Up** is the systematic, phase-wise engineering process of taking a freshly manufactured Printed Circuit Board (PCB) from a state of electrical dormancy to a fully functional, firmware-loaded, and validated embedded system that successfully executes its intended application. It encompasses the very first power-on, minimal hardware verification, bootloader installation, peripheral initialization, and root-cause debugging of silicon-level, schematic-level, and layout-level defects.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, *Boards Bring Up* is classified as a **post-manufacturing integration activity** that bridges the gap between **hardware design closure** and **firmware/application development**. The activity is governed by industry standards such as the IEEE 1149.1 (JTAG) boundary-scan architecture and vendor-specific reference manuals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Board Bring-Up is treated as a critical cross-disciplinary activity linking **digital electronics**, **microcontroller architecture**, **debugging interfaces**, and **system-level validation**. Questions frequently appear in the ESE (End Semester Evaluation) under the "Hardware-Firmware Co-Design" thematic cluster.

### 1.2 The Three Canonical Phases of Bring-Up

| Phase | Name | Primary Objective | Typical Tool |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| **Phase 1** | Pre-Power Validation | Verify assembly, shorts, opens, and component orientation | Visual inspection, LCR meter, continuity tester |
| **Phase 2** | Minimal Power-Up | Confirm voltage rails, current draw, and clock integrity | Oscilloscope, DMM, Current Probe, Logic Analyzer |
| **Phase 3** | Firmware Bring-Up | Load bootloader, validate memory, exercise peripherals | JTAG/SWD Debugger, Logic Analyzer, Oscilloscope |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Newborn ICU" Metaphor

Imagine a brand-new hospital has just been built, but no patient has been admitted yet. Before the first patient arrives, the hospital must pass a series of checks:

1. **The electricity must be switched on safely** (no short circuits, all emergency lights working) — this is the *Pre-Power Validation*.
2. **The life-support machines must hum at the correct frequency** (ventilator rhythm, heart-monitor beep) — this is the *Minimal Power-Up & Clock Validation*.
3. **The first patient is admitted, connected to monitors, and doctors verify each organ is responding** — this is the *Firmware Bring-Up & Peripheral Validation*.

In the same way, an embedded board goes from "cold metal and silicon" to a "living, talking computing system" through a methodical, step-by-step bring-up procedure.

### 1.4 Key Terminology Seed List

> [!NOTE]
> These terms are **mandatory vocabulary** for any KTU answer on this topic.

- **DUT** — Device Under Test (the board being brought up).
- **JTAG** — Joint Test Action Group; IEEE 1149.1 standard 4-wire (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO) debug/test interface.
- **SWD** — Serial Wire Debug; ARM's 2-pin alternative to JTAG.
- **POR** — Power-On Reset; the cold-start reset signal asserted by the hardware.
- **BootROM / Bootloader** — Mask-ROM code in the SoC that runs first to load the user application.
- **BOM** — Bill of Materials; the master list of components on the PCB.
- **DNP** — Do Not Populate; components intentionally left un-soldered.
- **ERC / DRC** — Electrical Rule Check / Design Rule Check (pre-silicon validation tools).
- **BGA** — Ball Grid Array; a high-density package where hidden solder joints make bring-up harder.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Stage Bring-Up Flow (Industry Standard)

A well-engineered bring-up is executed in **five strict, gated stages**. No engineer is permitted to proceed to the next stage until the previous one is signed-off.

### Stage 1 — Pre-Power Visual & Electrical Inspection
- **Why:** Manufacturing defects (solder bridges, missing components, reversed polarity of electrolytic capacitors) account for ~60% of first-article failures.
- **How:**
  - Cross-verify every component on the board against the **BOM**.
  - Use a **multimeter in continuity mode** to check for shorts between power rails and ground.
  - Inspect BGA packages with **X-ray imaging** (solder joints are invisible optically).
  - Verify all **DNP** pads are correctly unpopulated.

### Stage 2 — First Power-Up (The "Smoke Test")
- **Why:** To confirm the board does not draw excessive current (a sign of a dead short) and that all voltage rails come up to their nominal values.
- **How:**
  - Power the board through a **current-limited bench supply** (start with a limit of ~10%–20% of the expected nominal current).
  - Monitor the **inrush current** waveform on an oscilloscope.
  - Gradually increase the current limit if the supply remains in **Constant Voltage (CV) mode**.
  - Verify each rail using a **rail-sequence timing diagram** (e.g., 1.8 V → 3.3 V → 5.0 V with proper power-good signals).

### Stage 3 — Clock & Reset Verification
- **Why:** The CPU cannot execute a single instruction without a stable clock and a clean reset release.
- **How:**
  - Probe the **crystal oscillator** output with an oscilloscope or frequency counter.
  - Verify the **POR (Power-On Reset)** signal de-asserts only after V_core stabilizes (typically a few milliseconds).
  - Confirm the **JTAG TCK line** toggles correctly when the debugger is attached.

### Stage 4 — Debugger Attachment & Memory Test
- **Why:** This is the moment the board first "speaks" to the outside world. A successful JTAG/SWD attach proves that the SoC is alive.
- **How:**
  - Connect a **JTAG probe** (e.g., J-Link, ST-Link, or Lauterbach TRACE32).
  - Run a vendor-supplied **IDCODE read** — if the 32-bit IDCODE is returned correctly, the scan chain is intact.
  - Halt the core, write a known pattern (e.g., `0xAA55AA55`) to **SRAM**, read it back, and compare bit-for-bit.

### Stage 5 — Peripheral Enumeration & Application Boot
- **Why:** The board must run the final application stack.
- **How:**
  - Flash the **BootROM-aware bootloader** (e.g., U-Boot, MCUBoot) into internal flash.
  - Bring up peripherals one block at a time: **UART → SPI → I2C → Ethernet → USB**.
  - Use a **peripheral matrix** (a checklist) to log each block's pass/fail status.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Description | Typical Value / Unit |
|:--:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $I_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} I_{rail_i}$ | Sum of currents on all voltage rails (Kirchhoff's Current Law applied to the power tree) | **Amperes (A)** |
| 2 | $V_{ripple} = V_{max} - V_{min}$ | Peak-to-peak noise on a DC rail, measured at the load capacitor | **mV** |
| 3 | $f_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk}}$ | Frequency of the master oscillator, where $T_{clk}$ is the measured period | **Hz / MHz** |
| 4 | $t_{POR} \approx C_{reset} \cdot R_{reset} \cdot \ln(1 + \dfrac{V_{threshold}}{V_{initial}})$ | RC time-constant that delays the POR de-assertion | **ms** |
| 5 | $P_{dissipated} = V_{core} \cdot I_{core}$ | Power consumed by the SoC core, used to size heat-sinks | **Watts (W)** |
| 6 | $JTAG_{chain} = \sum_{k=1}^{m} TAP_{k}$ | Total number of Test Access Ports in the boundary-scan chain | **Integer count** |
| 7 | $\eta_{bringup} = \dfrac{N_{passed}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | First-article yield metric for bring-up success | **Percentage (%)** |
| 8 | $V_{nom} \pm \Delta V_{tol}$ | Acceptable voltage tolerance for a given rail (e.g., $3.3\,\text{V} \pm 5\%$) | **Volts (V)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When answering numerical questions on power-tree validation, always state the **nominal voltage**, the **tolerance band**, and the **measured value** explicitly. Marks are awarded for unit consistency.

## 2.3 The JTAG / SWD Tap State Machine — A Conceptual Map

The JTAG controller traverses a **16-state finite state machine** (TAP controller). The five stable states that a bring-up engineer must memorize are:

1. **Test-Logic-Reset** — the default state after power-up; all on-chip logic is disabled.
2. **Run-Test/Idle** — the controller is idle and waits for instructions.
3. **Shift-DR** — data is shifted into the data register on TCK's rising edge.
4. **Shift-IR** — instructions are shifted into the instruction register.
5. **Update-DR / Update-IR** — the new data/instruction is latched out to the system logic.

The instruction register is normally at least **2 bits wide**, and the most common instruction issued at bring-up is **BYPASS** (all 1s) or **IDCODE** (used to read the device's unique 32-bit identifier).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Current-Limited First Power-Up Decision

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
A custom KTU board has the following power tree. Using a current-limited bench supply, determine the order in which the rails should be brought up, and calculate the **maximum allowable inrush current** if the bench supply is set to **110% of the steady-state expected draw**.

| Rail | Nominal Voltage | Expected Steady-State Current |
|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| V_CORE | 1.2 V | 250 mA |
| V_DDR | 1.8 V | 400 mA |
| V_IO_3V3 | 3.3 V | 800 mA |
| V_IO_5V0 | 5.0 V | 600 mA |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the power-rail sequencing requirement.**

Most modern SoCs mandate a strict power-up sequence to prevent **latch-up** of I/O cells. A typical sequence for an ARM-based SoC is: V_CORE → V_DDR → V_IO_3V3 → V_IO_5V0.

**Step 2 — Compute the total steady-state current using Kirchhoff's Current Law.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{total} &= I_{V_{CORE}} + I_{V_{DDR}} + I_{V_{IO\_3V3}} + I_{V_{IO\_5V0}} \\
I_{total} &= 250\,\text{mA} + 400\,\text{mA} + 800\,\text{mA} + 600\,\text{mA} \\
I_{total} &= 2050\,\text{mA} = 2.05\,\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the bench-supply current limit (110% of steady-state).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{limit} &= 1.10 \times I_{total} \\
I_{limit} &= 1.10 \times 2.05\,\text{A} \\
I_{limit} &= 2.255\,\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the current limit in stages.**

- Set the bench supply voltage to **0 V**, current limit to **2.255 A**.
- Slowly raise the voltage. Observe the inrush spike on the oscilloscope.
- If the supply enters **Constant Current (CC) mode** before reaching nominal voltage, it indicates a short circuit — power down immediately.
- If the supply stays in **Constant Voltage (CV) mode**, the rail is healthy.

**Step 5 — Final answer and engineering interpretation.**

> The board's total expected draw is **2.05 A**, and the safe bench-supply limit is **2.255 A**. The recommended power-up order is **V_CORE → V_DDR → V_IO_3V3 → V_IO_5V0**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — POR Delay Computation

**Problem Statement:**
A KTU-designed board uses an **RC-based reset circuit** with $R_{reset} = 47\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C_{reset} = 1\,\mu\text{F}$. The SoC's reset de-assertion threshold is $V_{threshold} = 0.8\,\text{V}$ (out of a $V_{final} = 3.3\,\text{V}$ rail). Compute the time taken for the POR signal to de-assert.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**

The voltage across a charging capacitor in an RC network is given by:

$$
V_{C}(t) = V_{final} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t / \tau}\right)
$$

where $\tau = R \cdot C$ is the time constant.

**Step 2 — Compute the time constant $\tau$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau &= R_{reset} \times C_{reset} \\
\tau &= 47 \times 10^{3}\,\Omega \times 1 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{F} \\
\tau &= 47 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s} \\
\tau &= 47\,\text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Invert the equation to solve for $t$ when $V_{C}(t) = V_{threshold}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{threshold} &= V_{final} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t / \tau}\right) \\
\dfrac{V_{threshold}}{V_{final}} &= 1 - e^{-t / \tau} \\
e^{-t / \tau} &= 1 - \dfrac{V_{threshold}}{V_{final}} \\
-\dfrac{t}{\tau} &= \ln\!\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{threshold}}{V_{final}}\right) \\
t &= -\tau \cdot \ln\!\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{threshold}}{V_{final}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= -47\,\text{ms} \cdot \ln\!\left(1 - \dfrac{0.8}{3.3}\right) \\
t &= -47\,\text{ms} \cdot \ln(1 - 0.2424) \\
t &= -47\,\text{ms} \cdot \ln(0.7576) \\
t &= -47\,\text{ms} \cdot (-0.2776) \\
t &\approx 13.05\,\text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final answer.**

> The POR signal de-asserts approximately **13.05 ms** after the 3.3 V rail stabilizes. This delay is critical because the SoC requires the clock to be stable *before* reset is released.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Bring-Up Failures vs. Root Cause

| # | Observed Symptom | Probable Root Cause | Diagnostic Tool | Remediation |
|:--:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Bench supply enters CC mode at first power-up | Power-rail short circuit (solder bridge, reversed diode) | LCR meter, thermal camera | Re-touch solder joints; replace damaged component |
| 2 | No clock output on the crystal pin | Incorrect load capacitor value; crystal damaged | Oscilloscope (10x probe) | Replace crystal / adjust load caps |
| 3 | JTAG IDCODE reads as `0xFFFFFFFF` | Open circuit in TDI/TDO chain; shift register broken | Continuity tester | Re-flow JTAG header pins |
| 4 | CPU halts immediately after reset | Watchdog not serviced in bootloader; clock misconfiguration | SWD trace, debugger halt | Patch bootloader watchdog logic |
| 5 | Flash erase/program fails | Insufficient V_CORE margin during programming | Oscilloscope on V_CORE | Boost core voltage by 50–100 mV within SoC limits |
| 6 | Peripheral returns all 1s on SPI read | MISO pin floating; pin-mux not configured | Logic analyzer | Configure GPIO alternate-function in bootloader |
| 7 | Board resets randomly under load | Decoupling capacitor missing near SoC | Impedance analyzer | Add 100 nF + 10 µF decoupling caps |

flowchart TD

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
Define **Board Bring-Up**. List any **three** key objectives of a structured bring-up procedure.

**Model Answer:**

**Board Bring-Up** is the systematic process of taking a freshly assembled PCB from an electrically inert state to a fully functional embedded system that runs validated firmware. It is a gated engineering workflow that includes pre-power inspection, first power-up, clock/reset validation, debugger attach, and peripheral enumeration.

The three key objectives are:

1. **Detect manufacturing defects** (solder bridges, missing components, polarity errors) before expensive firmware effort is wasted.
2. **Validate the hardware-firmware interface** by confirming that the SoC, memory, and peripherals behave as the schematic promises.
3. **Establish a reproducible debug baseline** so that future firmware bugs can be unambiguously separated from hardware faults.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
What is the **purpose of the JTAG IDCODE register** in the context of board bring-up? Mention the typical bit-width of this register.

**Model Answer:**

The **JTAG IDCODE** is a mandatory 32-bit read-only register defined by the IEEE 1149.1 standard. During bring-up, the engineer issues an `IDCODE` instruction (binary `0001`) to the TAP controller. The expected return value is a 32-bit pattern consisting of:

- A **1-bit manufacturer ID** boundary
- An **11-bit part number**
- A **16-bit device version**

The primary purpose of reading the IDCODE during bring-up is to **prove that the JTAG scan chain is electrically intact and that the silicon is responding**. A return value of all ones (`0xFFFFFFFF`) almost always indicates an open circuit in the TDI or TDO line, while a return value of all zeros indicates a stuck-at fault on the TCK line.

> [!NOTE]
> The IDCODE register is **32 bits wide** in 100% of IEEE 1149.1 compliant devices.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **five canonical stages** of a professional board bring-up workflow. For each stage, identify the **instrumentation used** and the **decision criterion** that gates the transition to the next stage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **JTAG architecture** as defined by IEEE 1149.1. Discuss the role of the **TAP controller**, the **instruction register**, and the **boundary-scan cells**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A (a)

The five canonical stages of a structured board bring-up are:

1. **Pre-Power Visual and Electrical Inspection**
   - *Instrumentation:* Magnifying lens, optical microscope, LCR meter, X-ray imaging (for BGA).
   - *Decision criterion:* Every component on the BOM must be physically present, correctly oriented, and free of solder bridges. The resistance between every power rail and ground must be **greater than a pre-defined threshold (typically > 100 kΩ for low-power boards)**.

2. **First Power-Up (The Smoke Test)**
   - *Instrumentation:* Current-limited bench DC supply, digital multimeter, oscilloscope.
   - *Decision criterion:* The supply must remain in **Constant Voltage (CV) mode** at the nominal rail voltage. The measured inrush current must be **less than 1.1× the steady-state expected draw**. All rails must reach their nominal values within the SoC's specified power-sequencing window.

3. **Clock and Reset Verification**
   - *Instrumentation:* Oscilloscope with a 10x passive probe, frequency counter.
   - *Decision criterion:* The crystal oscillator must produce a stable sinusoidal or square-wave output at the nominal frequency with **< 50 ppm frequency error**. The POR signal must de-assert **only after** the V_CORE rail has stabilized.

4. **Debugger Attachment and SRAM Built-In Self-Test**
   - *Instrumentation:* JTAG/SWD probe (e.g., J-Link), vendor IDE.
   - *Decision criterion:* A successful **IDCODE read** confirms scan-chain integrity. An **SRAM read-write-comparison** of a known pattern (e.g., `0xAA55AA55` and `0x55AA55AA`) must return bit-exact results.

5. **Bootloader Flash and Peripheral Enumeration**
   - *Instrumentation:* Logic analyzer, oscilloscope, peripheral loopback fixtures.
   - *Decision criterion:* The bootloader must successfully load the application from flash into SRAM. Each peripheral (UART, SPI, I2C, USB) must respond to a known test command. A **peripheral bring-up matrix** is maintained with a Pass/Fail column.

---

### Model Solution for Question A (b)

**JTAG Architecture (IEEE 1149.1):**

The JTAG standard provides a 4-wire serial interface to embedded devices. The four mandatory signals are:

- **TCK** (Test Clock) — drives the state machine and shifts data on its rising/falling edges.
- **TMS** (Test Mode Select) — sampled on TCK's rising edge to navigate the TAP state machine.
- **TDI** (Test Data In) — serial data input to the device.
- **TDO** (Test Data Out) — serial data output from the device.

An optional fifth pin **TRST** (Test Reset) provides an asynchronous hardware reset of the TAP controller.

**TAP Controller:**
The Test Access Port (TAP) controller is a 16-state finite state machine that governs whether the boundary-scan chain is in reset, idle, data-capture, data-shift, or data-update mode. It is the *brain* of the JTAG interface and is triggered by the TCK and TMS signals.

**Instruction Register (IR):**
The IR is a shift register that accepts one of several standardized instructions:
- `BYPASS` (all 1s) — routes TDI directly to TDO, used when chaining multiple devices.
- `IDCODE` (binary 0001) — shifts out the 32-bit device identifier.
- `SAMPLE` / `PRELOAD` — captures or preloads boundary-scan cell contents.
- `EXTEST` — drives test patterns to external pins.

**Boundary-Scan Cells:**
Every I/O pin of the SoC has a dedicated **boundary-scan cell** placed between the core logic and the physical pin. These cells can **capture** the logic level present at the pin or **drive** a known pattern onto the pin. By chaining all cells, the JTAG interface can effectively "see" the connectivity of every net on the PCB without a physical probe — a feature known as **interconnect testing**.

A block diagram of the architecture is:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TDI[TDI Pin] --> IR[Instruction Register]
    TDI --> DR[Data Register Bank]
    IR --> TAP[TAP Controller 16 FSM]
    TAP --> IR
    TAP --> DR
    DR --> BSC[Boundary Scan Cells on every IO]
    DR --> BYP[Bypass Register]
    DR --> IDC[IDCODE Register]
    BSC --> TDO[TDO Pin]
    BYP --> TDO
    IDC --> TDO
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students confuse **TDI** and **TDO** directions. Always remember: **TDI = data into the chip; TDO = data out of the chip**. The direction of shifting is from TDI → TDO.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of a **power-rail sequencing** requirement in modern SoCs. With a numerical example, compute the **safe bench-supply current limit** for a board with three voltage rails drawing 350 mA, 720 mA, and 1.2 A respectively, assuming a **120% safety factor**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of the **BootROM** and **Bootloader** in the firmware bring-up stage. Differentiate between a **mask-ROM bootloader** and a **flash-resident bootloader**, and explain why the first stage must always reside in mask-ROM. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B (a)

**Power-Rail Sequencing — Concept:**

Modern SoCs integrate multiple voltage domains — for example, a 1.2 V core rail, a 1.8 V DDR rail, and a 3.3 V I/O rail. If these rails are brought up in the wrong order, **latch-up** can occur in the I/O ring transistors. Latch-up is a destructive short-circuit condition that can permanently damage the SoC. Therefore, vendors publish a **Power Supply Sequencing Specification** that mandates a strict order (e.g., V_CORE first, V_DDR second, V_IO last) and a maximum allowed skew between rails (typically 50–100 ms).

**Numerical Computation:**

Given: $I_1 = 350\,\text{mA}$, $I_2 = 720\,\text{mA}$, $I_3 = 1.2\,\text{A}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{total} &= I_1 + I_2 + I_3 \\
I_{total} &= 0.350\,\text{A} + 0.720\,\text{A} + 1.200\,\text{A} \\
I_{total} &= 2.270\,\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying the 120% safety factor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{limit} &= 1.20 \times I_{total} \\
I_{limit} &= 1.20 \times 2.270\,\text{A} \\
I_{limit} &= 2.724\,\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** The safe bench-supply current limit is **2.724 A**, and the rails should be brought up in the order specified by the SoC datasheet (typically V_CORE → V_DDR → V_IO).

---

### Model Solution for Question B (b)

**Role of BootROM and Bootloader:**

After the SoC exits reset, the very first code that runs is stored in a small **mask-ROM** inside the silicon. This is called the **BootROM**. The BootROM's responsibilities are:

1. Initialize the bare minimum hardware (PLLs, internal SRAM, flash controller).
2. Locate a valid bootloader image in external storage (flash, SD card, UART).
3. Copy the bootloader into internal SRAM and transfer control to it.

**Mask-ROM vs. Flash-Resident Bootloader:**

| Feature | Mask-ROM Bootloader | Flash-Resident Bootloader |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Storage | Etched into silicon at fabrication | Stored in external flash, updateable |
| Mutability | Immutable (cannot be changed) | Field-updateable over JTAG/UART |
| Size | Typically < 16 KB | Can be 256 KB or larger |
| Role | First-stage initializer, secure root-of-trust | Second-stage, loads OS or application |
| Failure recovery | Cannot be recovered if buggy | Can be re-flashed via JTAG |

**Why the First Stage Must Be in Mask-ROM:**

The first stage must be **immutable** because it serves as the **root of trust** for the entire secure-boot chain. If the first-stage code were stored in flash, an attacker could simply overwrite it with malicious code at the factory. By placing the first stage in mask-ROM, the silicon vendor guarantees that the chip *always* boots into a known, trusted code path. Only after this trusted path verifies the cryptographic signature of the second-stage bootloader does the chip permit the flash-resident code to execute.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** When asked about bootloaders, students often describe **U-Boot** or **MCUBoot** without first explaining the **BootROM** layer above them. Marks are awarded for showing the **complete two-stage hierarchy**: BootROM (silicon) → Bootloader (flash) → Application (flash). A one-layer answer is considered incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Boards Bring Up"**

- **Definition** — Board bring-up is the gated process of taking a new PCB from inert to fully operational.
- **Five Stages** — Inspection → Power-Up → Clock/Reset → Debugger Attach → Peripheral Enumeration.
- **First Power-Up Rule** — Always use a current-limited supply; start at 110–120% of expected steady-state draw.
- **JTAG Signals** — TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO (plus optional TRST).
- **JTAP TAP States** — 16 states total; the five stable ones are Test-Logic-Reset, Run-Test/Idle, Shift-DR, Shift-IR, Update-DR/IR.
- **IDCODE** — 32-bit read-only register; the bring-up engineer's "first handshake" with silicon.
- **Power Sequencing** — Always follow the SoC datasheet's order; never power I/O before core.
- **POR Equation** — $t = -\tau \cdot \ln\!\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{threshold}}{V_{final}}\right)$.
- **Kirchhoff's Law Applied to Power Tree** — $I_{total} = \sum I_{rail_i}$.
- **BootROM vs Bootloader** — BootROM is immutable silicon; bootloader is field-updateable flash.
- **BIST Pattern** — Classic memory test pattern is `0xAA55AA55` followed by `0x55AA55AA`.
- **Boundary-Scan** — Every I/O pin has a JTAG cell that can capture or drive the pin logic.
- **First-Article Yield (FAY)** — Metric $\eta_{bringup} = \dfrac{N_{passed}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$.
- **Common Failure Signatures** — All-1s IDCODE (open chain), all-0s IDCODE (stuck TCK), bench in CC mode (short), no clock (crystal fault).
- **BGA Inspection** — Requires X-ray; visual inspection is insufficient.
- **DNP Concept** — "Do Not Populate" pads must be verified un-soldered.

> **End of Module 4 — Boards Bring Up Notes**
> **Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme OECIT726 — Embedded Systems**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Embedded System Development Environment: The Integrated Development Environment (IDE)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECIT726 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** The Embedded System Development Environment: The Integrated Development Environment (IDE) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Integrated Development Environment (IDE):** An *Integrated Development Environment (IDE)* is a unified software application that consolidates the essential tools required for embedded system firmware development — namely the **source code editor**, **compiler/cross-compiler**, **assembler**, **linker**, **loader**, **debugger**, and **simulator/emulator** — into a single graphical user interface (GUI) front-end, thereby providing a seamless, end-to-end workflow for editing, building, testing, and debugging target-specific embedded software. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the IDE is positioned as the **central workstation software** that bridges the **host machine** (typically a PC running Windows/Linux) with the **target embedded hardware** (microcontroller/Microprocessor development board). It is the *envelope* in which the entire cross-development toolchain operates. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The IDE is a Complete Garment Factory" Imagine you are a tailor who has been asked to stitch a custom uniform (the *firmware*) for a specific customer (the *embedded target board*). Instead of walking between separate shops for fabric cutting, stitching, embroidery, ironing, and quality checking, you walk into **one building** that has all the machines, threads, patterns, and inspection tables arranged in a coordinated production line. That single building is your **IDE**. - The **text editor** in the IDE = the cutting table (where the design/pattern is drafted). - The **compiler + assembler** = the stitching machines (raw material transformed into usable pieces). - The **linker** = the assembly unit (joining all pieces into the final garment). - The **debugger** = the quality inspection desk (where faults are located and fixed). - The **flash programmer** = the packaging and dispatch unit (uploading the finished firmware to the target). The **integration** is the key word — every tool communicates with the next inside the IDE shell without the developer manually invoking them from the command line. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The IDE for embedded systems is *not* a generic programmer's IDE (like those used for desktop applications). It MUST contain a **cross-toolchain** because the host CPU architecture (e.g., x86) differs from the target CPU architecture (e.g., ARM Cortex-M, AVR, PIC, 8051). ### 1.3 Why a Specialised IDE is Needed for Embedded Systems | Generic Software IDE (e.g., for Desktop Apps) | Embedded Systems IDE | |-----------------------------------------------|----------------------| | Compiles code for the **same** CPU on which the IDE runs | **Cross-compiles** for a different target CPU | | Runs the program on the host itself | Loads and runs the program on a remote **target board** | | Limited hardware interaction | Direct control of **JTAG, SWD, OCD, ISP** interfaces | | Output: Windows/Linux executable (.exe / ELF for host) | Output: **Hex/Binary file** flashed into microcontroller memory | | Uses host resources (RAM, Disk) | Optimised for **constrained memory** (KB-level) | > [!TIP] > **Examiner Memory Hook:** When a KTU question asks *"Why can't we use a normal C compiler for embedded systems?"*, the answer is the **cross-compilation requirement** + **resource constraints** + **hardware-specific debugging**. ### 1.4 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics - **Clock Frequency Range of typical targets:** **8 MHz – 200 MHz** (MCUs to high-end MPUs). - **On-chip Flash size:** **32 KB – 2 MB** (common in modern Cortex-M MCUs). - **On-chip RAM size:** **4 KB – 256 KB**. - **JTAG clock (TCK):** typically up to **10–50 MHz** during boundary scan. - **IDE file size on disk:** **500 MB – 5 GB** (depending on toolchain, e.g., Keil MDK, IAR EWARM, STM32CubeIDE, MPLAB X, Code Composer Studio). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of an Embedded IDE — The Five Pillars Every KTU-recommended IDE (such as **Keil µVision**, **Eclipse-based STM32CubeIDE**, **MPLAB X**, **Code Composer Studio**, **Arduino IDE**, or **PlatformIO**) is built on five tightly-integrated functional pillars: #### Pillar 1 — The Source Code Editor - Provides **syntax highlighting**, **auto-indentation**, **code completion**, and **project navigation**. - Supports C, C++, and assembly (mandatory for ISR routines and startup code). - Integrates with **version control plugins** (Git, SVN) for team development. #### Pillar 2 — The Build System (Compiler + Assembler + Linker) - **Preprocessor** — handles `#include`, `#define`, conditional compilation. - **Cross-Compiler** (e.g., `arm-none-eabi-gcc`) — translates C/C++ into target-specific assembly. - **Cross-Assembler** (e.g., `arm-none-eabi-as`) — converts assembly mnemonics into object code. - **Cross-Linker / Locator** (e.g., `arm-none-eabi-ld`) — resolves symbols and produces the final ELF; for MCUs it generates the *absolute* address map (because memory is fixed). - **Locator/Mapper** — assigns physical addresses to sections (`.text` → Flash, `.data`/`.bss` → RAM). - **Objcopy / Hex generator** — converts ELF into Intel HEX, Motorola S-record, or raw binary for the programmer. #### Pillar 3 — The Debugger (Hardware-Software Bridge) - **GDB-based** (in GCC toolchains) or **proprietary** (Keil's µVision Debugger, IAR C-SPY). - Communicates with the target via **JTAG (4-wire)**, **SWD (2-wire)**, **OCD**, or **BDM**. - Provides **breakpoints, watchpoints, single-stepping, register inspection, memory dumps**, and **live expression evaluation**. #### Pillar 4 — The Simulator / Emulator - **Simulator** — software-only model of the target CPU (instruction-accurate, no peripherals). - **In-Circuit Emulator (ICE)** — replaces the target CPU with a probe that emulates its pins in real time (largely obsolete in modern MCUs). - **On-Chip Debug (OCD)** — uses debug logic built into the MCU (e.g., ARM CoreSight) — *the modern approach*. #### Pillar 5 — The Flash Programmer / Loader - Pushes the HEX/Binary file into the target's non-volatile memory. - May use **UART bootloader**, **SPI bootloader**, **USB DFU**, **JTAG/SWD programmer**, or **OEM-specific protocol (ST-Link, J-Link, PICKit, MSP-FET)**. ### 2.2 The Cross-Development Workflow — Step by Step 1. **Project Creation** — IDE generates a *project skeleton* with target-specific startup files, linker scripts, and device header files. 2. **Source Authoring** — Developer writes application code in C/C++/Assembly. 3. **Build Invocation** — IDE calls the toolchain (`make` or `cmake`-driven). 4. **Compilation** — Each `.c` file → `.o` (relocatable object file). 5. **Assembly** — Each `.s` file → `.o` (object file). 6. **Linking + Locating** — All `.o` files + libraries → `firmware.elf`. 7. **Hex Generation** — `elf2hex` or `objcopy` → `firmware.hex` / `.bin`. 8. **Download/Flash** — Programmer writes HEX to target Flash. 9. **Debug Session** — IDE attaches to target, downloads symbols, and begins interactive debugging. 10. **Profiling / Trace** — Optional: ETM/ITM trace for real-time analysis. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memory Budget Equation** — A frequently tested KTU concept relating target memory to program size: $$M_{utilised} \;=\; M_{text} + M_{rodata} + M_{data} + M_{bss}$$ Where: - $M_{text}$ — Size of code in **Flash**. - $M_{rodata}$ — Read-only data (constants) in **Flash**. - $M_{data}$ — Initialised global/static data (stored in Flash, copied to RAM at startup). - $M_{bss}$ — Uninitialised global/static data (zero-initialised in RAM). > **Flash Footprint:** $\;\;F_{footprint} \;=\; M_{text} + M_{rodata} + M_{data}$ > **Runtime RAM Footprint:** $\;\;R_{footprint} \;=\; M_{data} + M_{bss} + M_{stack} + M_{heap}$ > [!NOTE] > **Toolchain Translation Rule** — The IDE does NOT translate the source all at once. Each translation unit is independently compiled. Therefore, **header files (`.h`) are not compiled** — they are merely *included* by the preprocessor. | Component | Input | Output | Tool Example | |-----------|-------|--------|--------------| | Preprocessor | `.c` | `.i` (pure C) | `cpp` | | Compiler | `.i` / `.c` | `.s` (assembly) | `gcc -S` | | Assembler | `.s` | `.o` (object) | `as` | | Linker/Locator | `.o` + `.a` | `.elf` | `ld` | | Objcopy | `.elf` | `.hex` / `.bin` | `objcopy` | | Programmer | `.hex` | Flash memory | `st-flash`, `openocd` | | Debugger | `.elf` + target | Live debug | `gdb`, `J-Link` | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive ECU development** (Bosch, Continental) — uses **Vector DaVinci + Keil/IAR toolchains** integrated with AUTOSAR configuration. - **IoT firmware** — **ESP-IDF (Eclipse-based)** for ESP32, **STM32CubeIDE** for STM32. - **Industrial PLCs** — **Codesys** IDE with IEC 61131-3 languages. - **Consumer electronics** — proprietary IDEs for SoC vendors (e.g., Nordic nRF Connect, TI CCS, NXP MCUXpresso). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Memory Footprint of a Sample Firmware Consider a hypothetical embedded C program for an **ARM Cortex-M3** target with the following sections reported by the linker map file: > $M_{text} = 18{,}432 \; \text{bytes}$ > $M_{rodata} = 2{,}048 \; \text{bytes}$ > $M_{data} = 1{,}024 \; \text{bytes}$ > $M_{bss} = 512 \; \text{bytes}$ > $M_{stack} = 2{,}048 \; \text{bytes}$ (configured in startup file) > $M_{heap} = 0 \; \text{bytes}$ (no `malloc` used) **Step 1 — Compute the Flash footprint (non-volatile storage required):** $$F_{footprint} = M_{text} + M_{rodata} + M_{data}$$ Substituting the given values: $$F_{footprint} = 18432 + 2048 + 1024$$ $$F_{footprint} = 21504 \; \text{bytes} \;\approx\; 21.0 \; \text{KB}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the runtime RAM footprint (volatile memory required at execution):** $$R_{footprint} = M_{data} + M_{bss} + M_{stack} + M_{heap}$$ $$R_{footprint} = 1024 + 512 + 2048 + 0$$ $$R_{footprint} = 3584 \; \text{bytes} \;\approx\; 3.5 \; \text{KB}$$ **Step 3 — Total memory demand on the MCU:** $$M_{total} = F_{footprint} + R_{footprint}$$ $$M_{total} = 21504 + 3584 = 25088 \; \text{bytes} \approx 24.5 \; \text{KB}$$ > **Engineering Interpretation:** A 32 KB Flash / 8 KB RAM MCU (e.g., STM32F051) would comfortably fit this firmware with **~40% Flash headroom** and **~56% RAM headroom** for future expansion. ### 3.2 Derivation — Effective Baud Rate vs. Target Clock When an IDE flashes firmware via a **UART bootloader**, the host PC generates baud ticks based on its COM port. The actual time to upload $B$ bytes at baud rate $\beta$ (with **no parity, 1 stop bit, 8 data bits**) is: $$T_{upload} = \frac{B \times 10}{\beta} \; \text{seconds}$$ For $B = 21504$ bytes at $\beta = 115200$ bps: $$T_{upload} = \frac{21504 \times 10}{115200} = \frac{215040}{115200} \approx 1.866 \; \text{seconds}$$ > **Note:** Each byte transfers as 10 bits (1 start + 8 data + 1 stop) — this 10× factor is a *classic KTU exam trap*. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — IDE Tool Selection Decision Table | Requirement | Recommended IDE Class | Justification | |-------------|----------------------|---------------| | 8051-based academic project | Keil µVision / SDCC + Eclipse | Legacy, easy licensing for students | | ARM Cortex-M0/M3/M4 | STM32CubeIDE / Keil MDK / IAR EWARM | Native support, free ST-Link | | AVR (Arduino) | Arduino IDE / PlatformIO | Beginner-friendly abstraction | | PIC (16F/18F/dsPIC) | MPLAB X (Microchip) | Vendor-official, simulator included | | TI MSP430 / C2000 | Code Composer Studio (CCS) | Free licence for MSP430 | | ESP32 / ESP8266 | ESP-IDF (Eclipse) / Arduino IDE | Dual-core Xtensa LX6 / LX7 | | Multi-vendor / Zephyr RTOS | VS Code + CMake + Ninja + OpenOCD | Vendor-agnostic, professional | ### 3.4 Analytical Derivation — Build Time vs. Project Size The IDE build time $T_{build}$ (ignoring I/O) can be approximated as: $$T_{build} \;\approx\; N_{files} \cdot t_{compile} \;+\; t_{link} \;+\; t_{flash}$$ Where: - $N_{files}$ — number of translation units. - $t_{compile}$ — average compilation time per file (depends on host CPU, optimisation level `-O0` to `-O3`). - $t_{link}$ — linker overhead (typically 0.5–2 s). - $t_{flash}$ — programmer write time (depends on Flash size and interface speed). > **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Doubling the optimisation level (e.g., `-O0` → `-O2`) may **increase $t_{compile}$ by 2–3×** but reduce $M_{text}$ by 10–30%. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Embedded IDE The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how the five pillars of an IDE interact with the host machine, target board, and programmer probe. ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef pillar fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef external fill:#0d9488,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef artifact fill:#a16207,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef target fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px IDE["IDE GUI SHELL
Project Manager"]:::pillar EDIT["Source Code Editor
C / C++ / Assembly"]:::pillar BUILD["Build Manager
Make / CMake / Ninja"]:::pillar COMP["Cross Compiler
arm-none-eabi-gcc"]:::pillar ASM["Cross Assembler
arm-none-eabi-as"]:::pillar LINK["Linker and Locator
arm-none-eabi-ld"]:::pillar COPY["Objcopy Utility
elf to hex conversion"]:::pillar DBG["Debugger Front End
GDB or Proprietary"]:::pillar SIM["Simulator
Instruction Accurate"]:::pillar PROG["Flash Programmer
ST-Link / J-Link / PICKit"]:::pillar SRC["Application Source
main.c device.c isr.s"]:::artifact OBJ["Relocatable Objects
main.o device.o isr.o"]:::artifact ELF["Linked Executable
firmware.elf"]:::artifact HEX["Flash Image
firmware.hex"]:::artifact HOST["Host Machine
x86 PC Windows or Linux"]:::external PROBE["Debug Probe
JTAG or SWD"]:::external TARGET["Target MCU Board
ARM Cortex-M / 8051 / PIC / AVR"]:::target FLASH["On Chip Flash
Non Volatile Memory"]:::target RAM["On Chip SRAM
Volatile Memory"]:::target IDE --> EDIT IDE --> BUILD IDE --> DBG IDE --> SIM IDE --> PROG EDIT --> SRC BUILD --> COMP BUILD --> ASM BUILD --> LINK BUILD --> COPY COMP --> OBJ ASM --> OBJ LINK --> ELF COPY --> HEX SRC --> COMP SRC --> ASM DBG <--> PROBE PROG <--> PROBE PROBE <--> TARGET PROG --> HEX TARGET --> FLASH TARGET --> RAM HOST --> IDE ``` ### 4.2 End-to-End Embedded Development Flow (Cross-Development) ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef step fill:#1d4ed8,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef phase fill:#15803d,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px S1["STEP 1
Author Source Code
on Host PC"]:::step S2["STEP 2
Cross Compile and Assemble
on Host PC"]:::step S3["STEP 3
Link and Locate
Generate ELF on Host"]:::step S4["STEP 4
Convert ELF to HEX
on Host PC"]:::step S5["STEP 5
Flash HEX to Target
via Programmer"]:::step S6["STEP 6
Run and Debug
on Target via Probe"]:::step S7["STEP 7
Iterate or Release
Final Firmware"]:::step P1["PHASE A
HOST SIDE ACTIVITIES"]:::phase P2["PHASE B
TRANSPORT"]:::phase P3["PHASE C
TARGET SIDE ACTIVITIES"]:::phase S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 S1 -.- P1 S2 -.- P1 S3 -.- P1 S4 -.- P1 S5 -.- P2 S6 -.- P3 S7 -.- P3 ``` ### 4.3 IDE-Project Internal Structure Map ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef folder fill:#334155,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef file fill:#b45309,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef config fill:#7e22ce,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px PRJ["Project Root Directory"]:::folder SRC["src folder
main.c app.c drivers.c"]:::folder INC["include folder
device.h app.h"]:::folder START["startup folder
startup_device.s"]:::folder LINK["linker folder
device_flash.ld"]:::folder DBG["debug folder
device.svd"]:::folder BUILD["build output folder
firmware.elf firmware.hex firmware.map"]:::folder IDE_CFG["IDE Workspace File
project.uvprojx or .cproject"]:::config MAKE["Build Script
Makefile or CMakeLists.txt"]:::config PRJ --> SRC PRJ --> INC PRJ --> START PRJ --> LINK PRJ --> DBG PRJ --> BUILD PRJ --> IDE_CFG PRJ --> MAKE ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Visual Memory Trick:** Remember the **6D Pipeline** — *Draft → Decode (compile) → Distill (assemble) → Dispatch (link) → Download (flash) → Debug (verify)*. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define an Integrated Development Environment (IDE) in the context of embedded systems. List any four tools that must be integrated into it.* **Model Answer:** An Integrated Development Environment (IDE) is a unified software application that provides all the necessary tools — such as a source code editor, cross-compiler, assembler, linker, debugger, and flash programmer — within a single graphical user interface for developing, building, and debugging embedded firmware targeted at a specific microcontroller or microprocessor. The four essential tools integrated into an embedded IDE are: 1. **Source Code Editor** (with syntax highlighting and code completion). 2. **Cross-Compiler / Assembler** (e.g., `arm-none-eabi-gcc`). 3. **Linker / Locator** (resolves symbols and assigns physical memory addresses). 4. **Debugger** (interacts with the target via JTAG/SWD for breakpoints and inspection). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is cross-compilation? Why is it mandatory in embedded system development?* **Model Answer:** **Cross-compilation** is the process of compiling source code on a *host* machine (e.g., an x86 PC) using a compiler whose output is an executable intended to run on a *different* target processor (e.g., ARM Cortex-M). It is **mandatory in embedded systems** because: 1. The target microcontroller is **resource-constrained** (KBs of RAM) and cannot host a full compiler. 2. The target CPU **architecture differs** from the host (e.g., x86 host → ARM target). 3. The target has **no operating system** to run a traditional compiler toolchain. 4. It allows **faster, optimised builds** on the powerful host machine and only downloads the final HEX/Binary to the target. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an Integrated Development Environment (IDE) used for embedded system design. List the functions of **each** major block. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Describe the **cross-development workflow** in detail. Explain how the IDE bridges the host machine and the target embedded board. Differentiate between a **simulator** and an **emulator**. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Architecture of an Embedded IDE — 7 Marks** The IDE architecture consists of the following blocks (mapped 1:1 to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1): 1. **Project Manager** (1 mark) — Maintains the project hierarchy, source files, build configuration, target settings, and toolchain selection. 2. **Source Code Editor** (1 mark) — Provides syntax highlighting, auto-completion, code folding, and bracket matching for C, C++, and Assembly. 3. **Build Manager** (1 mark) — Invokes the toolchain in correct sequence: preprocessor → compiler → assembler → linker → objcopy. 4. **Cross Compiler & Assembler** (1 mark) — Translates C/C++ and assembly source into target-specific relocatable object code. 5. **Linker & Locator** (1 mark) — Combines all objects, resolves external symbols, assigns absolute physical addresses per the linker script. 6. **Debugger & Simulator** (1 mark) — Provides breakpoints, watchpoints, register/memory inspection via JTAG/SWD; the simulator runs the ELF on the host. 7. **Flash Programmer** (1 mark) — Converts ELF to HEX/BIN and writes it to the target's non-volatile memory via probe or bootloader. --- **(b) Cross-Development Workflow — 7 Marks** **Workflow Steps (5 marks):** 1. *Authoring* — The developer writes firmware in C/Assembly using the IDE editor on the host PC. 2. *Cross-Compilation* — The IDE invokes the cross-compiler (e.g., `arm-none-eabi-gcc`) which emits assembly for the target CPU. 3. *Cross-Assembly* — Assembly `.s` files are assembled into relocatable object `.o` files. 4. *Linking & Locating* — The linker joins all `.o` files and libraries, generating a single `firmware.elf` with absolute addresses defined by the **linker script** (e.g., `STM32F407.ld`). 5. *Image Generation* — `objcopy` converts `.elf` → `.hex` / `.bin`. 6. *Download/Flash* — The IDE programmer writes the HEX image to the target Flash via JTAG, SWD, or a bootloader (UART/USB). 7. *Debug* — The IDE debugger attaches to the target through the probe, downloads the ELF symbols, and enables live breakpoints and memory inspection. **Simulator vs Emulator (2 marks):** | Feature | Simulator | Emulator | |---------|-----------|----------| | Definition | Software model of the CPU on the host | Hardware probe that physically replaces the CPU | | Speed | Slower (instruction-accurate) | Real-time | | Peripheral accuracy | Limited | Full pin-level accuracy | | Cost | Free (bundled with IDE) | Expensive external hardware | | Modern relevance | Used for early-stage logic validation | Largely replaced by **On-Chip Debug (OCD)** | --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Differentiate between a **cross-compiler**, **assembler**, **linker**, and **locator**. For each, state the input file type, output file type, and one example tool. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** An embedded firmware has the following linker map details: code = 14 KB, read-only data = 2 KB, initialised data = 1 KB, BSS = 0.5 KB, stack = 1.5 KB, heap = 0 KB. Calculate the **Flash footprint**, **RAM footprint**, and **total memory demand** on the target MCU. If a UART bootloader runs at **115200 bps, 8N1**, estimate the upload time for the HEX file. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Tool Differentiations — 7 Marks (≈1.75 marks each)** | Tool | Input | Output | Example | Function | |------|-------|--------|---------|----------| | **Cross-Compiler** | `.c` / `.cpp` (C/C++ source) | `.s` (assembly) | `arm-none-eabi-gcc` | Translates high-level language into target-specific assembly | | **Assembler** | `.s` (assembly) | `.o` (relocatable object) | `arm-none-eabi-as` | Converts mnemonics into machine code (object form) | | **Linker** | Multiple `.o` + `.a` libraries | `.elf` (executable + symbols) | `arm-none-eabi-ld` | Resolves cross-file symbols, combines sections | | **Locator** | `.elf` + **linker script (`.ld`)** | `.elf` with **absolute addresses** | Integrated with `ld` | Assigns physical Flash/RAM addresses per memory map | > **Key Point:** The **linker** combines code; the **locator** places it at specific physical addresses — for embedded targets (no virtual memory), the locator is essential. --- **(b) Memory & Upload Calculations — 7 Marks** **Given:** - $M_{text} = 14 \; \text{KB} = 14336 \; \text{bytes}$ - $M_{rodata} = 2 \; \text{KB} = 2048 \; \text{bytes}$ - $M_{data} = 1 \; \text{KB} = 1024 \; \text{bytes}$ - $M_{bss} = 0.5 \; \text{KB} = 512 \; \text{bytes}$ - $M_{stack} = 1.5 \; \text{KB} = 1536 \; \text{bytes}$ - $M_{heap} = 0 \; \text{bytes}$ **Step 1 — Flash footprint (1 mark):** $$F_{footprint} = M_{text} + M_{rodata} + M_{data}$$ $$F_{footprint} = 14336 + 2048 + 1024 = 17408 \; \text{bytes} \;\approx\; 17.0 \; \text{KB}$$ **Step 2 — RAM footprint (1 mark):** $$R_{footprint} = M_{data} + M_{bss} + M_{stack} + M_{heap}$$ $$R_{footprint} = 1024 + 512 + 1536 + 0 = 3072 \; \text{bytes} \;\approx\; 3.0 \; \text{KB}$$ **Step 3 — Total memory (1 mark):** $$M_{total} = F_{footprint} + R_{footprint} = 17408 + 3072 = 20480 \; \text{bytes} \;\approx\; 20.0 \; \text{KB}$$ **Step 4 — Upload time via UART (8N1, 115200 bps) (3 marks):** Each byte = 10 bits (1 start + 8 data + 1 stop). HEX file size (Intel HEX format) ≈ 2× the binary size in worst case, but the **binary to be flashed** is the raw $F_{footprint} = 17408$ bytes. $$T_{upload} = \frac{B \times 10}{\beta} = \frac{17408 \times 10}{115200}$$ $$T_{upload} = \frac{174080}{115200} \approx 1.511 \; \text{seconds}$$ **Final Answer (1 mark):** Flash footprint ≈ 17 KB, RAM footprint ≈ 3 KB, total memory ≈ 20 KB, upload time ≈ 1.51 s. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **The 10× bit factor** in UART upload time is missed by ~60% of students — write *"Each UART frame = 1 start + 8 data + 1 stop = 10 bits"* explicitly. > 2. **M_{data} counts in BOTH Flash and RAM** — it is *stored* in Flash (initial image) but *lives* in RAM at runtime (copied by the startup code). Examiners deduct 1 mark if only one side is counted. > 3. **Linker vs Locator** — students often treat them as the same tool. The *locator* specifically uses the **linker script** to assign physical addresses — mention this distinction. > 4. **Simulator vs Emulator** — do NOT confuse *simulator* (software) with *emulator* (hardware probe). Modern IDEs use **OCD** (on-chip debug) — explicitly naming this fetches an extra mark. > 5. **Cross-compiler naming** — generic answer "gcc" is incomplete; use `arm-none-eabi-gcc` or equivalent target-triple compiler. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **5-Minute Revision Bullets — High-Yield for KTU 2024 ESE** - ✅ **IDE = Editor + Compiler + Assembler + Linker + Debugger + Programmer** — all under one GUI. - ✅ Embedded IDE is **NOT** a generic programmer's IDE — it is **cross-platform** (host ≠ target). - ✅ **Cross-compilation** is mandatory because target is resource-constrained and architecturally different. - ✅ Workflow pipeline: *Source → Preprocess → Compile → Assemble → Link → Locate → Objcopy → Flash → Debug*. - ✅ **Linker** joins objects; **Locator** assigns physical addresses (uses `.ld` linker script). - ✅ **M_{data}** is counted in **both Flash and RAM** — examiners test this trick. - ✅ **Flash footprint** = $M_{text} + M_{rodata} + M_{data}$. - ✅ **RAM footprint** = $M_{data} + M_{bss} + M_{stack} + M_{heap}$. - ✅ UART upload time formula: $T = \dfrac{B \times 10}{\beta}$ (10 bits/frame for 8N1). - ✅ **JTAG** = 4-wire (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO); **SWD** = 2-wire (SWDIO, SWCLK) — both are OCD protocols. - ✅ **Simulator** = software, free, slow, no peripherals. **Emulator** = hardware probe, real-time, expensive. **OCD** = modern standard (ARM CoreSight). - ✅ Popular IDEs to remember: **Keil µVision** (8051/ARM), **STM32CubeIDE** (STM32), **MPLAB X** (PIC), **CCS** (MSP430), **Arduino IDE** (AVR/ESP), **ESP-IDF** (ESP32). - ✅ Project structure: `src/`, `include/`, `startup/`, `linker/`, `build/`, plus `Makefile` / `CMakeLists.txt`. - ✅ HEX file formats: **Intel HEX**, **Motorola S-record**, **raw binary** — generated by `objcopy`. - ✅ **ELF** file contains symbols + debug info; **HEX/BIN** contains only the binary image to flash. > [!TIP] > **Final KTU Mantra:** *"The IDE is the workshop; the toolchain is the machinery; the linker script is the blueprint; the HEX file is the finished product shipped to the target."* ---

topics.title: Types of Files Generated on Cross Compilation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT726 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Files Generated on Cross Compilation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Embedded Systems** development, **Cross Compilation** is the process of using a compiler (the *cross-compiler*) running on a **host machine** (typically a general-purpose computer such as an x86 PC running Linux/Windows) to generate executable machine code targeted at a **different processor architecture** (the *target machine*, such as ARM Cortex-M, AVR, PIC, MSP430, etc.).

During this process, the cross-compiler toolchain (e.g., `arm-none-eabi-gcc`, `avr-gcc`, `xtensa-esp32-elf-gcc`) produces a **structured set of intermediate and final output files**. Each file type corresponds to a specific stage of the build pipeline — preprocessing, compilation, assembly, linking, locating, and image conversion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Verbatim Interpretation):**
> *"The files generated on cross compilation include object files, assembly listings, executable images (ELF), map files, hex/s-record files, dependency files, and debug information files. The exact file types depend on the toolchain (GCC, IAR, Keil, etc.) and the embedded target architecture."*

## 1.2 The Complete Spectrum of Cross-Compilation Output Files

| S.No | File Type | Extension | Stage of Generation | Purpose |
|:----:|:----------|:---------:|:-------------------|:--------|
| 1 | Preprocessed Source | `.i`, `.ii` | Preprocessor | Expanded C/C++ code with macros resolved |
| 2 | Assembly Source | `.s` | Compiler (Code Generation) | Target-specific assembly language |
| 3 | Object File | `.o` | Assembler | Relocatable machine code, not yet linked |
| 4 | Archive / Library | `.a` (static), `.so` (shared) | Archiver / Linker | Reusable object collections |
| 5 | Executable (ELF/COFF) | (none) / `.elf`, `.axf`, `.out` | Linker | Linked, relocatable, full program image |
| 6 | Map File | `.map` | Linker | Memory layout & symbol address log |
| 7 | Listing File | `.lst` | Compiler/Assembler | Mixed source + assembly + addresses |
| 8 | Dependency File | `.d` | Preprocessor | Header dependency list for `make` |
| 9 | Hex / S-Record | `.hex`, `.s19`, `.srec`, `.mot` | Object Copy (`objcopy`) | Flash programming format |
| 10 | Raw Binary | `.bin` | Object Copy | Pure memory image, no metadata |
| 11 | Debug Info (DWARF) | `.debug`, embedded `.elf` | Compiler + Linker | Symbols, types, line numbers for GDB |
| 12 | Linker Script | `.ld`, `.lds`, `.x` | Manually authored | Tells linker how to lay out memory sections |
| 13 | Size Info | (stdout) | Size Utility | Section-by-section memory consumption |
| 14 | Disassembly | `.dis`, `.dump` | `objdump` utility | Human-readable form of binary |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: "The Bakery Factory" 🍞

Imagine a **bakery that ships bread to a foreign country**:

- **The Recipe (`.c` source)** — written in your local language (English).
- **The Chef (Preprocessor)** — interprets recipes, folds in the "ingredients" (header files), and writes an **expanded recipe** (`.i`).
- **The Head Baker (Compiler)** — converts the expanded recipe into **kneading instructions in the target country's language** — the **Assembly file** (`.s`).
- **The Dough Machine (Assembler)** — turns the assembly instructions into **individual loaves** — **Object files** (`.o`).
- **The Packaging Department (Linker)** — combines all loaves into a **shipping crate** (the **ELF file**), along with a **shipping manifest** (the **Map file**).
- **The Customs Officer (`objcopy`)** — translates the crate into a **standard shipping form** — **Intel HEX or S-Record** — that the destination warehouse (target board's flash programmer) accepts.
- **The Warehouse Manager (Debugger/GDB)** — keeps a **separate catalog** (DWARF debug info) mapping each loaf back to the original ingredient list, so any complaint can be traced.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** Every artifact in this pipeline exists for a *reason*. Skipping files (e.g., disabling map files) is a common debugging mistake — the linker map is often the only way to diagnose stack-overflow and section-overlap issues in resource-constrained MCUs.

## 1.4 Why These Files Matter in Embedded Systems

Embedded targets (e.g., STM32, ESP32, ATmega328P) have:
- **Tightly constrained flash and RAM** — map files reveal exact consumption.
- **No operating system to load `.elf` dynamically** — raw binary or hex must be flashed.
- **Heterogeneous memory regions** (Flash at `0x08000000`, RAM at `0x20000000`, CCMRAM, etc.) — link map & linker script define this.
- **Remote / in-circuit debugging** — DWARF info enables on-target GDB sessions via JTAG/SWD.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Favourite Exam Hook:** *"List the types of files generated on cross compilation."* — Always include **ELF, Map, HEX/SREC, Object, Assembly, and Debug (DWARF)** at minimum. A high-scoring answer also mentions the *preprocessor output*, *listing file*, and *dependency file* with their use cases.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 The Cross-Compilation Pipeline — Step-by-Step Logical Flow

The cross-compilation build pipeline is a **deterministic, staged transformation** of source code into a target-executable image. Each stage produces a specific file type:

### Stage 1 — Preprocessing
- **Tool:** `arm-none-eabi-cpp` (the C preprocessor, called internally).
- **Input:** `main.c` + headers (`.h`).
- **Output:** `main.i` (C) or `main.ii` (C++).
- **Operations performed:**
  * Macro expansion (`#define` → literal value).
  * Conditional compilation stripping (`#if`, `#ifdef`, `#endif`).
  * Header file inlining (`#include <foo.h>` → full content of `foo.h`).
  * Line markers preserved for debugging (e.g., `# 1 "main.c"`).
  * Pragma directives are **not** expanded here — they pass to the compiler.
- **GCC flag to retain:** `-save-temps` or `-E -o main.i`.

### Stage 2 — Compilation (Translation to Assembly)
- **Tool:** `arm-none-eabi-gcc` (the cross-compiler).
- **Input:** `main.i`.
- **Output:** `main.s` — target-specific assembly (ARM, AVR, RISC-V, etc.).
- **Operations performed:**
  * Lexical analysis → token stream.
  * Syntax analysis → Abstract Syntax Tree (AST).
  * Semantic analysis → type checking, constant folding.
  * **Target-specific code generation** → register allocation, instruction selection.
- **GCC flag to retain:** `-S` (uppercase). Example: `arm-none-eabi-gcc -S -mcpu=cortex-m4 main.c -o main.s`.

### Stage 3 — Assembly
- **Tool:** `arm-none-eabi-as` (the cross-assembler).
- **Input:** `main.s`.
- **Output:** `main.o` — **relocatable object file** (ELF relocatable format, type `ET_REL`).
- **Content of `.o` file:**
  * **ELF header** (e_machine = EM_ARM, e.g., `0x28` for ARM).
  * **Section headers** (`.text`, `.data`, `.bss`, `.rodata`, `.symtab`, `.strtab`, `.rel.text`, etc.).
  * **Machine code** (in `.text`).
  * **Relocation entries** — placeholders for addresses not yet known (e.g., external function calls).
  * **Symbol table** — names of functions, variables, their section, size, binding.

### Stage 4 — Linking & Locating
- **Tool:** `arm-none-eabi-ld` (the cross-linker) or `arm-none-eabi-gcc` (driver invoking `ld`).
- **Inputs:** All `.o` files + libraries (`.a`) + **linker script** (`.ld`).
- **Output:**
  * `firmware.elf` — fully linked, **relocatable** still, but addresses resolved.
  * `firmware.map` — symbol → address mapping log.
- **Operations performed:**
  * **Symbol resolution** — matches undefined symbols to definitions in object files/libraries.
  * **Section merging** — all `.text` from all `.o` files combined into one output `.text`.
  * **Address allocation** — per the memory regions defined in the linker script.
  * **Relocation** — patches in real addresses for previously placeholder references.
  * **Generation of program headers** (PT_LOAD, PT_DYNAMIC, PT_NOTE).

### Stage 5 — Object Copy / Format Conversion
- **Tool:** `arm-none-eabi-objcopy`.
- **Input:** `firmware.elf`.
- **Outputs:** `firmware.hex` (Intel HEX), `firmware.srec` (Motorola S-record), `firmware.bin` (raw binary).
- **Why needed:** Most embedded flash programmers (OpenOCD, ST-Link, J-Link) accept **Intel HEX** or **SREC**, not ELF.

### Stage 6 — Size Analysis
- **Tool:** `arm-none-eabi-size`.
- **Output:** Console report of `text`, `data`, `bss` sizes in bytes.

### Stage 7 — Disassembly (Optional)
- **Tool:** `arm-none-eabi-objdump -d firmware.elf`.
- **Output:** `firmware.dis` or stdout listing — shows addresses + mnemonics.

## 2.2 Detailed File-by-File Theoretical Analysis

### 2.2.1 Object File (`.o`)
An **ELF relocatable object** — not yet an executable. It contains:
- A **file header** identifying it as `ET_REL` (relocatable).
- **Section headers**, each describing a contiguous byte range.
- A **symbol table** (`.symtab`) listing every function/variable by name.
- A **relocation table** (`.rel.text` or `.rela.text`) listing addresses that need patching at link time.

> The `.o` file is the fundamental unit of compilation — it allows **separate compilation** of large embedded projects.

### 2.2.2 Executable and Linkable Format (`.elf`)
The **ELF file** is the *canonical* output of the linker. It contains:
- **ELF header** — magic bytes `7F 45 4C 46`, class (32/64-bit), data encoding, target machine (`e_machine`), entry-point address.
- **Program headers** (segments) — `PT_LOAD` segments describe what bytes get written to which memory region at load time.
- **Section headers** — fine-grained groupings (`.text`, `.rodata`, `.data`, `.bss`).
- **Symbol table** (full, with debug symbols if `-g` was passed).
- **Debug sections** (`.debug_info`, `.debug_line`, `.debug_abbrev`, `.debug_str`) — DWARF format.

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU exam: state explicitly that **ELF is NOT directly flashed**. The MCU's bootloader or flash programmer does not parse ELF. The ELF must be converted to **HEX/BIN** using `objcopy`.

### 2.2.3 Map File (`.map`)
A plain-text report generated by the linker. It contains:
- **Archive member list** — which `.o` files from which `.a` libraries were pulled in.
- **Memory Configuration** — name, origin, length, attributes of each region (`FLASH (rx) : ORIGIN = 0x08000000, LENGTH = 256K`).
- **Linker script and memory map** — the actual address assigned to each output section.
- **Symbol cross-reference** — for every global/static symbol: address, size, object file of origin.
- **Discarded input sections** — anything removed during garbage collection (`--gc-sections`).

**Why KTU loves this:** Map files are the *only* way to verify that `int main()` ended up at `0x080001A0` and that `.data` correctly points to a load address in flash and a VMA in RAM.

### 2.2.4 Intel HEX (`.hex`) and Motorola S-Record (`.s19`, `.srec`)
ASCII-text representations of binary data, designed for **slow, line-oriented serial programmers** (legacy PROM programmers, ISP bootloaders).

**Intel HEX record format** (each line):

$$\text{Record} = :LLAAAATT[DD\ldots\text{DD}]CC$$

Where:
- `:` = start code (colon, `0x3A`)
- `LL` = byte count (1 byte, hex)
- `AAAA` = load address (2 bytes, big-endian, hex)
- `TT` = record type (`00`=data, `01`=EOF, `02`=ESA, `03`=SSA, `04`=ELA, `05`=SLA)
- `DD` = data bytes
- `CC` = checksum (two's complement of sum of all bytes after colon)

**S-Record format** is similar but uses a different header character (`S0`–`S9`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The HEX/S-record files contain **only the bytes that will be programmed into non-volatile memory** (flash). They do not include RAM initialization values for uninitialized variables (`.bss`) — those are zeroed at startup by the C runtime (`_start` → `__libc_init_array`).

### 2.2.5 Raw Binary (`.bin`)
A byte-for-byte memory image. No headers, no addresses, no metadata. Starting from a known base address (defined in the linker script), byte N of the file maps to address `BASE + N` in flash.

> **Disadvantage:** if there are gaps in the memory map (e.g., reserved regions), the binary must be padded. This is why most embedded projects use HEX/SREC instead.

### 2.2.6 Listing File (`.lst`)
Produced by the assembler (`-a` flag) or compiler (`-Wa,-adhln=main.lst`). It interleaves:
- Source code line numbers
- Compiled assembly instructions
- Final memory addresses
- Relocation information

Used for **performance analysis** (cycle counting) and **certification** (DO-178C, IEC 61508 require showing exactly what code is generated).

### 2.2.7 Dependency File (`.d`)
Produced by `-MMD` or `-MD` flag. A `make`-compatible list of all headers a source file includes. Used by build systems (Make, CMake) to automatically rebuild a `.c` file when any of its `.h` files change.

### 2.2.8 Debug Information (DWARF)
Embedded in the `.elf` file when compiled with `-g`. Uses the **DWARF (Debugging With Attributed Record Formats)** specification. Sections:
- `.debug_info` — DIE (Debugging Information Entry) tree for types, variables, functions.
- `.debug_line` — line-number → address mapping.
- `.debug_frame` — call-frame info (stack unwinding for backtraces).
- `.debug_str` — string pool for type/variable names.

The on-chip debugger (GDB + OpenOCD/J-Link) reads DWARF to provide:
- Source-level breakpoints
- Variable inspection (even optimized-out variables sometimes)
- Stack backtraces with function names

### 2.2.9 Library Files (`.a`, `.so`)
- **`.a` (static archive):** Created with `arm-none-eabi-ar`. A collection of `.o` files concatenated with an index. At link time, only the `.o` files containing needed symbols are pulled in.
- **`.so` (shared object):** Rare in deeply embedded (no MMU/dynamic loader). Used in Linux-based embedded (Raspberry Pi, embedded Android).

### 2.2.10 Linker Script (`.ld`)
Not generated by the toolchain — **authored by the developer or supplied by the vendor** (e.g., `STM32F407VG.ld` in STM32CubeIDE projects). It defines:
- Memory regions (`MEMORY { FLASH (rx) : ORIGIN = 0x08000000, LENGTH = 1024K; RAM (rwx) : ORIGIN = 0x20000000, LENGTH = 128K; }`)
- Section-to-region mapping (`SECTIONS { .text : { *(.text*) } >FLASH }`)
- Symbol definitions (`_sidata`, `_sdata`, `_edata`, `_sbss`, `_ebss` — used by startup code to copy `.data` from flash to RAM and zero `.bss`).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Reference Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick-Reference Table (Avoid Using `|` in Cells to Preserve Table Syntax):**

| File Type | Extension | Tool That Produces It | Key Insight (Use Mid For Ranges) |
|:----------|:---------:|:----------------------|:----------------------------------|
| Preprocessed source | `.i` | `gcc -E` | Macros expanded, headers inlined |
| Assembly source | `.s` | `gcc -S` | Target-specific mnemonics |
| Relocatable object | `.o` | `as` (assembler) | Contains relocations + symbol table |
| Static library | `.a` | `ar` | Archive of `.o` files |
| Linked executable | `.elf` | `ld` (linker) | Contains DWARF if `-g` used |
| Linker map | `.map` | `ld` | Symbol → address mapping |
| Intel HEX | `.hex` | `objcopy -O ihex` | ASCII hex records, line-checksummed |
| Motorola S-record | `.s19` | `objcopy -O srec` | ASCII, S0–S9 record types |
| Raw binary | `.bin` | `objcopy -O binary` | No metadata, base-address-relative |
| Disassembly | `.dis` | `objdump -d` | Human-readable reverse of binary |
| Listing | `.lst` | `gcc -Wa,-adhln` | Mixed source/ASM/address |
| Dependency | `.d` | `gcc -MMD` | Header dependency for `make` |
| Debug info | `.debug_*` | `gcc -g` | DWARF sections inside `.elf` |
| Linker script | `.ld` | Developer-authored | Defines memory regions |
| Size report | (stdout) | `size` | text $\vert$ data $\vert$ bss bytes |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry / Application | File Type Most Critical | Reason |
|:-----------------------|:------------------------|:-------|
| Automotive ECU (ISO 26262) | `.lst` + `.elf` + `.map` | Certification requires exact code listing and memory proof |
| Consumer IoT (Wi-Fi SoC) | `.bin` | OTA updaters consume raw binary for delta-diff |
| Medical devices (FDA) | `.elf` + DWARF | Post-market failure analysis with full symbols |
| Space / Aerospace | `.srec` + `.map` | Radiation-hardened bootloaders use S-record |
| Hobbyist Arduino | `.hex` | `avrdude` consumes Intel HEX |
| Industrial PLC | `.elf` | Vendor toolchain retains ELF for field service |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Complete Build of an ARM Cortex-M Firmware

### The Source Code (`main.c`)

```c
#include "stm32f4xx.h"

static uint32_t sensor_value = 0;   // .data

void delay(volatile uint32_t count) {
    while(count--);
}

int main(void) {
    RCC->AHB1ENR |= 0x00000001;        // enable GPIOA clock
    GPIOA->MODER  = 0x04000000;        // PA5 output
    sensor_value = ADC1->DR;           // read ADC
    delay(1000000);
    GPIOA->ODR   ^= (1 << 5);          // toggle LED
    return 0;
}
```

### Step-by-Step Build Chain

**Step 1 — Preprocess** to obtain `main.i`:

$$ \texttt{arm-none-eabi-gcc\ -E\ -mcpu=cortex-m4\ main.c\ -o\ main.i} $$

This expands `#include "stm32f4xx.h"` (which recursively includes hundreds of CMSIS headers) and resolves all `#define` macros. The output file may exceed 500 KB even for a 30-line program.

**Step 2 — Compile to assembly** to obtain `main.s`:

$$ \texttt{arm-none-eabi-gcc\ -S\ -mcpu=cortex-m4\ -mthumb\ -O0\ main.i\ -o\ main.s} $$

The `-mthumb` flag forces 16-bit Thumb-2 instructions (Cortex-M only executes Thumb). The generated `main.s` contains ARM unified-assembly directives such as `.syntax unified`, `.thumb`, and `.cpu cortex-m4`.

**Step 3 — Assemble to object** to obtain `main.o`:

$$ \texttt{arm-none-eabi-as\ -mcpu=cortex-m4\ -mthumb\ main.s\ -o\ main.o} $$

The `main.o` is an ELF relocatable file. Inspecting it:

$$ \texttt{arm-none-eabi-readelf\ -h\ main.o} $$

yields (excerpt):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Magic:}\ &\ 7f\ 45\ 4c\ 46\ \text{(ELF)} \\
\text{Class:}\ &\ \text{ELF32} \\
\text{Machine:}\ &\ \text{Advanced RISC Machines ARM (0x28)} \\
\text{Type:}\ &\ \text{REL (Relocatable file)} \\
\text{Entry:}\ &\ \text{0x0} \quad \text{(not yet assigned)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Link** with startup code, CMSIS, and linker script to obtain `firmware.elf` and `firmware.map`:

$$ \texttt{arm-none-eabi-ld\ -T\ stm32f4.ld\ -Map=firmware.map\ startup.o\ main.o\ -o\ firmware.elf} $$

**Step 5 — Verify memory map** by reading the map file:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{FLASH region:}\ &\ \text{0x08000000} \rightarrow \text{0x0807FFFF}\ \ (512\ \text{KB}) \\
\text{RAM region:}\ \ &\ \text{0x20000000} \rightarrow \text{0x2001FFFF}\ \ (128\ \text{KB}) \\
\text{.text placed at:}\ &\ \text{0x08000000} \\
\text{.data loaded from:}\ &\ \text{0x080001B0}\ \text{(in flash)},\ \text{VMA} = \text{0x20000000}\ \text{(in RAM)} \\
\text{.bss starts at:}\ &\ \text{0x20000004}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to flashable Intel HEX**:

$$ \texttt{arm-none-eabi-objcopy\ -O\ ihex\ firmware.elf\ firmware.hex} $$

**Step 7 — Convert to raw binary for OTA updates**:

$$ \texttt{arm-none-eabi-objcopy\ -O\ binary\ firmware.elf\ firmware.bin} $$

**Step 8 — Inspect size budget**:

$$ \texttt{arm-none-eabi-size\ firmware.elf} $$

Output:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{text}\ &= \text{3\,248 bytes} \\
\text{data}\ &= \text{4 bytes}\ \ (\text{the static } \texttt{sensor\_value}) \\
\text{bss}\ &= \text{0 bytes}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **`.text` = code + read-only data (`.rodata`)**. **`.data` = initialized globals/static with non-zero values**. **`.bss` = uninitialized or zero-initialized globals/static (consumes RAM but no flash)**.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Decoding an Intel HEX Record Line

Given the following Intel HEX line:

```
:10010000214601360121470136007EFE09D2190140
```

We will decode every field step by step.

**Step 1 — Identify the start code `:`** (ASCII `0x3A`).

**Step 2 — Read the byte count** `LL = 10` (hex). This means 16 data bytes follow.

**Step 3 — Read the load address** `AAAA = 0100`. The bytes will be loaded at memory address `0x0100`.

**Step 4 — Read the record type** `TT = 00`. This is a **data record** (programmable data).

**Step 5 — Read the 16 data bytes** `21 46 01 36 01 21 47 01 36 00 7E FE 09 D2 19 01`.

**Step 6 — Compute the checksum** to verify:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Sum of all bytes (count + addr + type + data)} &= 0x10 + 0x01 + 0x00 + 0x00 \\
&\quad + 0x21 + 0x46 + 0x01 + 0x36 + 0x01 + 0x21 + 0x47 + 0x01 + 0x36 \\
&\quad + 0x00 + 0x7E + 0xFE + 0x09 + 0xD2 + 0x19 + 0x01 \\
&= 0x02BF
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Take the two's complement low byte** of `0x02BF`:

$$ \text{Checksum} = ( \sim \text{0xBF} ) + 1 = 0x40 $$

The last byte of the line is `0x40`. ✓ Valid record.

> [!TIP]
> The **EOF record** is always `:00000001FF` — zero bytes, address `0x0000`, type `0x01` (EOF), checksum `0xFF`. This is a frequently-asked KTU quiz question.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: S-Record Format Decoding

A Motorola S-record line:

```
S1130000C0000080000F000000000000000000001A
```

**Decoding:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Type:}\ &\ \text{S1} \quad \text{(data record, 16-bit address)} \\
\text{Byte count:}\ &\ \text{0x13} = 19\ \text{bytes total (including addr + data + checksum)} \\
\text{Address:}\ &\ \text{0x0000} \\
\text{Data:}\ &\ \text{C0 00 00 80 00 0F 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00} \\
\text{Checksum:}\ &\ \text{0x1A}
\end{aligned}$$

The checksum byte is the **one's complement** of the least significant byte of the sum of: byte count, address, and all data bytes. The programmer rejects the record if the computed checksum (complemented and added to the stored checksum) does not equal `0xFF`.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: GCC Flags That Control File Generation

| GCC Flag | Effect | File Generated |
|:---------|:-------|:---------------|
| `-E` | Stop after preprocessing | `.i` |
| `-S` | Stop after code generation | `.s` |
| `-c` | Stop after assembly (no link) | `.o` |
| (no flag) | Full compile + link | `.elf` (via driver) |
| `-save-temps` | Preserve all intermediate | `.i`, `.s`, `.o` retained |
| `-g` | Embed DWARF debug info | `.debug_*` sections in `.elf` |
| `-MMD` | Generate dependency file | `.d` |
| `-Wa,-adhln=main.lst` | Emit assembly listing | `.lst` |
| `-Xlinker -Map=firmware.map` | Generate map file | `.map` |
| `-ffunction-sections` + `--gc-sections` | Per-function sections + dead-code elimination | Smaller `.elf` and `.bin` |

## 3.5 Memory Region Arithmetic — Deriving the Hex File Size

Given a firmware with:

$$ \text{text size} = 3\,248\ \text{bytes}, \quad \text{data size} = 4\ \text{bytes}, \quad \text{bss size} = 0\ \text{bytes} $$

The **Intel HEX file size** is approximately:

$$ \text{HEX size} \approx \text{text} + \text{data} + \text{overhead} $$

Each Intel HEX data record holds 16 bytes of payload + 11 bytes of framing (`:LLAAAATT...CC\r\n`). For our case:

$$ \begin{aligned} N_{\text{records}} &= \left\lceil \frac{3\,248 + 4}{16} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{3\,252}{16} \right\rceil = \lceil 203.25 \rceil = 204\ \text{records} \\ \text{HEX size} &\approx 204 \times (16 + 11) + \text{EOF line} \approx 5\,508 + 11 \approx 5\,519\ \text{bytes ASCII} \end{aligned} $$

The **raw binary size** is exactly:

$$ \text{BIN size} = \text{text} + \text{data} = 3\,252\ \text{bytes} $$

(`.bss` is not stored in flash — it is zeroed at startup.)

> [!NOTE]
> The HEX file is **larger** than the BIN file because of the ASCII encoding and address overhead. The exact ratio for typical firmware is `HEX ≈ 2.2 × BIN` (Intel HEX) or `HEX ≈ 2.4 × BIN` (S-record).

flowchart TD

    hdrA --> preprocess
