- **DWT (Data Watchpoint and Trace):** Provides watchpoints, PC sampling, and exception/interrupt tracing.
- **ITM (Instrumentation Trace Macrocell):** Allows software-driven printf-style debug over SWO (Serial Wire Output).
- **ETM (Embedded Trace Macrocell):** Non-intrusive real-time instruction trace via trace port pins (4-bit or wider).
- **TPIU (Trace Port Interface Unit):** Formats ETM/ITM data for output via SWO or parallel trace port.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Insight:** The hierarchy is:
> **External Probe → JTAG/SWD Pins → DAP → AHB-AP → FPB/DWT/ITM/ETM → Core Registers & Memory**

---

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Tool Family

| Tool | Why It Exists | Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| **ISS (Software Simulator)** | Zero hardware cost, fully observable | Not real-time, peripheral models may be inaccurate |
| **OCD (JTAG/SWD)** | Real silicon, real-time, non-intrusive | Limited breakpoints, intrusive when halted |
| **ICE (Hardware Emulator)** | Transparent replacement of target | Expensive, obsolete for most modern MCUs |
| **Logic Analyzer** | Captures multi-signal timing | No source-level correlation |
| **Oscilloscope** | Analog signal integrity check | Cannot read register state |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> This section provides exhaustive derivations of the key timing equations used in emulator and debugger design. Every algebraic step is fully expanded as required by the KTU 2024 OBE evaluation rubric.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum JTAG TCK Frequency for Reliable Boundary-Scan

> **Problem:** Given a target board with a maximum PCB trace length of $L = 20$ cm between the JTAG header and the target MCU, and a propagation delay of the FR4 trace of $t_{pd} = 5$ ns/cm, derive the maximum TCK frequency such that the **TDI → TDO round-trip** satisfies the setup time of the JTAG master.

### Step 1 — Compute the round-trip propagation delay

The signal travels from the JTAG master → target TDI, through internal JTAG registers, and back out via TDO → master. The PCB trace is traversed **twice** (forward and return path):

$$t_{round\_trip} = 2 \times L \times t_{pd}$$

Substituting $L = 20$ cm and $t_{pd} = 5$ ns/cm:

$$t_{round\_trip} = 2 \times 20 \times 5 = 200 \text{ ns}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the setup-time constraint

The IEEE 1149.1 standard requires that the round-trip delay not exceed **half the TCK period** to guarantee sampling:

$$t_{round\_trip} \leq \frac{T_{TCK}}{2}$$

Rearranging for $T_{TCK}$:

$$T_{TCK} \geq 2 \times t_{round\_trip}$$

$$T_{TCK} \geq 2 \times 200 \times 10^{-9} = 400 \text{ ns}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to frequency

$$f_{TCK} \leq \frac{1}{T_{TCK}} = \frac{1}{400 \times 10^{-9}} = 2.5 \text{ MHz}$$

**Conclusion:** The maximum reliable TCK frequency is **2.5 MHz** for a 20 cm JTAG trace. Exceeding this risks setup-time violations and unstable debugging.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Software Breakpoint Mechanism Address Calculation

> **Problem:** A user sets a breakpoint at source line 47 of `main.c`, which the compiler has mapped to address `0x0800 1234`. Show how the debugger patches the code in Flash/RAM.

### Step 1 — Determine instruction width

For ARM Cortex-M (Thumb-2), all instructions are **16-bit or 32-bit**. A `BKPT #imm` instruction is **16 bits**.

### Step 2 — Read the original instruction

The debugger performs a DAP read at the target address:

$$I_{orig} = MEM_{read}(0x08001234) = 0x483B \quad \text{(example LDR instruction)}$$

### Step 3 — Save the original

The debugger stores $I_{orig}$ in a host-side breakpoint table:

$$BP_{table}[0] = \{addr: 0x08001234,\ orig: 0x483B\}$$

### Step 4 — Patch the instruction

The debugger writes a `BKPT #0xFE` opcode (encoding `0xBEXE`, where `X` is the immediate):

$$I_{patch} = 0xBE00 \quad \text{(BKPT \#0)}$$

$$\text{or}$$

$$I_{patch} = 0xBEFF \quad \text{(BKPT \#0xFF for safety)}$$

### Step 5 — DAP write the patch

The debugger performs a DAP write:

$$MEM_{write}(0x08001234, 0xBE00)$$

### Step 6 — On hit, the core enters Debug state

When the PC reaches `0x08001234`, the `BKPT` instruction triggers a **DebugMonitor exception** (if enabled) or a **HardFault** that the debugger intercepts. The debugger then:
1. Halts the core via DHCSR.
2. Notifies the IDE: "Breakpoint 1 hit at line 47."
3. Awaits user action (step, continue, inspect).

### Step 7 — On continue, the debugger restores

$$MEM_{write}(0x08001234, 0x483B)$$

This restores the original instruction for normal execution, and the cycle repeats if the user re-encounters the breakpoint.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: DWT Watchpoint Range Masking

> **Problem:** A user wants to trigger a watchpoint whenever any access occurs in the address range `[0x2000_0000, 0x2000_0FFF]` (1 KB SRAM region). Derive the comparator value and mask for the DWT.

### Step 1 — Convert range to a function

The DWT uses an **equality comparator**. A range check is achieved by masking out the lower address bits and comparing the upper bits.

For a 1 KB region: $2^{10} = 1024$ bytes, so the **lower 10 bits** are don't-care.

$$MASK = 0xFFFFFC00 \quad \text{(keep bits 31..10)}$$

$$COMPARE = 0x20000000 \quad \text{(base address, lower 10 bits = 0)}$$

### Step 2 — Verify the access window

For any access address $A$:

$$(A \land MASK) = COMPARE \quad \text{?}$$

$$A = 0x20000500$$

$$A \land MASK = 0x20000500 \land 0xFFFFFC00 = 0x20000400 \neq 0x20000000$$

**Incorrect!** The base must be aligned to the mask:

$$COMPARE = 0x20000000 \quad \text{(aligned to 1 KB boundary)}$$

$$MASK = 0xFFFFFC00$$

Re-checking with $A = 0x20000500$:

$$A \land MASK = 0x20000500 \land 0xFFFFFC00 = 0x20000400 \neq 0x20000000$$

This still fails because the base is not in the lower 1 KB block. To match the upper 1 KB `[0x20000400, 0x200007FF]`, we need:

$$COMPARE = 0x20000400, \quad MASK = 0xFFFFFC00$$

Now any access with bits 31..10 equal to `0x200004` will trigger. This confirms the alignment rule.

### Step 3 — General formula

For an address range of size $2^{k}$ bytes starting at aligned base $B$:

$$MASK = \sim(2^{k} - 1)$$

$$COMPARE = B$$

$$\text{Trigger condition: } (A \land MASK) = COMPARE$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Debug Tool Selection Decision Table

| Criterion | ISS (QEMU) | OCD (JTAG/SWD) | ICE (Lauterbach) | Logic Analyzer |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cost** | Free | Low (USD 20–200) | High (USD 5,000+) | Medium (USD 500+) |
| **Real-Time Execution** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Register Visibility** | ✅ Full | ✅ Full | ✅ Full + Trace | ❌ No |
| **Source-Level Step** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **Interrupt Latency Accuracy** | ❌ Approximate | ✅ Accurate | ✅ Accurate | ✅ Accurate |
| **Peripheral Modeling** | ⚠️ Limited | ✅ Real HW | ✅ Real HW | N/A |
| **Used in KTU Labs** | ✅ Yes (ARM/MIPS) | ✅ Yes (STM32) | ❌ Rare | ⚠️ Optional |

---

## 3.5 Derivative Analysis: Why SWD Replaced JTAG in Most Cortex-M Designs

JTAG requires **4 signals minimum** (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO), with optional TRST and RTCK. SWD requires only **2 signals** (SWDIO, SWCLK), plus optional SWO for trace.

**Pin savings:**

$$\Delta_{pins} = N_{JTAG} - N_{SWD} = 4 - 2 = 2 \text{ pins freed}$$

For a **QFN-32 package** MCU, saving 2 pins is significant (a 6.25% pin budget recovery). KTU board exams frequently cite this in design questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: The Complete On-Chip Debug (OCD) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Host PC running IDE] --> B[JTAG or SWD Probe Hardware]
    B --> C[TAP Controller FSM 16 states]
    C --> D[DAP Debug Access Port]
    D --> E[AHB AP Bridge]
    E --> F1[FPB Flash Patch and Breakpoint]
    E --> F2[DWT Data Watchpoint and Trace]
    E --> F3[ITM Instrumentation Trace Macrocell]
    E --> F4[ETM Embedded Trace Macrocell]
    E --> F5[TPIU Trace Port Interface Unit]
    F1 --> G1[Core Registers and Memory]
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> H[SWO Serial Wire Output]
    F4 --> I[4 bit Parallel Trace Port]
    F5 --> H
    F5 --> I
    G1 --> J[Target MCU ARM Cortex M]
    J --> K[Peripherals GPIO UART ADC TIM]
    H --> B
    I --> B
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The IDE on the host PC communicates with the JTAG/SWD probe, which drives the TAP controller of the target MCU. The TAP controller feeds the DAP, which in turn bridges to the AHB-AP (Advanced High-performance Bus Access Port). From there, four sub-modules route debug operations: FPB for breakpoints, DWT for watchpoints, ITM for printf-style trace, and ETM for full instruction trace. Trace data exits via the TPIU through SWO (1-bit) or the parallel trace port (multi-bit).

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Setting and Hitting a Software Breakpoint

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant IDE as Host IDE
    participant DBG as Debugger Probe
    participant TAP as Target TAP Controller
    participant CORE as Target Core
    participant MEM as Flash or RAM
    IDE->>DBG: Set breakpoint at line 47
    DBG->>TAP: Send HALT request via DHCSR
    TAP->>CORE: Halt at next instruction
    CORE-->>DBG: Core halted ACK
    DBG->>MEM: Read original instruction at PC
    MEM-->>DBG: Returns 0x483B
    DBG->>IDE: Save original to BP table
    IDE->>DBG: User clicks Run
    DBG->>MEM: Write BKPT 0xBE00 at PC
    DBG->>CORE: Resume execution
    CORE->>MEM: Fetch instruction at line 47
    MEM-->>CORE: Returns 0xBE00 BKPT
    CORE->>DBG: Debug exception triggered
    DBG->>IDE: Breakpoint hit notification
    IDE->>DBG: User inspects variables
    DBG->>IDE: Read register file
```

> **Reading the Sequence:** This diagram walks through the *exact* message exchange when a software breakpoint fires. Note the **read-modify-write** pattern: the original instruction is read, the BKPT is written, and on hit, the debugger reports back to the IDE before any user action occurs.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram: JTAG TAP Controller (Simplified)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> TLR
    TLR --> RTI : TMS low for 1 TCK
    RTI --> SDR : TMS 1,0 then SelectDR
    RTI --> SIR : TMS 1,1 then SelectIR
    SDR --> RTI : TMS 1,1 update
    SIR --> RTI : TMS 1,1 update
    SDR --> SDR : ShiftDR state
    SIR --> SIR : ShiftIR state
    RTI --> TLR : TMS high for 5 TCK
```

> **Reading the State Diagram:** The TAP controller is a 16-state FSM. The five primary stable states are: **TLR (Test-Logic-Reset), RTI (Run-Test/Idle), SDR (Shift-DR), SIR (Shift-IR), and Update-DR/IR**. The debugger advances the FSM by driving TMS high or low on each rising edge of TCK.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Debug Tool Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 5: Application Code user main c]
    L2[Layer 4: RTOS or Bare Metal Scheduler]
    L3[Layer 3: HAL Hardware Abstraction Layer]
    L4[Layer 2: CMSIS Drivers peripheral library]
    L5[Layer 1: CoreSight Debug Hardware FPB DWT ITM ETM]
    L6[Layer 0: Physical JTAG or SWD Pins]
    L6 --> L5
    L5 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
    EX1[External Debugger Segger J Link] -.-> L6
    EX2[Software Simulator QEMU] -.-> L3
    EX3[Logic Analyzer Saleae] -.-> L6
```

> **Reading the Stack:** The debugger operates at multiple levels: at the **physical layer** (L0) it drives JTAG/SWD pins. At the **hardware layer** (L5) it interacts with CoreSight modules. At the **HAL layer** (L3) it can also intercept software calls (e.g., via semihosting). Different tools target different layers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]**
**Differentiate between a Software Simulator (ISS) and an In-Circuit Emulator (ICE) in embedded system debugging. List two advantages of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | ISS (Instruction Set Simulator) | ICE (In-Circuit Emulator) |
|---|---|---|
| **Hardware Required** | None (runs on PC) | Special hardware probe replacing target MCU |
| **Real-Time Execution** | No (step-by-step on host) | Yes (real-time) |
| **Cost** | Free / very low | High (USD 1,000–10,000) |
| **Peripheral Accuracy** | Approximate (modeled) | Exact (real silicon) |

**Two advantages of ISS:**
1. Enables software development before hardware is available.
2. Full visibility of internal state without any breakpoints needed.

**Two advantages of ICE:**
1. Real-time behavior including interrupts and timing.
2. Can debug hardware-software interaction with real peripherals.

**(Award: 1 mark definition + 1 mark table + 1 mark advantages = 3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**Explain the role of the JTAG TAP controller. What are the mandatory JTAG signals as per IEEE 1149.1?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **JTAG TAP (Test Access Port) Controller** is a 16-state finite state machine defined by the IEEE 1149.1 standard. It sequences the boundary-scan test logic, instruction register (IR), and data register (DR) operations. The TAP controller is clocked by the **TCK** input and steered by the **TMS** (Test Mode Select) signal. It enables testing of interconnections on PCBs, programming of Flash memory, and on-chip debugging.

**Mandatory JTAG signals (as per IEEE 1149.1):**

1. **TCK (Test Clock):** Synchronizes all JTAG operations.
2. **TMS (Test Mode Select):** Selects the next state in the TAP FSM.
3. **TDI (Test Data In):** Serial data input to the instruction or data register.
4. **TDO (Test Data Out):** Serial data output from the active register.
5. **TRST (Test Reset):** Optional but recommended — asynchronous reset of the TAP FSM.

**(Award: 1 mark role + 1 mark list of signals + 1 mark explanations = 3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Module internal choice — within each module, students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model QP]*

**Q.A. (a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an **In-Circuit Emulator (ICE)**. How does it provide transparent visibility into the target processor's internal state?

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
An In-Circuit Emulator (ICE) is a hardware debugging tool that physically replaces the target microcontroller on the application board. It provides a **"bond-out"** version of the silicon that exposes internal buses, register files, and signals to the outside world via a probe pod connected to the host PC.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
*(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid architecture diagram — the ICE block contains: Bond-out silicon + Trace logic + Breakpoint comparators + Emulation memory + Host interface.)*

**Working (3 marks):**
1. The ICE probe contains a **bond-out chip** with extra package pins exposing internal address, data, and control buses.
2. The host PC downloads the user's program into a **dual-ported emulation memory** accessible by both the bond-out core and the host.
3. The user sets breakpoints in the IDE; these are converted to **address comparators** inside the ICE.
4. When the PC matches a breakpoint, the ICE asserts a **HALT** signal that halts the bond-out core on the next cycle.
5. The host can read the entire register file, program counter, and memory through the dual-port memory.
6. **Trace logic** captures every bus cycle in real-time into a circular trace buffer (typically 64 KB – 4 MB deep).

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- True real-time execution
- Unlimited software breakpoints
- Non-intrusive trace (even on halted state)

---

**Q.A. (b) [7 Marks]** Compare **JTAG** and **SWD** debug interfaces in detail. List at least four points of comparison and state why SWD is preferred for ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | JTAG (IEEE 1149.1) | SWD (ARM proprietary) |
|---|---|---|
| **Minimum Pin Count** | 4 (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO) | 2 (SWDIO, SWCLK) |
| **Trace Support** | Via TDO only (1-bit) | Via SWO (1-bit) or parallel |
| **Multi-Drop Support** | Daisy-chain (multiple devices) | Single target only (no chain) |
| **Standard** | Open IEEE standard | ARM proprietary |
| **Speed** | Up to 50 MHz TCK | Up to 50 MHz SWCLK |
| **Package Suitability** | Large packages (QFP) | Small packages (QFN, WLCSP) |

**Why SWD is preferred for Cortex-M (3 marks):**
1. **Pin savings:** 2 fewer pins (TCK+TMS → SWCLK; TDI+TDO → SWDIO bidirectional), critical for small packages.
2. **Lower power:** Less pin toggling reduces EMI and dynamic power.
3. **Built-in support:** All ARM Cortex-M cores integrate SWD natively; JTAG is optional.
4. **Faster error detection:** Parity bit on every SWD packet catches transmission errors.
5. **No need for TRST:** SWD resets via a special sequence on SWDIO.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model QP — Alternative Choice]*

**Q.B. (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **ARM CoreSight** debug architecture. List its sub-components and describe the role of the **DWT (Data Watchpoint and Trace)** unit in detail.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Introduction to CoreSight (1 mark):**
CoreSight is ARM's standardized on-chip debug and trace infrastructure embedded in Cortex-M and Cortex-A processors. It provides a unified, scalable framework for non-intrusive debugging, real-time trace, and performance profiling.

**Sub-Components (2 marks):**
1. **DAP (Debug Access Port):** External serial interface bridge.
2. **AHB-AP:** Converts DAP transactions to AHB bus cycles.
3. **FPB (Flash Patch and Breakpoint):** Hardware breakpoints and Flash remapping.
4. **DWT (Data Watchpoint and Trace):** Watchpoints, PC sampling, exception tracing.
5. **ITM (Instrumentation Trace Macrocell):** Software-instrumented trace (printf-style).
6. **ETM (Embedded Trace Macrocell):** Non-intrusive instruction trace.
7. **TPIU (Trace Port Interface Unit):** Formats and outputs trace data.

**DWT Detailed Role (4 marks):**
The DWT unit provides:
1. **Hardware Watchpoints:** Comparators that monitor memory accesses (read, write, or read-write) on a configurable address range. Typical count: 4 comparators.
2. **PC Sampling:** Periodically samples the program counter for code coverage profiling.
3. **Exception Tracing:** Logs every exception entry and exit with a timestamp, useful for profiling interrupt latency.
4. **Data Tracing:** Captures data values written to specified addresses (used in combination with ITM).
5. **Performance Counters:** Counts events like cycle count, fold instructions, and LSU operations.

**Practical Use Case:** A developer debugging a memory corruption bug sets a DWT watchpoint on the suspect address; the moment any write occurs, the DWT triggers a debug exception, allowing the developer to inspect the call stack and identify the rogue pointer.

---

**Q.B. (b) [7 Marks]** Describe the difference between a **hardware breakpoint** and a **software breakpoint**. When is each preferred? Show the memory layout of a software breakpoint in RAM.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Hardware Breakpoint:** Uses on-chip comparator hardware (FPB unit in ARM). The core compares the fetched address against pre-loaded comparator values; on match, it enters Debug state. Does not modify memory.
- **Software Breakpoint:** The debugger patches the user code in memory by replacing the original instruction with a `BKPT` opcode. When executed, the `BKPT` triggers a debug exception.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Hardware Breakpoint | Software Breakpoint |
|---|---|---|
| **Implementation** | On-chip comparator | Instruction patching |
| **Memory Modification** | No | Yes (in RAM/Flash) |
| **Limit** | 2–16 (FPB units) | Unlimited (in RAM) |
| **Works in Flash** | Yes | Sometimes (needs Flash patch) |
| **Works in ROM** | Yes | No (cannot patch ROM) |
| **Latency** | 1 cycle | Variable |

**Memory Layout of Software Breakpoint (3 marks):**
Consider the following code in RAM at address `0x2000 1000`:

```
Address       Original              After Breakpoint Set
0x20001000    0x4A05  (LDR r0,[pc,#20])   0xBE00  (BKPT #0)
0x20001002    0xF7FF  ...                  0xF7FF  (unchanged)
0x20001004    ...
```

The IDE displays: `main.c:42   int x = compute();   [BP#1]`

When the user clicks "Continue," the debugger:
1. Halt state: `MEM[0x20001000] = 0xBE00` (patched)
2. Resumes core
3. On hit, the `BKPT` triggers Debug exception
4. Debugger reports "BP#1 hit at main.c:42"
5. On next continue, the debugger restores: `MEM[0x20001000] = 0x4A05`

**When each is preferred (1 mark — bonus):**
- **Hardware breakpoints** are preferred for debugging code in **Flash or ROM** where patching is impossible, and for debugging code that self-modifies.
- **Software breakpoints** are preferred when the user needs **more breakpoints than available FPB comparators**, and the code resides in **RAM**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Conflating Simulator with Emulator:** A *simulator* models the **hardware**; an *emulator* runs the actual **target binary**. Writing "simulator = emulator" costs 2 marks immediately.
>
> 2. **Listing only 3 JTAG signals:** The IEEE 1149.1 standard defines **5 signals** (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO, TRST). Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for incomplete lists.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to state that software breakpoints modify memory:** Many students write "software breakpoints are faster" — this is *wrong*. They are **slower** (require read-modify-write) and **intrusive** (modify code). Hardware breakpoints are the non-intrusive ones.
>
> 4. **Writing "JTAG has 4 pins" without listing them:** Always name the pins — **TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO**. Generic statements lose marks.
>
> 5. **Confusing DWT with FPB:** The **FPB** handles **instruction** breakpoints; the **DWT** handles **data** watchpoints. Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks in CoreSight questions.
>
> 6. **Not mentioning the TAP FSM has 16 states:** This is a KTU favorite one-liner. State explicitly: *"The TAP controller is a 16-state finite state machine defined by IEEE 1149.1."*
>
> 7. **Skipping the reset signal TRST:** Many students omit TRST. Examiners may award or deduct a 0.5 mark depending on whether they deem it mandatory or optional.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must memorize before the exam):**

- [ ] **Emulator** = hardware/software replicating target processor behavior; **Debugger** = tool to observe and control execution state.
- [ ] **Three tool families:** ISS (software), OCD (JTAG/SWD), ICE (hardware bond-out).
- [ ] **JTAG mandatory signals:** TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO (TRST optional) — total **4 minimum, 5 with TRST**.
- [ ] **SWD signals:** SWDIO (bidirectional data), SWCLK (clock) — only **2 pins**, plus optional SWO.
- [ ] **TAP Controller:** 16-state FSM per IEEE 1149.1; states include TLR, RTI, Shift-DR, Shift-IR, Update-DR, Update-IR.
- [ ] **ARM CoreSight components:** DAP, AHB-AP, **FPB (instruction breakpoints)**, **DWT (data watchpoints)**, ITM (printf trace), ETM (instruction trace), TPIU (trace output).
- [ ] **Hardware breakpoints** use on-chip comparators (FPB), typically 2–16 units, **non-intrusive**, work in Flash/ROM.
- [ ] **Software breakpoints** patch memory with `BKPT` opcode (0xBE00 in Thumb), **intrusive**, unlimited in RAM.
- [ ] **Clock period formula:** $T = 1/f_{TCK}$. **Max TCK** constrained by PCB round-trip delay: $f_{TCK} \leq 1/(2 L t_{pd})$.
- [ ] **Watchpoint mask formula:** $MASK = \sim(2^{k} - 1)$ for a $2^{k}$ byte range, $COMPARE = \text{aligned base address}$.
- [ ] **SWD pin savings over JTAG:** 2 pins freed — critical for QFN/WLCSP packages.
- [ ] **JTAG vs SWD trade-off:** JTAG supports **daisy-chaining** of multiple devices; SWD supports only **single target**.
- [ ] **DWT** does: watchpoints, PC sampling, exception tracing, data tracing, performance counters.
- [ ] **FPB** does: instruction breakpoints and Flash patch/remap.
- [ ] **ITM** does: software-driven trace (printf redirection via SWO).
- [ ] **ETM** does: full non-intrusive real-time instruction trace (expensive, requires 4+ trace pins).
- [ ] **Key IDE tools:** Keil µVision, IAR Embedded Workbench, Eclipse + GCC + OpenOCD, STM32CubeIDE.
- [ ] **Key hardware probes:** Segger J-Link, ST-Link/V2, PEmicro Multilink, Lauterbach TRACE32, Black Magic Probe.
- [ ] **Trace buffer typical depth:** 64 KB to 4 MB.
- [ ] **Real-time debug requirement (ICE):** $t_{emul\_cycle} \leq t_{target\_cycle}$ (emulation must not slow the core).
- [ ] **JTAG clock range:** 1 kHz – 50 MHz (typical); 100 kHz is safe for long cables.
- [ ] **Common KTU buzzwords to use in answers:** *bond-out silicon, dual-port emulation memory, TAP state machine, boundary scan, instruction register, data register, DAP, AHB-AP, FPB, DWT, ITM, ETM, TPIU, SWO, breakpoint comparator, watchpoint, semihosting.*

---

> **End of Module 4 — Topic: Emulators and Debugging | OECIT726 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Target Hardware Debugging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT726 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Target Hardware Debugging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Target Hardware Debugging** is the process of identifying, isolating, and correcting hardware and software defects in an embedded system *in-situ* (i.e., while the target processor is embedded in its actual operating circuit). It involves specialized electronic instruments, on-chip debug modules, and standardized boundary-scan interfaces that grant the developer controlled visibility and controllability over CPU registers, memory, and I/O peripherals without removing the MCU from the board.

The "Target Hardware" is the *physical embedded system under test (SUT)* — typically a populated PCB containing the microcontroller, sensors, actuators, and power rails. The "Debugger" or "Emulator" is the *host tool* (PC + probe) that communicates with the target.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme vocabulary, Target Hardware Debugging is broadly classified into **three architectural tiers**:

| Tier | Mechanism | Visibility | Intrusiveness |
|------|-----------|------------|---------------|
| **Software Debug** | Breakpoints, single-step | Source-level | Low (uses on-chip resources) |
| **On-Chip Debug (OCD)** | JTAG / SWD / BDM | Register & memory | Medium (uses test pins) |
| **In-Circuit Emulator (ICE)** | Bond-out chip replacement | Full bus cycle | High (replaces target CPU) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Car Mechanic with a Windshield"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a mechanic inspecting a running car's engine. The hood is closed (firmware is locked inside the MCU). Without debug tools, the mechanic must *guess* what's happening inside — the equivalent of `printf()` debugging. Target Hardware Debugging is like installing a **transparent acrylic engine cover (the JTAG/BDM interface)** plus a **dashboard camera (trace port)** and a **mechanic's kill-switch (hardware breakpoint)**. Now the mechanic can see piston timing, fuel injection, and even *pause the engine mid-stroke* to inspect a frozen piston.

Another analogy: think of debugging as performing an **MRI on the CPU**:
- The **MRI machine** = the debug probe (e.g., J-Link, PE Micro, XDS).
- The **scan coils** = JTAG/SWD pins.
- The **patient** = the live, running target board.
- The **radiologist's monitor** = the IDE (Keil, IAR, Eclipse, Code Composer Studio).

## 1.3 Why Target Hardware Debugging is Harder than Desktop Debugging

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Unlike a desktop application where you can attach `gdb` to a running process, an embedded target often has:
> 1. **No operating system** to host the debugger (bare-metal).
> 2. **No console** for `printf` (no standard I/O).
> 3. **No virtual memory** — every address is physical.
> 4. **Real-time constraints** — pausing the CPU can disrupt peripherals (UART overruns, motor stalling).
> 5. **Limited resources** — on-chip breakpoints are scarce (e.g., 2–8 hardware breakpoints on ARM Cortex-M).

## 1.4 Syllabus-Highlighted Debug Interfaces (KTU 2024 Module 4)

The KTU Module 4 syllabus emphasizes the following hardware-level mechanisms:

1. **BDM (Background Debug Mode)** — Motorola / NXP (HCS08, ColdFire, PowerPC).
2. **JTAG (Joint Test Action Group, IEEE 1149.1)** — ARM, MIPS, PPC, AVR.
3. **SWD (Serial Wire Debug)** — ARM Cortex-M (2-wire alternative to JTAG).
4. **On-Chip Debug (OCD) modules** — built into the silicon.
5. **In-Circuit Emulator (ICE)** — older but still referenced.
6. **ROM Emulator** — replaces target flash during development.
7. **Logic Analyzer & Oscilloscope** — bus-level / signal-level debugging.
8. **Bond-out chips** — special test-package MCUs exposing internal buses.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Target Hardware Debugging

### Pillar 1 — On-Chip Debug Module (OCD)

Modern MCUs integrate a **dedicated debug logic block** directly in silicon. It runs *transparently* to the user's application and exposes internal state through a serial interface.

**Architecture (generic OCD block):**
- **Debug Control Register (DCR)** — configures mode (halt, step, run).
- **Breakpoint Unit** — compares PC, data addresses, or data values against programmed trigger values.
- **Trace Module (ETM, SWO, TPIU)** — streams execution history off-chip.
- **Debug Exception Handler** — responds to debug events (e.g., enters debug halt state on a breakpoint match).

> [!NOTE]
> **How it works internally:** When the breakpoint comparator matches, the OCD asynchronously asserts a `DBG_REQUEST` to the CPU core. The core completes the current instruction, then enters **Debug State** — a privileged halt where the host can read/write all registers and memory.

### Pillar 2 — Boundary Scan / JTAG (IEEE 1149.1)

JTAG was originally designed for **board-level interconnect testing** but was repurposed for silicon debug. It uses a **4-wire (or 5-wire with TRST)** Test Access Port (TAP):

| Pin | Name | Direction | Purpose |
|-----|------|-----------|---------|
| **TCK** | Test Clock | Input | Synchronous clock for TAP state machine |
| **TMS** | Test Mode Select | Input | State machine control |
| **TDI** | Test Data In | Input | Serial data into target |
| **TDO** | Test Data Out | Output | Serial data out of target |
| **TRST** | Test Reset *(optional)* | Input | Asynchronous TAP reset |

The heart of JTAG is the **TAP Controller** — a 16-state finite state machine (FSM). The host (probe) sequences TMS bits to navigate states such as `Test-Logic-Reset`, `Run-Test/Idle`, `Shift-DR`, `Shift-IR`, `Pause-DR`, `Update-DR`.

**Key JTAG Registers:**
- **BYPASS (1-bit)** — pass-through between TDI and TDO.
- **IDCODE (32-bit)** — manufacturer, part number, version.
- **BOUNDARY-SCAN (n × n)** — one cell per pin for interconnect test.
- **DEVICE ID** — identifies the silicon.
- **ICE registers (private)** — used by the on-chip debug logic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why JTAG dominates:** It is *non-intrusive* (shares pins with GPIO via alternate-function mux), *standardized* (IEEE 1149.1), and *chainable* — multiple devices on one PCB can be daisy-chained through a single 4-wire port. This is critical for multi-MCU boards.

### Pillar 3 — Serial Wire Debug (SWD)

ARM introduced SWD as a **2-wire alternative** to JTAG for pin-constrained Cortex-M devices:

| Pin | Name | Function |
|-----|------|----------|
| **SWDIO** | Serial Wire Data I/O | Bidirectional data |
| **SWCLK** | Serial Wire Clock | Clock |

SWD uses a **packet-based protocol** with start, stop, parity, and ACK bits, and supports **SWO (Serial Wire Output)** as a separate pin for trace streaming. It saves 2–3 pins and is more power-efficient in low-power designs.

### Pillar 4 — Background Debug Mode (BDM)

BDM is Motorola/NXP's proprietary debug interface (used in HCS08, ColdFire, HC12, older PowerPC). It uses a single bidirectional `BKGD` pin plus a `RESET` line.

**BDM Commands (partial list):**

| Command | Mnemonic | Function |
|---------|----------|----------|
| `0x28` | `READ_BYTE` | Read 8-bit memory/register |
| `0x29` | `WRITE_BYTE` | Write 8-bit memory/register |
| `0x2C` | `READ_PC` | Read program counter |
| `0x2B` | `WRITE_PC` | Write program counter |
| `0x34` | `GO` | Resume execution |
| `0x35` | `NOP` | Idle / sync |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why BDM exists:** Pre-JTAG era, BDM was designed specifically for *background* (non-intrusive) debugging. The CPU continues executing user code unless explicitly halted by the host. Many BDM implementations also allow **instruction substitution** — replacing a fetched opcode with a BDM command opcode, enabling read/write of CPU registers *while the application runs*.

## 2.2 Breakpoints — Hardware vs Software

| Property | Hardware Breakpoint | Software Breakpoint |
|----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Mechanism** | On-chip comparator watches PC/data | Debugger patches instruction with `BKPT` (0xBE on ARM) |
| **Resource** | Limited (2–8 units) | Unlimited (uses code memory) |
| **ROM compatible?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (needs writable memory) |
| **Intrusiveness** | None (no code modification) | Slight (instruction replaced) |
| **Trigger types** | PC match, data R/W match, complex sequences | PC match only |

**Software breakpoint math:** Each break requires one half-word or word in the instruction stream. For a 32-bit ARM instruction:
$$\text{Bytes consumed per SW break} = 4 \text{ bytes} \times N_{\text{breaks}}$$

**Hardware breakpoint math:** Number of breakpoints is fixed by silicon:
$$N_{\text{HW breaks}} = N_{\text{BP comparators}} \quad (\text{typically } 2 \text{ to } 8)$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every quantitative relationship you must memorize for Module 4 numerical problems.

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | JTAG chain length | $T_{\text{chain}} = N_{\text{devices}} \times t_{\text{bit}} + t_{\text{setup}}$ | seconds | $t_{\text{bit}} = 1 / f_{\text{TCK}}$ |
| 2 | SWD bit time | $t_{\text{SW}} = 1 / f_{\text{SWCLK}}$ | seconds | ARM spec: up to 50 MHz |
| 3 | Software breakpoint memory | $M_{\text{consumed}} = 4 \cdot N_{\text{break}} \text{ (ARM-32)}$ | bytes | Replaces original instruction |
| 4 | Hardware breakpoint limit | $N_{\text{HW}} = N_{\text{BP-comparators}}$ | integer | Set by silicon, fixed |
| 5 | Trace bandwidth | $B_{\text{trace}} = f_{\text{CPU}} \times W_{\text{inst}} \times \rho_{\text{compress}}$ | bits/s | $\rho$ = ETM compression ratio (typ. 5–20×) |
| 6 | JTAG TAP TCK max | $f_{\text{TCK,max}} \le f_{\text{CPU}} / 4$ | Hz | Rule of thumb, not absolute |
| 7 | Probe loading | $C_{\text{load,total}} = C_{\text{probe}} + C_{\text{trace}} + C_{\text{pin}}$ | farads | Keep < **20 pF** for high-speed TCK |
| 8 | Debugger latency | $t_{\text{halt}} = t_{\text{detect}} + t_{\text{pipe-drain}} + t_{\text{ack}}$ | seconds | Typically 5–50 µs |
| 9 | ROM emulator wait-state | $t_{\text{access}} = t_{\text{RAM}} + t_{\text{cable}} + t_{\text{mux}}$ | seconds | Cable adds 5–20 ns |
| 10 | SWO baud | $B_{\text{SWO}} = f_{\text{CPU}} / N_{\text{prescaler}}$ | bps | 2 MHz typical on Cortex-M |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** In a production team, the *trace bandwidth equation* (#5) determines whether you can use cheap SWO (1–2 Mbaud) or must invest in a parallel ETM trace port (8-/16-bit, hundreds of MHz). This single decision affects BOM and probe cost by an order of magnitude.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Target Hardware Debugging is not an academic exercise — it is the **single most important cost-driver in embedded firmware development**:

- **Automotive ECU calibration:** Engineers use JTAG + ETM trace to validate ISO 26262 functional safety.
- **Medical device firmware:** FDA requires traceable verification → JTAG logs become audit evidence.
- **IoT firmware update (OTA):** Engineers debug bootloader handoff using SWD single-wire.
- **Aerospace:** Bond-out chips + ICE used for MIL-STD-1553 bus verification.
- **Consumer electronics (phones):** JTAG-locked (security fuses blown) — debug disabled after production.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — JTAG TAP State Machine Timing

The JTAG TAP controller requires the host to drive TMS for one TCK cycle to transition between states. To enter `Shift-DR` from `Test-Logic-Reset`, the host must traverse a deterministic path.

**Given:**
- $f_{\text{TCK}} = 10 \text{ MHz}$ (typical JTAG clock)
- $N_{\text{devices}} = 4$ (chain of 4 MCUs)
- $L_{\text{IR}} = 4$ (Instruction Register length per device)
- We want to shift a 4-bit IDCODE command into the first device.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — TCK period:**
$$t_{\text{TCK}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{TCK}}} = \frac{1}{10 \times 10^{6}} = 100 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2 — TAP navigation to Shift-IR:**
From `Test-Logic-Reset`, the canonical path to `Shift-IR` is:
$$\text{TLR} \to \text{Run-Test/Idle} \to \text{Sel-DR} \to \text{Sel-IR} \to \text{Capture-IR} \to \text{Shift-IR}$$

This requires **5 TCK transitions** through states, plus **1 capture cycle** = **6 TCK cycles minimum**.

$$t_{\text{nav}} = 6 \times t_{\text{TCK}} = 6 \times 100 \text{ ns} = 600 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 3 — IR shift (4 bits for one device in chain of 4):**
Because the chain is serial, the total IR length is:
$$L_{\text{IR,total}} = L_{\text{IR}} \times N_{\text{devices}} = 4 \times 4 = 16 \text{ bits}$$

$$t_{\text{IR shift}} = L_{\text{IR,total}} \times t_{\text{TCK}} = 16 \times 100 \text{ ns} = 1600 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 4 — Exit to Update-IR:**
After the final shift bit, one more TCK transitions to `Exit1-IR`, then one to `Update-IR`.

$$t_{\text{update}} = 2 \times t_{\text{TCK}} = 200 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 5 — Total IR transaction time:**
$$t_{\text{IR,total}} = t_{\text{nav}} + t_{\text{IR shift}} + t_{\text{update}} = 600 + 1600 + 200 = 2400 \text{ ns} = 2.4 \text{ \mu s}$$

**Step 6 — Data register shift (32-bit IDCODE, 4 devices in chain):**
$$L_{\text{DR,total}} = 32 \times 4 = 128 \text{ bits}$$
$$t_{\text{DR,total}} = t_{\text{nav}} + L_{\text{DR,total}} \times t_{\text{TCK}} + t_{\text{update}} = 600 + 12800 + 200 = 13600 \text{ ns} = 13.6 \text{ \mu s}$$

**Step 7 — Total IDCODE read transaction:**
$$t_{\text{total}} = t_{\text{IR,total}} + t_{\text{DR,total}} = 2.4 + 13.6 = 16.0 \text{ \mu s}$$

**Step 8 — Throughput in transactions per second:**
$$R_{\text{TAP}} = \frac{1}{t_{\text{total}}} = \frac{1}{16.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 62500 \text{ transactions/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** A chained JTAG bus with 4 devices and 10 MHz TCK can perform ~62,500 register reads per second. This is more than enough for stepping, but is the *bottleneck* for high-bandwidth trace streaming — hence the need for parallel ETM ports.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Software vs Hardware Breakpoint Trade-off

**Scenario:** ARM Cortex-M4F, 32-bit instructions, 256 KB flash. Available hardware breakpoint comparators = **6**. You need to set **20 breakpoints** in your firmware.

**Step 1 — Hardware breakpoint feasibility:**
$$N_{\text{required}} = 20, \quad N_{\text{available}} = 6$$
$$N_{\text{required}} > N_{\text{available}} \implies \text{not all can be HW}$$

**Step 2 — Hardware breakpoints used:**
$$N_{\text{HW}} = \min(N_{\text{required}}, N_{\text{available}}) = 6$$

**Step 3 — Remaining software breakpoints:**
$$N_{\text{SW}} = N_{\text{required}} - N_{\text{HW}} = 20 - 6 = 14$$

**Step 4 — Flash memory consumed by SW breakpoints:**
$$M_{\text{flash}} = N_{\text{SW}} \times 4 \text{ bytes} = 14 \times 4 = 56 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 5 — Verification (does it fit?):**
$$M_{\text{flash}} = 56 \text{ bytes} \ll 256 \text{ KB} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Limitation check (ROM compatibility):**
If the target runs from **ROM (no flash overlay)**, the IDE *cannot* patch the instruction stream.
$$N_{\text{SW,ROM}} = 0, \quad N_{\text{eff}} = N_{\text{HW}} = 6 \quad \text{(only 6 breaks possible)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often assume breakpoints are "free". In ROM-based targets (e.g., boot ROM, secure enclave), only hardware breakpoints work. Always state the breakpoint mechanism in exam answers.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Trace Bandwidth Requirement

**Scenario:** A Cortex-M7 at $f_{\text{CPU}} = 216 \text{ MHz}$, 32-bit instructions, ETM compression ratio $\rho = 10$, SWO baud 2 Mbaud.

**Step 1 — Raw instruction trace rate:**
$$R_{\text{inst}} = f_{\text{CPU}} = 216 \times 10^{6} \text{ instructions/s}$$

**Step 2 — Raw bit rate (assuming every instruction is traced):**
$$B_{\text{raw}} = R_{\text{inst}} \times W_{\text{inst}} = 216 \times 10^{6} \times 32 = 6.912 \text{ Gbit/s}$$

**Step 3 — With ETM compression:**
$$B_{\text{ETM}} = \frac{B_{\text{raw}}}{\rho} = \frac{6.912 \text{ Gbit/s}}{10} = 691.2 \text{ Mbit/s}$$

**Step 4 — SWO feasibility check:**
$$B_{\text{SWO,max}} = 2 \text{ Mbit/s}$$
$$B_{\text{ETM}} = 691.2 \text{ Mbit/s} \gg B_{\text{SWO,max}}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** SWO is **infeasible** for full instruction trace on M7. Must use **parallel ETM (TPIU)** port with 4-, 8-, or 16-bit data width.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** This single calculation explains why high-performance MCUs (Cortex-M7, Cortex-A) require a dedicated **trace connector** on the PCB (Mictor-38 or smaller Samtec). Skipping this connector saves cost but cripples post-mortem analysis.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Debug Tool

| Target Class | Recommended Interface | Why |
|--------------|----------------------|-----|
| Small Cortex-M0 (8–32 pins) | **SWD (2-wire)** | Pin-limited, low-cost |
| Mid-range Cortex-M3/M4 | **JTAG or SWD** | Both supported |
| High-perf Cortex-M7/A | **JTAG + ETM** | Trace bandwidth needs |
| Legacy Motorola HCS08/ColdFire | **BDM** | Vendor-proprietary |
| Multi-MCU board | **JTAG chain** | Single connector for all |
| Secure / locked production chip | **Disabled** (fuses blown) | No post-production debug |
| In-system flash programming | **JTAG / SWD + bootloader** | Combined debug + ISP |
| Hard real-time motor control | **Trace + logic analyzer** | Need cycle-accurate visibility |
| Boot ROM / bootloader | **Hardware breakpoints only** | No flash to patch |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic OCD Architecture on an MCU

```mermaid
graph TD
    HOST[Host PC + IDE]
    PROBE[Debug Probe e.g. J-Link]
    TAP[TAP Controller JTAG SWD BDM]
    DCR[Debug Control Register]
    BPCOMP1[BP Comparator 1]
    BPCOMP2[BP Comparator 2]
    BPCOMPN[BP Comparator N]
    CPU[CPU Core]
    ROM[ROM or Flash]
    RAM[RAM]
    IO[Peripherals and GPIO]
    TRACE[Trace Module ETM SWO]
    SWO_PIN[SWO Pin]
    TPIU[TPIU Parallel Trace]

    HOST --> PROBE
    PROBE --> TAP
    TAP --> DCR
    DCR --> CPU
    TAP --> BPCOMP1
    TAP --> BPCOMP2
    TAP --> BPCOMPN
    BPCOMP1 --> CPU
    BPCOMP2 --> CPU
    BPCOMPN --> CPU
    CPU --> ROM
    CPU --> RAM
    CPU --> IO
    CPU --> TRACE
    TRACE --> SWO_PIN
    TRACE --> TPIU
    TPIU --> PROBE
    SWO_PIN --> PROBE
    PROBE --> HOST
```

## 4.2 JTAG TAP State Machine (High-Level Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    TLR[Test Logic Reset]
    RTI[Run Test Idle]
    SDR[Shift DR]
    CDR[Capture DR]
    E1DR[Exit1 DR]
    PDR[Pause DR]
    E2DR[Exit2 DR]
    UDR[Update DR]
    SIR[Shift IR]
    CIR[Capture IR]
    E1IR[Exit1 IR]
    PIR[Pause IR]
    E2IR[Exit2 IR]
    UIR[Update IR]

    TLR --> RTI
    RTI --> SDR
    RTI --> SIR
    SDR --> E1DR
    E1DR --> UDR
    E1DR --> PDR
    PDR --> E2DR
    E2DR --> SDR
    E2DR --> UDR
    UDR --> RTI
    SIR --> E1IR
    E1IR --> UIR
    E1IR --> PIR
    PIR --> E2IR
    E2IR --> SIR
    E2IR --> UIR
    UIR --> RTI
    UDR --> TLR
    UIR --> TLR
```

## 4.3 Debug Workflow — From Power-On to Breakpoint Hit

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Power On Target Board] --> B[Probe Detects TAP IDCODE]
    B --> C{Chain Valid?}
    C -- No --> Z[Check wiring and pullups]
    C -- Yes --> D[Issue Debug Request via JTAG SWD or BDM]
    D --> E[CPU Enters Debug State Halt]
    E --> F[Host Reads PC and Registers]
    F --> G[Download Flash Image]
    G --> H[Set Breakpoints HW or SW]
    H --> I[Issue GO command]
    I --> J[CPU Resumes Execution]
    J --> K{Breakpoint Match?}
    K -- No --> J
    K -- Yes --> L[CPU Halt and Notify Host]
    L --> F
```

## 4.4 Multi-Device JTAG Chain (Boundary Scan Daisy Chain)

```mermaid
graph LR
    PROBE[Debug Probe TDI Source]
    D1[Device 1 MCU]
    D2[Device 2 FPGA]
    D3[Device 3 DSP]
    D4[Device 4 CPLD]
    TDO_OUT[TDO to Probe Input]

    PROBE -->|TDI| D1
    D1 -->|TDO| D2
    D2 -->|TDO| D3
    D3 -->|TDO| D4
    D4 -->|TDO| TDO_OUT
    PROBE -.->|TCK and TMS shared| D1
    PROBE -.->|TCK and TMS shared| D2
    PROBE -.->|TCK and TMS shared| D3
    PROBE -.->|TCK and TMS shared| D4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** All devices share `TCK` and `TMS`. Data enters at `TDI`, propagates serially through each device's IR/DR, and exits at `TDO`. The host must shift `(L_{IR1} + L_{IR2} + L_{IR3} + L_{IR4})` bits to load one instruction — hence the chain length penalty from Section 3.1.

## 4.5 Comparison Matrix Diagram — SWD vs JTAG vs BDM

```mermaid
graph TD
    SWD[SWD 2 wire ARM Cortex]
    JTAG[JTAG 4 or 5 wire IEEE 1149.1]
    BDM[BDM 1 wire plus RESET Motorola]

    SWD --> SWD1[Pin Count 2 to 3]
    SWD --> SWD2[Speed up to 50 MHz]
    SWD --> SWD3[Used in Cortex M series]

    JTAG --> J1[Pin Count 4 to 5]
    JTAG --> J2[Speed up to 100 MHz]
    JTAG --> J3[Chainable multi device]

    BDM --> B1[Pin Count 1 to 2]
    BDM --> B2[Speed 1 to 5 MHz]
    BDM --> B3[Used in HCS08 and ColdFire]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define On-Chip Debugging (OCD). Mention any two advantages of on-chip debug modules over external in-circuit emulators.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
On-Chip Debugging (OCD) is a hardware-assisted debugging technique where dedicated debug logic is integrated *within the MCU silicon*. This logic includes breakpoint comparators, a debug control register, and a serial communication interface (JTAG, SWD, or BDM). The host debugger communicates with the on-chip module to halt the CPU, read/write registers and memory, set breakpoints, and stream execution trace — all without replacing the target processor.

**Two advantages (1 mark):**
1. **Low cost:** No bond-out chip or expensive pod is required; only a low-cost serial probe is needed.
2. **Non-intrusive:** The original silicon runs the application, so timing, electrical characteristics, and pin behavior are 100% representative of the production hardware.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Differentiate between hardware breakpoints and software breakpoints. State one scenario where each is mandatory.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Hardware Breakpoint | Software Breakpoint |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Mechanism | On-chip comparator watches PC address | Original instruction replaced with `BKPT` opcode |
| Memory impact | None (no code modification) | Consumes 2–4 bytes per break |
| ROM support | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |

**Mandatory scenarios (1 mark):**
- **Hardware breakpoint mandatory:** Debugging code that resides in **ROM, boot loader, or flash-locked regions** where the instruction stream cannot be patched.
- **Software breakpoint mandatory:** When the number of required breakpoints **exceeds the number of on-chip comparators** (e.g., 20 breaks on a chip with only 6 HW comparators).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, Part B questions of Module 4 offer an *internal choice* between two 14-mark questions, each with two 7-mark sub-parts. Both choices are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** 

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an on-chip debug (OCD) module. Describe the role of the TAP controller in JTAG-based OCD. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A JTAG chain contains 4 ARM Cortex-M4 devices. Each has a 4-bit Instruction Register and a 32-bit IDCODE Data Register. The TCK frequency is 8 MHz. Calculate: (i) the time to load one IDCODE instruction into the first device, (ii) the time to shift out its 32-bit IDCODE value, and (iii) the maximum number of IDCODE reads per second. **(7 marks)**

#### **Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Block diagram (3 marks):**
Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes. Mark allocation: 1 mark for host + probe, 1 mark for TAP + DCR, 1 mark for breakpoint comparators and trace module.

**Explanation (4 marks):**
- The **TAP controller** is a 16-state FSM clocked by TCK, sequenced by TMS. It acts as the *gatekeeper* between the host's serial protocol and all on-chip debug/test registers.
- The host drives a specific TMS sequence to navigate from `Test-Logic-Reset` → `Shift-IR` → load the desired instruction (e.g., `IDCODE`, `BYPASS`, `DEBUG_REQUEST`) → `Update-IR` → then re-navigates to `Shift-DR` → shifts the actual data (register read/write, breakpoint programming) → `Update-DR`.
- In JTAG-based OCD, the TAP controller interfaces to the **ICE registers** that drive the on-chip debug logic (breakpoint comparators, DCR, trace module). Without the TAP controller, no external host could ever access the debug logic.

#### **Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $N = 4$, $L_{IR} = 4$ bits, $L_{DR} = 32$ bits, $f_{TCK} = 8$ MHz.

**Step 1 — TCK period (1 mark):**
$$t_{TCK} = \frac{1}{f_{TCK}} = \frac{1}{8 \times 10^6} = 125 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2 — Total IR length (chain of 4):**
$$L_{IR,total} = 4 \times 4 = 16 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 — (i) Time to load IDCODE into first device (2 marks):**
- TAP navigation (TLR → Shift-IR): **6 TCK cycles** = 6 × 125 ns = 750 ns
- IR shift: **16 TCK cycles** = 16 × 125 ns = 2000 ns
- Update-IR: **2 TCK cycles** = 2 × 125 ns = 250 ns

$$t_{IR,total} = 750 + 2000 + 250 = 3000 \text{ ns} = 3.0 \text{ \mu s}$$

**Step 4 — (ii) Time to shift out 32-bit IDCODE (2 marks):**
The total DR length is:
$$L_{DR,total} = 32 \times 4 = 128 \text{ bits}$$
- TAP navigation (TLR → Shift-DR): **6 TCK cycles** = 750 ns
- DR shift: **128 TCK cycles** = 128 × 125 ns = 16000 ns
- Update-DR: **2 TCK cycles** = 250 ns

$$t_{DR,total} = 750 + 16000 + 250 = 17000 \text{ ns} = 17.0 \text{ \mu s}$$

**Step 5 — (iii) Maximum IDCODE reads per second (2 marks):**
$$t_{read} = t_{IR,total} + t_{DR,total} = 3.0 + 17.0 = 20.0 \text{ \mu s}$$
$$R_{read} = \frac{1}{t_{read}} = \frac{1}{20.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 50{,}000 \text{ IDCODE reads/s}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the difference between JTAG, SWD, and BDM debug interfaces. Compare them on the basis of pin count, speed, and target MCU family. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Cortex-M7 MCU runs at 200 MHz. An engineer wants to stream full instruction trace via ETM. Given: instruction width = 32 bits, ETM compression ratio = 12, SWO maximum baud = 2 Mbaud. Determine: (i) raw trace bit rate, (ii) compressed ETM rate, and (iii) whether SWO is feasible. Justify your answer. **(7 marks)**

#### **Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Comparison table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | JTAG | SWD | BDM |
|-----------|------|-----|-----|
| Pin count | 4 (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO) + optional TRST | 2 (SWDIO, SWCLK) + optional SWO | 1 (BKGD) + RESET |
| Max speed | Up to 100 MHz TCK | Up to 50 MHz SWCLK | 1–5 MHz |
| Target family | ARM, MIPS, PPC, AVR, FPGAs | ARM Cortex-M only | Motorola/NXP HCS08, ColdFire, HC12 |
| Daisy-chain | ✅ Yes (multi-device) | ❌ No (point-to-point) | ❌ No |
| Standard | IEEE 1149.1 | ARM proprietary | Motorola proprietary |
| Trace support | ETM/TPIU parallel | SWO (1 pin) | None typically |

**Explanation (2 marks):**
- **JTAG** is the *universal* debug standard; its 4-wire TAP is implemented on virtually every serious MCU/FPGA/DSP.
- **SWD** was introduced by ARM for pin-constrained Cortex-M parts (e.g., 20-pin QFN devices where 4 JTAG pins would consume 20% of the package).
- **BDM** is a *legacy* Motorola interface still used in automotive MCUs (HCS08, S08, ColdFire) where single-wire control and instruction-substitution debugging are valued.

#### **Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $f_{CPU} = 200$ MHz, $W_{inst} = 32$ bits, $\rho = 12$, $B_{SWO,max} = 2$ Mbaud.

**Step 1 — Instruction execution rate (1 mark):**
$$R_{inst} = f_{CPU} = 200 \times 10^6 \text{ instructions/s}$$

**Step 2 — (i) Raw trace bit rate (2 marks):**
$$B_{raw} = R_{inst} \times W_{inst} = 200 \times 10^6 \times 32 = 6.4 \text{ Gbit/s}$$

**Step 3 — (ii) Compressed ETM rate (2 marks):**
$$B_{ETM} = \frac{B_{raw}}{\rho} = \frac{6.4 \times 10^9}{12} \approx 533.3 \text{ Mbit/s}$$

**Step 4 — (iii) SWO feasibility check (2 marks):**
$$B_{SWO,max} = 2 \text{ Mbit/s} = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ bit/s}$$
$$B_{ETM} = 533.3 \text{ Mbit/s} \gg 2 \text{ Mbit/s}$$

$$\text{Deficit} = \frac{B_{ETM}}{B_{SWO,max}} = \frac{533.3}{2} \approx 266.7\times$$

**Conclusion:** SWO is **completely infeasible** — the trace data is approximately **267 times** faster than the SWO link can carry. The engineer **must** use the **parallel TPIU (Trace Port Interface Unit)** with 4-, 8-, or 16-bit width, and the PCB must include a high-speed trace connector (e.g., 20-pin or 38-pin Samtec/Mictor).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Module 4 Common Mistakes**
> 1. **Confusing JTAG and SWD pin counts.** JTAG = 4 (or 5 with TRST); SWD = 2 (or 3 with SWO). Mixing these up costs 1 mark per sub-question.
> 2. **Forgetting chain length in JTAG math.** When $N$ devices are chained, the IR and DR lengths are *multiplied* by $N$, not added once.
> 3. **Stating "debugger" when the question asks for "emulator".** An ICE replaces the CPU; a debugger uses on-chip resources. This is a 1–2 mark difference.
> 4. **Ignoring ROM vs flash in breakpoint questions.** Software breakpoints *cannot* work in ROM — examiners test this exact edge case.
> 5. **Forgetting the TAP navigation cycles (6 TCK minimum) before shifting.** Many students calculate only the shift time, missing the state-machine setup.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Target Hardware Debugging**

- ✅ **Target Hardware Debugging** = debug the *actual* embedded system *in situ*, with the CPU soldered on the board.
- ✅ **Three tiers:** Software debug → On-Chip Debug (OCD) → In-Circuit Emulator (ICE).
- ✅ **JTAG (IEEE 1149.1)** = 4-wire TAP (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO) + optional TRST. Uses a 16-state FSM. Chainable across multiple devices.
- ✅ **SWD** = 2-wire ARM Cortex-M alternative (SWDIO, SWCLK) + optional SWO. Saves 2–3 pins. Not chainable.
- ✅ **BDM** = Motorola 1-wire interface (BKGD + RESET) used in HCS08, ColdFire, HC12. Supports instruction substitution.
- ✅ **On-Chip Debug (OCD) module** = silicon block containing breakpoint comparators, DCR, trace module, and TAP interface.
- ✅ **Hardware breakpoints** use on-chip comparators → ROM-compatible, non-intrusive, limited count (2–8 typical).
- ✅ **Software breakpoints** patch code with `BKPT` (0xBE on ARM) → unlimited count, but not ROM-compatible.
- ✅ **In-Circuit Emulator (ICE)** = a *bond-out* chip in a special test package that exposes internal buses. Largely obsolete today.
- ✅ **ROM Emulator** = plug-in box that mimics target flash during development, allowing fast code download cycles.
- ✅ **Trace bandwidth equation:** $B = f_{CPU} \times W_{inst} / \rho$ — determines whether SWO is feasible or parallel ETM is required.
- ✅ **JTAG chain transaction time** = TAP navigation + (L × t_TCK) + Update state. Always include all three.
- ✅ **JTAG vs SWD speed:** JTAG up to 100 MHz TCK; SWD up to 50 MHz SWCLK.
- ✅ **Probe loading capacitance** must stay below **20 pF** for high TCK frequencies.
- ✅ **ARM Cortex-M** is the dominant KTU-curriculum architecture — expect SWD + JTAG dual-mode questions.
- ✅ **Lock bits / security fuses** can permanently disable all debug access — once blown, the chip is *undebuggable* (used in production for IP protection).
- ✅ **Logic analyzers and oscilloscopes** are *external* debug tools for bus-level and signal-level visibility; they complement but do not replace on-chip debug.
- ✅ **Bond-out chip** = a specially manufactured version of the MCU with extra pins exposing internal address/data buses — used in ICE.
- ✅ **SWO (Serial Wire Output)** is a 1-pin trace stream from the ITM (Instrumentation Trace Macrocell) of Cortex-M, suitable only for low-bandwidth event/printf-style trace.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boundary Scan
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT726 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Boundary Scan

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Scan** is a structured design-for-test (DFT) methodology standardized under **IEEE 1149.1** (commonly referred to as the **JTAG** standard, named after the Joint Test Action Group). It embeds a dedicated test infrastructure — comprising a Test Access Port (TAP), a TAP Controller state machine, an Instruction Register (IR), and Boundary Scan Cells (BSCs) — into an integrated circuit (IC) to facilitate the testing of interconnections between chips on a Printed Circuit Board (PCB), the testing of the IC itself, and the programming of non-volatile memory, without requiring physical test probes ("bed-of-nails" fixtures).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 OECIT726 syllabus (Module 4) positions Boundary Scan as a critical *test and debug* mechanism for embedded hardware, particularly the IEEE 1149.1 standard architecture and its TAP controller state machine.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a large office building (the **PCB**) with many rooms (**ICs**) connected by a network of hallways and pipes (**copper traces, signal lines, interconnects**). Traditionally, to check if a hallway is broken, you would need to physically inspect every stretch of flooring — this is the old **"bed-of-nails"** test method, which becomes impossible as PCBs shrink to smartphone scale.

**Boundary Scan** solves this by placing a *tiny, identical security camera* (a **Boundary Scan Cell**) at the **doorway of every room** — right where a wire leaves an IC pin. Each camera can:
- **SAMPLE** the signal leaving/entering the room (read mode),
- **PRELOAD** a known value into the wire (write mode),
- **EXTEST** — drive a known value down the hallway to see if it arrives correctly at the neighboring room's camera.

A central **security chief** (the **TAP Controller**) walks a uniformed guard (a **test instruction**) through the building, telling every camera when to record and when to play back. The four control wires (TDI, TDO, TMS, TCK) act like walkie-talkie channels the chief uses to coordinate everything from outside.

## 1.3 Core Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Value / Definition |
|---|---|
| Standard Designation | **IEEE Std 1149.1-2013** (current revision) |
| Mandatory TAP Signals | **TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO** (4 wires) |
| Optional TAP Signal | **TRST** (Test Reset, active low) |
| TAP Controller States | **16 states** (finite state machine) |
| Supply Voltage Independence | Logic is independent of Vcc level |
| Boundary Scan Cell Count | One per functional I/O pin |

> [!NOTE]
> The term **JTAG** originally referred to the Joint Test Action Group consortium (founded 1985), but in modern embedded parlance, "JTAG" and "IEEE 1149.1 Boundary Scan" are used interchangeably.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Five Architectural Building Blocks

The IEEE 1149.1 architecture is built from **five mandatory and optional sub-blocks**. Each is explained below with its "Why" and "How".

### Block 1 — Test Access Port (TAP)
**Why?** Provides a standardized, minimal-pin (4-wire) serial interface to test the chip from the outside, regardless of the chip's functional complexity.
**How?** Four mandatory signals + one optional reset:

| Pin | Full Name | Direction | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TCK** | Test Clock | Input | Synchronizes all TAP operations; independent of system clock |
| **TMS** | Test Mode Select | Input | Sampled on rising edge of TCK; controls state transitions of TAP FSM |
| **TDI** | Test Data In | Input | Serial data input into the shift registers (IR or selected DR) |
| **TDO** | Test Data Out | Output | Serial data output from the shift registers; tri-stated except when shifting |
| **TRST*** | Test Reset | Input | Asynchronous reset of the TAP controller (optional) |

### Block 2 — TAP Controller (16-State FSM)
**Why?** Determinism in test mode. A state machine guarantees that scan operations occur in a controlled, repeatable sequence regardless of operator skill.
**How?** A synchronous finite state machine whose state transitions are governed by the **TMS** signal sampled on each rising edge of TCK. It navigates between two parallel paths: the **DR (Data Register) path** and the **IR (Instruction Register) path**.

### Block 3 — Instruction Register (IR)
**Why?** Tells the chip *which* test operation to perform (e.g., bypass, interconnect test, sample).
**How?** A shift register, at least **2 bits** wide (typically 4–8 bits), into which an instruction opcode is serially shifted via TDI. The decoded instruction then selects which Data Register is connected between TDI and TDO.

### Block 4 — Data Registers (DRs)
**Why?** The actual mechanism for shifting, capturing, and updating test data.
**How?** Multiple registers exist, with one selected at a time by the active instruction:

- **Bypass Register (BR)** — mandatory; a single flip-flop that creates a 1-bit TDI→TDO path, isolating a chip from the scan chain.
- **Boundary Scan Register (BSR)** — mandatory; the chain of all Boundary Scan Cells wrapping every functional I/O pin.
- **Device Identification Register (IDCODE)** — optional; a 32-bit register holding the manufacturer's ID, part number, and version.
- **User Code Register** — optional; user-programmable ID.

### Block 5 — Boundary Scan Cells (BSCs)
**Why?** The actual test "eyes and hands" at every pin.
**How?** A small 2-to-1 multiplexer + flip-flop circuit is inserted between the **core logic** of the IC and the **physical pin**. Each cell can:
1. Operate transparently (normal functional mode).
2. **Capture** the logic value at the pin or core.
3. **Shift** data serially along the BSR chain.
4. **Update** (drive) a value onto the pin from the cell.

## 2.2 The 16 TAP Controller States — Grouped by Function

| Group | State | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Steady States** | Test-Logic-Reset | Resets the TAP; selects normal IC operation |
| | Run-Test/Idle | Idle state between scan operations |
| **DR Path** | Select-DR-Scan | Prepares for a Data Register scan |
| | Capture-DR | Loads parallel data into selected DR |
| | Shift-DR | Shifts data serially between TDI and TDO |
| | Exit1-DR | Exits shifting toward Update or Pause |
| | Pause-DR | Holds shift state temporarily |
| | Exit2-DR | Either resume shift or go to Update |
| | Update-DR | Parallel-loads shifted data into DR output latches |
| **IR Path** | Select-IR-Scan | Prepares for an Instruction Register scan |
| | Capture-IR | Loads a fixed pattern (usually `01...10`) into IR |
| | Shift-IR | Shifts new instruction opcode into IR |
| | Exit1-IR | Exit options for IR |
| | Pause-IR | Hold IR shifting |
| | Exit2-IR | Exit options for IR |
| | Update-IR | Latches new instruction into the instruction decoder |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Students must be able to draw or trace the **state transition diagram** and identify the **two parallel rails** (DR path on top, IR path on bottom) joined by the Select-* states.

## 2.3 Mandatory IEEE 1149.1 Instructions

| Instruction | Opcode Bits | Function | Mandatory? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BYPASS** | All 1s (e.g., `111...1`) | Routes TDI→TDO through the 1-bit Bypass Register | ✓ Yes |
| **EXTEST** | All 0s (e.g., `000...0`) | Tests off-chip interconnects; BSR cells drive pins with test data and capture pin responses | ✓ Yes |
| **SAMPLE/PRELOAD** | (e.g., `0001`) | Two functions in one: samples real-time pin data into BSR (non-intrusive observation), or preloads BSR with values to be driven on next EXTEST | ✓ Yes |
| **INTEST** | Vendor-specific | Tests the on-chip core logic by applying test vectors from BSR to core inputs and capturing core outputs | Optional |
| **IDCODE** | (e.g., `0010`) | Selects the 32-bit Device ID Register for shifting out | Optional |
| **USERCODE** | Vendor-specific | Selects user-defined identification register | Optional |
| **HIGHZ** | (e.g., `0100`) | Places all output pins in high-impedance state | Optional |
| **CLAMP** | Vendor-specific | Drives BSR-stored values onto outputs while bypass is selected | Optional |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Relationship / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Test Clock Frequency | $f_{TCK}$ | Hz; typically ≤ $\frac{f_{SYS}}{4}$ |
| Shift Register Length | $L_{chain} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} L_i$ | bits; sum of all chip BSR lengths in chain |
| Instruction Register Length | $L_{IR} \geq 2$ | bits; minimum 2 |
| Time to Load Instruction | $T_{IR} = (L_{IR} + 2) \cdot T_{TCK}$ | seconds; +2 for Select-IR-Scan and Update-IR overhead |
| Time to Shift DR Data | $T_{DR} = (L_{chain} + 2) \cdot T_{TCK}$ | seconds; +2 for state transition overhead |
| Number of TAP States | 16 | states |
| Capture-IR Default Value | `01...10` | — bit pattern, mandatory |
| TDO Tri-state Condition | Active only in Shift-IR & Shift-DR | logic condition |
| Interconnect Test Resolution | 1 logic bit per pin | — granularity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Boundary Scan eliminates **ICT (In-Circuit Test) fixtures**, enables **at-speed functional testing** of high-density BGA packages (where physical probe access is impossible), and provides a unified debug interface for **in-field firmware programming** of flash memory in microcontrollers and FPGAs.

## 2.5 Why It Matters in Real Embedded Engineering

- **BGA & QFN packages** have hidden solder balls/pads — only JTAG can test them.
- **In-System Programming (ISP)** of MCUs (ARM Cortex, AVR, MSP430) uses JTAG/SWD to flash firmware after PCB assembly.
- **FPGA configuration** (Xilinx, Altera/Intel) historically used JTAG for bitstream loading.
- **Multi-chip chain debugging** — boundary scan links multiple ICs into one serial chain governed by one TCK.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Time to Execute a Complete EXTEST Sequence

The EXTEST instruction is the most commonly tested scenario in KTU questions. We derive the total time required to execute one complete boundary scan interconnect test.

### Step 1 — Define the Variables
- $N$ = number of ICs in the scan chain
- $L_i$ = length of the BSR in IC $i$ (bits)
- $L_{IR}$ = length of the Instruction Register in each IC (assume same for all)
- $f_{TCK}$ = TCK clock frequency
- $T_{TCK} = \frac{1}{f_{TCK}}$ = TCK clock period (seconds)

### Step 2 — Express the Total Chain Length
The BSRs of all chips are connected in series. The total scan chain length is:

$$
L_{chain} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} L_i = L_1 + L_2 + L_3 + \cdots + L_N
$$

This is because, when EXTEST is active, the BSR of each IC is concatenated serially between TDI of the first IC and TDO of the last IC.

### Step 3 — Compute the Instruction Loading Time
Shifting an instruction into the IR requires one TCK cycle per IR bit, plus **2 extra TCK cycles** for the state machine transitions into and out of Shift-IR (Select-IR-Scan → Capture-IR → Shift-IR, and Shift-IR → Exit1-IR → Update-IR).

$$
T_{IR} = (L_{IR} + 2) \cdot T_{TCK}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the DR Shift Time for the BSR
Similarly, shifting test data through the BSR chain requires one TCK per bit plus 2 overhead cycles.

$$
T_{DR} = (L_{chain} + 2) \cdot T_{TCK}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Total EXTEST Time
A complete EXTEST cycle consists of: (1) loading EXTEST into IR, (2) shifting test vector into BSR, (3) one TCK cycle for the Update-DR state to apply the test pattern, and (4) one TCK cycle for the Capture-DR state to capture the response.

$$
T_{EXTEST} = T_{IR} + T_{DR} + 2 \cdot T_{TCK}
$$

### Step 6 — Substitute the Full Expressions
Substituting $T_{IR}$ and $T_{DR}$ from Steps 3 and 4:

$$
T_{EXTEST} = (L_{IR} + 2) \cdot T_{TCK} + (L_{chain} + 2) \cdot T_{TCK} + 2 \cdot T_{TCK}
$$

### Step 7 — Simplify by Combining Like Terms
Group the constant terms and the variable terms:

$$
T_{EXTEST} = (L_{IR} + L_{chain}) \cdot T_{TCK} + 6 \cdot T_{TCK}
$$

### Step 8 — Final Compact Form
Factor out $T_{TCK}$ to obtain the final working formula:

$$
T_{EXTEST} = \left(L_{IR} + L_{chain} + 6\right) \cdot T_{TCK}
$$

### Step 9 — Numerical Worked Example
Suppose we have **3 ICs** with BSR lengths $L_1 = 120$, $L_2 = 200$, $L_3 = 80$ bits, $L_{IR} = 4$ bits per chip, and $f_{TCK} = 10$ MHz.

First, compute the chain length:

$$
L_{chain} = 120 + 200 + 80 = 400 \text{ bits}
$$

Next, compute $T_{TCK}$:

$$
T_{TCK} = \frac{1}{10 \times 10^6} = 1 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} = 100 \text{ ns}
$$

Now apply the final formula:

$$
T_{EXTEST} = (4 + 400 + 6) \cdot 100 \text{ ns} = 410 \cdot 100 \text{ ns} = 41{,}000 \text{ ns} = 41 \text{ µs}
$$

### Step 10 — Interpretation
Thus, a single EXTEST vector takes **41 microseconds** to apply. For a typical interconnect test suite of 1000 vectors, the total test time would be:

$$
T_{suite} = 1000 \times 41 \text{ µs} = 41 \text{ ms}
$$

This is the standard figure embedded engineers use to estimate factory test throughput.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Instruction-to-Register Selection

| Active Instruction | DR Selected (TDI→TDO Path) | Length of DR | Captured Data | Driven Data |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **BYPASS** | Bypass Register | 1 bit | `0` | N/A (no driving) |
| **EXTEST** | Boundary Scan Register | $L_{BSR}$ | Pin values | Test pattern (parallel update) |
| **SAMPLE** | Boundary Scan Register | $L_{BSR}$ | Real-time pin/core values | None (passive) |
| **PRELOAD** | Boundary Scan Register | $L_{BSR}$ | None | Preloads BSR outputs (no drive yet) |
| **INTEST** | Boundary Scan Register | $L_{BSR}$ | Core output values | Test pattern to core inputs |
| **IDCODE** | IDCODE Register | 32 bits | Hard-wired manufacturer ID | None |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Boundary Scan Cell Operating Modes

| Mode | Multiplexer Select | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Normal** | Functional path | BSC is transparent; core logic drives/reads pin normally |
| **Capture** | Capture path | Pin or core value is loaded into the BSC flip-flop |
| **Shift** | Shift path | Data serially moves along the BSR chain |
| **Update** | Update path | BSC update flip-flop loads the shifted value to drive the pin |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Logic Check:** In **EXTEST**, the cell drives the pin (Update) and captures the pin response (Capture) — this two-step handshake is what makes interconnect testing possible.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IEEE 1149.1 Architecture — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    extTCK([TCK Input])
    extTMS([TMS Input])
    extTDI([TDI Input])
    extTRST([TRST Input - optional])
    extTDO([TDO Output])

    tapC["TAP CONTROLLER\n16-State FSM\nDrives state-dependent control signals"]

    instReg["INSTRUCTION REGISTER IR\nShift register; min 2 bits\nHolds current opcode"]

    instDec["INSTRUCTION DECODER\nDecodes IR output\nGenerates select signals for DRs"]

    bypassReg["BYPASS REGISTER\n1-bit DR\nTDI to TDO short path"]

    bsrReg["BOUNDARY SCAN REGISTER BSR\nChain of all BSCs\nLength = sum of pin counts"]

    idcodeReg["IDCODE REGISTER\n32-bit DR\nManufacturer plus part number"]

    drMux["OUTPUT MULTIPLEXER\nSelects active DR\nbased on decoded instruction"]

    coreLogic["IC CORE LOGIC\nFunctional circuitry\nexcluded during EXTEST"]

    pinPad([I/O PINS / PACKAGE PADS])

    extTCK --> tapC
    extTMS --> tapC
    extTRST --> tapC
    extTDI --> instReg
    extTDI --> bypassReg
    extTDI --> bsrReg
    extTDI --> idcodeReg

    tapC -- control lines --> instReg
    tapC -- control lines --> bypassReg
    tapC -- control lines --> bsrReg
    tapC -- control lines --> idcodeReg

    instReg --> instDec
    instDec -- select signals --> drMux

    bypassReg --> drMux
    bsrReg --> drMux
    idcodeReg --> drMux
    drMux --> extTDO

    bsrReg -- scan cells inserted here --> pinPad
    coreLogic -- normal functional path --> pinPad
```

## 4.2 TAP Controller State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TLR([Test Logic Reset])
    RTI([Run Test Idle])
    SDS([Select DR Scan])
    CDR([Capture DR])
    SDR([Shift DR])
    E1DR([Exit1 DR])
    PDR([Pause DR])
    E2DR([Exit2 DR])
    UDR([Update DR])
    SIS([Select IR Scan])
    CIR([Capture IR])
    SIR([Shift IR])
    E1IR([Exit1 IR])
    PIR([Pause IR])
    E2IR([Exit2 IR])
    UIR([Update IR])

    TLR -->|TMS=0| RTI
    RTI -->|TMS=1| TLR
    RTI -->|TMS=0| SDS
    SDS -->|TMS=0| CDR
    SDS -->|TMS=1| SIS
    CDR -->|TMS=0| SDR
    CDR -->|TMS=1| E1DR
    SDR -->|TMS=0| SDR
    SDR -->|TMS=1| E1DR
    E1DR -->|TMS=0| PDR
    E1DR -->|TMS=1| UDR
    PDR -->|TMS=0| PDR
    PDR -->|TMS=1| E2DR
    E2DR -->|TMS=0| SDR
    E2DR -->|TMS=1| UDR
    UDR -->|TMS=0| RTI
    UDR -->|TMS=1| TLR
    SIS -->|TMS=0| CIR
    SIS -->|TMS=1| TLR
    CIR -->|TMS=0| SIR
    CIR -->|TMS=1| E1IR
    SIR -->|TMS=0| SIR
    SIR -->|TMS=1| E1IR
    E1IR -->|TMS=0| PIR
    E1IR -->|TMS=1| UIR
    PIR -->|TMS=0| PIR
    PIR -->|TMS=1| E2IR
    E2IR -->|TMS=0| SIR
    E2IR -->|TMS=1| UIR
    UIR -->|TMS=0| RTI
    UIR -->|TMS=1| TLR
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each arrow label `TMS=X` means *"on the next rising edge of TCK, if TMS equals X, the FSM transitions along this arrow."* All transitions occur on the **rising edge of TCK**.

## 4.3 Multi-Chip Boundary Scan Chain — PCB-Level View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    tester([Boundary Scan Master\nExternal Tester])
    ic1["IC 1\nBSR Length L1"]
    ic2["IC 2\nBSR Length L2"]
    ic3["IC 3\nBSR Length L3"]

    tester -- TDI --> ic1
    ic1 -- daisy chain --> ic2
    ic2 -- daisy chain --> ic3
    ic3 -- TDO --> tester

    tester -- shared TCK --> ic1
    tester -- shared TCK --> ic2
    tester -- shared TCK --> ic3

    tester -- shared TMS --> ic1
    tester -- shared TMS --> ic2
    tester -- shared TMS --> ic3

    ic1 --- pcb1([PCB Trace A])
    ic2 --- pcb1
    ic2 --- pcb2([PCB Trace B])
    ic3 --- pcb2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** A single 4-wire TAP (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO) can test an entire PCB by daisy-chaining the BSRs of every compliant IC. This is the **Interconnect Test** paradigm.
