# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define Boundary Scan. List the four mandatory signals of the IEEE 1149.1 Test Access Port (TAP).**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Scan** is a design-for-test technique standardized by **IEEE 1149.1** that embeds test cells at every I/O pin of an IC, enabling testing of inter-chip connections on a PCB via a 4-wire serial interface, eliminating the need for physical test probes.

The four mandatory TAP signals are:

1. **TCK (Test Clock)** — synchronizes all boundary scan operations; applied independently of the system clock.
2. **TMS (Test Mode Select)** — sampled on the rising edge of TCK; controls transitions of the 16-state TAP controller FSM.
3. **TDI (Test Data In)** — serial input to the Instruction Register or selected Data Register.
4. **TDO (Test Data Out)** — serial output from the currently selected register; tri-stated except during shifting.

*(3 marks — definition 1 mark, four signals with 0.5 each)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Differentiate between the BYPASS and EXTEST instructions of IEEE 1149.1.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | BYPASS | EXTEST |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Isolates the IC from the scan chain | Tests interconnections between ICs on a PCB |
| Data Register Selected | 1-bit Bypass Register | Boundary Scan Register (BSR) |
| Effect on Pins | No effect — chip operates normally | BSR cells drive test patterns onto output pins and capture pin responses |
| Scan Chain Length | Effective 1 bit per IC | Full BSR length per IC |
| Typical Opcode | All 1s (e.g., `1111`) | All 0s (e.g., `0000`) |

*(3 marks — purpose 1 mark, DR selected 1 mark, opcode / pin effect 1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Model Paper]`

**Q: (a) Explain the architecture of the IEEE 1149.1 Boundary Scan standard with a neat block diagram. List and briefly describe the three mandatory instructions. (7 marks)**

**(b) Draw and explain the 16-state TAP controller state diagram. Discuss the role of TMS and TCK in controlling state transitions. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Architecture of IEEE 1149.1 Boundary Scan**

The IEEE 1149.1 architecture consists of the following major blocks:

1. **Test Access Port (TAP):** A 4-pin (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO) serial interface that serves as the gateway for all test data and control into and out of the chip. The optional **TRST** pin provides an asynchronous reset of the TAP controller.

2. **TAP Controller:** A 16-state finite state machine clocked by TCK and directed by TMS. It generates control signals that synchronize test operations.

3. **Instruction Register (IR):** A shift register of minimum 2 bits, into which test instruction opcodes are shifted from TDI. The IR holds the currently active instruction and feeds it to the instruction decoder.

4. **Instruction Decoder:** Decodes the IR contents to generate select signals for the appropriate Data Register.

5. **Data Registers (DRs):** Multiple DRs may exist, but only one is active at a time:
   - **Bypass Register (BR):** 1-bit, mandatory.
   - **Boundary Scan Register (BSR):** Chain of BSCs at every I/O pin, mandatory.
   - **Device Identification (IDCODE) Register:** 32-bit, optional.

6. **Boundary Scan Cells (BSCs):** Inserted between the core logic and the I/O pad. Each cell contains capture and update flip-flops plus a multiplexer.

**Three Mandatory Instructions:**

- **BYPASS:** Opcode = all 1s. Selects the 1-bit Bypass Register, allowing TDI-to-TDO to be a single flip-flop. Used to logically remove a chip from a multi-chip scan chain to reduce test time.
- **EXTEST:** Opcode = all 0s. Selects the BSR for testing off-chip interconnects. The BSR drives test patterns onto output pins and captures responses on input pins.
- **SAMPLE/PRELOAD:** Opcode = e.g., `0001`. Two functions in one: SAMPLE non-intrusively captures real-time pin data into the BSR for observation; PRELOAD loads test data into the BSR update latches in preparation for the next EXTEST.

*(7 marks: architecture blocks 4 marks, three instructions 3 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**The 16-State TAP Controller State Diagram**

*(Refer to the Mermaid state diagram in SECTION 4.2.)*

**Grouping of States:**

- **Two steady states:** Test-Logic-Reset, Run-Test/Idle.
- **Data Register path (8 states):** Select-DR-Scan, Capture-DR, Shift-DR, Exit1-DR, Pause-DR, Exit2-DR, Update-DR.
- **Instruction Register path (6 states):** Select-IR-Scan, Capture-IR, Shift-IR, Exit1-IR, Pause-IR, Exit2-IR, Update-IR.

**Role of TMS and TCK:**

- **TCK (Test Clock):** Provides the master timing for all TAP operations. **All state transitions and data shifts occur synchronously on the rising edge of TCK.** TCK is decoupled from the system clock, allowing test operations even when the system clock is absent or faulty.

- **TMS (Test Mode Select):** A single bit that, when sampled at the rising edge of TCK, decides the next state of the FSM. By sequencing TMS high or low in a controlled pattern, the external tester walks the FSM through Capture-DR/IR, Shift-DR/IR, Update-DR/IR, and back to Run-Test/Idle.

**Typical EXTEST Sequence:**

1. Force TMS = 1 for 5 TCK cycles → reach Test-Logic-Reset.
2. TMS = 0 → Run-Test/Idle → Select-DR-Scan → Select-IR-Scan.
3. Shift EXTEST opcode through IR (TMS = 0 during shift, TMS = 1 to exit).
4. Update-IR latches the instruction.
5. Enter Select-DR-Scan → Capture-DR → Shift-DR (shift test vector into BSR) → Update-DR (apply test pattern to PCB).
6. Capture-DR reads responses; Shift-DR shifts them out for comparison.

*(7 marks: state diagram 3 marks, role of TMS/TCK 2 marks, EXTEST sequence walk-through 2 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Supplementary]`

**Q: (a) With a diagram, explain the structure of a Boundary Scan Cell. Discuss its four modes of operation. (7 marks)**

**(b) A PCB has 4 ICs in a boundary scan chain with BSR lengths 150, 220, 90, and 130 bits respectively. The IR length of each IC is 4 bits, and the TCK frequency is 5 MHz. Compute the total time to load the EXTEST instruction and shift one test vector through the chain. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Structure of a Boundary Scan Cell (BSC):**

A BSC is a small sequential circuit inserted between the **core logic** of the IC and the **I/O pad (pin)**. A typical BSC contains:

1. A **Capture flip-flop** (FF1) — captures the value at the pin or core.
2. An **Update flip-flop** (FF2) — holds the value to be driven onto the pin.
3. A **2-to-1 multiplexer (M1)** — selects between normal mode and capture mode.
4. A **2-to-1 multiplexer (M2)** — selects between shift and update.
5. **Three control signals** from the TAP controller: **ShiftDR, ClockDR, UpdateDR**.

**Four Modes of Operation:**

| Mode | Function | Active Signal |
|---|---|---|
| **Normal Mode** | BSC is transparent; core drives the pin and reads from the pin | ShiftDR = 0, UpdateDR = 0 |
| **Capture Mode** | Value at the pin (or core output) is loaded into the capture flip-flop | ShiftDR = 0, ClockDR pulse in Capture-DR |
| **Shift Mode** | Data serially propagates along the BSR chain from one BSC to the next | ShiftDR = 1, ClockDR pulses in Shift-DR |
| **Update Mode** | Value in the capture FF is transferred to the update FF and driven onto the pin | UpdateDR pulse in Update-DR |

**Working in EXTEST:** In Update Mode, the BSR drives a known pattern onto the output pin. This pattern travels through the PCB trace and arrives at the input pin of the next IC. That pin's BSC, in Capture Mode, samples the arriving value, which is then shifted out via the scan chain for comparison with the expected value. A mismatch indicates an open, short, or stuck-at fault on the interconnect.

*(7 marks: cell structure 3 marks, four modes 3 marks, EXTEST application 1 mark)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- 4 ICs with BSR lengths: $L_1 = 150$, $L_2 = 220$, $L_3 = 90$, $L_4 = 130$ bits.
- $L_{IR} = 4$ bits per IC.
- $f_{TCK} = 5$ MHz, so $T_{TCK} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^6} = 0.2 \text{ µs} = 200 \text{ ns}$.

**Step 1 — Total BSR chain length:**

$$
L_{chain} = L_1 + L_2 + L_3 + L_4 = 150 + 220 + 90 + 130 = 590 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Instruction loading time:**

$$
T_{IR} = (L_{IR} + 2) \cdot T_{TCK} = (4 + 2) \cdot 200 \text{ ns} = 6 \cdot 200 \text{ ns} = 1200 \text{ ns} = 1.2 \text{ µs}
$$

**Step 3 — DR shift time (one test vector):**

$$
T_{DR} = (L_{chain} + 2) \cdot T_{TCK} = (590 + 2) \cdot 200 \text{ ns} = 592 \cdot 200 \text{ ns} = 118{,}400 \text{ ns} = 118.4 \text{ µs}
$$

**Step 4 — Total time for one EXTEST cycle (IR load + DR shift + Update + Capture overhead = 2 TCK):**

$$
T_{EXTEST} = T_{IR} + T_{DR} + 2 \cdot T_{TCK}
$$

Substituting:

$$
T_{EXTEST} = 1.2 \text{ µs} + 118.4 \text{ µs} + 0.4 \text{ µs} = 120.0 \text{ µs}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{T_{EXTEST} = 120 \text{ µs}}
$$

**Interpretation:** Each interconnect test vector on this 4-IC PCB requires **120 microseconds** to apply and capture. For a production test of 500 vectors, total test time = $500 \times 120 \text{ µs} = 60 \text{ ms}$, which is well within the budget of in-circuit testers.

*(7 marks: chain length 1 mark, $T_{IR}$ 2 marks, $T_{DR}$ 2 marks, total time 2 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the +2 overhead** in $T_{IR}$ and $T_{DR}$ formulas. Many students write $(L_{chain}) \cdot T_{TCK}$, losing 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing SAMPLE and EXTEST.** SAMPLE is non-intrusive (no driving); EXTEST actively drives pins. Examiners deduct marks if this distinction is muddled.
> 3. **Drawing the TAP state diagram with the DR and IR paths merged or incorrectly labelled.** Always show the two parallel rails joined by Select-DR-Scan and Select-IR-Scan.
> 4. **Forgetting the Capture-IR default pattern** `01...10` when describing the IR.
> 5. **Stating that BSR length equals number of pins.** It equals the number of *functional I/O pins wrapped with BSCs*, which may exclude power, ground, or analog pins.
> 6. **Mixing up TRST with TMS.** TRST is an optional asynchronous reset; TMS is the synchronous state controller.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Boundary Scan = IEEE 1149.1 = JTAG.** These three terms are synonymous in modern embedded parlance.
- **Four mandatory TAP pins:** **TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO**; **TRST** is optional.
- **TAP Controller = 16-state FSM**; transitions occur on **rising edge of TCK**; **TMS** decides the next state.
- **State diagram has two parallel rails** (DR path and IR path) joined by Select-DR-Scan / Select-IR-Scan.
- **Three mandatory instructions:** **BYPASS** (1-bit), **EXTEST** (BSR, drive+ capture), **SAMPLE/PRELOAD** (BSR, observe or preload).
- **BYPASS opcode = all 1s; EXTEST opcode = all 0s** (typical convention).
- **Boundary Scan Cell** sits between core logic and I/O pad; has four modes: Normal, Capture, Shift, Update.
- **EXTEST sequence** = Test-Logic-Reset → Run-Test/Idle → Select-IR-Scan → Capture-IR → Shift-IR → Update-IR → Select-DR-Scan → Capture-DR → Shift-DR → Update-DR → Repeat.
- **TDO is tri-stated** except during Shift-DR and Shift-IR states.
- **Capture-IR loads `01...10`** by default into the IR.
- **Time formula:** $T_{EXTEST} = (L_{IR} + L_{chain} + 6) \cdot T_{TCK}$ — remember the **+6** constant.
- **Use cases:** PCB interconnect test, BGA/QFN testing, in-system programming (ISP), FPGA configuration, multi-IC debug chains.
- **Key advantage:** Eliminates "bed-of-nails" test fixtures; works for hidden-pad packages.
- **Chain length = sum of all IC BSR lengths** in the daisy-chained scan path.
- **One TCK, one TMS** are shared by all ICs in the chain; only TDI/TDO differ per chip.
- **JTAG vs SWD:** SWD (Serial Wire Debug) is a 2-wire ARM alternative, but it is *not* boundary scan — keep the distinction clear.
- **IDCODE register is 32 bits**, contains manufacturer ID, part number, version.
